topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Purposes of Estimates; Contingencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Purposes of Estimates — The KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** An **estimate** is a detailed calculation of the quantities of various items of work and their probable cost, prepared before the execution of a construction project. The **purposes of estimates** refer to the various technical, administrative, financial, and legal objectives that an estimate serves throughout the life-cycle of a civil engineering project — from conception and design to execution, financing, and even post-construction valuation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCCET601 – Quantity Surveying and Valuation*, Module 1 explicitly mandates that students master **why** an estimate is prepared (its purpose) before they learn **how** to prepare one (its methods). The Board Examiner expects students to relate every purpose to a real stakeholder — the client, the contractor, the bank, the government auditor, or the insurer.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are planning a one-week trip to Munnar with your family of four. Before leaving, you make a rough plan — fuel cost (≈ ₹3,000), hotel (≈ ₹8,000), food (≈ ₹5,000), entry tickets (≈ ₹2,000), and a small buffer (≈ ₹2,000) for unexpected things like a sudden downpour forcing you to take a taxi. The total **trip estimate** is ₹20,000. 
> 
> The purposes of this estimate are: (1) to know **how much money you need**, (2) to **request funds** from your parents, (3) to **compare** Munnar with Wayanad, (4) to **control overspending** during the trip, and (5) to cover the **unforeseen** taxi ride — that buffer is your **contingency**.
> 
> A construction estimate works **exactly the same way**, except the numbers are in **lakhs and crores**, the stakeholders are banks/government departments, and the "buffer for taxi" is a formally calculated **percentage called contingency** (usually **3 % to 5 %** of the project cost).

## Key Stakeholders and Their Reasons for an Estimate

The KTU syllabus classifies the purposes of an estimate under the following functional heads:

1. **Administrative Sanction** — For the owner/employer to approve the project budget.
2. **Technical Sanction** — For the engineer-in-charge to certify that the estimate is technically sound and executable.
3. **Tendering & Contractual Purposes** — To invite bids and award the work.
4. **Financial / Banking Purposes** — To obtain loans and release funds in phases.
5. **Execution Control** — To monitor the actual expenditure against the estimated cost (a baseline for Earned Value Management).
6. **Statutory & Legal Purposes** — For valuation, insurance, arbitration, and dispute resolution.
7. **Resource Planning** — To plan materials, labour, machinery, and time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The phrase *"Purposes of Estimates"* is a frequently repeated **2-mark / 3-mark** definition question. Students must memorise the list and be able to map each purpose to a specific stakeholder.

---

## Contingencies — The KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** **Contingencies** are a **percentage provision** added to the estimated cost of a work to cover the cost of **unforeseen items, probable changes in design, minor alterations, price fluctuations, and incidental expenses** that are not explicitly itemised in the detailed estimate but are likely to arise during execution.

In every KTU Board Question Paper, the topic of **Contingencies** is clubbed with estimates. The examiner typically asks: *"What are contingencies? Why are they provided? What percentage is usually adopted in PWD/CPWD works?"*

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of contingencies as the **"rainy-day fund"** in your monthly budget. You plan for rent, food, and transport — but you keep a small extra amount (say 5 %) for things like an unexpected medical bill, a friend's wedding contribution, or a sudden repair. You don't know **what** the extra expense will be, but you know **something unexpected will happen**. The builder does the same — he adds a contingency to his estimate so that an unexpected rock in the excavation, a slight design change, or a cement price hike doesn't financially paralyse the project.

## Why Contingencies Cannot Be Zero

A *zero contingency* estimate is academically incorrect and professionally dangerous. The KTU Board expects the student to understand that:

- No estimate, however detailed, can foresee every site condition.
- Hidden strata, underground water, rock outcrops, and seasonal price escalations are routine in Kerala.
- Government departments (PWD, CPWD, KWA, LSGD) **mandate** a minimum contingency percentage as part of their schedule of rates and standard data book.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember purposes of estimates — "A T F E S":**
> **A** — Administrative approval
> **T** — Technical sanction
> **F** — Financing / Funds arrangement
> **E** — Execution control / Cost monitoring
> **S** — Statutory, Legal & Insurance valuation

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember types of contingencies — "2 P":**
> **P**hysical Contingencies (unforeseen site/execution items)
> **P**rice Contingencies (inflation, market rate fluctuation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Purposes of Estimates

The KTU 2024 scheme treats this as a foundational concept. Each purpose is mapped to a stakeholder and a project stage.

### A. Administrative & Financial Purposes

- **To obtain Administrative Approval (AA)** from the competent authority of the department (e.g., the State Government for works above ₹1 Cr in PWD Kerala). The estimate is the basis on which AA is granted.
- **To obtain Technical Sanction (TS)** from the engineering authority (e.g., Chief Engineer / Superintending Engineer in PWD). TS certifies that the detailed estimate is technically complete and rates are as per the current **Schedule of Rates (SOR)**.
- **To arrange finance** — Banks (SBI, Canara, Federal Bank) and financial institutions release loan amounts in stages based on the approved estimate.
- **To control and regulate capital expenditure** — The estimate acts as a **cost benchmark**. Variations beyond 5 % usually require revised administrative approval.

### B. Tendering & Contractual Purposes

- **To invite tenders** (Item-rate, Lump-sum, EPC, Design-Build). The estimate becomes the basis for the **Engineer's Estimate** (also called the **Schedule of Rates estimate**) on which tenders are floated.
- **To compare tenders** — A bid that is ± 10 % of the estimated cost is generally considered reasonable. Bids far below indicate unbalanced quoting; bids far above indicate cartelisation.
- **To prepare contract documents**, including Bills of Quantities (BoQ), drawings, and conditions of contract.

### C. Execution & Resource Planning Purposes

- **To plan materials, labour, and machinery** — Quantities of cement, steel, aggregates, bricks, and timber are derived from the estimate, enabling advance procurement.
- **To schedule construction** — The estimate is input to the **Construction Programme** and the **Network Diagram (CPM/PERT)**.
- **To monitor progress and payment** — Running Account (RA) bills are processed by measuring executed quantities and applying estimated rates.

### D. Statutory, Legal & Valuation Purposes

- **For valuation of property** — At the time of sale, purchase, mortgage, lease, or rent fixation, the present value is computed from the original estimate adjusted for depreciation and escalation.
- **For insurance** — The estimated replacement cost is declared as the **sum insured** for fire, flood, or theft policies.
- **For arbitration & court cases** — The original estimate and its supporting documents are admissible evidence to settle disputes between owner and contractor.
- **For taxation & stamp duty** — In Kerala, the **Building Tax** and **Building Numbering** processes require a copy of the approved estimate.

## 2.2 Contingencies — Types, Rates, and Application

### 2.2.1 Types of Contingencies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 1 — Physical Contingencies (also called Work Contingencies or Engineering Contingencies):**
> These cover unforeseen physical items encountered during execution, such as:
> - Extra excavation in rock or soft soil.
> - Dewatering due to high water table.
> - Additional shoring / sheet piling.
> - Minor design changes directed by the engineer-in-charge.
> - Excess handling of materials.
> 
> **Standard Rate (as per CPWD / PWD Kerala):** **3 % to 5 %** of the estimated cost for new construction; **5 % to 10 %** for maintenance / repair works.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 2 — Price Contingencies (also called Escalation Contingencies or Market Contingencies):**
> These cover the cost rise due to inflation in material rates and labour wages between the date of estimate preparation and the date of actual procurement / execution.
> 
> **Standard Rate:** Calculated using a **price escalation formula** based on **Wholesale Price Index (WPI)** or **Consumer Price Index (CPI)** for the relevant sector. For KTU problem-solving, a flat **3 % to 5 %** is generally adopted unless the problem specifies a multi-year estimate.

### 2.2.2 Percentage Rates Adopted by Various Departments (KTU Reference)

| Department / Organisation | New Construction | Repair / Maintenance | Special Works |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CPWD (Central) | 3 % | 5 % | 5 – 10 % |
| PWD Kerala | 3 % | 5 % | 5 – 8 % |
| LSGD (Panchayat / Municipality) | 3 % | 5 % | — |
| KWA (Water Authority) | 3 % | 5 % | 5 – 7 % |
| KSEB (Electricity Board) | 3 % | 5 % | — |
| Indian Railways | 3 % | 5 % | 5 – 8 % |
| MES (Military) | 3 % | 5 % | 5 % |
| NHAI / PWD NH Division | 3 % | — | 5 % (for bridges) |

> [!NOTE]
> The above table is a **memorisation-friendly KTU cheat sheet**. Board questions often ask: *"What percentage of contingency is provided in CPWD works for new construction?"* — The exact answer is **3 %**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total Estimated Cost | $E_{total} = E_{base} + C$ | ₹ (Rupees) | $E_{base}$ = Detailed estimate, $C$ = Contingency amount |
| 2 | Contingency Amount (Percentage Method) | $C = \dfrac{p}{100} \times E_{base}$ | ₹ | $p$ = Contingency percentage |
| 3 | Total Estimated Cost (Expanded) | $E_{total} = E_{base}\left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$ | ₹ | Direct formula in one step |
| 4 | Addl. Contractor Profit (CPWD Works) | $CP = \dfrac{10}{100} \times W$ | ₹ | $W$ = Work component cost |
| 5 | Establishment & Audit Charges | $EA = \dfrac{\% \text{ as per rules}}{100} \times E$ | ₹ | CPWD: 1.5 % to 2 % |
| 6 | Rate per unit area (Plinth Area) | $R_{pa} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A_{plinth}}$ | ₹/m² | Used for approximate estimate |
| 7 | Rate per unit volume (Cube Rate) | $R_{cv} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{V_{building}}$ | ₹/m³ | Used for multi-storey buildings |
| 8 | Price Escalation (Annual) | $E_{future} = E_{present} \times (1 + r)^{n}$ | ₹ | $r$ = annual rate, $n$ = years |
| 9 | Standard Contingency — New Work | $p = 3\%$ | — | CPWD / PWD Kerala standard |
| 10 | Standard Contingency — Repair Work | $p = 5\% \text{ to } 10\%$ | — | Higher due to unpredictability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU 2024:** In valuation problems, contingency is **NOT** added to the **replacement cost** of an old building. Contingency is a **forward-looking** provision for new/ongoing works only. This is a frequently tested distinction.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Banks (Loan Disbursement):** A bank like the **State Bank of Travancore** will not release a home loan construction-stage amount without an approved estimate. The contingency provision reassures the bank that cost overruns can be absorbed.
- **Insurance Companies (Premium Fixation):** The **sum insured** for a building is the total estimated replacement cost, which includes contingency. The annual premium is typically **0.2 % to 0.5 %** of this sum insured.
- **Project Management (Earned Value):** The estimate with contingency is the **Budget at Completion (BAC)**. The **Contingency Reserve** is released progressively as risks are mitigated, and the **Management Reserve** is held by senior management for "unknown-unknowns."
- **Government Audit (AG Kerala / C&AG):** Contingency expenditure without proper authorisation is one of the top 5 audit objections. The estimate must clearly state the contingency percentage and the officer empowered to sanction its use.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Estimated Cost Formula

The total estimated cost of a work consists of three parts in the CPWD / PWD framework:

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} + C + EA
$$

where:
- $E_{base}$ = Base estimated cost (sum of all item-rate quantities)
- $C$ = Contingency provision
- $EA$ = Establishment and audit charges

For most KTU 2024 problems, **Establishment and audit charges are either given separately or are not required**. The standard simplified form is:

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} + C
$$

By definition, contingency $C$ is a fixed percentage $p$ of the base estimate:

$$
C = \frac{p}{100} \times E_{base}
$$

Substituting this into the total cost equation:

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} + \frac{p}{100} \times E_{base}
$$

Factorising the right-hand side:

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} \left( 1 + \frac{p}{100} \right)
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for total estimated cost inclusive of contingency. It is the single most tested formula in Module 1 problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factorisation matters:** When the KTU problem gives you $E_{total}$ and asks for $E_{base}$, you must rearrange. Many students make the mistake of subtracting $p$ % from $E_{total}$ directly, which is **wrong**. The correct reverse formula is derived below.

## 3.2 Reverse Formula — Finding Base Estimate from Total Cost

Given $E_{total}$ and $p$, we want $E_{base}$:

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} \left( 1 + \frac{p}{100} \right)
$$

Divide both sides by $\left( 1 + \frac{p}{100} \right)$:

$$
E_{base} = \frac{E_{total}}{1 + \frac{p}{100}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 100:

$$
E_{base} = \frac{100 \times E_{total}}{100 + p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This reverse formula is **the most common 3-mark KTU exam trap.** If $E_{total} = ₹ 20,00,000$ and $p = 5\%$, students wrongly compute $E_{base} = 20,00,000 - 5\% = 19,00,000$. The correct answer is $E_{base} = \dfrac{20,00,000}{1.05} = ₹ 19,04,761.90$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**
> A new building has a detailed estimated cost of ₹ **18,00,000** (Rupees Eighteen Lakhs) based on PWD Kerala Schedule of Rates 2024. As per CPWD norms, a contingency of **3 %** is to be provided. Compute (a) the contingency amount, (b) the total estimated cost, and (c) the rate per m² if the plinth area is **120 m²**.

### Part (a) — Contingency Amount

$$
C = \frac{p}{100} \times E_{base}
$$

Substitute $p = 3$ and $E_{base} = 18,00,000$:

$$
C = \frac{3}{100} \times 18,00,000
$$

$$
C = 0.03 \times 18,00,000
$$

$$
C = 54,000
$$

> The contingency amount is **₹ 54,000**.

### Part (b) — Total Estimated Cost

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} + C
$$

$$
E_{total} = 18,00,000 + 54,000
$$

$$
E_{total} = 18,54,000
$$

> The total estimated cost is **₹ 18,54,000**.

### Part (c) — Rate per m²

$$
R_{pa} = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{plinth}}
$$

$$
R_{pa} = \frac{18,54,000}{120}
$$

$$
R_{pa} = 15,450 \text{ per m}^2
$$

> The rate per m² is **₹ 15,450 / m²**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Purposes to Stages of a Project

| Sl. No. | Purpose of Estimate | Project Stage | Stakeholder | Document Produced |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | To obtain Administrative Approval (AA) | Pre-tender | Government / Owner | AA Order |
| 2 | To obtain Technical Sanction (TS) | Pre-tender | Chief Engineer | TS Certificate |
| 3 | To arrange finance from banks | Pre-tender | Bank Manager | Loan Sanction Letter |
| 4 | To invite tenders | Tender stage | Contractors | Notice Inviting Tender (NIT) |
| 5 | To compare & evaluate bids | Tender stage | Tender Committee | Comparative Statement |
| 6 | To award contract & sign agreement | Tender stage | Owner + Contractor | Work Order / Agreement |
| 7 | To procure materials in advance | Execution | Purchase Officer | Indent / Purchase Order |
| 8 | To schedule labour & machinery | Execution | Site Engineer | Resource Deployment Plan |
| 9 | To process running account bills | Execution | Contractor / Engineer | RA Bill |
| 10 | To monitor cost vs. budget | Execution | Project Manager | Earned Value Report |
| 11 | To compute valuation for sale / mortgage | Post-construction | Valuer / Bank | Valuation Report |
| 12 | For insurance of structure | Post-construction | Insurance Co. | Insurance Policy |
| 13 | For arbitration / dispute resolution | Anytime | Arbitrator / Court | Evidence Exhibit |
| 14 | For tax / stamp duty assessment | Post-construction | Revenue Dept. | Building Tax Receipt |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Contingency Types

| Parameter | Physical Contingency | Price Contingency |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Nature** | Unforeseen items of work | Future price rise |
| **Cause** | Site condition, design change | Inflation, market fluctuation |
| **Standard Rate** | 3 % (new) / 5 % (repair) | 3 – 5 % per year (compounded) |
| **Calculation** | Flat % of base estimate | $E_{base} \times (1+r)^{n} - E_{base}$ |
| **Sanctioning Authority** | Engineer-in-charge | Departmental HQ |
| **Documentation** | Site Order Book + Revised Estimate | Escalation Certificate |
| **KTU Question Frequency** | Very High (almost every year) | Moderate |
| **Audit Risk** | Moderate | High (must justify) |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Verification Using the Worked Example

Let us cross-check the worked example by computing the rate per m² using $E_{base}$ alone (sometimes required when the question says *"excluding contingency"*):

$$
R_{base} = \frac{E_{base}}{A_{plinth}} = \frac{18,00,000}{120} = 15,000 \text{ per m}^2
$$

$$
R_{total} = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{plinth}} = \frac{18,54,000}{120} = 15,450 \text{ per m}^2
$$

Difference:

$$
\Delta R = 15,450 - 15,000 = 450 \text{ per m}^2
$$

Verification through percentage:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R_{base}} \times 100 = \frac{450}{15,000} \times 100 = 3\%
$$

> The 3 % difference **exactly matches** the contingency percentage — confirming that the contingency has been correctly applied. This percentage-cross-check is a useful self-validation technique in the KTU exam hall.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define an *estimate* in Quantity Surveying. List any **four** major purposes of preparing an estimate for a construction project.

**Model Answer:**
> An **estimate** is a detailed calculation of the quantities of various items of work, multiplied by their respective unit rates as per the prevailing Schedule of Rates (SOR), to arrive at the probable cost of a proposed construction project **before** the actual execution begins.
> 
> **Four major purposes of an estimate are:**
> 1. To obtain **Administrative Approval (AA)** and **Technical Sanction (TS)** from the competent authority.
> 2. To **arrange finance** from banks and financial institutions for the project.
> 3. To **invite tenders** and award the work to a suitable contractor.
> 4. To **control and monitor expenditure** during execution, ensuring the actual cost does not deviate significantly from the sanctioned estimate.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 3, Q1(c)]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** What is meant by *contingency* in a construction estimate? Mention the standard percentage of contingency adopted by **CPWD** for (i) new construction works and (ii) repair and maintenance works.

**Model Answer:**
> **Contingency** is a percentage provision added to the detailed estimated cost of a work to cover the cost of **unforeseen items, minor design changes, and price escalations** that are likely to arise during execution but cannot be predicted accurately in advance.
> 
> **Standard CPWD Contingency Percentages:**
> - **(i) New construction works:** **3 %** of the detailed estimated cost.
> - **(ii) Repair and maintenance works:** **5 %** of the detailed estimated cost (may go up to 10 % for major special repairs).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Main Exam, Module 1, Q2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **various purposes of preparing an estimate** in a civil engineering project. Classify these purposes under administrative, financial, contractual, and statutory heads.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential building has a detailed estimated cost of **₹ 24,00,000** based on PWD Kerala SOR 2024. Compute (i) the contingency amount at 3 %, (ii) the total estimated cost, and (iii) the cost per m² of plinth area, given that the plinth area of the building is **150 m²**. If the owner wants a **revised estimate** after two years assuming an annual price escalation of **6 %**, compute the revised base estimate.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Detailed Purposes of an Estimate

The purposes of preparing an estimate are classified as follows:

**1. Administrative Purposes**
- To obtain **Administrative Approval (AA)** from the competent government authority (e.g., State Government for works above ₹1 Cr in PWD Kerala).
- To enable the department to **prioritise** the project against competing demands for funds.

**2. Financial Purposes**
- To **arrange finance** from banks, NABARD, HUDCO, or financial institutions.
- To release funds **in phases** (mobilisation advance, running account bills, final bill).
- To establish a **cost ceiling** — variations beyond 5 % require revised sanction.

**3. Contractual Purposes**
- To **invite tenders** (Item-rate, Lump-sum, EPC).
- To evaluate and **compare bids** — a bid within ± 10 % of the estimated cost is considered reasonable.
- To prepare **contract documents** (BoQ, drawings, GCC, SCC).

**4. Statutory & Legal Purposes**
- For **valuation** at the time of sale, purchase, mortgage, or rent.
- For **insurance** — the sum insured is the estimated replacement cost.
- For **arbitration and court cases** — original estimate is admissible evidence.
- For **tax assessment** (Building Tax, Property Tax, Stamp Duty in Kerala).

**5. Resource Planning Purposes**
- To plan materials, labour, machinery, and time in advance.
- To integrate the estimate with the **construction programme (CPM/PERT)**.

**6. Execution Control Purposes**
- To act as a **baseline** for **Earned Value Management (EVM)**.
- To enable monitoring of cost, time, and quality against the plan.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $E_{base} = ₹ 24,00,000$
- $p = 3\%$
- $A_{plinth} = 150 \text{ m}^2$
- Annual escalation $r = 6\%$
- Years $n = 2$

**(i) Contingency Amount**

$$
C = \frac{p}{100} \times E_{base} = \frac{3}{100} \times 24,00,000 = 72,000
$$

> **Contingency = ₹ 72,000**

**(ii) Total Estimated Cost**

$$
E_{total} = E_{base} + C = 24,00,000 + 72,000 = 24,72,000
$$

> **Total Estimated Cost = ₹ 24,72,000**

**(iii) Cost per m² of Plinth Area**

$$
R_{pa} = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{plinth}} = \frac{24,72,000}{150} = 16,480
$$

> **Cost per m² = ₹ 16,480 / m²**

**Revised Base Estimate after 2 Years at 6 % Annual Escalation**

$$
E_{revised} = E_{base} \times (1 + r)^{n}
$$

$$
E_{revised} = 24,00,000 \times (1 + 0.06)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{revised} = 24,00,000 \times (1.06)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{revised} = 24,00,000 \times 1.1236
$$

$$
E_{revised} = 26,96,640
$$

> **Revised Base Estimate = ₹ 26,96,640**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Physical Contingency** and **Price Contingency**. Explain with examples how each is calculated and sanctioned. State the standard rates adopted by **CPWD** for various types of works.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The sanctioned estimated cost of a highway project is **₹ 1,20,00,000** inclusive of 5 % contingency. (i) Calculate the **base estimated cost** (excluding contingency). (ii) If the actual physical contingency expenditure turned out to be **₹ 4,50,000**, what is the **unutilised contingency** available for price escalation? (iii) If the project is delayed by **18 months** and the price escalation is **8 % per annum** (compounded), compute the **additional cost due to price escalation** on the base estimate.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Difference Between Physical and Price Contingency

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Physical Contingency | Price Contingency |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Definition | Covers unforeseen items of work | Covers inflation in material & labour rates |
| 2 | Example | Extra rock excavation, dewatering | Cement price rise, wage hike |
| 3 | Calculation | Flat % of base estimate | Compounded using WPI / CPI |
| 4 | Sanctioned by | Engineer-in-charge | Departmental HQ / Chief Engineer |
| 5 | Documentation | Site Order Book entry | Escalation Certificate |
| 6 | CPWD Rate | 3 % (new), 5 % (repair) | Variable; tied to WPI |

**CPWD Standard Rates:**
- New building construction — **3 %**
- Repair and maintenance — **5 %**
- Special works (bridges, marine structures) — **5 % to 8 %**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $E_{total} = ₹ 1,20,00,000$ (inclusive of contingency)
- $p = 5\%$
- Actual physical contingency utilised = ₹ 4,50,000
- Annual price escalation $r = 8\%$
- Project delay $n = 1.5$ years

**(i) Base Estimated Cost (Reverse Formula)**

$$
E_{base} = \frac{100 \times E_{total}}{100 + p} = \frac{100 \times 1,20,00,000}{105}
$$

$$
E_{base} = \frac{1,20,00,00,000}{105} = 1,14,28,571.43
$$

> **Base Estimated Cost ≈ ₹ 1,14,28,571.43**

**(ii) Total Contingency Provision vs. Physical Contingency Used**

Total contingency provision:

$$
C_{total} = E_{total} - E_{base} = 1,20,00,000 - 1,14,28,571.43 = 5,71,428.57
$$

Unutilised contingency:

$$
C_{unutilised} = C_{total} - C_{used} = 5,71,428.57 - 4,50,000 = 1,21,428.57
$$

> **Unutilised Contingency ≈ ₹ 1,21,428.57**

**(iii) Additional Cost Due to Price Escalation (Compounded)**

$$
E_{escalated} = E_{base} \times (1 + r)^{n}
$$

$$
E_{escalated} = 1,14,28,571.43 \times (1 + 0.08)^{1.5}
$$

$$
E_{escalated} = 1,14,28,571.43 \times (1.08)^{1.5}
$$

$$
E_{escalated} = 1,14,28,571.43 \times 1.1216 \text{ (approx)}
$$

$$
E_{escalated} \approx 1,28,17,800
$$

Additional cost due to escalation:

$$
\Delta E = E_{escalated} - E_{base} = 1,28,17,800 - 1,14,28,571.43 \approx 13,89,228.57
$$

> **Additional Escalation Cost ≈ ₹ 13,89,228.57**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams (Module 1):**
> 
> 1. **Wrong Reverse Formula (Loses 1 Mark):** When $E_{total}$ is given and $E_{base}$ is asked, students subtract $p$ % from $E_{total}$. **Correct method:** Use $E_{base} = \dfrac{100 \times E_{total}}{100+p}$.
> 
> 2. **Confusion Between Contingency and Establishment Charges (Loses 1 Mark):** Contingency is **3 %**, but Establishment & Audit charges are **1.5 % to 2 %**. They are **NOT the same**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Years for Compounding (Loses 1 Mark):** If the problem says "18 months delay at 8 % p.a. compounded", use $n = 1.5$ years. Many students write $n = 1$ or $n = 2$.
> 
> 4. **Writing "Contingency = 5 %" Without Specifying New/Repair (Loses 0.5 Mark):** Always qualify — *"5 % for repair works"*.
> 
> 5. **In the Purposes Question, Writing Only 2–3 Points (Loses 2 Marks):** The examiner expects a **classified list** of 8–10 purposes. Writing a single paragraph with 3 points yields at most 3 out of 7.
> 
> 6. **Skipping Units in Numerical Answers (Loses 0.5 Mark):** Always write **₹** and **m²** explicitly. Unitless answers invite deduction.
> 
> 7. **Using PWD Rates When CPWD is Specified (Loses 1 Mark):** CPWD is **3 %** for new and **5 %** for repair. PWD Kerala is identical, but for different organisations (NHAI, KWA, KSEB) the rates may differ.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Purposes of Estimates & Contingencies**

- [ ] An **estimate** is a pre-execution calculation of probable cost based on quantities × SOR rates.
- [ ] Purposes of estimate cover **6 heads:** Administrative, Financial, Contractual, Statutory, Resource Planning, Execution Control.
- [ ] **Administrative Approval (AA)** is given by the Government; **Technical Sanction (TS)** is given by the Chief Engineer.
- [ ] Estimates enable **bank loan sanction, tender invitation, contract award, and RA bill processing**.
- [ ] Post-construction, the estimate is the basis for **valuation, insurance, and tax assessment**.
- [ ] **Contingency** is a **percentage provision** for unforeseen items and price rise.
- [ ] Two types: **Physical Contingency** (unforeseen work items) and **Price Contingency** (inflation).
- [ ] **CPWD rates:** **3 %** for new works, **5 %** for repair works, up to **10 %** for major special repairs.
- [ ] **PWD Kerala rates** are similar to CPWD: 3 % and 5 %.
- [ ] **Canonical formula:** $E_{total} = E_{base} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$.
- [ ] **Reverse formula:** $E_{base} = \dfrac{100 \times E_{total}}{100 + p}$.
- [ ] **Price escalation (compound):** $E_{future} = E_{base} \times (1 + r)^{n}$.
- [ ] Contingency is **NOT added** to the replacement cost of an **old/valued** building.
- [ ] The unutilised contingency is the **difference** between provision and actual expenditure.
- [ ] Always specify the **organisation** (CPWD / PWD / KWA / NHAI) when stating a contingency percentage.
- [ ] In the exam, **show the reverse formula derivation** whenever you use it, to earn the "method" mark.
- [ ] Use **units** (₹, m², m³) consistently — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- [ ] The contingency amount is sanctioned by the **Engineer-in-charge** (within limit) or by the **Chief Engineer** (above limit) as per departmental delegation.
- [ ] **Audit objection risk** is high if contingency is spent without proper written sanction and supporting Site Order Book entries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work-charge Establishment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Work-charge Establishment

# WORK-CHARGE ESTABLISHMENT (WCE)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Work-charge Establishment (WCE)** is a special category of temporary establishment sanctioned specifically for the execution of a particular public work (or a group of works) in which the wages of the laborers, the pay and allowances of the supervisory staff (Junior Engineer, Overseer, Beldar, Mate, etc.), and the cost of materials are all debited **directly to the work account** rather than to the regular departmental establishment budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A WCE comes into existence only when a sanctioned work is taken up and is automatically abolished as soon as the work is completed or stopped. It is governed by the **CPWD / State PWD Code** and the **General Financial Rules (GFR)** of the Government of India / State Government.

The term *establishment* here means the **gang of men (work-charged staff)** engaged temporarily on a particular work, plus the supervisory hierarchy, whose total cost is treated as a direct charge against that specific work.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you hire a private tutor to teach *only* your child for a particular exam. You pay the tutor *per session* and *per subject*. The moment the exam is over, you stop paying. The tutor is *not* on your permanent household payroll — the cost is charged **directly to the "exam preparation" project**.

In the same way:

- The **Government** = a huge household with a permanent staff.
- A **specific construction project** (e.g., a new district hospital building) = a "private task."
- The **WCE** = the temporary group of workers (masons, helpers, JEs, Overseers) hired *only* for that building.
- The **salary of WCE staff** = charged *only* to that building's account — like the tutor's fee charged to the "exam budget."

When the building is finished, the establishment is *automatically dissolved* — no more salaries are drawn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Regular Establishment:**
> In a **regular establishment**, the staff is permanent and their salaries are paid from the *Establishment Budget* of the department (e.g., PWD Establishment).
> In a **work-charge establishment**, the staff is *temporary* and salaries are paid from the *Work Budget* of the project.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard working hours:** 8 hours/day or 48 hours/week (subject to labour laws).
- **Minimum wages:** As per the *Minimum Wages Act, 1948* and periodic revision by the Central / State Government.
- **Establishment charge percentage:** Usually **1.5% to 2%** of the estimated cost of the work is kept as establishment charge over and above the work estimate, OR alternatively the actual cost of WCE staff is debited directly.
- **Sanctioning authority for WCE:** Executive Engineer (for works up to a certain limit), Superintending Engineer, Chief Engineer, or Government (depending on estimated cost thresholds).

### 1.4 Why is the Concept Important?

In a KTU examination, this topic tests:
1. **CO1:** *Understand the estimation and valuation workflow of civil engineering projects.*
2. **CO2:** *Apply procedures for sanctioning and executing works through various modes including WCE.*

The WCE mode is **economical for small, scattered, or seasonal works** where creating a permanent establishment is unjustified.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of a Work-Charge Establishment

A WCE typically consists of the **following categories of staff**, all paid from the work account:

| Sl. No. | Category | Role | Charged To |
|:-------:|:---------|:-----|:-----------|
| 1 | Junior Engineer (JE) | Site supervision | Work account |
| 2 | Overseer / Work Inspector | Field execution | Work account |
| 3 | Mate (Muccadam) | Gang supervisor | Work account |
| 4 | Beldar / Mazdoor | Unskilled labour | Work account |
| 5 | Mason / Carpenter / Plumber / Painter | Skilled labour | Work account |
| 6 | Chowkidar / Watchman | Site security | Work account |
| 7 | Driver (for departmental vehicles) | Vehicle operation | Work account |
| 8 | Bhisti / Water-carrier | Water supply at site | Work account |
| 9 | Storekeeper / Clerk | Material accounting | Work account |

> [!NOTE]
> The key feature is that **all these posts are temporary** and exist *only* for the duration of the work.

## 2.2 Conditions for Sanctioning a WCE

According to the **CPWD Code / Kerala PWD Manual**, a WCE may be sanctioned **only** when **ALL** the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **The work is of a definite nature** — its scope, location, and estimated cost are known.
2. **The work is sanctioned** by the competent authority with a definite budget allotment.
3. **The work is not a permanent, ongoing maintenance activity** that should be handled by a regular establishment.
4. **It is uneconomical** to deploy a regular establishment for the work.
5. **The cost of the WCE staff** is debitable to the work estimate, and the sanctioning authority is satisfied that it will be within the sanctioned estimate.
6. The work is **time-bound** and expected to be completed within a reasonable period.
7. For **maintenance works**, special sanction of the competent authority (e.g., Chief Engineer) is required to treat them on work-charge basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever asked "When can a WCE be sanctioned?" — list the conditions above in **bullet points**. Do NOT write them as a paragraph. Examiners award marks for clear, itemised answers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|:--------|:---------------|:--------------|
| Total Cost of WCE | $\sum (\text{Daily wage} \times \text{No. of staff} \times \text{No. of days worked})$ | INR |
| Establishment Charge % on work | $\text{EC} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of WCE staff}}{\text{Total work estimate}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) |
| Per-day cost per worker | $\text{PD} = \text{Basic wage} + \text{DA} + \text{HRA} + \text{OT (if any)}$ | INR/day |
| Productivity rate (for output) | $\text{Output} = \text{No. of workers} \times \text{Rate per worker per day}$ | m³/day or m²/day |
| Standard men per supervisor | 1 Mate per **20 to 25** Mazdoors | Ratio |
| WCE justified if | $\text{Work value} \geq \text{Cost of WCE for project duration}$ | Logical check |
| Abolition trigger | WCE ends on **completion / abandonment / stoppage** of work | Code rule |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never** use the vertical pipe `|` inside a cell (e.g., for absolute value). Use the LaTeX macros `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as shown in the table above.

## 2.4 Advantages of Work-Charge Establishment

- **Economical** for small works — no idle permanent staff.
- **Direct control** — cost of supervision is directly visible in the work estimate.
- **Flexibility** — staff strength can be increased or decreased as per work progress.
- **Accountability** — every rupee spent on staff is justified by the work output.
- **Quick deployment** — no lengthy recruitment process.
- **Suitable for seasonal works** like desilting of tanks, monsoon repairs, etc.

## 2.5 Disadvantages of Work-Charge Establishment

- **No job security** for staff → low morale, high turnover.
- **No benefits** like provident fund, gratuity, pension (in some cases).
- **Difficult to attract skilled staff.**
- **Staff has no transfer rights** — they are tied to the work.
- **Risk of fictitious engagement** of staff even after work is over (financial irregularity).
- **No career progression** since posts are temporary.

## 2.6 Mode of Execution — Comparison

| Parameter | Departmental (WCE) | Contract (Piece-work / Lump-sum) | PPP / EPC |
|:----------|:-------------------|:--------------------------------|:----------|
| Execution by | Department's own WCE staff | Private contractor | Private agency |
| Payment of labour | From work estimate (WCE) | By contractor | By agency |
| Materials | Procured by department | Issued to contractor | Procured by agency |
| Supervision | Departmental JE/AE | Departmental JE supervises | Independent engineer |
| Risk | On department | On contractor | Shared |
| Best for | Small / urgent works | Standard works | Large infra projects |

> [!NOTE]
> In Kerala PWD practice, WCE is most commonly used for **maintenance works, small repairs, and original works below the tender limit**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedures, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

Since "Work-charge Establishment" is fundamentally a **procedural and administrative concept**, the "derivations" in this section take the form of:

1. **Procedure to sanction a WCE** (step-by-step workflow).
2. **Numerical Example 1:** Cost calculation of a WCE.
3. **Numerical Example 2:** Establishment charge as a % of work estimate.
4. **Numerical Example 3:** Productivity / output estimation of a WCE gang.
5. **Sanctioning Authority Matrix** (analytical table).

---

## 3.1 Procedure to Sanction a Work-Charge Establishment

The following is the **standard administrative procedure** (as per CPWD / Kerala PWD Code) to sanction a WCE:

> **Step 1** — Identify the work and prepare a **detailed estimate** (including cost of materials, labour, and contingency).
>
> **Step 2** — The **Executive Engineer (EE)** proposes to the competent authority that the work be executed through a WCE, citing the conditions of Section 2.2.
>
> **Step 3** — The proposal is **sent through the Superintending Engineer (SE)** to the sanctioning authority.
>
> **Step 4** — The **competent authority** (EE / SE / CE / Government, depending on the cost) **sanctions the work** and explicitly approves the WCE.
>
> **Step 5** — The EE issues a **formal order** sanctioning the WCE, listing:
> - Name of work
> - Estimated cost
> - Names and pay scales of staff
> - Period of engagement
> - Source of funds (Budget Head)
>
> **Step 6** — A **WCE register** is opened in the division. Staff are engaged, attendance is recorded, and wages are paid against muster rolls.
>
> **Step 7** — **Monthly accounts** are submitted to the EE, showing expenditure on staff and materials separately.
>
> **Step 8** — On **completion of the work**, the EE issues a closure order, and the WCE is **automatically abolished**.
>
> **Step 9** — A **completion certificate and final account** are prepared, and the WCE file is closed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked for "the procedure to execute a work through WCE," write the above as a **numbered list**. Many students lose marks by writing it as a paragraph.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — Cost of a WCE

A small WCE is sanctioned for a road-repair work. The sanctioned staff is:

| Staff | Number | Daily wage (INR) |
|:------|:------:|:----------------:|
| Overseer | 1 | 600 |
| Mate | 1 | 450 |
| Mazdoor (Beldar) | 12 | 350 |
| Watchman | 1 | 300 |

The work is expected to last **45 days**. Calculate the **total wage cost of the WCE**.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the daily wage of each category.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Overseer}} &= \text{INR } 600 \\
W_{\text{Mate}} &= \text{INR } 450 \\
W_{\text{Mazdoor}} &= \text{INR } 350 \\
W_{\text{Watchman}} &= \text{INR } 300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the number of staff in each category.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{Overseer}} &= 1 \\
N_{\text{Mate}} &= 1 \\
N_{\text{Mazdoor}} &= 12 \\
N_{\text{Watchman}} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **daily cost per category** (number × wage).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Overseer/day}} &= 1 \times 600 = \text{INR } 600 \\
C_{\text{Mate/day}} &= 1 \times 450 = \text{INR } 450 \\
C_{\text{Mazdoor/day}} &= 12 \times 350 = \text{INR } 4{,}200 \\
C_{\text{Watchman/day}} &= 1 \times 300 = \text{INR } 300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum up to get the **total daily cost of the WCE**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Daily Total}} &= 600 + 450 + 4{,}200 + 300 \\
&= \text{INR } 5{,}550 \text{ per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by the number of working days (45) to get the **total WCE cost for the project duration**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Total WCE}} &= C_{\text{Daily Total}} \times \text{No. of days} \\
&= 5{,}550 \times 45 \\
&= \text{INR } 2{,}49{,}750
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of arithmetic:** $5{,}550 \times 45 = 5{,}550 \times (50 - 5) = 2{,}77{,}500 - 27{,}750 = 2{,}49{,}750$. ✔

### Result

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Total WCE}} = \text{INR } 2{,}49{,}750}}
$$

This is the **direct establishment cost** to be debited to the work account.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — Establishment Charge as a % of Work Estimate

In the same example, the **estimated cost of the road-repair work** (materials + labour + contingency) is **INR 15,00,000**. Calculate the **establishment charge percentage**.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the total WCE staff cost (from Example 1).

$$
C_{\text{WCE}} = \text{INR } 2{,}49{,}750
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the total estimated cost of the work.

$$
C_{\text{Work}} = \text{INR } 15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the establishment-charge-percentage formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EC \%} &= \frac{C_{\text{WCE}}}{C_{\text{Work}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2{,}49{,}750}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2{,}49{,}75{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 16.65\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The result **16.65%** is unusually high. In practice, establishment charges are kept in the range of **1.5% to 5%**. This indicates that the WCE is **over-staffed** for the work value, OR the work period (45 days) is too long, OR the estimate is too small. **Always check the sanity of the result** — examiners often include a "comment on the result" sub-part.

### Comment

The high EC% suggests that **either** the WCE should be downsized **or** the work should be executed through a contractor. If a contractor's quote is, say, 10% of the work value, the departmental WCE is uneconomical.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example 3 — Productivity / Output of a WCE Gang

Given:
- 12 Mazdoors working.
- Standard output of a Mazdoor in earthwork = **2.5 m³/day** (as per CPWD Schedule of Rates / Kerala PWD DSR).
- Project duration = **45 days**.

Calculate the **total expected output** of the WCE gang.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the daily output per worker.

$$
O_{\text{per worker}} = 2.5 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the number of workers.

$$
N = 12 \ \text{mazdoors}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **daily gang output**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{\text{per day}} &= N \times O_{\text{per worker}} \\
&= 12 \times 2.5 \\
&= 30 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by the number of days to get **total gang output over the project**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{\text{Total}} &= O_{\text{per day}} \times \text{No. of days} \\
&= 30 \times 45 \\
&= 1{,}350 \ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Result

$$
\boxed{O_{\text{Total}} = 1{,}350 \ \text{m}^3 \ \text{of earthwork}}
$$

This output figure is used to **cross-check the WCE's productivity** against the estimated quantity in the DPR.

---

## 3.5 Sanctioning Authority — Analytical Matrix

| Estimated Cost of Work (INR) | Sanctioning Authority | Mode of Execution Recommended |
|:----------------------------:|:---------------------|:------------------------------|
| Up to 1,00,000 | Executive Engineer (EE) | WCE / Piece-work |
| 1,00,001 to 10,00,000 | Superintending Engineer (SE) | WCE / Tender |
| 10,00,001 to 1,00,00,000 | Chief Engineer (CE) | Tender (Lump-sum / Item-rate) |
| Above 1,00,00,000 | Government / Board | Tender / PPP |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorise the **EE → SE → CE → Government** hierarchy of sanction. Examiners often test this as a 3-mark question.

---

## 3.6 Sanctioning Authority for WCE Staff Posts

| Post in WCE | Sanctioning Authority |
|:------------|:---------------------|
| Mate, Beldar, Mazdoor | Junior Engineer / Overseer (with EE approval) |
| Overseer / Work Inspector | Executive Engineer |
| Junior Engineer (for the work) | Superintending Engineer |
| Multiple JEs / Senior staff | Chief Engineer |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section uses **Mermaid block diagrams** to map the **functional architecture** of a Work-charge Establishment. Mermaid safeguards followed (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels).

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Organization Hierarchy of a WCE

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Chief Engineer PWD] --> B1[Superintending Engineer]
    B1 --> C1[Executive Engineer]
    C1 --> D1[Assistant Engineer]
    C1 --> D2[Assistant Engineer]
    D1 --> E1[Junior Engineer WCE]
    D2 --> E2[Junior Engineer WCE]
    E1 --> F1[Overseer / Work Inspector]
    E2 --> F2[Overseer / Work Inspector]
    F1 --> G1[Mate Muccadam]
    F2 --> G2[Mate Muccadam]
    G1 --> H1[Skilled Workers Mason Carpenter]
    G1 --> H2[Unskilled Workers Beldar Mazdoor]
    G2 --> H3[Skilled Workers Plumber]
    G2 --> H4[Unskilled Workers Beldar Mazdoor]
    F1 --> H5[Chowkidar Watchman]
    F2 --> H6[Storekeeper Clerk]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Executive Engineer sanctions the WCE. The AE supervises the JEs, who in turn supervise Overseers, Mates, and finally the skilled/unskilled workers. The Watchman and Storekeeper support the establishment.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Workflow for Sanctioning a WCE

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1 Identify Work and Prepare Estimate] --> step2[Step 2 EE Proposes WCE Mode]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Forwarded through SE]
    step3 --> step4{Step 4 Competent Authority Sanctioned}
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 EE Issues WCE Sanction Order]
    step4 --> step6[Step 4B Returned for Revision]
    step6 --> step2
    step5 --> step7[Step 6 Open WCE Register Engage Staff]
    step7 --> step8[Step 7 Daily Muster Roll Monthly Accounts]
    step8 --> step9{Step 8 Work Completed}
    step9 --> step10[Step 9A Issue Completion Certificate Close WCE]
    step9 --> step11[Step 9B Work Stopped / Abandoned]
    step11 --> step10
    step10 --> step12[Step 10 WCE Abolished Automatically]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **closed-loop administrative workflow** for sanctioning, operating, and closing a WCE. The diamond-shaped nodes are decision points (Sanctioned? Completed?). The loop from "Returned for Revision" back to "EE Proposes" is the **feedback loop** in case the initial proposal is rejected.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Funding & Accounting Flow of a WCE

```mermaid
graph LR
    fundA[Government Budget Head] --> fundB[Departmental Allocation]
    fundB --> fundC[Division EE Account]
    fundC --> fundD1[Material Procurement]
    fundC --> fundD2[Labour Wages WCE Staff]
    fundC --> fundD3[Contingency]
    fundD1 --> workA[Debited to Work Account]
    fundD2 --> workA
    fundD3 --> workA
    workA --> finalA[Final Account and Completion Report]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Government releases funds under a specific Budget Head. The EE controls the Division's account. From this account, money flows into **three sub-heads** — Materials, Labour (WCE staff), and Contingency. All three are **directly debited to the work account**, and the final account is prepared on completion.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Logic: When to Use WCE vs Contractor

```mermaid
graph TD
    qstart{Is the work small and urgent} -->|Yes| q1{Are skilled staff available in house}
    qstart -->|No| contra[Execute through Contractor]
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is estimated cost within EE power}
    q1 -->|No| contra
    q2 -->|Yes| wce[Sanction Work Charge Establishment]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Is estimated cost within SE power}
    q3 -->|Yes| wce
    q3 -->|No| contra
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **decision tree** that helps an engineer decide *algorithmically* whether to use WCE or contractor mode. The three sequential filters are: **work size → skill availability → sanctioning limit**. Only if all three filters return "Yes" is the WCE sanctioned.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern. For each part, the model answer, marks split, and common pitfalls are provided. Question papers follow the KTU pattern of **Part A (3 marks, no choice)** and **Part B (14 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B)**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Work-charge Establishment. Mention any two conditions under which it is sanctioned.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — split as 1.5 + 1.5)

A **Work-charge Establishment (WCE)** is a temporary establishment set up for the execution of a specific public work, in which the wages and allowances of the supervisory staff and labourers engaged are debited **directly to the work account** instead of being charged to the regular establishment budget. (1½ marks)

The WCE is sanctioned when:
1. The work is of a **definite nature** with a known scope and estimate. (¾ mark)
2. It is **uneconomical to deploy a regular establishment** for the work. (¾ mark)

*(Any two valid conditions from Section 2.2 are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the categories of staff usually employed in a Work-charge Establishment.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The categories of staff in a WCE include: (List any 6 for full marks)
1. Junior Engineer (JE) — site supervision.
2. Overseer / Work Inspector — field execution.
3. Mate (Muccadam) — gang supervisor.
4. Beldar / Mazdoor — unskilled labour.
5. Mason / Carpenter — skilled labour.
6. Chowkidar / Watchman — site security.
*(Storekeeper and Bhisti may also be included.)*

*(½ mark per correct entry × 6 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each**, OR a single 14-mark sub-question. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the procedure to execute a public work through a Work-charge Establishment. State the conditions under which such an establishment can be sanctioned. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A work-charge establishment has the following sanctioned staff for a building-construction work lasting 60 days:

| Staff | Number | Daily wage (INR) |
|:------|:------:|:----------------:|
| Junior Engineer | 1 | 800 |
| Overseer | 2 | 600 |
| Mate | 2 | 450 |
| Mazdoor | 20 | 350 |
| Watchman | 1 | 300 |

Calculate the total wage cost of the WCE, and find the establishment charge as a % of the total work estimate of INR 25,00,000. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Procedure and conditions (7 marks):**

*Conditions for sanctioning a WCE — 3 marks:*
- Work is of a definite nature with known scope.
- Work is sanctioned and budgeted.
- It is uneconomical to use a regular establishment.
- The work is time-bound.

*Procedure for execution — 4 marks (numbered list, ½ mark each step):*
1. Prepare detailed estimate.
2. EE proposes WCE mode.
3. Forwarded through SE to competent authority.
4. Sanctioning authority approves.
5. EE issues WCE sanction order listing staff, period, funds.
6. Open WCE register, engage staff.
7. Record daily attendance on muster rolls.
8. Submit monthly accounts to EE.
9. On completion, issue completion certificate.
10. WCE is automatically abolished.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Daily cost per category (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{JE/day}} &= 1 \times 800 = 800 \\
C_{\text{Overseer/day}} &= 2 \times 600 = 1{,}200 \\
C_{\text{Mate/day}} &= 2 \times 450 = 900 \\
C_{\text{Mazdoor/day}} &= 20 \times 350 = 7{,}000 \\
C_{\text{Watchman/day}} &= 1 \times 300 = 300
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Total daily cost (1 mark):*

$$
C_{\text{Daily Total}} = 800 + 1{,}200 + 900 + 7{,}000 + 300 = \text{INR } 10{,}200
$$

*Step 3 — Total WCE cost for 60 days (1 mark):*

$$
C_{\text{Total WCE}} = 10{,}200 \times 60 = \text{INR } 6{,}12{,}000
$$

*Step 4 — Establishment charge % (1.5 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EC \%} &= \frac{6{,}12{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 24.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Comment (1.5 marks):*

The EC% is **24.48%**, which is **far above the normal range of 1.5%–5%**. This indicates that the WCE is **over-staffed** for the work value, OR the duration of 60 days is excessive. It would be more **economical to execute the work through a contractor** or to reduce the WCE staff strength.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of executing works through Work-charge Establishment. Compare it briefly with the contract system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WCE gang of 15 mazdoors can lay 1.2 m³ of brickwork per worker per day. Calculate:
- (i) the daily output of the gang,
- (ii) the total output in 30 days,
- (iii) the time required to complete 900 m³ of brickwork with the same gang. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Advantages, Disadvantages & Comparison (7 marks):**

*Advantages of WCE (2 marks — 4 points, ½ mark each):*
1. Economical for small works.
2. Direct control and accountability.
3. Flexibility to scale staff up/down.
4. Quick deployment.

*Disadvantages of WCE (2 marks — 4 points, ½ mark each):*
1. No job security, low morale.
2. No PF/pension benefits.
3. Difficulty in attracting skilled staff.
4. Risk of fictitious engagement of staff.

*Comparison with contract system (3 marks — table or list):*
- **Execution by:** Department vs private contractor.
- **Labour payment:** From WCE/work estimate vs by contractor.
- **Materials:** Procured by department vs issued or procured by contractor.
- **Risk:** On department vs on contractor.
- **Best suited for:** Small/urgent works vs large/standard works.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

*Given:* $N = 15$ mazdoors, $O_{\text{per worker per day}} = 1.2 \ \text{m}^3$, $D = 30$ days.

*(i) Daily gang output (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{\text{per day}} &= N \times O_{\text{per worker per day}} \\
&= 15 \times 1.2 \\
&= 18 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(ii) Total output in 30 days (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{\text{Total}} &= O_{\text{per day}} \times D \\
&= 18 \times 30 \\
&= 540 \ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*(iii) Time required for 900 m³ (3 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{\text{Total quantity}}{O_{\text{per day}}} \\
&= \frac{900}{18} \\
&= 50 \ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in WCE questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing the procedure as a paragraph** instead of a numbered list — loses 2 to 3 marks. KTU examiners want **itemised steps**.
> 2. **Forgetting to "comment"** on the EC% result. Numerical questions often have a 2-mark "comment" sub-part that students skip.
> 3. **Confusing "WCE" with "Departmental Execution"** — WCE is a *type* of departmental execution, not its synonym. Always clarify that the work is done departmentally *through* a WCE.
> 4. **Not specifying "abolition"** — every WCE answer should mention that the establishment is **automatically abolished** on completion. Examiners award 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Arithmetic slip in wage calculation** — always show the **daily total** as a separate step. Jumping straight to the final answer loses the working marks.
> 6. **Using "Contractor" and "Tender" interchangeably** — they are *related* but not the same. A tender is the *process*; a contractor is the *party* awarded the work.
> 7. **Forgetting the sanctioning authority hierarchy** (EE → SE → CE → Government). This is a recurring 2-mark sub-part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Work-charge Establishment**

- **Definition:** Temporary establishment whose staff cost is debited **directly to a specific work**.
- **Trigger:** Sanctioned work is taken up; **Abolished** when work ends.
- **Composition:** JE, Overseer, Mate, Beldar, Mason, Watchman, etc.
- **Sanctioning authority hierarchy:** EE (small) → SE → CE → Government (largest).
- **Conditions:** Definite scope, sanctioned estimate, uneconomical to use regular establishment, time-bound.
- **Advantages:** Economical, flexible, direct control, quick.
- **Disadvantages:** No job security, no benefits, risk of fictitious engagement.
- **Best suited for:** Small, scattered, urgent, or seasonal works.
- **Establishment charge %:** Normally **1.5% to 5%**; higher % = over-staffed or under-estimated work.
- **Key formulas:**
  - $C_{\text{Total WCE}} = (\sum N_i \times W_i) \times D$
  - $\text{EC \%} = (C_{\text{WCE}} / C_{\text{Work}}) \times 100$
  - $O_{\text{Total}} = N \times O_{\text{per worker per day}} \times D$
- **Decision logic:** Use WCE if work is small, urgent, and skilled staff is available *in-house*; else use contractor.
- **Accounts:** Material, Labour (WCE), Contingency — all three debited to the work account.
- **One-line Mantra for Viva:** *"A WCE is a temporary, project-bound establishment whose entire cost is charged to that one work — no permanent liability, no separate budget."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tools and Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of civil engineering construction projects, the terms **"Tools," "Plant," and "Equipment"** carry distinct but interrelated meanings as defined under KTU 2024 Quantity Surveying & Valuation syllabus:

**Tools** are small, hand-operated, low-cost, and short-lived implements used by workmen to perform manual construction tasks. They are usually portable, manually powered, and have a service life of less than **2 years** with negligible salvage value. Examples include shovels, pickaxes, hammers, chisels, trowels, spanners, and measuring tapes.

**Plant** (also called Construction Plant or Heavy Plant) refers to the large, mechanically powered, high-capital-cost machines used to execute heavy construction operations such as earthwork, concreting, lifting, transportation, and material processing. Plant is characterized by high initial cost, long economic life (typically **5 to 15 years**), and significant depreciation. Examples include excavators, bulldozers, cranes, concrete mixers, and dump trucks.

**Equipment** is a broader umbrella term that collectively refers to both tools and plant used on a construction site. In KTU valuation contexts, "equipment" includes any movable asset deployed for project execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under PCCET601 Module 1, students must be able to *differentiate between tools and plant*, classify various types of construction plant, and evaluate the economic considerations (ownership cost, operating cost, depreciation) that affect plant selection and rate analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are renovating your home:

- A **hammer, screwdriver, and measuring tape** in your toolbox are the **"tools."** They cost a few hundred rupees, last a few years, and you don't worry about their depreciation seriously. You just consume them and replace them.

- A **bulldozer or tower crane** brought to a large construction site is the **"plant."** It may cost **₹50 lakh to ₹5 crore**, must be carefully maintained, depreciated, insured, and its usage hours are meticulously tracked because every running hour costs the contractor money (fuel, operator wages, wear-and-tear).

**In one sentence:** Tools are the *consumable fingers* of a project, while plant is the *muscle* — and like any muscle, it must be economically justified before deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric used in KTU:** Plant capacity is universally expressed in **kW (kilowatts)** or **HP (horsepower)** for prime movers, **m³/hr (cubic meters per hour)** for output capacity, and **tonnes** for lifting capacity. The conversion **1 HP ≈ 0.746 kW** is frequently tested.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Quantity Surveying

In rate analysis (covered later in the syllabus), the cost of plant forms a major component of the unit rate of any heavy construction item such as:
- Excavation (₹/m³) — includes excavator hourly cost
- Concrete work (₹/m³) — includes mixer, vibrator, transit mixer cost
- Pile driving (₹/m) — includes piling rig cost

A quantity surveyor who cannot correctly compute plant cost will produce **unrealistic rates**, leading to contract losses or disputes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Construction Plant

Construction plant can be classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know classification by **function** and by **ownership pattern**.

#### A. Classification by Function

**1. Earthmoving Equipment**
- Excavators (hydraulic, cable-operated)
- Bulldozers
- Scrapers
- Draglines
- Clamshells
- Backhoes
- Loaders (skid-steer, wheel loader)

**2. Earth Compaction Equipment**
- Smooth-wheeled rollers
- Sheep-foot rollers
- Pneumatic-tyred rollers
- Vibratory rollers
- Rammers and plate compactors

**3. Concreting Equipment**
- Concrete mixers (tilting drum, non-tilting drum, pan mixer)
- Batching plants
- Transit mixers
- Concrete pumps (boom pump, line pump)
- Vibrators (needle, surface, form vibrators)

**4. Hoisting and Lifting Equipment**
- Tower cranes
- Mobile cranes (hydraulic, telescopic)
- Crawler cranes
- Winches
- Hoists
- Forklifts

**5. Material Handling & Conveying Equipment**
- Belt conveyors
- Dump trucks / tippers
- Chutes
- Cranes with grabs

**6. Piling Equipment**
- Pile drivers (drop hammer, diesel hammer, hydraulic hammer)
- Bored piling rigs
- Vibro hammers

**7. Asphalt & Road Equipment**
- Hot mix plants
- Pavers
- Rollers
- Bitumen sprayers

**8. Dewatering Equipment**
- Centrifugal pumps
- Submersible pumps
- Well-point systems

**9. Demolition Equipment**
- Hydraulic breakers
- Wrecking balls
- Concrete crushers

**10. Drilling and Blasting Equipment**
- Compressors
- Jackhammers
- Drill jumbos

#### B. Classification by Ownership Pattern

| Ownership Type | Description | Cost Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Own Plant** | Owned by the contractor | Depreciation + interest on capital |
| **Hired Plant** | Rented from third parties | Rental rate per hour/day |
| **Leased Plant** | Long-term lease | Fixed monthly lease + maintenance |

### 2.2 Economic Considerations of Plant

The KTU syllabus stresses the **economic life** and **cost components** of plant. These determine the *hourly owning cost* and *hourly operating cost* — both critical inputs to rate analysis.

#### Cost Components

1. **Fixed / Ownership Costs** (incurred regardless of usage)
   - Initial delivered cost (IDC)
   - Interest on invested capital
   - Depreciation
   - Insurance
   - Road tax / registration
   - Garage / storage charges

2. **Variable / Operating Costs** (incurred only when used)
   - Fuel cost (diesel/electricity)
   - Lubricants
   - Operator and helper wages
   - Routine maintenance and spares
   - Major overhaul / repair
   - Tyre / track replacement

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Straight-line depreciation (per year) | ₹/year |
| 2 | $D_n = (C-S) \times \dfrac{n}{N(N+1)/2}$ | Sum-of-years-digits depreciation for year $n$ | ₹ |
| 3 | $D_n = C \times d \times (1-d)^{n-1}$ | Declining balance (DB) depreciation for year $n$, where $d$ = rate | ₹ |
| 4 | $\text{Book Value at end of year } n = C \times (1-d)^n$ | Declining balance book value | ₹ |
| 5 | $\text{Hourly Owning Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Total Ownership Cost per Year}}{\text{Working Hours per Year}}$ | Spread fixed cost over usage | ₹/hr |
| 6 | $\text{Hourly Operating Cost} = \text{Fuel} + \text{Lube} + \text{Maintenance} + \text{Operator Wages}$ | Variable cost per working hour | ₹/hr |
| 7 | $\text{Total Hourly Cost} = \text{Hourly Owning} + \text{Hourly Operating}$ | Composite plant rate | ₹/hr |
| 8 | $\text{Output rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Hourly Cost}}{\text{Output per hour}}$ | Rate per unit work (₹/m³, ₹/tonne) | ₹/unit |
| 9 | $\text{Economic Life} = \text{Year at which average cost per hour is minimum}$ | Optimal replacement year | years |
| 10 | $\text{Capital Recovery Factor} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual equivalent of capital cost | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** The relationship between *economic life* and *average cost per hour* is a frequently tested concept. As the plant ages, hourly owning cost increases (more repairs) but depreciated value decreases. The **minimum of the sum curve** gives the economic life.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

This framework is used by:
- **Contractors** for tender rate preparation
- **Project managers** for equipment selection
- **Quantity surveyors** for BOQ and rate analysis
- **Equipment leasing firms** for setting rental rates
- **Banks and financial institutions** for asset valuation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Straight-Line Method of Depreciation

The straight-line method assumes that the asset loses its value **uniformly** over its useful life.

**Assumptions:**
- Initial cost (delivered price) = $C$
- Salvage value at end of useful life = $S$
- Useful life = $N$ years

**Derivation:**

$$
\text{Depreciation per year } D = \frac{\text{Total Depreciable Value}}{\text{Useful Life}}
$$

$$
D = \frac{C - S}{N}
$$

**Book value at end of year $k$** is the original cost minus accumulated depreciation:

$$
BV_k = C - k \cdot D = C - k \cdot \frac{C-S}{N}
$$

**Verification at end of life ($k = N$):**

$$
BV_N = C - N \cdot \frac{C-S}{N} = C - (C - S) = S \quad \checkmark
$$

**Worked Example (KTU style):**

A concrete mixer costs **₹5,00,000** delivered, has a salvage value of **₹50,000**, and an estimated useful life of **10 years**. Compute the annual depreciation and the book value at the end of year 6.

**Solution:**

$$
D = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{10} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{10} = \text{₹45,000 per year}
$$

$$
BV_6 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 6 \times 45{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}70{,}000 = \text{₹2,30,000}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Straight-line method is the **simplest** and is most suitable for assets like *buildings* and *light tools*. For heavy plant where early-life depreciation is higher, the declining balance method is preferred.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Declining Balance Method (DB)

In this method, a **fixed percentage $d$** of the *book value at the beginning of the year* is written off each year.

**Depreciation in year $n$:**

$$
D_n = BV_{n-1} \times d
$$

Since $BV_{n-1} = C \times (1-d)^{n-1}$:

$$
D_n = C \times d \times (1-d)^{n-1}
$$

**Book value at end of year $n$:**

$$
BV_n = C \times (1-d)^n
$$

**Worked Example:**

A hydraulic excavator costs **₹30,00,000**. Using declining balance at rate $d = 0.20$ (20%), compute depreciation for years 1 to 3.

**Year 1:**

$$
D_1 = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 \times (1-0.20)^0 = \text{₹6,00,000}
$$

$$
BV_1 = 30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹24,00,000}
$$

**Year 2:**

$$
D_2 = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = \text{₹4,80,000}
$$

$$
BV_2 = 24{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹19,20,000}
$$

**Year 3:**

$$
D_3 = 19{,}20{,}000 \times 0.20 = \text{₹3,84,000}
$$

$$
BV_3 = 19{,}20{,}000 - 3{,}84{,}000 = \text{₹15,36,000}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The depreciation amount **decreases every year** in the DB method, which realistically reflects the actual loss in market value of construction plant.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hourly Owning Cost

A contractor's owning cost includes *depreciation, interest, insurance, and taxes* spread over the **annual working hours**.

**Step 1:** Compute total fixed cost per year (excluding operating cost):

$$
C_{\text{fixed,year}} = D_{\text{annual}} + I_{\text{interest}} + I_{\text{insurance}} + T_{\text{tax}}
$$

**Step 2:** Annual working hours (typical KTU assumption):

$$
H_{\text{year}} = \text{Shift hours} \times \text{Working days per year} = 8 \times 250 = 2000 \text{ hrs/year}
$$

**Step 3:** Hourly owning cost:

$$
C_{\text{own,hr}} = \frac{C_{\text{fixed,year}}}{H_{\text{year}}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Hourly Cost and Unit Rate

The composite hourly rate is:

$$
C_{\text{total,hr}} = C_{\text{own,hr}} + C_{\text{op,hr}}
$$

where

$$
C_{\text{op,hr}} = C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{lube}} + C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{operator}}
$$

The **unit rate of work** (₹ per m³, per tonne, etc.) is:

$$
R = \frac{C_{\text{total,hr}}}{Q_{\text{hr}}}
$$

where $Q_{\text{hr}}$ is the plant's output per hour.

### 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example: Full Rate Computation

**Problem:** A bulldozer is being used for excavation in a road project. The following data is provided:

- Delivered cost: **₹40,00,000**
- Salvage value: **₹4,00,000**
- Useful life: **10 years**
- Working hours per year: **2,000 hrs**
- Interest on capital: **12% per annum** (on average investment)
- Insurance and taxes: **3% per annum** (on initial cost)
- Fuel cost: **₹800 per hour**
- Lubricant cost: **20% of fuel cost**
- Maintenance and spares: **₹250 per hour**
- Operator wages: **₹150 per hour**
- Average output: **30 m³ per hour**

Find: (a) Hourly owning cost, (b) Hourly operating cost, (c) Total hourly cost, (d) Unit rate of excavation in ₹/m³.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Hourly Owning Cost:**

Annual straight-line depreciation:

$$
D = \frac{40{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{10} = \text{₹3,60,000 per year}
$$

Annual interest on average investment (average of initial and salvage):

$$
\text{Average investment} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{2} = \text{₹22,00,000}
$$

$$
I_{\text{interest}} = 0.12 \times 22{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹2,64,000 per year}
$$

Annual insurance and taxes:

$$
I_{\text{ins}} = 0.03 \times 40{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹1,20,000 per year}
$$

Total fixed cost per year:

$$
C_{\text{fixed,year}} = 3{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}64{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹7,44,000 per year}
$$

Hourly owning cost:

$$
C_{\text{own,hr}} = \frac{7{,}44{,}000}{2000} = \text{₹372 per hour}
$$

**Step (b) — Hourly Operating Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{op,hr}} = 800 + (0.20 \times 800) + 250 + 150
$$

$$
C_{\text{op,hr}} = 800 + 160 + 250 + 150 = \text{₹1,360 per hour}
$$

**Step (c) — Total Hourly Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total,hr}} = 372 + 1360 = \text{₹1,732 per hour}
$$

**Step (d) — Unit Rate:**

$$
R = \frac{1{,}732}{30} = \text{₹57.73 per m}^3 \approx \text{₹58 per m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** The unit rate derived above is the **direct plant cost**. In actual BOQ rate analysis, this is multiplied by a factor of 1.15 to 1.25 to account for contractor's profit, overheads, and contingencies.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Depreciation

| Asset Type | Recommended Method | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Hand tools (shovels, hammers) | Negligible / expensed | Cost is too small to depreciate |
| Light vehicles, mixers | Straight-line | Uniform wear over life |
| Heavy plant (excavator, crane) | Declining balance | High early depreciation matches market reality |
| Buildings, offices | Straight-line | Long, uniform useful life |
| IT equipment | Double-declining balance | Rapid obsolescence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Classification of Construction Plant

The following Mermaid diagram presents the complete taxonomy of construction plant as expected in KTU 2024 Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["CONSTRUCTION PLANT AND EQUIPMENT"]
    root --> tools["TOOLS"]
    root --> plant["PLANT"]
    root --> equip["EQUIPMENT"]

    tools --> t1["Hand Tools"]
    tools --> t2["Power Tools"]
    tools --> t3["Measuring Tools"]
    t1 --> t1a["Shovels and Pickaxes"]
    t1 --> t1b["Hammers and Chisels"]
    t2 --> t2a["Drilling Machines"]
    t2 --> t2b["Grinders and Saws"]
    t3 --> t3a["Tape and Levels"]
    t3 --> t3b["Theodolite Total Station"]

    plant --> p1["Earthmoving Plant"]
    plant --> p2["Compaction Plant"]
    plant --> p3["Concreting Plant"]
    plant --> p4["Hoisting Plant"]
    plant --> p5["Piling Plant"]
    plant --> p6["Material Handling Plant"]

    p1 --> p1a["Excavators"]
    p1 --> p1b["Bulldozers"]
    p1 --> p1c["Loaders"]
    p1 --> p1d["Scrapers"]

    p2 --> p2a["Smooth Rollers"]
    p2 --> p2b["Sheepfoot Rollers"]
    p2 --> p2c["Vibratory Rollers"]

    p3 --> p3a["Concrete Mixers"]
    p3 --> p3b["Batching Plants"]
    p3 --> p3c["Transit Mixers"]
    p3 --> p3d["Concrete Pumps"]

    p4 --> p4a["Tower Cranes"]
    p4 --> p4b["Mobile Cranes"]
    p4 --> p4c["Hoists and Winches"]

    p5 --> p5a["Pile Drivers"]
    p5 --> p5b["Bored Piling Rigs"]

    p6 --> p6a["Dump Trucks"]
    p6 --> p6b["Belt Conveyors"]
    p6 --> p6c["Forklifts"]

    equip --> e1["Light Equipment"]
    equip --> e2["Heavy Equipment"]
    equip --> e3["Specialized Equipment"]
```

### 4.2 Cost-Flow Architecture: How Plant Cost Enters Rate Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Initial Cost of Plant C"] --> B["Useful Life N years"]
    A --> C["Salvage Value S"]
    B --> D["Annual Depreciation D"]
    C --> D
    A --> E["Interest Insurance Tax"]
    D --> F["Annual Fixed Cost"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Hourly Owning Cost"]
    H["Annual Working Hours"] --> G
    I["Fuel Cost"] --> J["Hourly Operating Cost"]
    K["Lubricants"] --> J
    L["Maintenance"] --> J
    M["Operator Wages"] --> J
    G --> N["Total Hourly Cost"]
    J --> N
    N --> O["Output per Hour Q"]
    O --> P["Unit Rate R = Cost divided by Q"]
    P --> Q["BOQ Rate Analysis"]
```

### 4.3 Economic Life Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start: Use of Plant Begins"]
    start --> y1["Year 1"]
    y1 --> y2["Year 2"]
    y2 --> y3["Year n"]
    y3 --> calc["Compute Average Cost per Hour = Cumulative Cost divided by Cumulative Hours"]
    calc --> compare{"Is this the minimum?"}
    compare -- Yes --> replace["Replace Plant at End of Year n"]
    compare -- No --> y3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between "Tools" and "Plant" used in a construction project. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tools | Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low cost (a few hundred to a few thousand rupees) | High cost (lakhs to crores of rupees) |
| **Power source** | Mostly hand-operated | Mechanically powered (engine/electric motor) |
| **Economic life** | Less than 2 years | 5 to 15 years |
| **Depreciation** | Negligible | Computed using formal methods |
| **Operation** | Operated directly by workmen | Requires trained operator |
| **Examples** | Shovel, hammer, trowel, measuring tape | Excavator, bulldozer, tower crane, concrete mixer |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples + 1 mark for differentiation table content)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any six types of construction plant used for earthwork and concreting operations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Earthwork Plant:**
1. Excavator (hydraulic)
2. Bulldozer
3. Loader / Skid-steer loader
4. Scraper

**Concreting Plant:**
5. Concrete mixer (tilting drum)
6. Transit mixer
7. Concrete pump (boom type)
8. Needle vibrator

*(Any six, 0.5 mark each, total 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3 (a).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A wheel loader has the following data:**
- Delivered cost: **₹25,00,000**
- Salvage value: **₹2,50,000**
- Estimated useful life: **8 years**
- Working hours per year: **2,000 hrs**
