- Interest on capital: **10% per annum** (on average investment)
- Insurance and taxes: **2.5% per annum** (on initial cost)
- Fuel consumption: **8 litres/hour** at **₹95/litre**
- Lubricant cost: **25% of fuel cost**
- Maintenance and spares: **₹200 per hour**
- Operator wages: **₹120 per hour**
- Average output: **40 m³ per hour**

**Compute:**
- (i) Annual depreciation
- (ii) Total annual fixed cost
- (iii) Hourly owning cost
- (iv) Hourly operating cost
- (v) Total hourly cost
- (vi) Unit rate of loading in ₹/m³

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual depreciation (straight-line):**

$$
D = \frac{25{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000}{8} = \frac{22{,}50{,}000}{8} = \text{₹2,81,250 per year}
$$

**(ii) Total annual fixed cost:**

Average investment:

$$
\frac{25{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000}{2} = \text{₹13,75,000}
$$

Interest:

$$
0.10 \times 13{,}75{,}000 = \text{₹1,37,500}
$$

Insurance and taxes:

$$
0.025 \times 25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹62,500}
$$

Total fixed cost per year:

$$
2{,}81{,}250 + 1{,}37{,}500 + 62{,}500 = \text{₹4,81,250}
$$

**(iii) Hourly owning cost:**

$$
C_{\text{own}} = \frac{4{,}81{,}250}{2000} = \text{₹240.63 per hour} \approx \text{₹241/hr}
$$

**(iv) Hourly operating cost:**

$$
\text{Fuel} = 8 \times 95 = \text{₹760/hr}
$$

$$
\text{Lube} = 0.25 \times 760 = \text{₹190/hr}
$$

$$
\text{Maintenance} = \text{₹200/hr}
$$

$$
\text{Operator} = \text{₹120/hr}
$$

$$
C_{\text{op}} = 760 + 190 + 200 + 120 = \text{₹1,270/hr}
$$

**(v) Total hourly cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 241 + 1270 = \text{₹1,511/hr}
$$

**(vi) Unit rate:**

$$
R = \frac{1{,}511}{40} = \text{₹37.78 per m}^3 \approx \text{₹37.80/m}^3
$$

---

**Q3 (b).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the concept of "economic life" of a construction plant. How is it determined graphically and analytically?**

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **economic life** of a construction plant is the period of use (in years or working hours) at which the **average cost per unit of work is minimum**. Beyond this period, the cumulative repair and maintenance costs rise steeply, offsetting the reduced depreciation, making replacement economical.

**Factors affecting economic life (1 mark):**
- Wear and tear due to usage intensity
- Technological obsolescence
- Cost of spares and skilled labour availability
- Hours of operation per year
- Site conditions (abrasive soils, dusty environment)

**Graphical method (2 marks):**
1. Plot a graph of **average cost per hour (Y-axis)** vs. **Year of use (X-axis)**.
2. Plot another curve of **repairs and maintenance cost per hour** (rising with age).
3. The lowest point on the **sum of owning + operating cost per hour** curve indicates the economic life.

**Analytical method (2 marks):**
For each year $n$, compute:

$$
\text{Average cost per hour}_n = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(C_{\text{own},k} + C_{\text{op},k})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} H_k}
$$

The year $n^*$ for which this average cost is **minimum** is the economic life. Typical values are **5 to 10 years** for earthmoving plant, **10 to 12 years** for stationary plant like batching plants, and **3 to 5 years** for light vehicles.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q4 (a).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A construction firm purchases an excavator for ₹35,00,000. The salvage value after 5 years is estimated at ₹5,00,000. Compute the depreciation for each year using:**
- (i) Straight-line method
- (ii) Declining balance method (rate = 30%)
- (iii) Sum-of-years-digits method

**Prepare a comparison table and comment on which method is most appropriate.**

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C = 35{,}00{,}000$
- $S = 5{,}00{,}000$
- $N = 5$ years

**(i) Straight-line method (1.5 marks):**

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5} = \text{₹6,00,000 per year}
$$

| Year | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | 35,00,000 |
| 1 | 6,00,000 | 29,00,000 |
| 2 | 6,00,000 | 23,00,000 |
| 3 | 6,00,000 | 17,00,000 |
| 4 | 6,00,000 | 11,00,000 |
| 5 | 6,00,000 | 5,00,000 |

**(ii) Declining balance method at $d = 0.30$ (2.5 marks):**

$D_n = C \times d \times (1-d)^{n-1}$

| Year | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10,50,000 | 24,50,000 |
| 2 | 7,35,000 | 17,15,000 |
| 3 | 5,14,500 | 12,00,500 |
| 4 | 3,60,150 | 8,40,350 |
| 5 | 2,52,105 | 5,88,245 |

*Note: Book value at end of year 5 (₹5,88,245) exceeds the salvage value of ₹5,00,000 because the rate does not exactly write off to the residual value. The firm may either (a) stop depreciation when BV = S, or (b) adjust the last year depreciation.*

**(iii) Sum-of-years-digits method (2.5 marks):**

Sum of digits $= 1+2+3+4+5 = 15$
Depreciable value $= 35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000$

$$
D_n = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{(6-n)}{15}
$$

| Year | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5/15 | 10,00,000 | 25,00,000 |
| 2 | 4/15 | 8,00,000 | 17,00,000 |
| 3 | 3/15 | 6,00,000 | 11,00,000 |
| 4 | 2/15 | 4,00,000 | 7,00,000 |
| 5 | 1/15 | 2,00,000 | 5,00,000 |

**Comment (0.5 mark):**
For a **heavy construction excavator**, the **declining balance method** is most appropriate as it reflects the high front-loaded wear and tear and faster loss of resale value in early years.

---

**Q4 (b).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Discuss the factors that govern the selection of construction plant for a project. What is "plant layout" in the context of a construction site?**

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Factors governing plant selection (5 marks):**

1. **Nature and scope of work:** The type of project (building, road, bridge, tunnel) dictates whether excavators, cranes, or pavers are required.

2. **Volume of work:** Large volumes justify owned plant; small jobs are better served by hired plant.

3. **Time schedule:** Tight schedules demand high-capacity, multiple units; relaxed schedules allow smaller, slower equipment.

4. **Availability of plant:** Locally available vs. imported — affects cost, spares, and operator availability.

5. **Site conditions:** Soil type, ground bearing capacity, space constraints, accessibility, and climate influence choice (e.g., crawler-mounted for soft soil, wheeled for hard surfaces).

6. **Cost considerations:** Initial cost, owning cost, operating cost, and resale value. Hired vs. owned economics must be compared.

7. **Operating costs and efficiency:** Fuel efficiency, operator skill needed, maintenance cost.

8. **Reliability and durability:** Proven equipment with good after-sales support.

9. **Standardisation:** Preference for equipment whose spares and mechanics are readily available.

10. **Government regulations and safety:** Emission norms, operator certification, noise limits.

**Plant layout (2 marks):**
A **plant layout** is the systematic arrangement of equipment, material storage, batching areas, approach roads, and services on a construction site to ensure:
- Smooth material flow (unidirectional)
- Minimum re-handling
- Safety clearance from power lines and excavations
- Easy access for trucks and workers
- Optimum use of available site area

The layout is typically drawn on a **site plan with clear zoning** for aggregate stockpiles, cement silos, mixing units, curing yards, and equipment parking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct salvage value** in straight-line depreciation — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using initial cost** instead of **average investment** for interest calculation — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not converting annual cost to hourly cost** before dividing by output — entire unit-rate calculation becomes wrong.
> 4. **Confusing "working hours per year"** with "calendar hours" (8760). Use **2,000 hrs/year** unless specified.
> 5. **Mixing units:** Always express fuel in ₹/hr, output in m³/hr or tonne/hr; never mix kg and tonnes.
> 6. **Forgetting the "operator wages"** in operating cost is a frequent 0.5-mark loss.
> 7. In classification questions, students often miss **conveying equipment** — read the question carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Tools vs Plant:** Tools are < ₹10,000 typically, hand-operated, life < 2 years. Plant is mechanically powered, life 5–15 years, requires formal depreciation.

- ✅ **Classification by function:** Earthmoving, Compaction, Concreting, Hoisting, Piling, Material Handling, Drilling, Demolition.

- ✅ **Ownership cost components:** Depreciation + Interest (on average investment) + Insurance + Tax + Storage.

- ✅ **Operating cost components:** Fuel + Lubricant + Maintenance + Operator wages.

- ✅ **Straight-line depreciation formula:** $D = (C - S) / N$ — used for buildings, light assets.

- ✅ **Declining balance:** $D_n = C \times d \times (1-d)^{n-1}$ — used for heavy construction plant.

- ✅ **Sum-of-years-digits:** $D_n = (C-S) \times (N-n+1) / [N(N+1)/2]$ — used for medium-life plant.

- ✅ **Total hourly cost** = Owning cost/hr + Operating cost/hr.

- ✅ **Unit rate** = Total hourly cost / Output per hour.

- ✅ **Economic life** = year at which average cost per hour is minimum.

- ✅ **Working hours per year** (KTU standard assumption) = 2,000 hours (8 hrs/day × 250 working days).

- ✅ **HP to kW conversion:** 1 HP = 0.746 kW.

- ✅ **Plant layout principles:** Minimum re-handling, unidirectional flow, safety clearances, zoning.

- ✅ **Hired plant is preferred** for small, short-duration jobs; **owned plant** for large, long-duration, repetitive work.

- ✅ **Always express unit rates** to two decimal places and include proper units (₹/m³, ₹/tonne, ₹/m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** In numerical problems, KTU examiners award partial credit for correct *formula selection* even if arithmetic slips occur. Always write the formula first, substitute values in a separate step, then compute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Percentage Charge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Percentage Charge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Percentage Charge Method of Valuation** is a standardized indirect method of building valuation in which the **annual rental value (or capitalized value)** of a property is derived by applying a stipulated **percentage rate** to the **total present-day cost of construction** of the building (including sanitary, electrical, water-supply, and other essential services).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this method is officially classified under **Methods of Valuation** (Module 1 – Introduction to Quantity Surveying and Valuation). The relevant standard reference range used in Indian valuation practice is **6% to 10%** depending on building typology, structural health, and maintenance condition.

The general working expression is:

$$\text{Annual Rental Value (ARV)} = \text{Total Present Cost of Construction} \times \frac{P}{100}$$

where $P$ is the **percentage rate** prescribed for the category of the structure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Subscription Discount" Model**
>
> Imagine you buy a premium gym membership worth **₹1,00,000** (the *replacement cost* of the asset). Instead of paying the full amount, you take a *monthly* subscription (the *annual rent*) at a *fixed percentage* of the original cost — say **8% per year**. So you pay **₹8,000 per year** as rent, which represents a fair return on the owner's investment plus depreciation.
>
> In the same way, a building owner is entitled to a **fair yearly return** on the capital locked up in the building. The government / valuer fixes that "fair return" as a *percentage* of the present-day cost — this is the **Percentage Charge Method**.

**Key Intuition:** The method essentially converts a *one-time lump-sum construction cost* into an *equivalent annual income stream* (or vice versa) using a single multiplier — the percentage rate.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard percentage rates are widely used in Indian PWD/CPWD/Valuation practice (must be memorized):

| **Building Category** | **% Charge per Annum** | **Equivalent YP at 8% interest** |
|-----------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **RCC / Framed structures** (well-maintained) | **6 %** | 12.5 |
| **RCC / Framed structures** (average) | **7 %** | ≈ 14.3 |
| **Load-bearing masonry** (well-maintained) | **8 %** | 12.5 |
| **Load-bearing masonry** (average) | **9 %** | ≈ 11.1 |
| **Old / dilapidated structures** | **10 %** | 10.0 |
| **Commercial buildings (prime)** | **8 – 10 %** | 10 – 12.5 |
| **Godowns / industrial sheds** | **10 – 12 %** | 8.33 – 10.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Assumption in KTU Board Papers:**
> When not specified otherwise, examiners expect students to assume the rate of interest as **8% per annum** (which corresponds to **YP = 12.5 years**) unless the question explicitly states a different rate.

---

## 1.4 The Two Distinct "Percentage Charge" Concepts in Module 1

It is **critically important** for KTU 2024 students to distinguish between the two concepts that often share the same name:

1. **Percentage Charge on Construction Cost (Additive Percentages)**
   - These are *additive percentages* applied during *detailed estimate preparation* to cover:
     - Water supply & sanitary installations: **5 – 8 %**
     - Electrification & internal wiring: **5 – 8 %**
     - Compound wall, gates, paths: **3 – 5 %**
     - Contingencies: **3 – 5 %**
     - Work-charged establishment: **1.5 – 2.5 %**
     - Tools & Plant: **1 – 2 %**
   - These percentages **add up** to the building cost to arrive at the *total project cost*.

2. **Percentage Method of Valuation (Multiplicative Percentage)**
   - A *single percentage* of the total present construction cost is treated as the **gross annual rent**.
   - This percentage is then capitalized using **Years' Purchase (YP)** to obtain the **capitalized value** of the property.
   - Used for **rental fixation, premium assessment, tax valuation, insurance.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Trap:** Marks are routinely lost when students *mix up* the two concepts. A KTU 2024 question may ask for either:
> (a) "Add the percentage charges for water supply, electrification, etc. to obtain the total cost" — *Concept 1*.
> (b) "Apply the percentage method to find the annual rent" — *Concept 2*.
> Always read the question stem carefully!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Does the Method Work?

The Percentage Charge Method rests on three core economic and engineering principles:

- **Principle of Substitution:** A buyer will not pay more for a property than the cost of acquiring an equivalent substitute. The *present-day replacement cost* forms the upper bound of value.
- **Principle of Anticipation:** The value of a property = *Present Worth* of all *future net benefits* (rent, salvage) it is expected to generate over its economic life.
- **Principle of Supply & Demand:** The fixed percentage (e.g., 6% for RCC) is essentially an *empirically observed fair return rate* in the regional real-estate market, adjusted for *depreciation, obsolescence, and risk*.

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**

1. Compute the **Present-Day Cost of Construction (C)** of the building — using current PWD/CPWD SSR (Schedule of Rates) multiplied by the *plinth area* (in m²).
2. Apply the prescribed **Percentage Rate (P%)** to obtain the **Gross Annual Rent (R)**:

$$R = C \times \frac{P}{100}$$

3. Deduct the **Annual Repairs & Maintenance Allowance (A)**, if specified, to obtain the **Net Annual Rent (NAR)**:

$$\text{NAR} = R - A$$

4. Capitalize the net annual rent using **Years' Purchase (YP)** at the *standard rate of interest (i)* to get the **Capitalized Value (CV)**:

$$CV = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}$$

where,

$$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{i} \quad \text{(for perpetuity)}$$

or, for a **fixed life of *n* years** with **scrap value *S***:

$$\text{YP} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}} + \frac{S}{C} \times \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

5. The capitalized value is the **true present market value** of the property under the percentage method.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Standard Unit** | **Remarks** |
|-------|--------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Gross Annual Rent | $R = C \times P/100$ | ₹ per year | $C$ = Present cost, $P$ = % rate |
| 2 | Net Annual Rent | $\text{NAR} = R - A$ | ₹ per year | $A$ = annual repairs allowance |
| 3 | Years' Purchase (Perpetuity) | $\text{YP} = 1/i$ | years | When life is infinite |
| 4 | Years' Purchase (Finite Life) | $\text{YP} = \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right] / \left[i(1+i)^{n}\right]$ | years | When life is $n$ years, scrap = 0 |
| 5 | Years' Purchase (With Scrap) | $\text{YP}_{\text{net}} = \text{YP} + \left(S/C\right) \cdot \left[1/(1+i)^{n}\right]$ | years | With salvage value $S$ |
| 6 | Capitalized Value | $CV = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}$ | ₹ | Final answer |
| 7 | Plinth Area Rate Estimate | $C = \text{Plinth Area (m²)} \times \text{Rate (₹/m²)}$ | ₹ | Foundation of $C$ |
| 8 | Total Project Cost (Additive %) | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{building}} \times (1 + \Sigma p_i / 100)$ | ₹ | Concept 1 — additive % |
| 9 | Depreciation (Straight Line) | $D = (C - S) / n$ | ₹/year | Linear method |
| 10 | Book Value after $t$ years | $V_t = C - t \cdot D$ | ₹ | For sinking fund method use $V_t = C \cdot (1 - t/n)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe `|` symbol is used inside table cells above** to maintain Mermaid/Markdown compatibility — absolute value and "divided by" notation is used in plain text form instead.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Percentage Charge Method is used extensively in:

- **PWD/CPWD valuations** for fixing government rental of buildings.
- **Municipal property tax assessment** by local self-government bodies in Kerala.
- **Insurance underwriting** to determine the *replacement value* of structures.
- **Court-of-law compensation** in land-acquisition disputes under the *Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013*.
- **Premium fixation** for lease of government / public-sector properties.
- **Stamp duty & registration value** assessment by the Sub-Registrar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Kerala Building Tax (KBT)** and **Land Tax** framework, the percentage method directly influences the *annual value* of buildings declared for taxation — making it a high-yield topic in valuation modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Years' Purchase (YP) Formula

The **Years' Purchase (YP)** is the *most important* derived concept in the Percentage Charge Method. It is a multiplier that converts a *uniform annual income* into a *lump-sum capitalized value*.

### 3.1.1 YP for Perpetuity (Infinite Life)

Consider a property yielding a *constant* net annual income $N$ forever, and let the rate of interest be $i$ (as a decimal).

The present value of the first year's income is $N / (1+i)$.  
The present value of the second year's income is $N / (1+i)^{2}$.  
Continuing infinitely, the total capitalized value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} + \frac{N}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{N}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots \quad \text{(infinite GP)} \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} \cdot \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{1+i} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots \right] \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{1+i}} \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1+i}{i} \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{YP} = \text{CV} / N$, we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{YP} = \frac{1}{i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Summing an infinite geometric progression with first term $N/(1+i)$ and common ratio $1/(1+i)$ yields $N/i$, and dividing by $N$ isolates YP.

For $i = 0.08$ (i.e., 8% interest), $\text{YP} = 1/0.08 = 12.5$ years.

---

### 3.1.2 YP for Finite Life (No Salvage Value)

For a property with a finite economic life of $n$ years and *zero* salvage value, the GP is *finite*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} + \frac{N}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{N}{(1+i)^{n}} \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}} \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i/(1+i)} \\
\text{CV} &= \frac{N \cdot \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^{n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \frac{N \cdot \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right]}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{YP} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** A finite GP with $n$ terms and common ratio $r = 1/(1+i)$ gives a closed-form sum $a(1-r^{n})/(1-r)$, which after algebraic manipulation becomes the standard YP formula.

---

### 3.1.3 YP With Salvage Value

If the property has a salvage value $S$ at the end of $n$ years, the present value of the salvage is $S/(1+i)^{n}$, and the total capitalized value becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{YP}_{\text{net}} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}} \;+\; \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** YP splits into two terms: a *finite annuity term* (income) plus a *lump-sum* discounted salvage term.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 2024 Board Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (Worked-Out Model):**
> A residential building has a plinth area of **120 m²**. The present-day construction rate is **₹2,500/m²** (including all basic finishes). As per the percentage method, the building fetches an annual rent equal to **8%** of the present cost. The annual repairs allowance is **₹4,000**. Calculate the **annual rent, net annual rent, and capitalized value** of the building assuming an interest rate of **8% per annum** and a perpetual life.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Present Cost of Construction (C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \text{Plinth Area} \times \text{Rate per m}^{2} \\
C &= 120 \;\text{m}^{2} \times 2{,}500 \;\text{₹/m}^{2} \\
C &= 3{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Percentage Charge to Get Gross Annual Rent (R)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= C \times \frac{P}{100} \\
R &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{8}{100} \\
R &= 24{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Net Annual Rent (NAR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAR} &= R - A \\
\text{NAR} &= 24{,}000 - 4{,}000 \\
\text{NAR} &= 20{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Years' Purchase (YP) at 8% Interest, Perpetuity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YP} &= \frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Capitalized Value (CV)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{NAR} \times \text{YP} \\
\text{CV} &= 20{,}000 \times 12.5 \\
\text{CV} &= 2{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Annual Rent = ₹24,000 | NAR = ₹20,000 | **Capitalized Value = ₹2,50,000**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How the Method Adapts to Scenarios

| **Scenario** | **Adjustment to Base Formula** | **Effect on CV** |
|--------------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| Building age > 10 years | Add depreciation @ 1–2% per year of age | CV decreases |
| Rent fixed in *gross* terms (no repairs) | Skip Step 3; use $R$ directly | CV is higher |
| Rent fixed for *finite* lease (say 30 years) | Use finite-life YP formula | CV is lower |
| Salvage / scrap value > 0 | Add $(S/N)(1+i)^{-n}$ to YP | CV slightly higher |
| Location premium (urban 25% above normal) | Multiply $C$ by 1.25 first | CV scales up |
| Percentage rate reduced to 6% (luxury market) | $R = 0.06 \times C$ | CV decreases by 25% |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always work in a *table format* showing each adjustment step. Examiners award step marks liberally if the workflow is clearly displayed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Percentage Charge Method

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **end-to-end functional flow** of the Percentage Charge Method as applied in a KTU-style valuation problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Plinth Area and Construction Rate Given"] --> B["Compute Present Cost C = Area x Rate"]
    B --> C{"Additive Percentage Charges Applicable?"}
    C -->|"Yes (Concept 1)"| D["Add Water Supply, Electrical, Contingency %"]
    C -->|"No (Concept 2)"| E["Use C as Base for Percentage Method"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Compute Gross Annual Rent R = C x P divided by 100"]
    F --> G{"Annual Repairs Allowance A Specified?"}
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Compute NAR = R minus A"]
    G -->|"No"| I["Treat R as NAR"]
    H --> J["Determine YP based on Life and Interest Rate"]
    I --> J
    J --> K{"Life is Perpetual or Finite?"}
    K -->|"Perpetual"| L["YP = 1 divided by i"]
    K -->|"Finite with Scrap"| M["YP = Annuity Term + Salvage Discount Term"]
    L --> N["Compute Capitalized Value CV = NAR x YP"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["FINAL: Present Market Value of Building"]
    O --> P["Apply Depreciation if Building Aged"]
    P --> Q["Final Valuation Report"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a *functional decision node* in valuation practice. The diamond (rhombus) blocks represent *decision points* where the valuer must choose between alternative formulations. The entire flow is *deterministic* given the input parameters — which is why this method is preferred for *quick*, *standardized* valuations in PWD/government workflows.

## 4.2 Concept-Mapping Diagram — Two Meanings of "Percentage Charge"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC["Percentage Charge Topic"]
    PC --> C1["Concept 1: Additive Percentages on Construction Cost"]
    PC --> C2["Concept 2: Percentage Method of Valuation"]
    C1 --> C1a["Water Supply and Sanitary 5 to 8 percent"]
    C1 --> C1b["Electrification 5 to 8 percent"]
    C1 --> C1c["Contingency 3 to 5 percent"]
    C1 --> C1d["Establishment 1.5 to 2.5 percent"]
    C1 --> C1e["Total Project Cost = C x 1 plus sum of percentages"]
    C2 --> C2a["Gross Annual Rent R = C x P divided by 100"]
    C2 --> C2b["Net Annual Rent NAR = R minus A"]
    C2 --> C2c["Years Purchase YP = 1 divided by i or finite form"]
    C2 --> C2d["Capitalized Value CV = NAR x YP"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This disambiguation diagram is a high-yield visual aid** — examiners have been observed to set "compare and contrast" questions on these two concepts in KTU 2024 model papers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1(a)]**

**Q1.** Define the **Percentage Method of Valuation**. State any **two situations** where it is preferred over other methods of valuation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Percentage Method of Valuation is a method in which the *annual rental value* of a building is determined by applying a *stipulated percentage rate* to the *present-day cost of construction* of the building. The standard rates vary from **6% to 10%** depending on the type, condition, and use of the building.

**Situations where it is preferred:**
1. **Quick preliminary valuation** of government buildings, PWD/CPWD properties, and large inventories where detailed estimation is impractical.
2. **Municipal tax assessment** of residential and commercial properties, where a *uniform* and *standardized* approach is required for fairness and ease of administration.
3. **Insurance valuation**, where a fast and reproducible estimate of *replacement value* is required for premium fixation.

> *(Any two well-explained situations = 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1(b)]**

**Q2.** What are the **standard percentage rates** of valuation prescribed for the following building categories?
(a) Well-maintained RCC framed building
(b) Average load-bearing masonry building
(c) Old dilapidated structure

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Category** | **Standard % Rate per Annum** |
|--------------|-------------------------------|
| (a) Well-maintained RCC framed building | **6 %** |
| (b) Average load-bearing masonry building | **9 %** |
| (c) Old dilapidated structure | **10 %** |

> *(1 mark for each correct rate.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

A two-storey residential building has the following data:

- Plinth area per floor: **100 m²**
- Present construction rate: **₹3,000 per m²**
- Percentage charge as per valuation table: **8% per annum**
- Annual repair allowance: **₹5,000**
- Rate of interest: **8% per annum**
- Expected future life: **40 years**
- Salvage value: **₹50,000**

**Compute:**
**(a)** The annual rent and net annual rent. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The capitalized value (present market value) of the building. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Annual Rent and NAR (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Total Present Cost of Construction (C)**

Plinth area for two storeys = $100 \times 2 = 200 \;\text{m}^{2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 200 \;\text{m}^{2} \times 3{,}000 \;\text{₹/m}^{2} \\
C &= 6{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Gross Annual Rent (R)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= C \times \frac{P}{100} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{8}{100} \\
R &= 48{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Net Annual Rent (NAR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAR} &= R - A = 48{,}000 - 5{,}000 \\
\text{NAR} &= 43{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (a) Answer:** Gross Annual Rent = **₹48,000/year** | NAR = **₹43,000/year** *(7 marks: 3 for C, 2 for R, 2 for NAR)*

---

**Part (b) — Capitalized Value (7 marks):**

**Step 4: Compute Years' Purchase (YP) — Finite Life with Salvage**

For $i = 0.08$, $n = 40$ years:

Annuity factor (finite life, no salvage):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YP}_{\text{annuity}} &= \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = \frac{(1.08)^{40} - 1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^{40}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $(1.08)^{40} \approx 21.7245$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YP}_{\text{annuity}} &= \frac{21.7245 - 1}{0.08 \times 21.7245} = \frac{20.7245}{1.73796} \approx 11.925 \;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

Salvage discount term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YP}_{\text{salvage}} &= \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}} = \frac{50{,}000}{43{,}000} \times \frac{1}{21.7245} \\
\text{YP}_{\text{salvage}} &= 1.1628 \times 0.04603 \approx 0.0535 \;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total YP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YP}_{\text{total}} &= 11.925 + 0.0535 = 11.978 \;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Capitalized Value (CV)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}_{\text{total}} \\
\text{CV} &= 43{,}000 \times 11.978 \\
\text{CV} &\approx 5{,}15{,}054 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (b) Answer:** **Capitalized Value ≈ ₹5,15,054** *(7 marks: 3 for YP computation, 2 for salvage term, 2 for final CV)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** A godown has a plinth area of **250 m²** and is to be valued using the percentage method. The present construction rate is **₹1,800 per m²**. The prescribed percentage charge is **10%** and the annual repair allowance is **₹3,000**. The rate of interest is **9% per annum** and the building has a **perpetual life**. Find the **annual rent, NAR, and capitalized value**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with a neat labelled diagram, the **functional workflow of the percentage method** as applied in the valuation of a building. List **any four standard percentage charges** added to the construction cost during detailed estimation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Valuation of Godown (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Present Cost (C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 250 \;\text{m}^{2} \times 1{,}800 \;\text{₹/m}^{2} = 4{,}50{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Gross Annual Rent (R)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 4{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{10}{100} = 45{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net Annual Rent (NAR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAR} &= 45{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 42{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: YP (Perpetuity, $i = 0.09$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YP} &= \frac{1}{0.09} \approx 11.11 \;\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Capitalized Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= 42{,}000 \times 11.11 \approx 4{,}66{,}620 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (a) Answer:** R = **₹45,000** | NAR = **₹42,000** | **CV ≈ ₹4,66,620** *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 split)*

---

**Part (b) — Functional Workflow and Additive Percentages (7 marks):**

**Workflow of the Percentage Method (5 marks — write in steps):**

1. **Step 1 — Data Collection:** Measure plinth area and obtain present-day construction rate from PWD/CPWD SSR.
2. **Step 2 — Cost Computation:** $C = \text{Plinth Area} \times \text{Rate per m}^{2}$.
3. **Step 3 — Apply Percentage:** $R = C \times P/100$ to obtain gross annual rent.
4. **Step 4 — Deduct Repairs:** $\text{NAR} = R - A$.
5. **Step 5 — Determine YP:** Based on life and rate of interest.
6. **Step 6 — Capitalize:** $\text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}$.

*(A flowchart as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes can be redrawn for full 5 marks.)*

**Four Standard Percentage Charges Added to Construction Cost (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Water supply and sanitary installations** — 5% to 8%
2. **Electrification and internal wiring** — 5% to 8%
3. **Contingencies** — 3% to 5%
4. **Work-charged establishment** — 1.5% to 2.5%

> **Part (b) Answer:** Workflow steps 1–6 (5 marks) + 4 percentage charges with ranges (2 marks) = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Percentage Charge Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Mixing the two concepts:** Adding "5% water supply" *and* "8% of construction cost as rent" in the same problem when only one is asked — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert interest rate to decimal:** Using $i = 8$ instead of $i = 0.08$ gives YP = 0.125 (absurd) — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 3. **Skipping the NAR deduction:** Not subtracting the annual repair allowance before capitalizing — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 4. **Wrong salvage-value discount:** Students add the full salvage value to CV instead of *discounting* it to present worth — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 5. **Not showing intermediate steps:** Examiners award step-marks (1 mark per logical step). A single line of "final answer" with no working = *up to 3 marks lost*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Percentage Charge Method**

- **Definition:** Valuation by applying a stipulated % (6%–10%) of the *present-day construction cost* as the *gross annual rent*.
- **Two distinct meanings:** (a) *Additive percentages* on construction cost (water supply, electrification, contingency, establishment, T&P); (b) *Multiplicative percentage* for annual rent computation.
- **Standard % rates:** 6% (RCC well-maintained), 8% (masonry well-maintained), 9% (average masonry), 10% (old/dilapidated), 8–10% (commercial), 10–12% (industrial/godowns).
- **Core formula chain:** $C \to R = C \times P/100 \to \text{NAR} = R - A \to \text{YP} = 1/i \to \text{CV} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}$.
- **YP for perpetuity:** $1/i$; **YP for finite life:** $[(1+i)^{n} - 1]/[i(1+i)^{n}]$; **add** $S/C \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$ for salvage.
- **Standard interest assumption in KTU papers:** **8% per annum** → YP = 12.5 years (perpetuity).
- **Repairs allowance:** Always *deduct* from gross rent to get NAR before capitalizing.
- **Salvage value:** Must be *discounted* to present worth using $(1+i)^{n}$ — never add at face value.
- **Applications:** PWD rentals, municipal tax, insurance, court compensation, lease premium, stamp duty.
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing "percentage of cost added" (Concept 1) with "percentage of cost as rent" (Concept 2).
- **Examiner's tip:** Present answers in a *clear tabulated step-form* — step-marks are awarded generously for visible workflow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Day Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Day Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Day Work** is a method of valuation and payment in civil engineering contracts where work of a **non-measurable, incidental, or emergent nature** is executed and paid for on a **daily basis** based on the actual cost of labor, materials, and plant employed, plus an agreed percentage addition towards contractor's profit, supervision, and overheads.

According to the Indian Standard IS 1200 and KTU Board Examinations, day work is invoked when:
- The nature of work is **too small or scattered** to be itemized in the Bill of Quantities (BoQ).
- Work arises from **contingencies, hidden defects, or emergency repairs** that could not have been foreseen.
- Items that **cannot be measured in standard units** (e.g., cu.m, sq.m, kg) accurately.
- Works of a **specialized, exploratory, or experimental** character.

The authority to certify day work rests with the **Engineer-in-Charge / Project Manager** whose certificate forms the basis of payment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Plumbing Emergency"**
> Imagine a homeowner has a fixed contract price of ₹5,00,000 for building a house. Suddenly, during construction, the plumber discovers an underground rock formation while laying a 2-meter pipe trench. The contractor cannot quote a unit rate (per cubic meter) for "rock-breaking" because:
> 1. The exact volume of rock is unknown.
> 2. It wasn't in the original BoQ.
> 3. Stopping work is not feasible.
>
> Solution: The contractor logs the **hours worked** (labor), **diesel consumed** (plant), and **explosives used** (materials) for that specific day. The Engineer signs a **Day Work Order**. The contractor gets reimbursed for these actual costs plus a **15–25%** margin for profit.

In essence, **Day Work = Time-Based Reimbursement + Marked-Up Margin**, replacing measurement-based pricing with **time-and-material (T&M)** pricing.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Contractor's Profit & Overhead | **15% to 25%** | Usually 20% in Kerala PWD/CPWD contracts |
| Supervisor's allowance (often included) | 5% to 10% | Sometimes separate from main overhead |
| Daily working hours | **8 hours** | As per Factories Act 1948 / BOCW Act 1996 |
| Wage component split | Wages + DA (Dearness Allowance) + EPF/ESI | Statutory compliance mandatory |
| Documentation | Day Work Register / Daily Labour Report (DLR) | Signed by Site Engineer & Contractor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions That Justify Day Work (The "Why")

Under KTU Board evaluation, examiners expect students to know the *operational logic* of when day work is triggered. Day work is justified when **any one** of the following conditions is satisfied:

1. **Unforeseen / Contingent Work:** Work arising out of natural calamities, accidental damage, or subsurface conditions not visible at tender stage.
2. **Non-Itemized Minor Works:** Small alterations, repairs, or additions worth less than a defined threshold (often < 1% of contract value).
3. **Specialized / Sub-Surface Work:** Exploratory drilling, dewatering trial pits, anti-termite treatment touch-ups.
4. **Emergency & Safety Works:** Immediate shoring, barricading, demolition of unsafe structures.
5. **Supplementary Items:** Items not covered under any existing BoQ item.

## 2.2 Operational Components of Day Work

A day work claim has **three distinct cost heads**, each with a separate valuation methodology:

### A. Labour Component
$$C_L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times H_i)$$
Where:
- $C_L$ = Total cost of labour
- $W_i$ = Wages per hour (or per day) of the $i^{th}$ category of worker
- $H_i$ = Total hours (or days) worked by the $i^{th}$ category

> Categories typically include: Skilled (Mason, Plumber, Electrician), Semi-skilled, Unskilled (Mazdoor), and Supervisor/Foreman.

### B. Material Component
$$C_M = \sum_{j=1}^{m} (Q_j \times P_j)$$
Where:
- $C_M$ = Total cost of materials
- $Q_j$ = Quantity of the $j^{th}$ material consumed
- $P_j$ = Market/purchase rate of the $j^{th}$ material

> The contractor must produce **purchase vouchers, GST invoices, and material consumption records** to substantiate $C_M$.

### C. Plant & Machinery Component
$$C_P = \sum_{k=1}^{p} (R_k \times T_k)$$
Where:
- $C_P$ = Total cost of plant usage
- $R_k$ = Hourly (or daily) hire charge of the $k^{th}$ equipment
- $T_k$ = Total hours (or days) the $k^{th}$ equipment was deployed

## 2.3 The Master Day Work Formula

The **Total Day Work Cost (TDWC)** is computed as:

$$\boxed{TDWC = (C_L + C_M + C_P) \times \left(1 + \frac{P_o}{100}\right)}$$

Where:
- $P_o$ = Percentage addition for contractor's profit and overheads (typically **15% to 25%**).

### 2.3.1 Alternative Expanded Form

Some textbooks (B.N. Dutta, S.C. Rangwala) express the cost in a more granular way:

$$TDWC = C_L(1 + s) + C_M + C_P + \text{Transport} + \text{Sundries}$$

Where $s$ = supervision allowance percentage (often included within $P_o$ for simplicity).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Sl. No. | Formula / Term | Description | Typical Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $C_L = \Sigma(W_i \times H_i)$ | Labour cost aggregation | ₹ |
| 2 | $C_M = \Sigma(Q_j \times P_j)$ | Material cost aggregation | ₹ |
| 3 | $C_P = \Sigma(R_k \times T_k)$ | Plant & machinery cost | ₹ |
| 4 | $TDWC = (C_L + C_M + C_P) \times (1 + P_o/100)$ | **Master day work formula** | ₹ |
| 5 | $P_o$ | Profit + Overhead % (CPWD/PWD default) | 15% – 25% |
| 6 | Standard working day | Per Indian labour law | **8 hours** |
| 7 | Supporting documents | Muster roll, bin card, plant log, vouchers | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Dispute Avoidance:** Provides a transparent mechanism to settle claims without renegotiating the entire BoQ.
- **Project Continuity:** Prevents work stoppage during unforeseen events (e.g., cave-ins, floods, hidden utilities).
- **Audit Trail:** Creates a documented cost record, critical for future **insurance claims** and **arbitration proceedings**.
- **Fair Compensation:** Aligns with the principle of *privity of contract* — contractor recovers actual cost, owner pays only for verified work.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Day Work Formula

The formula is **not derived from first principles** (like Newton's laws); rather, it is a **composite cost model** built on the principle of *cost-plus-fixed-percentage* contracting. Below is the rigorous step-by-step logical construction.

### Step 1 — Identify the Three Independent Cost Streams

In any physical construction activity, regardless of type, three resources are consumed:

$$\text{Resource Pool} = \{\text{Labour}\} \cup \{\text{Materials}\} \cup \{\text{Plant}\}$$

### Step 2 — Express Each Resource as a Linear Function of Usage

Because labour, materials, and plant are each priced per unit of consumption:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot H_i \quad \text{(sum of wage rate} \times \text{hours)} \\
C_M &= \sum_{j=1}^{m} Q_j \cdot P_j \quad \text{(sum of quantity} \times \text{unit price)} \\
C_P &= \sum_{k=1}^{p} R_k \cdot T_k \quad \text{(sum of hire rate} \times \text{deployment time)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Aggregate the Direct Cost

The total direct cost (DC) is the arithmetic sum of the three streams:

$$DC = C_L + C_M + C_P$$

### Step 4 — Add Contractor's Mark-Up

The contractor is entitled to a return on cost, denoted as $P_o$ percent. Applying this on the direct cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TDWC &= DC + \left(\frac{P_o}{100} \times DC\right) \\
&= DC \times \left(1 + \frac{P_o}{100}\right) \\
&= (C_L + C_M + C_P) \times \left(1 + \frac{P_o}{100}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **final master formula** examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim in the derivation question.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> During the construction of a flyover, an unforeseen hard rock stratum was encountered during pier excavation. The contractor executed the rock-breaking work on a day-work basis. Following records were submitted for one day (8 working hours):
>
> | Resource | Details |
> |---|---|
> | Skilled labour (Mason/Operator) | 4 workers × 8 hrs @ ₹120/hr |
> | Unskilled labour (Mazdoor) | 6 workers × 8 hrs @ ₹80/hr |
> | Supervisor | 1 person × 8 hrs @ ₹150/hr |
> | Diesel for breaker machine | 25 litres @ ₹95/litre |
> | Explosives (gelatine sticks) | 12 kg @ ₹450/kg |
> | Compressor (1 no.) | 1 day @ ₹3,500/day |
> | Tipper truck (1 no.) | 1 day @ ₹2,800/day |
>
> Contractor's profit & overhead: **20%**.
> Calculate the total day work amount payable.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Labour Cost ($C_L$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= (4 \times 8 \times 120) + (6 \times 8 \times 80) + (1 \times 8 \times 150) \\
&= 3{,}840 + 3{,}840 + 1{,}200 \\
&= 8{,}880 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Material Cost ($C_M$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= (25 \times 95) + (12 \times 450) \\
&= 2{,}375 + 5{,}400 \\
&= 7{,}775 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Plant Cost ($C_P$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 3{,}500 + 2{,}800 = 6{,}300 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Direct Cost (DC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DC &= C_L + C_M + C_P \\
&= 8{,}880 + 7{,}775 + 6{,}300 \\
&= 22{,}955 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the 20% Mark-Up**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TDWC &= DC \times \left(1 + \frac{20}{100}\right) \\
&= 22{,}955 \times 1.20 \\
&= 27{,}546 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The contractor is entitled to **₹27,546** for that day of work, subject to certification by the Engineer-in-Charge.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Day Work vs. Item Rate

| Parameter | Item Rate Contract | Day Work |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement basis | Quantity × Unit Rate | Time × Actual Cost + % |
| Pre-conditions | BoQ item must exist | No BoQ item required |
| Documentation | Measurement Book (MB) | Day Work Register + Vouchers |
| Verification | Cross-checking dimensions | Cross-checking hours & invoices |
| Risk on contractor | Higher (rate fluctuation) | Lower (passes through to client) |
| Best suited for | Predictable, repeatable work | Unforeseen, scattered, emergency work |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Day Work Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Event: Unforeseen / Emergency Work Identified] --> B{Engineer-in-Charge Inspection}
    B -- Reject --> C[Work Stopped / Re-measured Normally]
    B -- Approve --> D[Issue Day Work Order in Writing]
    D --> E[Contractor Executes Work with Resources]
    E --> F[Resource Pool Mobilization]
    F --> F1[Labour: Skilled / Semi-skilled / Unskilled]
    F --> F2[Materials: Consumables / Explosives / Sundries]
    F --> F3[Plant: Tippers / Compressors / Generators]
    F1 --> G[Daily Log Entry: Hours Worked]
    F2 --> H[Material Consumption Record with Vouchers]
    F3 --> I[Plant Deployment Logbook]
    G --> J[Engineer Verification and Certification]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Direct Cost: CL + CM + CP]
    K --> L[Apply Profit and Overhead Percentage Po]
    L --> M[Final Day Work Bill Generation]
    M --> N[Payment Approval and Disbursement]
```

## 4.2 Cost Aggregation Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Resources[Resource Inputs]
        RL[Labour Wage Data]
        RM[Material Purchase Vouchers]
        RP[Plant Hire Records]
    end
    subgraph CostBlocks[Cost Computation Blocks]
        CLblock[CL = sum Wi x Hi]
        CMblock[CM = sum Qj x Pj]
        CPblock[CP = sum Rk x Tk]
    end
    subgraph Aggregation[Aggregation Layer]
        DC[Direct Cost DC = CL + CM + CP]
        PO[Profit and Overhead Po percent]
    end
    subgraph Output[Final Output]
        TDWC[Total Day Work Cost TDWC]
    end
    RL --> CLblock
    RM --> CMblock
    RP --> CPblock
    CLblock --> DC
    CMblock --> DC
    CPblock --> DC
    DC --> TDWC
    PO --> TDWC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The block diagram above is the **canonical cost-flow architecture** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce when asked to "explain the methodology of day work with a flow diagram." It cleanly separates the **three resource streams** from the **aggregation logic** and the **final mark-up application**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Day Work" as applied in civil engineering contracts. State any two situations where day work is adopted.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Day Work** is a contractual method of valuing and paying for work that cannot be measured and valued at the rates in the Bill of Quantities. Payment is made on the basis of the **actual cost of labour, materials, and plant** used, plus an agreed percentage for contractor's profit and overheads.
>
> **Two situations justifying day work:**
> 1. **Unforeseen or contingent works** such as rock encountered unexpectedly during excavation, or damage due to floods.
> 2. **Minor scattered works** of an emergent nature (e.g., anti-termite touch-ups, exploratory trial pits) which are too small to merit an item rate.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the documents that must be maintained to support a day work claim.**

**Model Answer:**

> The supporting documents for a day work claim include:
> 1. **Day Work Order** issued in writing by the Engineer-in-Charge before commencement.
> 2. **Daily Labour Muster Roll** recording categories, hours, and wages of each worker.
> 3. **Material Consumption Register / Bin Card** with supporting purchase vouchers and GST invoices.
> 4. **Plant Logbook** showing equipment deployed, hours of operation, and hire charges.
> 5. **Engineer's Certificate** verifying the entries, signed jointly with the contractor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the procedure for executing and valuing day work. State the formula used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The following data relates to one day of emergency repair work executed on a damaged retaining wall under day work:

| Resource | Details |
|---|---|
| Masons | 3 nos. × 8 hrs @ ₹110/hr |
| Helpers | 5 nos. × 8 hrs @ ₹75/hr |
| Cement used | 8 bags @ ₹420/bag |
| Sand | 0.6 cu.m @ ₹1,800/cu.m |
| Aggregate | 0.8 cu.m @ ₹1,500/cu.m |
| Mixer machine | 1 day @ ₹1,200/day |
| Water tanker | 1 day @ ₹900/day |

Contractor's profit & overhead = **20%**. Calculate the total day work amount payable.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure and Formula:**

1. **Identification:** The Site Engineer identifies work that cannot be measured under existing BoQ items.
2. **Written Order:** A formal Day Work Order is issued by the Engineer-in-Charge authorizing the contractor to proceed.
3. **Recording:** The contractor maintains a Day Work Register documenting labour hours, materials consumed, and plant deployed, with vouchers.
4. **Verification:** The Engineer (or his authorized representative) jointly verifies the entries daily.
5. **Valuation:** Direct cost is computed as $C_L + C_M + C_P$ and marked up by $P_o$%.

**Formula:**

$$TDWC = (C_L + C_M + C_P) \times \left(1 + \frac{P_o}{100}\right)$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Labour Cost:

$$C_L = (3 \times 8 \times 110) + (5 \times 8 \times 75) = 2{,}640 + 3{,}000 = 5{,}640 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 2 — Material Cost:

$$C_M = (8 \times 420) + (0.6 \times 1{,}800) + (0.8 \times 1{,}500)$$
$$= 3{,}360 + 1{,}080 + 1{,}200 = 5{,}640 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 3 — Plant Cost:

$$C_P = 1{,}200 + 900 = 2{,}100 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 4 — Direct Cost:

$$DC = 5{,}640 + 5{,}640 + 2{,}100 = 13{,}380 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 5 — Total Day Work Cost:

$$TDWC = 13{,}380 \times 1.20 = \mathbf{16{,}056 \text{ ₹}}$$

> **Final Answer:** ₹16,056 is payable as the day work bill.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare **Day Work** and **Item Rate** methods of valuation. Under what contractual conditions is each preferred?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A contractor carried out emergency dewatering of a flooded basement for 3 consecutive days. The resources deployed per day were:

- 2 pump operators × 8 hrs @ ₹100/hr
- 4 helpers × 8 hrs @ ₹70/hr
- Diesel: 40 litres/day @ ₹92/litre
- 2 dewatering pumps @ ₹800/day each
- 1 generator @ ₹1,500/day

Contractor's profit & overhead = **25%**. Calculate the total day work bill for 3 days.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Day Work | Item Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Pricing basis** | Actual cost + % | Pre-agreed unit rates × measured quantity |
| **Documentation** | Day Work Register + Vouchers | Measurement Book (MB) |
| **When preferred** | Unforeseen, scattered, or non-measurable work | Predictable, repetitive, large-volume work |
| **Risk on contractor** | Low (cost is reimbursed) | High (carries rate-fluctuation risk) |
| **Owner risk** | High (less price competition) | Low (price discovered via tender) |
| **Audit complexity** | High (voucher verification needed) | Moderate (cross-check measurements) |

Day work is preferred for **emergencies and contingent works**; item rate is preferred for **planned, measurable works** in the BoQ.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Per Day Computation:**

Labour:

$$C_L = (2 \times 8 \times 100) + (4 \times 8 \times 70) = 1{,}600 + 2{,}240 = 3{,}840 \text{ ₹/day}$$

Materials (Diesel):

$$C_M = 40 \times 92 = 3{,}680 \text{ ₹/day}$$

Plant:

$$C_P = (2 \times 800) + 1{,}500 = 1{,}600 + 1{,}500 = 3{,}100 \text{ ₹/day}$$

Direct Cost (per day):

$$DC_{\text{day}} = 3{,}840 + 3{,}680 + 3{,}100 = 10{,}620 \text{ ₹/day}$$

Total Day Work Cost per day (with 25% mark-up):

$$TDWC_{\text{day}} = 10{,}620 \times 1.25 = 13{,}275 \text{ ₹/day}$$

**For 3 Days:**

$$TDWC_{\text{3 days}} = 13{,}275 \times 3 = \mathbf{39{,}825 \text{ ₹}}$$

> **Final Answer:** ₹39,825 is the total day work bill for 3 days.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by hours/days:** Students often compute $C_L$ as just $W_i$ (rate) without multiplying by $H_i$ (hours/days). This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the profit percentage:** The $P_o$ mark-up is mandatory. Writing $DC = TDWC$ is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing units:** Wages may be quoted per hour while plant is quoted per day. Standardize to a common basis (per day) before summation.
> 4. **No written Day Work Order:** In theory questions, students must mention that day work is invalid **without a written order** from the Engineer. This single line can fetch 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing day work with petty maintenance:** Day work is for **contracted works**, not internal repairs. Mention the contractual context.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Day Work (Module 1)**
>
> - **Definition:** Time-based, cost-plus-percentage method for non-measurable or unforeseen works.
> - **Three cost heads:** Labour ($C_L$), Materials ($C_M$), Plant ($C_P$).
> - **Master Formula:** $TDWC = (C_L + C_M + C_P) \times (1 + P_o/100)$.
> - **Standard $P_o$:** 15% to 25% (CPWD/PWD default = 20%).
> - **Prerequisite:** Must be backed by a **written Day Work Order** from the Engineer-in-Charge.
> - **Documentation:** Muster roll + Material vouchers + Plant logbook + Engineer's certificate.
> - **Working hours:** **8 hours per day** (Factories Act / BOCW Act).
> - **Applies to:** Emergency repairs, hidden subsurface conditions, minor scattered works, specialized exploratory tasks.
> - **Auditability:** All vouchers and logs must be **jointly signed** by Engineer and Contractor.
> - **Distinguish from:** Item rate (measurement-based), Lump sum (single-price), Cost-plus (entire project, not just incidentals).
> - **Examiner's favourite:** Always present the **master formula** in the very first line of any numerical answer.
> - **Common keywords to use:** "non-measurable," "cost-plus-percentage," "contingent," "Engineer's certificate," "joint verification."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prime Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prime Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prime Cost (PC)** is the **direct cost** incurred in the actual execution of a specific item of work, comprising the actual expenditure on materials, labour, and other direct expenses (such as tools, plant, and scaffolding) required at the construction site. It is the **sum of all first-hand expenses** that are directly chargeable to a particular unit of work, *excluding* contractor's profit, head office overheads, and contingency allowances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Prime Cost is the actual cost of materials, labour, and other direct expenses necessary to execute a unit quantity of work at the site level. It forms the fundamental base upon which all-in rate, estimated cost, and tendered rates are progressively built."*

Mathematically, the relationship in the cost hierarchy is:

$$\text{Prime Cost (PC)} < \text{Total Cost (TC)} < \text{All-in Rate (AIR)} < \text{Tendered Rate}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing **masala chai** (spiced tea) at home. The prime cost of one cup is what you actually spend on the *direct* ingredients that go into that single cup — milk, tea leaves, sugar, water, and gas used *only* to boil that one cup. The cup itself, the spoon, and the gas cylinder's depreciation are not counted because they serve many cups. Similarly, **Prime Cost in construction** is the *first-hand* money spent on materials consumed, wages paid to the mason who lays bricks, and fuel used *only* for that one cubic meter of brickwork — nothing more, nothing less.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Prime Cost = "What goes INTO the work" — not what surrounds, manages, or profits from it.

## 1.3 Standard Components of Prime Cost

The three primary pillars of prime cost are:

1. **Cost of Materials** (at site, including carriage)
2. **Cost of Labour** (skilled + unskilled directly engaged)
3. **Direct Expenses / Sundries** (scaffolding, tools, water, electricity, plant hire *for that specific item*)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Estimators' Handbook:**
> - Brickwork: $\text{Rs./m}^3$ (cubic meter)
> - Concrete: $\text{Rs./m}^3$
> - Plastering/Painting: $\text{Rs./m}^2$ (square meter)
> - Steel reinforcement: $\text{Rs./kg}$ or $\text{Rs./quintal}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Breakdown of Prime Cost Components

### A. Cost of Materials (Direct Materials)

- Quantity of material is computed from **standard data books** (e.g., PWD Schedule of Rates, IS 7272).
- Rate is taken as the **delivered cost at site**, i.e., market rate + transportation + storage handling.
- Includes wastage allowance (typically **2% to 5%** depending on material).
- **Wasage Formula:**

$$\text{Material Cost} = \text{Net Quantity} \times (1 + \text{Wastage \%}) \times \text{Rate per unit}$$

### B. Cost of Labour (Direct Labour)

- Labour is classified as **skilled** (mason, carpenter, plumber) and **unskilled** (helper/mazdoor).
- Daily wages are taken from the **Standard Schedule of Rates (SSR)** published annually by PWD Kerala.
- Total labour cost for a unit of work:

$$\text{Labour Cost} = \text{No. of skilled labour} \times \text{Skilled wage} + \text{No. of unskilled labour} \times \text{Unskilled wage}$$

- For per-unit work, labour is converted: $\text{No. of labour} = \text{Quantity of work per day per labour}$ (productivity constant).

### C. Direct Expenses / Sundries

- **Scaffolding** (proportional to item), **Tools & Plants (T&P)**, **Water charges**, **Electricity charges** consumed at site directly for that item.
- Usually expressed as a **percentage of (Materials + Labour)** in rate analysis.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|------:|:--------|:------------|:-------------|
| 1 | $PC = M + L + S$ | Prime Cost = Materials + Labour + Sundries | $\text{Rs.}$ |
| 2 | $M = Q \times (1 + w) \times r$ | Material cost with wastage | $\text{Rs./unit work}$ |
| 3 | $L = n_s \cdot w_s + n_u \cdot w_u$ | Labour cost (skilled + unskilled) | $\text{Rs./unit work}$ |
| 4 | $S = k\% \times (M + L)$ | Sundries as % of M+L | $\text{Rs./unit work}$ |
| 5 | $TC = PC + OH$ | Total Cost = PC + Overheads | $\text{Rs./unit work}$ |
| 6 | $AIR = TC + CP$ | All-in-Rate = TC + Contractor's Profit | $\text{Rs./unit work}$ |
| 7 | $TR = AIR + Cont.$ | Tendered Rate includes contingency | $\text{Rs./unit work}$ |

> Symbols: $Q$ = quantity, $w$ = wastage fraction, $r$ = material rate, $n_s, n_u$ = number of skilled/unskilled labour, $w_s, w_u$ = wages, $k$ = sundries %, $OH$ = Overheads, $CP$ = Contractor's Profit.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rate Analysis:** Forms the **foundation block** of every rate analysis sheet submitted with estimates.
- **Tendering:** Enables contractors to quote competitive yet profitable rates.
- **Valuation:** For depreciation-based valuation (e.g., for old buildings), the **replacement cost** is built from prime cost.
- **Dispute Resolution:** In arbitration, prime cost is the reference point for "what was *actually* spent."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: Prime Cost of 1 m³ of Brickwork in CM 1:6

**Given Data (Standard PWD Kerala rates):**
- Bricks required: **500 nos. per m³** (standard consumption)
- Brick rate at site: **Rs. 10 per brick**
- Mortar required: **0.30 m³ per m³ of brickwork**
- Mortar ratio: 1:6 (Cement : Sand) → Sum of ratio = 7
- Cement: 1440 kg/m³ density, bag = 50 kg → **Rs. 400 per bag**
- Sand (M-sand): **Rs. 1500 per m³**
- Mason productivity: **0.8 m³/day** → so for 1 m³ → 1/0.8 = 1.25 mason-days
- Mazdoor productivity: **0.5 m³/day** → for 1 m³ → 1/0.5 = 2.0 mazdoor-days
- Mason wage: **Rs. 900/day**; Mazdoor wage: **Rs. 600/day**
- Sundries: **2% of (M + L)**
- Wastage: Bricks 5%, Cement 2%, Sand 7%

### Step 1: Cost of Bricks

$$\text{Net bricks} = 500 \text{ nos.}$$
$$\text{Bricks with wastage} = 500 \times (1 + 0.05) = 525 \text{ nos.}$$
$$\text{Cost of bricks} = 525 \times 10 = \text{Rs. } 5250$$

### Step 2: Cost of Cement in Mortar

$$\text{Volume of wet mortar} = 0.30 \text{ m}^3$$
$$\text{Volume of dry mortar (with 30% bulking)} = 0.30 \times 1.30 = 0.39 \text{ m}^3$$
$$\text{Cement volume} = \frac{1}{7} \times 0.39 = 0.0557 \text{ m}^3$$
$$\text{Cement mass} = 0.0557 \times 1440 = 80.21 \text{ kg}$$
$$\text{No. of bags} = \frac{80.21}{50} = 1.604 \text{ bags}$$
$$\text{Cost with 2\% wastage} = 1.604 \times 1.02 \times 400 = \text{Rs. } 654.43$$

### Step 3: Cost of Sand in Mortar

$$\text{Sand volume (dry)} = \frac{6}{7} \times 0.39 = 0.3343 \text{ m}^3$$
$$\text{Cost with 7\% wastage} = 0.3343 \times 1.07 \times 1500 = \text{Rs. } 536.56$$

### Step 4: Total Material Cost (M)

$$M = 5250 + 654.43 + 536.56 = \text{Rs. } 6440.99$$

### Step 5: Cost of Labour (L)

$$\text{Mason cost} = 1.25 \times 900 = \text{Rs. } 1125$$
$$\text{Mazdoor cost} = 2.0 \times 600 = \text{Rs. } 1200$$
$$L = 1125 + 1200 = \text{Rs. } 2325$$

### Step 6: Sundries (S)

$$S = 0.02 \times (6440.99 + 2325) = 0.02 \times 8765.99 = \text{Rs. } 175.32$$

### Step 7: Prime Cost

$$PC = M + L + S$$
$$PC = 6440.99 + 2325 + 175.32$$
$$\boxed{PC = \text{Rs. } 8941.31 \text{ per m}^3 \text{ of brickwork}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: From PC to Tendered Rate

| Cost Layer | Formula | Value (Rs./m³) | Component Included |
|------------|:-------:|---------------:|:-------------------|
| **Prime Cost (PC)** | $M + L + S$ | **8941.31** | Materials + Labour + Sundries |
| Add: Overhead (15%) | $PC \times 0.15$ | 1341.20 | Site supervision, office, insurance |
| **Total Cost (TC)** | $PC + OH$ | **10282.51** | All direct + indirect |
| Add: Contractor Profit (10%) | $TC \times 0.10$ | 1028.25 | Profit margin |
| **All-in Rate (AIR)** | $TC + CP$ | **11310.76** | Ready-to-quote rate |
| Add: Contingency (3%) | $AIR \times 0.03$ | 339.32 | Risk allowance |
| **Tendered Rate** | $AIR + Cont.$ | **11650.08** | Final quoted rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway:** Prime Cost represents approximately **77%** of the tendered rate in this example. The remaining 23% comprises overheads, profit, and contingency — illustrating why precise PC estimation is critical for project economics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Cost Build-Up

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unit Quantity of Work] --> B[Cost of Materials]
    A --> C[Cost of Labour]
    A --> D[Direct Sundries]

    B --> B1[Quantity from IS 7272 / SSR]
    B1 --> B2[Wastage Adjustment 2 to 5 percent]
    B2 --> B3[Delivered Rate at Site]
    B3 --> BM[Material Cost M]

    C --> C1[Skilled Labour Wages]
    C1 --> C2[Unskilled Labour Wages]
    C2 --> CM[Labour Cost L]

    D --> D1[Scaffolding Share]
    D --> D2[Tools and Plant Hire]
    D --> D3[Water and Electricity Direct]
    D1 --> DM
    D2 --> DM
    D3 --> DM[Direct Sundries S]

    BM --> PC[Prime Cost PC = M plus L plus S]
    CM --> PC
    DM --> PC

    PC --> E[Add Overheads 10 to 15 percent]
    E --> F[Total Cost TC]
    F --> G[Add Contractor Profit 8 to 12 percent]
    G --> H[All in Rate AIR]
    H --> I[Add Contingency 2 to 5 percent]
    I --> J[Tendered Rate]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style PC fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#f57c00,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Cost Stack Diagram (Visual Proportion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Prime Cost 77 percent] --> L2[Overhead 12 percent]
    L2 --> L3[Profit 9 percent]
    L3 --> L4[Contingency 2 percent]
    L4 --> L5[Tendered Rate 100 percent]

    style L1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style L2 fill:#0277bd,color:#ffffff
    style L3 fill:#f57c00,color:#ffffff
    style L4 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style L5 fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The first two layers (Prime Cost + Overhead) constitute **~89%** of any tendered rate. A 5% error in prime cost estimation cascades into a **~3.85% error** in the final tender — directly impacting project profitability and competitiveness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define Prime Cost. List its three main components.**

**Model Answer:**
Prime Cost is the actual direct cost incurred at the construction site for executing one unit quantity of a specific work item, comprising the actual expenditure on materials, labour, and other direct expenses (sundries). It is the sum of first-hand costs chargeable to the item, excluding contractor's profit, head office overheads, and contingency.

The three main components are:
1. **Cost of Materials** (with carriage and wastage)
2. **Cost of Labour** (skilled and unskilled)
3. **Direct Sundries** (scaffolding, tools, water, electricity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bloom's Level:** Remember (L1) | **CO Mapped:** CO1 – Understand cost components

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Prime Cost and Total Cost.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Prime Cost | Total Cost |
|-----------|:----------:|:----------:|
| Definition | Direct cost of materials + labour + sundries | Prime Cost + Overhead charges |
| Includes Overheads? | **No** | **Yes** (10–15%) |
| Site-level or Office-level? | Purely site-level | Includes site + office overheads |
| Used in | Rate analysis, day-work valuation | Estimate preparation, tendering |
| Formula | $PC = M + L + S$ | $TC = PC + OH$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bloom's Level:** Understand (L2) | **CO Mapped:** CO1, CO2

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**a) Explain in detail the components of Prime Cost with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Prime Cost comprises three components as elaborated below:

**(i) Cost of Materials:** This is the actual delivered cost of all materials consumed in the work item at the site. It is calculated as:
$M = \text{Quantity} \times (1 + \text{Wastage \%}) \times \text{Rate at site}$

*Example:* For 1 m³ of brickwork, 500 bricks are needed; with 5% wastage = 525 bricks. At Rs. 10/brick, material cost = Rs. 5250.

**(ii) Cost of Labour:** This is the wages paid to skilled and unskilled workers directly engaged on that item. Standard productivities are used to convert "labour-days" per unit work.
$L = (\text{No. of skilled} \times \text{Skilled wage}) + (\text{No. of unskilled} \times \text{Unskilled wage})$

*Example:* 1.25 mason-days × Rs. 900 + 2.0 mazdoor-days × Rs. 600 = Rs. 2325.

**(iii) Direct Sundries:** Small site expenses proportional to the work — local scaffolding, hand tools, water, electricity, and minor plant. Usually taken as 1.5%–2% of (M + L).
$S = 0.02 \times (M + L) = 0.02 \times 8765.99 = \text{Rs. } 175.32$

**Total Prime Cost** $= M + L + S = \text{Rs. } 8941.31$ per m³ of brickwork.

**b) Calculate the prime cost of 10 m³ of R.C.C. work in M20 grade (1:1.5:3). Given: Cement Rs. 400/bag, sand Rs. 1500/m³, 20mm aggregate Rs. 1800/m³, crusher-run labour Rs. 1000/m³, vibrator hire Rs. 200/m³, mason Rs. 950/day, mazdoor Rs. 600/day. Wastage: cement 2%, sand 8%, aggregate 5%. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Dry material quantities for 1 m³ wet concrete (sum ratio = 7.5):**

- Wet concrete volume = 1 m³
- Dry volume = $1 \times 1.54 = 1.54 \text{ m}^3$ (using 54% bulking factor)
- Cement volume = $\frac{1}{7.5} \times 1.54 = 0.2053 \text{ m}^3$
- Cement mass = $0.2053 \times 1440 = 295.7 \text{ kg}$ → No. of bags = $\frac{295.7}{50} = 5.91$ bags
- Sand volume = $\frac{1.5}{7.5} \times 1.54 = 0.308 \text{ m}^3$
- Aggregate volume = $\frac{3}{7.5} \times 1.54 = 0.616 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 2: Material cost per m³:**

- Cement: $5.91 \times 1.02 \times 400 = \text{Rs. } 2411.28$
- Sand: $0.308 \times 1.08 \times 1500 = \text{Rs. } 498.96$
- Aggregate: $0.616 \times 1.05 \times 1800 = \text{Rs. } 1164.24$
- **Total M** = $2411.28 + 498.96 + 1164.24 = \text{Rs. } 4074.48$

**Step 3: Labour cost per m³ (productivity: mason = 0.8 m³/day, mazdoor = 0.5 m³/day):**

- Mason = $\frac{1}{0.8} \times 950 = \text{Rs. } 1187.50$
- Mazdoor = $\frac{1}{0.5} \times 600 = \text{Rs. } 1200$
- Vibrator operator included in mazdoor
- **Total L** = $\text{Rs. } 2387.50$

**Step 4: Sundries per m³:**

- Crusher-run labour = Rs. 1000
- Vibrator hire = Rs. 200
- **Total S** = $\text{Rs. } 1200$

**Step 5: Prime cost per m³:**

$$PC = M + L + S = 4074.48 + 2387.50 + 1200 = \text{Rs. } 7661.98 \text{ per m}^3$$

**Step 6: For 10 m³:**

$$PC_{10} = 7661.98 \times 10 = \boxed{\text{Rs. } 76{,}619.80}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **forget the bulking factor (1.54)** for dry volume calculation, leading to under-estimation of cement by ~54%. This is the single most common error in concrete PC problems and costs **2–3 marks per question**. Also, ensure you use the **wet-to-dry volume ratio of 1.54** for concrete, NOT for mortar (which uses 1.30).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**a) Discuss the role and significance of Prime Cost in construction rate analysis and project valuation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Prime Cost plays a **pivotal foundational role** in construction economics for the following reasons:

1. **Base of Rate Analysis:** Every entry in the rate analysis sheet (Standard Schedule of Rates, Kerala PWD) begins with Prime Cost. Without a correct PC, no reliable rate can be derived.

2. **Tendering Decisions:** Contractors use PC to determine the **break-even point** and then add overheads, profit, and contingency to arrive at competitive bids.

3. **Valuation of Existing Structures:** Under methods like the **Replacement Cost Method** and the **Depreciation Method**, the cost of reproducing a building is built up by summing the prime cost of all its components (foundation, walls, roof, finishes, services).

4. **Cost Control & Monitoring:** During execution, actual expenditure is benchmarked against estimated PC. Deviations trigger **corrective action** (rework, value engineering).

5. **Dispute & Arbitration:** In legal disputes, PC serves as an **objective reference** of direct expenditure, free from subjective profit claims.

6. **Insurance & Claim Settlement:** Insurance assessors use PC as the basis for loss-of-damage claims on partially destroyed structures.

**b) A contractor incurs the following expenses for executing 50 m² of 12 mm cement plaster (1:4). Calculate the Prime Cost.**

- Cement: 6 bags × Rs. 400/bag
- Sand: 0.18 m³ × Rs. 1500/m³
- Mason: 0.5 day × Rs. 900/day
- Mazdoor: 1.0 day × Rs. 600/day
- Scaffolding share: Rs. 150
- Wastage: cement 2%, sand 10%

**Model Solution:**

**Material Cost (M):**
- Cement: $6 \times 1.02 \times 400 = \text{Rs. } 2448$
- Sand: $0.18 \times 1.10 \times 1500 = \text{Rs. } 297$
- **M = 2448 + 297 = Rs. 2745**

**Labour Cost (L):**
- Mason: $0.5 \times 900 = \text{Rs. } 450$
- Mazdoor: $1.0 \times 600 = \text{Rs. } 600$
- **L = 1050**

**Sundries (S):**
- Scaffolding = Rs. 150
- Water, tools (assumed 1% of M+L) = $0.01 \times 3795 = \text{Rs. } 37.95$
- **S = 150 + 37.95 = Rs. 187.95**

**Prime Cost:**
$$PC = 2745 + 1050 + 187.95 = \boxed{\text{Rs. } 3982.95}$$

Per m² PC $= 3982.95 / 50 = \text{Rs. } 79.66 \text{ per m}^2$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Do NOT confuse plaster's "wet mortar" approach (use 1.30 bulking) with concrete's "dry mix" approach (use 1.54). Also, always specify whether the PC is for the **whole work** (Rs. 3982.95) or **per unit** (Rs. 79.66/m²) — both are required for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Prime Cost:**

- **Definition:** PC = Materials + Labour + Direct Sundries (all site-level, first-hand).
- **Excludes:** Contractor's profit, head office overheads, contingency, GST (usually).
- **Materials:** Always include **wastage %**; rate is **delivered at site**.
- **Labour:** Use **productivity constants** from IS 7272 or SSR Kerala; convert to "man-days per unit."
- **Concrete:** Wet→Dry conversion factor = **1.54**; Mortar = **1.30**.
- **Cement bags:** Always 50 kg; density of cement = **1440 kg/m³**.
- **Sundries:** Typically **1.5%–2% of (M+L)**; always mention components.
- **Hierarchy:** PC → TC (add OH) → AIR (add profit) → Tendered Rate (add contingency).
- **PC is ~75–80%** of the final tendered rate — get it right, the rest follows.
- **Always show units** (Rs./m³, Rs./m², Rs./kg) in the final answer.
- **Common KTU errors to avoid:** Forgetting bulking, ignoring wastage, mixing up skilled/unskilled wage rates, and not stating "per unit" in the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Provisional Sum and Provisional Quantity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Provisional Sum and Provisional Quantity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Provisional Sum — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROVISIONAL SUM (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus — PCCET601 / Module 1)**
> A **Provisional Sum** is a specific sum of money provided in a Bill of Quantities (BOQ) or contract document to cover the cost of work that is **anticipated but cannot be accurately designed, measured, or detailed** at the time of preparing the tender/estimate. It is essentially a "placeholder budget" inserted by the estimator/consultant for works whose nature is foreseen, but whose final scope is uncertain.

**Standard Reference Marking in BOQ:** Usually written as **"P.S."** or marked in the "Remarks" column as **"Provisional"**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- A **lump-sum monetary value**, NOT a measured quantity.
- Reserved for **undefined or partially defined work items**.
- Adjusted/actualized later through **valuation of variations** or **actual measurements**.
- The contractor typically recovers this sum **on actual execution**, supported by vouchers, quotations, or day-work records.

---

### 1.2 Provisional Quantity — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROVISIONAL QUANTITY (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus — PCCET601 / Module 1)**
> A **Provisional Quantity** is an **estimated quantity of work** (e.g., 50 m³ of rock excavation, 200 m of additional cable laying) shown in the BOQ for items of work that are **expected to arise but cannot be accurately measured** during the estimation stage. Unlike provisional sums, the unit of measurement and rate analysis structure is already known — only the quantity is an estimate.

**Key Characteristics:**
- A **quantity value** (with units like m, m², m³, kg, nos.), NOT a monetary sum.
- Rate is tendered normally; only the quantity is "provisional."
- Final payment is made on **actual measurement** at site.
- Often marked as **"Prov. Qty"** or **"P.Q."** in the quantity column.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Renovation Contract
