Imagine you are renovating your ancestral home. You sign a contract with a civil engineer, and two situations arise:

> **Analogy 1 — Provisional Sum:**
> You tell the contractor: *"I know the termite-affected wooden roof needs replacement, but I haven't yet consulted a specialist to confirm the exact extent of damage. Just keep a **budget of ₹50,000** aside for this. We'll adjust it later based on the actual work."*
> → Here, the **money is fixed, the scope is unknown** = **Provisional Sum**.

> **Analogy 2 — Provisional Quantity:**
> You tell the contractor: *"I expect the boundary wall plastering to need about **120 m² of re-plastering**, but exact area will be known only after dismantling the old plaster. Bill me per square metre at the agreed rate."*
> → Here, the **rate is fixed, quantity is approximate** = **Provisional Quantity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Memory Hook:**
> - **Provisional Sum** → "Money kept aside" (₹ value)
> - **Provisional Quantity** → "Quantity kept aside" (m, m², m³ value)

---

### 1.4 Why Are They Required? (Syllabus Highlight)

| Situation in Project | Engineer's Problem | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-soil investigation not done | Strata below 2 m unknown | **P.S.** for unexpected rock excavation |
| Client undecided on finishes | Final tile brand/variety not finalized | **P.S.** for premium finish upgrades |
| Underground utilities suspected | Old water/sewer lines may be hit | **P.Q.** for additional pipe work |
| Site has probable loose soil | Anticipate deeper foundation in pockets | **P.Q.** for extra PCC in foundations |

Standard provision as per CPWD/IS practice: typically **3% to 5%** of the estimated cost is reserved as **provisional sums** to cover unforeseen items.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — When and How They Are Used

**Step 1 — Identification of Uncertainty**
The estimator/consultant reviews the available drawings, soil reports, and client briefs. Items that **cannot be measured from drawings** are flagged.

**Step 2 — Classification of Uncertainty**
The flagged item is then classified as either:
- A **known rate, uncertain quantity** situation → insert as **Provisional Quantity**.
- A **completely unknown scope and rate** situation → insert as **Provisional Sum**.

**Step 3 — Inclusion in BOQ**
- For **Provisional Quantity**: enter the item description, unit, **estimated (provisional) quantity**, and leave the **rate column** for contractor's tendered rate.
- For **Provisional Sum**: enter the item description as "Provisional Sum for …" and put the **lump-sum value** in the amount column.

**Step 4 — Adjustment at Site**
- After execution: actual quantity is measured and paid at tendered rate.
- For sums: actual expenditure (with proof) is reimbursed.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Comparison Table

| Parameter | Provisional Sum | Provisional Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of Entry** | Monetary lump-sum | Estimated quantity (with unit) |
| **What is Uncertain?** | Scope and/or rate of work | Quantity of work |
| **What is Known?** | Approximate budget | Item, unit, and rate structure |
| **Marking in BOQ** | "P.S." in remarks | "Prov. Qty" in quantity column |
| **Final Settlement** | Actual cost / quotation / voucher | Actual measurement at site |
| **Example** | ₹75,000 for unforeseen rock blasting | 25 m³ of rock excavation (approximate) |
| **Effect on Tender** | Contractor generally does NOT price this | Contractor DOES price the unit rate |
| **Treatment in Final Bill** | Deducted/added via Variation Order | Measured and paid on actuals |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly applicable** to KTU ESE numericals from Module 1.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PS_i$ | Total provisional sum = sum of individual P.S. entries | ₹ (INR) |
| 2 | $PQ_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PQ_i$ | Total provisional quantity = sum of all provisional quantities | m, m², m³, kg, nos. |
| 3 | $Amount_{PQ} = PQ_{actual} \times R_{tendered}$ | Payment for provisional quantity item | ₹ |
| 4 | $Variation = PQ_{actual} - PQ_{original}$ | Change in quantity (positive = excess work) | Same unit as item |
| 5 | $P.S.\ Reserve\ \% = \dfrac{\sum PS_i}{Estimated\ Cost} \times 100$ | Percentage reserve for contingencies | % |
| 6 | $Final\ Cost = \sum (Actual\ Qty \times Rate) + \sum (Actual\ PS)$ | Total project cost after actuals are known | ₹ |
| 7 | $Contingency\ Reserve = (3\%\ to\ 5\%) \times Estimated\ Cost$ | General thumb-rule (CPWD) for P.S. reserve | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE on Table Symbols:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ in mathematical expressions is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Civil Engineering Contracts** — Used in all major infrastructure (highways, bridges, buildings) to handle subsurface uncertainty.
2. **Mechanical Contracts** — P.S. for "supply of imported spares" where brand/availability fluctuates.
3. **Electrical Contracts** — P.Q. for "additional cable length" in complex industrial wiring.
4. **Government Tendering (CPWD/PWD/KPWD)** — Mandated under Standard Contract Forms for fair tendering.
5. **Dispute Resolution** — Acts as a pre-agreed mechanism to handle scope variations without contract re-negotiation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Provisional Quantity Adjustment

**Problem Statement:**
A contractor tenders for earthwork excavation. The BOQ contains a **Provisional Quantity of 450 m³** of soft soil excavation at a tendered rate of **₹180 per m³**. During execution, the actual measured quantity comes to **528 m³**. Calculate the amount payable to the contractor and the percentage variation.

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data**
- Original Provisional Quantity: $PQ_{orig} = 450\ m^3$
- Tendered Rate: $R = \vert Rs.\ 180/m^3$
- Actual Measured Quantity: $PQ_{actual} = 528\ m^3$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Payable Amount**
The contractor is paid based on **actual measurement** at the **tendered rate** (since rate was firm; only quantity was provisional).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Amount_{payable} &= PQ_{actual} \times R_{tendered} \\
&= 528\ m^3 \times Rs.\ 180\ per\ m^3 \\
&= Rs.\ 95{,}040
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Variation in Quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Variation &= PQ_{actual} - PQ_{orig} \\
&= 528\ m^3 - 450\ m^3 \\
&= 78\ m^3\ (excess)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Percentage Variation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% Variation &= \frac{Variation}{PQ_{orig}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{78}{450} \times 100 \\
&= 17.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Summary Table**

| Description | Value |
|---|---|
| Original Provisional Quantity | 450 m³ |
| Actual Quantity Executed | 528 m³ |
| Tendered Rate | ₹180/m³ |
| **Amount Payable** | **₹95,040** |
| Excess Quantity | 78 m³ |
| % Variation | +17.33% (excess) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** A 17.33% variation may trigger a **rate revision clause** in many contracts (typically > 10% or > 25% variation). Students must mention this clause awareness in answers to score full marks.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Provisional Sum Settlement

**Problem Statement:**
A residential building contract has a **Provisional Sum of ₹1,50,000** kept for "anti-termite treatment and unforeseen waterproofing works." At site, the actual work executed involves:
- Anti-termite treatment (specialist agency quote): ₹98,500
- Waterproofing of terrace (extra area discovered): ₹62,300
- Contingency 5% allowed on direct cost.

Calculate the final settlement and determine if the P.S. was sufficient.

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data**
- Provisional Sum: $PS = Rs.\ 1{,}50{,}000$
- Anti-termite cost: $C_1 = Rs.\ 98{,}500$
- Waterproofing cost: $C_2 = Rs.\ 62{,}300$
- Contingency on direct cost: $K = 5\%$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Direct Cost of Actual Work**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{direct} &= C_1 + C_2 \\
&= Rs.\ 98{,}500 + Rs.\ 62{,}300 \\
&= Rs.\ 1{,}60{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add 5% Contingency on Direct Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{contingency} &= C_{direct} \times \frac{K}{100} \\
&= 1{,}60{,}800 \times 0.05 \\
&= Rs.\ 8{,}040
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Total Actual Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{direct} + C_{contingency} \\
&= 1{,}60{,}800 + 8{,}040 \\
&= Rs.\ 1{,}68{,}840
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Provisional Sum and Determine Shortfall**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Shortfall &= C_{total} - PS \\
&= 1{,}68{,}840 - 1{,}50{,}000 \\
&= Rs.\ 18{,}840\ (deficit)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Summary**

| Description | Value |
|---|---|
| Provisional Sum Kept | ₹1,50,000 |
| Direct Cost Incurred | ₹1,60,800 |
| 5% Contingency | ₹8,040 |
| **Total Actual Cost** | **₹1,68,840** |
| **Shortfall (Extra Required)** | **₹18,840** |
| Decision | P.S. was **INSUFFICIENT**; need additional V.O. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The shortfall ₹18,840 must be sanctioned by the client through a **Variation Order (V.O.)** or drawn from the **project's general contingency reserve**.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Combined Application

**Problem Statement:**
A small bridge project has the following entries in BOQ:
- Item 1: Concrete in pile cap — 80 m³ at ₹6,500/m³ (firm quantity)
- Item 2: **Provisional Quantity** of extra PCC in pile cap — 35 m³ at ₹6,500/m³
- Item 3: **Provisional Sum** for dewatering during floods — ₹2,00,000

At actual execution:
- Firm quantity executed fully.
- Provisional quantity actually needed: 42 m³.
- Actual dewatering cost: ₹1,78,500.

Calculate the final contractor payment and the deviation analysis.

**Step 1 — Firm Quantity Payment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 80\ m^3 \times Rs.\ 6{,}500/m^3 = Rs.\ 5{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Provisional Quantity Payment (on actuals)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= 42\ m^3 \times Rs.\ 6{,}500/m^3 = Rs.\ 2{,}73{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Provisional Sum Settlement (on actuals)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_3 &= Rs.\ 1{,}78{,}500\ (actual\ dewatering\ cost)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Final Payment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= A_1 + A_2 + A_3 \\
&= 5{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}73{,}000 + 1{,}78{,}500 \\
&= Rs.\ 9{,}71{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Deviation Analysis**

| Item | Original Provision | Actual | Deviation | % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCC (firm) | 80 m³ | 80 m³ | 0 m³ | 0% |
| PCC (Provisional) | 35 m³ | 42 m³ | +7 m³ | +20% |
| Dewatering (P.S.) | ₹2,00,000 | ₹1,78,500 | −₹21,500 | −10.75% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow — Classifying an Item as P.S. or P.Q.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Estimator Reviews BOQ Item"] --> B{"Is the SCOPE of work KNOWN?"}
    B -- "Yes, scope known" --> C{"Is the QUANTITY measurable from drawings?"}
    B -- "No, scope undefined" --> D["Mark as PROVISIONAL SUM (P.S.)"]
    C -- "Yes, fully measurable" --> E["Firm Quantity (Normal BOQ Entry)"]
    C -- "No, quantity uncertain" --> F["Mark as PROVISIONAL QUANTITY (P.Q.)"]
    D --> G["Lump-sum amount entered"]
    F --> H["Estimated quantity + rate column open for tendering"]
    G --> I["Settled later by actual cost / V.O."]
    H --> J["Settled later by actual site measurement"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Settlement Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Tender Stage BOQ"] --> B["P.S. and P.Q. Entries Identified"]
    B --> C["Contract Awarded"]
    C --> D["Construction at Site"]
    D --> E{"Type of Entry"}
    E -- "Provisional Quantity" --> F["Site Measurement of Actual Qty"]
    E -- "Provisional Sum" --> G["Vouchers / Quotations / Day-work"]
    F --> H["Payment = Actual Qty × Tendered Rate"]
    G --> I["Payment = Actual Cost Reimbursed"]
    H --> J["Final Bill Generation"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Variation Order Issued if Deviation > Threshold"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Relationship of Concepts

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ESTIMATE PREPARATION"] --> B["Certain Items"]
    A --> C["Uncertain Items"]
    B --> B1["Measured Quantities × Scheduled Rates"]
    C --> C1{"Nature of Uncertainty"}
    C1 -- "Quantity not measurable" --> C2["PROVISIONAL QUANTITY (P.Q.)"]
    C1 -- "Scope not defined" --> C3["PROVISIONAL SUM (P.S.)"]
    C2 --> D["Settled by ACTUAL MEASUREMENT"]
    C3 --> E["Settled by ACTUAL COST / VOUCHERS"]
    D --> F["FINAL CONTRACT VALUE"]
    E --> F
    B1 --> F
```

### 4.4 Treatment in Standard Contract Forms (CPWD/IS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Contract Document Contains:"] --> B["Item-wise BOQ"]
    A --> C["Provisions for P.S. and P.Q."]
    B --> B1["Firm quantities with tendered rates"]
    C --> C1["P.Q. - measured on completion"]
    C --> C2["P.S. - reimbursed on actuals"]
    C1 --> D["Engineer Certifies Measurement"]
    C2 --> E["Contractor Submits Vouchers"]
    D --> F["Payment Processed"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Variation Statement Prepared"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define the term "Provisional Sum" as used in quantity surveying. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Provisional Sum** is a lump-sum amount of money provided in a Bill of Quantities (BOQ) for works that are **anticipated but cannot be accurately measured or defined** at the time of preparing the estimate. It serves as a budgetary reserve for unforeseen or partially defined items and is later adjusted based on actual cost incurred.

**Example:** A provisional sum of ₹1,00,000 kept in the BOQ of a hill-road project for "rock blasting beyond 2 m depth — actual quantity to be assessed during execution."

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for definition, 1 mark for example, 0.5 mark for clarity of expression.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Distinguish between "Provisional Sum" and "Provisional Quantity" in three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. | Provisional Sum | Provisional Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lump-sum monetary value inserted for undefined work. | Estimated quantity (in units) inserted for partially defined work. |
| 2 | Scope of work is uncertain. | Only quantity is uncertain; rate structure is known. |
| 3 | Settled by actual cost, vouchers, or quotations. | Settled by actual site measurement at the tendered rate. |

*(1 mark per point — total 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** A BOQ for an industrial building contains the following entry:
*"Provisional Quantity — Additional RCC in isolated footings below 1.5 m depth = 60 m³ at ₹4,800/m³."*
At site, the actual executed quantity was **74 m³**.
Calculate:
(i) Amount payable to contractor.
(ii) Excess quantity and percentage variation.
Comment on the contractual implications if the contract specifies that **any variation beyond 20% requires rate renegotiation**.

**(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Data**
- $PQ_{orig} = 60\ m^3$
- $PQ_{actual} = 74\ m^3$
- $R = Rs.\ 4{,}800/m^3$

**Step 2 — Calculate Payable Amount**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 74\ m^3 \times Rs.\ 4{,}800/m^3 \\
&= Rs.\ 3{,}55{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Excess Quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Excess &= 74 - 60 = 14\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate % Variation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\ var &= \frac{14}{60} \times 100 = 23.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Contractual Comment**
Since **23.33% > 20%**, the rate renegotiation clause is **triggered**. The contractor and engineer must jointly revise the rate for the excess portion (typically using CPWD Clause 12 — variation exceeding 10%/20%).

*(Mark split: 2 marks amount, 1 mark excess qty, 1 mark % variation, 3 marks contractual comment with clause reference.)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **role and importance** of provisional sums in public-sector construction contracts. Explain how they contribute to **fair tendering** and **risk-sharing** between owner and contractor.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Structure:**

1. **Definition and context (1 mark):** Re-define P.S. with reference to CPWD/KPWD practice.
2. **Why they are needed (2 marks):** Subsurface uncertainty, client indecision, late-stage design changes, imported material price fluctuations.
3. **Risk-sharing mechanism (2 marks):** Owner bears scope risk (since quantity is unknown); contractor bears execution risk at agreed rates.
4. **Fair tendering (1 mark):** All bidders price the same P.S. → no ambiguity → level playing field.
5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Provisional sums are an indispensable risk-allocation tool; their proper estimation is a mark of a competent quantity surveyor.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** For a highway project, the engineer has inserted the following provisional items in the BOQ:

| Item | Description | Type | Provision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rock excavation beyond 1 m depth | Provisional Quantity | 120 m³ @ ₹850/m³ |
| 2 | Dewatering during monsoon | Provisional Sum | ₹3,00,000 |
| 3 | Reinforcement for unforeseen RCC | Provisional Quantity | 1,800 kg @ ₹72/kg |

After execution, actuals were:
- Rock excavation: 142 m³
- Dewatering cost (with vouchers): ₹2,65,400
- Reinforcement used: 2,150 kg

Compute the total payment due and identify any item that exceeded the **±15% variation** limit.

**(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Payment for Rock Excavation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 142\ m^3 \times Rs.\ 850 = Rs.\ 1{,}20{,}700
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Payment for Dewatering (P.S. — actuals)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= Rs.\ 2{,}65{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Payment for Reinforcement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_3 &= 2{,}150\ kg \times Rs.\ 72 = Rs.\ 1{,}54{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Payment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= 1{,}20{,}700 + 2{,}65{,}400 + 1{,}54{,}800 \\
&= Rs.\ 5{,}40{,}900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Variation Analysis**

| Item | Original | Actual | Variation | % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rock excavation | 120 m³ | 142 m³ | +22 m³ | +18.33% ⚠️ |
| Dewatering | ₹3,00,000 | ₹2,65,400 | −₹34,600 | −11.53% |
| Reinforcement | 1,800 kg | 2,150 kg | +350 kg | +19.44% ⚠️ |

**Step 6 — Conclusion**
Both **rock excavation (+18.33%)** and **reinforcement (+19.44%)** exceed the **±15% limit** → rate renegotiation triggered per contract conditions.

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for 3 payments, 1 mark total, 3 marks variation table & identification.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **procedure for settlement of provisional sums** in a running contract. Highlight the **documentation** required from the contractor.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Initiation (1 mark):** Engineer identifies that the P.S. item has been triggered.
2. **Contractor submission (2 marks):** Contractor submits — (a) detailed measurement sheets, (b) supplier invoices/vouchers, (c) day-work records, (d) sub-contractor quotations (if any).
3. **Engineer's scrutiny (2 marks):** Site Engineer verifies measurements, checks authenticity of vouchers, and prepares a "Statement of Expenditure."
4. **Approval and payment (1 mark):** Client/Department approves and payment is processed through the next Running Account Bill (RA Bill).
5. **Final reconciliation (1 mark):** At project closure, all P.S. entries are reconciled with actuals; savings/shortfalls are noted in the final statement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls on Provisional Sum / Quantity Questions**
> 1. **Confusing P.S. with P.Q.:** Most common mistake. Always clarify whether the question asks for a *monetary sum* or a *quantity with unit*.
> 2. **Forgetting rate renegotiation clauses:** Examiners award 1–2 marks for mentioning the **±10% to ±20% variation clause** (CPWD Clause 12 / IS 1200).
> 3. **Not showing the unit in the answer:** Always write "m³" or "₹/m³" — losing the unit loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the variation % calculation:** Even if not asked, mentioning % variation earns a bonus mark in Part (a) solutions.
> 5. **Ignoring documentation in theory answers:** A theoretical answer on P.S. settlement that does NOT mention **vouchers, day-works, and measurement sheets** is incomplete.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Provisional Sum = Money** (lump sum, scope undefined) — Marked **"P.S."**
- ✅ **Provisional Quantity = Quantity** (estimated, rate known) — Marked **"P.Q."** or **"Prov. Qty"**
- ✅ **P.S. is settled on ACTUAL COST** (vouchers); **P.Q. is settled on ACTUAL MEASUREMENT** at tendered rate.
- ✅ Typical P.S. reserve in estimates: **3% to 5%** of estimated cost (CPWD norm).
- ✅ Variation beyond **±20%** typically triggers rate renegotiation.
- ✅ CPWD Clause 12 / IS 1200 governs these provisions in government contracts.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ Always calculate and comment on **percentage variation**, even if not explicitly asked.
- ✅ Documentation required for P.S.: invoices, vouchers, day-work records, sub-contractor quotations.
- ✅ Both P.S. and P.Q. are tools of **risk allocation** between owner and contractor.
- ✅ Final bill reconciliation MUST list actuals vs. provisional entries for every item.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhead Charges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Overhead Charges # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **OVERHEAD CHARGES (Definition as per KTU/CPWD/IS 1200 Standards):** > *Overhead charges* are the **indirect costs** incurred during the execution of a construction project which **cannot be directly attributed** or conveniently charged to a specific item of work, unit, or trade. They represent the collective expenditure on administration, supervision, site management, temporary works, tools & plants, and statutory obligations that are necessary to keep the project operational but are not physically embedded into any single measured item of the Bill of Quantities (BOQ). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, overheads are typically expressed as a **percentage addition** over the **prime cost (direct cost)** of the work, forming part of the **allied charges** or **percentage on-cost** in rate analysis. > [!NOTE] > **Synonyms used in valuation texts:** *Establishment charges, On-cost, Oncosts, Sundry charges, Percentage addition, Preliminary & General (P&G) charges.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are running a **tea stall (chai kadai)** outside a college campus. The **direct cost** of one cup of chai is just the milk, tea powder, sugar, and water — say ₹10. But to *actually serve* that chai to the customer, you also need: - Rent of the stall (indirect) - Salary of the helper boy (indirect) - Gas cylinder refill (indirect) - Electricity for the bulb and fan (indirect) - Cleaning supplies and your own time (indirect) These **extra expenses** that make the business run, but don't go *into* the cup itself, are the **overheads** of the tea stall. In construction, the same logic applies: the steel, cement, and bricks form the direct cost, while the site engineer's salary, site office rent, water for curing, electricity for the mixer, and bar-bending tools form the **overhead charges**. ## 1.3 Classification of Overhead Charges Overhead charges in civil engineering projects are broadly classified into **three major categories** as accepted by KTU/CPWD practice: > [!NOTE] > **Three Pillars of Overhead Charges:** > 1. **General / Establishment Overheads** (10% to 15% of total cost) > 2. **Specific / Service Overheads** (Water Supply, Sanitary, Electrical — usually 8% to 10% each) > 3. **Contingencies & Work-Charged Establishment** (3% to 5%) ### A. General / Establishment Overheads These cover the **general site administration** costs which benefit the **entire project**. ### B. Specific / Service Overheads These cover the **execution** of a specific trade (e.g., sanitary, water supply, electrical) by a specialized contractor. ### C. Contingencies A **reserve fund** to absorb unforeseen or incidental expenditures. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU/CPWD Percentage Benchmarks (2024 Scheme):** > - **Establishment charges:** **10% to 15%** of the estimated cost of works. > - **Contingencies (work charged):** **3% to 5%** of the estimated cost. > - **Tools & Plant (T&P):** Usually **2.5% to 5%** of the cost. > - **Water Supply & Sanitary Works:** **8% to 10%** on the relevant cost. > - **Electrical Installations:** **8% to 10%** on the relevant cost. > - **Centage charge (for Government departments):** **10% to 15%**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Three Categories of Overhead Charges ### Category 1: General / Establishment Overheads (Indirect) These are **project-wide** costs that cannot be linked to any individual item in the BOQ. **Components included:** - **Site office rent & maintenance** (temporary office sheds, godowns, site hutments) - **Salary of site staff** (Engineer, Supervisor, Mistri, Storekeeper, Watchman) - **Office stationery, postage, telephone bills, internet charges** - **Water charges** for the site (drinking and construction use) - **Electricity charges** for the site office and lighting - **Survey instruments** hire charges (theodolite, level, etc.) - **Labour welfare, insurance, ESI, PF contributions** - **Insurance** of the work (Contractor's All Risk Policy – CAR) - **Royalty** for earth, sand, aggregate (where applicable as overhead) - **Local taxes / octroi** during transit > [!NOTE] > **Standard percentage addition:** Generally taken as **10% to 15%** of the prime cost (estimated cost of works excluding land cost). ### Category 2: Specific / Service Overheads When a portion of work is **entrusted to a specialist agency** (sub-contractor), a separate percentage is added to cover that agency's **supervision and indirects**. **Components included:** - Supervision of the specialized trade - Tools & plants specific to that trade - Testing of materials used in that trade - Profit margin of the sub-contractor (where applicable) **Standard percentages (as per KTU/CPWD):** | S. No. | Trade / Service | Standard % Addition | |--------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | Water Supply & Sanitary Works | **8% to 10%** | | 2 | Electrical Installations | **8% to 10%** | | 3 | Lifts & Escalators | **5% to 8%** | | 4 | HVAC / Air-conditioning | **5% to 8%** | | 5 | Fire-fighting | **8%** | | 6 | Horticulture & Landscaping | **5%** | ### Category 3: Contingencies A **prudent provision** to cover unforeseen expenses such as: - Price escalation of materials - Design changes during execution - Minor architectural revisions - Sinking funds for repairs **Standard percentage:** **3% to 5%** of the total estimated cost of works. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for any KTU ESE question on Overhead Charges. | # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical % Range | |---|---------|----------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Total Cost of Project (TCP)** | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{prime}} + C_{\text{overheads}}$ | — | | 2 | **Establishment Charge Amount** | $E = \dfrac{p_1}{100} \times C_{\text{prime}}$ | $p_1 \in [10,\,15]\%$ | | 3 | **Contingency Amount** | $C_{\text{cont}} = \dfrac{p_2}{100} \times C_{\text{prime}}$ | $p_2 \in [3,\,5]\%$ | | 4 | **Water Supply & Sanitary Overhead** | $W = \dfrac{p_3}{100} \times C_{\text{WS\&S}}$ | $p_3 = 8\%$ to $10\%$ | | 5 | **Electrical Overhead** | $P_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{p_4}{100} \times C_{\text{elec}}$ | $p_4 = 8\%$ to $10\%$ | | 6 | **Grand Total Cost** | $C_{\text{grand}} = C_{\text{prime}} + E + C_{\text{cont}} + W + P_{\text{elec}}$ | — | | 7 | **Unit Rate with Overheads** | $R_{\text{unit}} = R_{\text{direct}} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$ | $p$ = total overhead % | | 8 | **Centage Charge (Govt. Dept.)** | $\text{Centage} = \dfrac{\text{Establishment cost of Dept.}}{\text{Capital cost of work}} \times 100$ | $10\%$ to $15\%$ | > **Note on the table:** The symbol $\vert$ (vertical bar) inside any formula is intentionally written as $\,\vert\,$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.3 The "Why & How" Logic **Why are overheads added as a percentage, not a lump sum?** Because the percentage is **proportional to the risk, supervision effort, and indirect consumption** of resources — a ₹10 Cr project will obviously have more staff salary, more insurance, more paper work than a ₹10 Lakh project, but in the *same proportion*. **How is the percentage decided?** - Based on **CPWD/DSR (Delhi Schedule of Rates) guidelines** for Government works. - Based on **market survey** for private works. - Adjusted by the **client/Engineer-in-Charge** based on project scale, location, and duration. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > In **Tender preparation**, every contractor adds a **"P&G" (Preliminary & General) line item** in their BOQ — this *is* the overhead. In **Public Works Department (PWD)** estimates, the *Abstract Estimate* always shows **separate rows for Establishment, Contingency, and Service Overheads** — making overheads a **mandatory disclosure** under the Kerala PWD Code and CPWD Works Manual. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Fundamental Cost Build-Up Equation Let us define the variables used in a **standard KTU/CPWD rate analysis** with overheads: - $C_p$ = Prime cost (direct cost of materials, labour, plant for measured work) - $E$ = Establishment / General overhead amount - $C_c$ = Contingency amount - $W$ = Water supply & sanitary overhead amount - $P_e$ = Electrical installation overhead amount - $C_g$ = Grand total cost to be included in the Estimate The **fundamental build-up equation** is: $$ C_g = C_p + E + C_c + W + P_e $$ Where each overhead is a **fraction of the prime cost** (or of its relevant trade cost): $$ E = k_1 \cdot C_p, \quad C_c = k_2 \cdot C_p, \quad W = k_3 \cdot C_{\text{WS\&S}}, \quad P_e = k_4 \cdot C_{\text{elec}} $$ Here $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$ are the respective **percentage fractions** (e.g., $k_1 = 0.10$ for 10%). ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Derivation (KTU Standard Problem) > **Problem (Worked Example):** > The estimated prime cost of a residential building construction is **₹ 50,00,000** (Fifty Lakhs). The estimated cost of Water Supply & Sanitary works is **₹ 6,00,000** and Electrical installations cost is **₹ 4,00,000**. Adopt standard overhead percentages: **Establishment = 12%, Contingencies = 3%, WS&S = 8%, Electrical = 10%**. Calculate the **total cost of the project including all overhead charges**. ### Step 1: List the given data $$ C_p = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}, \quad C_{\text{WS\&S}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}, \quad C_{\text{elec}} = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ $$ k_1 = 12\% = 0.12, \quad k_2 = 3\% = 0.03, \quad k_3 = 8\% = 0.08, \quad k_4 = 10\% = 0.10 $$ ### Step 2: Compute Establishment Charges $$ E = k_1 \cdot C_p $$ $$ E = 0.12 \times 50{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{E = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}} $$ > **Logic:** 12% of the prime cost accounts for site staff, rent, stationery, insurance, etc. ### Step 3: Compute Contingency Charges $$ C_c = k_2 \cdot C_p $$ $$ C_c = 0.03 \times 50{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{C_c = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}} $$ > **Logic:** 3% is kept as a buffer for unforeseen expenses. ### Step 4: Compute Water Supply & Sanitary Overhead $$ W = k_3 \cdot C_{\text{WS\&S}} $$ $$ W = 0.08 \times 6{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{W = 48{,}000 \text{ INR}} $$ ### Step 5: Compute Electrical Overhead $$ P_e = k_4 \cdot C_{\text{elec}} $$ $$ P_e = 0.10 \times 4{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{P_e = 40{,}000 \text{ INR}} $$ ### Step 6: Compute Grand Total Cost $$ C_g = C_p + E + C_c + W + P_e $$ $$ C_g = 50{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 48{,}000 + 40{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{C_g = 58{,}38{,}000 \text{ INR} \;\;(\text{₹ 58.38 Lakhs})} $$ ### Step 7: Express Overheads as a Single Composite % The **total overhead percentage** relative to the prime cost is: $$ k_{\text{total}} = \frac{E + C_c + W + P_e}{C_p} \times 100 $$ $$ k_{\text{total}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 48{,}000 + 40{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 $$ $$ k_{\text{total}} = \frac{8{,}38{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 16.76\% $$ > **Insight:** Even though individual overhead rates are 3%, 8%, 10%, 12% — the **composite overhead on prime cost is 16.76%**. This is the figure to use in **rate rounding** or **tender price index**. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Unit Rate with Overheads (Rate Analysis Form) For any **single item of work** (say, 1 m³ of RCC M25), the **final all-in rate** $R$ is given by: $$ R = (M + L + P) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k_{\text{oh}}}{100}\right) $$ Where: - $M$ = Material cost per unit work - $L$ = Labour cost per unit work - $P$ = Plant/machinery cost per unit work - $k_{\text{oh}}$ = Overhead percentage (composite) **Derivation (rate of 1 m³ of RCC M25):** - Cost of cement per m³ (after wastage): ₹2,400 - Cost of sand per m³: ₹800 - Cost of aggregate per m³: ₹1,200 - Cost of steel per m³: ₹3,500 - Labour (mix, place, vibrate, cure) per m³: ₹1,500 - Plant (mixer, vibrator) per m³: ₹400 - **Prime cost** $= 2400 + 800 + 1200 + 3500 + 1500 + 400 = 8{,}800$ ₹/m³ - Apply 16.76% overhead: $8800 \times 1.1676 = 10{,}274.88$ ₹/m³ - Add contractor's profit (say 10%): $10274.88 \times 1.10 = 11{,}302.37$ ₹/m³ - **Final all-in rate ≈ ₹ 11,302/m³ of RCC M25** ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Overhead % to Apply? | Project Scenario | Establishment % | Contingency % | Service OH % | |------------------|-----------------|----------------|----------------| | Small residential (≤ ₹ 50 Lakh) | **10%** | **3%** | **8%** | | Medium commercial (₹ 50 L – 5 Cr) | **12%** | **3%** | **9%** | | Large institutional (> ₹ 5 Cr) | **15%** | **5%** | **10%** | | Remote / difficult terrain | **15%** | **5%** | **10%** | | Government/CPWD works | **CPWD Manual %** | **CPWD Manual %** | **CPWD Manual %** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of Cost Build-Up with Overheads The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **hierarchical flow** of cost components from the **lowest-level direct cost** to the **final project grand total** through various overhead layers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Prime Cost Cp
Materials + Labour + Plant] --> B[Add Establishment Overhead E
at 10 to 15 percent] B --> C[Add Contingency Charge Cc
at 3 to 5 percent] C --> D[Add Water Supply and Sanitary OH W
at 8 to 10 percent of WS and S cost] C --> E[Add Electrical OH Pe
at 8 to 10 percent of Electrical cost] D --> F[Grand Total Project Cost Cg] E --> F F --> G[Add Contractors Profit 10 percent] G --> H[Add Applicable GST 18 percent] H --> I[Final Tender Quoted Price] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — How Overheads are Determined in a Typical KTU/CBEE Estimate ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1: Prepare Detailed BOQ] --> P2[Step 2: Compute Prime Cost Cp] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Identify Project Category
Small Medium Large] P3 --> P4{Step 4: Select OH Percentage
from CPWD DSR Table} P4 --> P5[Step 5: Apply E and Cc on Cp] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Apply Trade Specific OH
on respective trade costs] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Sum all components to Cg] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Round off to nearest lakh] P8 --> P9[Step 9: Submit Abstract Estimate for Sanction] ``` ## 4.3 Tree Diagram — Classification of Overhead Charges (Mind-Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Overhead Charges in Construction] --> G1[General Establishment OH] ROOT --> S1[Specific Service OH] ROOT --> C1[Contingencies] G1 --> G1a[Site Office and Staff Salary] G1 --> G1b[Office and Stationery] G1 --> G1c[Site Water and Electricity] G1 --> G1d[Insurance and Royalty] G1 --> G1e[Survey Instruments and T and P] S1 --> S1a[Water Supply and Sanitary 8 to 10 percent] S1 --> S1b[Electrical Installations 8 to 10 percent] S1 --> S1c[Lift and HVAC 5 to 8 percent] S1 --> S1d[Fire Fighting 8 percent] S1 --> S1e[Horticulture 5 percent] C1 --> C1a[Material Price Escalation 1 to 2 percent] C1 --> C1b[Design Change Buffer 1 percent] C1 --> C1c[Misc Unforeseen 1 to 2 percent] ``` ## 4.4 Sequential Layer Diagram — Composite Overhead Build-Up ```mermaid flowchart TB L0[Layer 0: Direct Cost Cp = 100 percent]:::base L1[Layer 1: Plus Establishment E = plus 12 percent]:::layer1 L2[Layer 2: Plus Contingency Cc = plus 3 percent]:::layer2 L3[Layer 3: Plus Service OH W and Pe = plus 1.76 percent composite]:::layer3 L4[Layer 4: Grand Total Cost Cg = 116.76 percent of Cp]:::layer4 L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 classDef base fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer2 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef layer4 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Define the term "Overhead Charges" as applied to a building estimate. List any four items that are included under general establishment overheads.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** *Overhead charges are the indirect costs incurred during the execution of a construction project which cannot be directly attributed to any specific item of work. They are usually added as a percentage (10%–15%) over the prime cost to cover site management, supervision, and administrative expenses.* **Four items under Establishment Overheads (1.5 Marks):** 1. Site office rent and maintenance 2. Salary of site engineer, supervisor, and watchman 3. Water and electricity charges at site 4. Insurance of works (CAR policy) and stationery --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Distinguish between "Establishment Charges" and "Contingencies" in a project estimate. State the standard percentage range adopted for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Establishment Charges | Contingencies | |--------|----------------------|---------------| | **Purpose** | To cover recurring site administration costs | To cover unforeseen / incidental expenses | | **Nature** | Predictable, budgeted | Reserve / buffer fund | | **Standard %** | **10% to 15%** of prime cost | **3% to 5%** of prime cost | | **Example** | Site engineer salary, office rent | Price escalation, minor design change | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks > `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q.A.** **(a)** Explain in detail the **different types of overhead charges** that are added to the prime cost of a building estimate. State the standard percentage adopted for each type. > **(b)** The estimated prime cost of construction of a hostel building is **₹ 1,20,00,000**. The cost of internal water supply and sanitary installations is **₹ 8,00,000** and the cost of internal electrical installations is **₹ 6,00,000**. Using standard percentage rates, calculate the **total cost of the project including all overheads**. Adopt: Establishment 12%, Contingency 3%, WS&S 8%, Electrical 10%. #### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks The overhead charges added to the prime cost of a building estimate are classified into **three broad categories**: **1. General / Establishment Overhead Charges (3 Marks):** These are the indirect costs incurred for **general site administration** which are not attributable to any individual item of work. Standard percentage: **10% to 15%**. Items included: - Salary of site engineer, supervisor, store-keeper, watchman - Site office rent, maintenance of temporary sheds - Stationery, postage, telephone, internet - Site water and electricity charges - Insurance (CAR policy) and labour welfare measures - Survey instruments, minor tools and plants - Royalty, local taxes, octroi **2. Specific / Service Overhead Charges (2 Marks):** Added when **specialist sub-agencies** execute specific trades. Standard percentage: **8% to 10%** for WS&S, Electrical, Fire-fighting, and **5% to 8%** for Lifts, HVAC, Horticulture. **3. Contingencies (2 Marks):** A **reserve fund** to cover unforeseen / incidental expenditure. Standard: **3% to 5%** of prime cost. Examples: material price escalation, minor design revisions. #### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** $$ C_p = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{\text{WS\&S}} = 8{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{\text{elec}} = 6{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ k_1 = 0.12, \quad k_2 = 0.03, \quad k_3 = 0.08, \quad k_4 = 0.10 $$ **Step 1: Establishment Charges** (1.5 Marks) $$ E = 0.12 \times 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}40{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 2: Contingency Charges** (1.5 Marks) $$ C_c = 0.03 \times 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 3: Water Supply & Sanitary Overhead** (1.5 Marks) $$ W = 0.08 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 64{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 4: Electrical Overhead** (1 Mark) $$ P_e = 0.10 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 5: Grand Total Cost** (1.5 Marks) $$ C_g = C_p + E + C_c + W + P_e $$ $$ C_g = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}40{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 64{,}000 + 60{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{C_g = 1{,}39{,}24{,}000 \text{ INR} \;(\text{₹ 1.3924 Crore})} $$ --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) > `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Q.B.** **(a)** Differentiate between **Direct Cost (Prime Cost)** and **Overhead Cost** in a construction project. Why are overheads added as a percentage of the prime cost and not as a lump sum? Explain with a suitable example. > **(b)** A contractor is required to execute a building project. The prime cost of works is **₹ 80,00,000**, the cost of water supply and sanitary works is **₹ 5,00,000**, and the cost of electrical works is **₹ 3,50,000**. If establishment charges are **15%** and contingencies are **3%**, and the service overheads for WS&S and Electrical are **10%** each, determine the **abstract estimate total**. #### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks | Aspect | Direct Cost (Prime Cost) | Overhead Cost | |--------|--------------------------|---------------| | **Definition** | Cost of materials, labour, plant that are directly used in measured items of BOQ | Indirect costs not attributable to any single item | | **Visibility** | Physically visible in the work (e.g., 1 bag cement) | Non-physical (e.g., engineer's salary) | | **Measurability** | Measured in m, m², m³, kg, nos. | Expressed as a % addition | | **Variability** | Varies directly with quantity of work | Approximately constant as a % | **Why percentage and not lump sum? (3 Marks)** - Overheads **scale proportionally** with project cost (a bigger project needs more staff, larger office, more insurance). - A percentage maintains **fairness** across projects of different sizes. - A lump sum would either over-charge a small project or under-fund a large one. **Example:** For a ₹10 L project, 12% establishment = ₹1.2 L. For a ₹10 Cr project, 12% = ₹1.2 Cr — both **proportionate** to effort, whereas a flat ₹2 L lump sum would be 20% for the small project and 0.2% for the large one — illogical. #### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** $$ C_p = 80{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{\text{WS\&S}} = 5{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{\text{elec}} = 3{,}50{,}000 $$ $$ k_1 = 0.15, \quad k_2 = 0.03, \quad k_3 = k_4 = 0.10 $$ **Step 1: Establishment Charges** (1.5 Marks) $$ E = 0.15 \times 80{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 2: Contingency Charges** (1.5 Marks) $$ C_c = 0.03 \times 80{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 3: Water Supply & Sanitary Overhead** (1.5 Marks) $$ W = 0.10 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 4: Electrical Overhead** (1 Mark) $$ P_e = 0.10 \times 3{,}50{,}000 = 35{,}000 \text{ INR} $$ **Step 5: Grand Total** (1.5 Marks) $$ C_g = 80{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 35{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{C_g = 95{,}25{,}000 \text{ INR} \;(\text{₹ 95.25 Lakhs})} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU students make in Overhead Charges questions:** > 1. **Applying service overhead on Prime Cost** instead of on the **respective trade cost** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this. Service OH (WS&S, Electrical) is calculated on the *cost of that specific trade*, not on the full prime cost. > 2. **Confusing percentage values** — Students often write 8% for electrical and 10% for WS&S (or vice versa). Memorize: **8–10%** for both, but verify with the question's given value. > 3. **Forgetting to add the prime cost itself** in the grand total — final $C_g$ *must* include $C_p$ plus all overheads. > 4. **Omitting rounding-off** — Final answer should be rounded to the nearest **hundred / thousand / lakh** as appropriate; examiners appreciate clarity in units. > 5. **Writing "establishment" and "contingency" interchangeably** — they are *not* the same; establishment is for site management, contingency is for unforeseen works. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Recall Master List — Overhead Charges** - **Definition:** Indirect, non-measurable costs added as a % on prime cost. - **Three Categories:** *(i)* General / Establishment, *(ii)* Specific / Service, *(iii)* Contingencies. - **Establishment %:** **10% to 15%** of prime cost — covers site staff, office, stationery, water, electricity, insurance, survey instruments. - **Contingency %:** **3% to 5%** of prime cost — covers unforeseen expenses, price escalation. - **WS&S / Electrical Service OH:** **8% to 10%** — applied on the *respective trade cost*, NOT on the prime cost. - **Lift / HVAC Service OH:** **5% to 8%** — applied on the respective trade cost. - **Tools & Plant (T&P):** Often clubbed with establishment; **2.5% to 5%** if shown separately. - **Centage Charge:** Used in government departmental estimates — **10% to 15%** of capital cost. - **Fundamental Formula:** $C_g = C_p + E + C_c + W + P_e + \ldots$ - **Composite Overhead %:** $k_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\sum \text{All overheads}}{C_p} \times 100$ - **Why percentage?** Because overheads scale **proportionally** with project size. - **Real-world use:** Mandatory line items in **CPWD, PWD Kerala, KTU Board, and Private Tender** estimates under the head *"Percentage on-cost / P&G / Sundries"*. - **Examiner tip:** Always state the **category name and standard %** *before* computing — earns 1 free mark for "process". - **Unit consistency:** Always quote in **₹, ₹ Lakhs, or ₹ Crores** — examiners deduct marks for inconsistent units.

topics.title: Cost Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Index (CI)** is a statistical measure that quantifies the relative change in the cost of constructing a standardized building or a defined basket of construction inputs over a specified period of time, expressed as a ratio (or percentage) relative to a chosen **Base Year** (Base Period) whose index is conventionally fixed at **100**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Cost Index is the principal tool used in **Valuation Engineering** to convert historical costs to present-day costs, to compute **escalation**, and to grant compensation for **replacement value** of buildings, roads, and other civil engineering structures.

The General Form of the Cost Index is:

$$CI_t = \frac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100$$

Where:
- $CI_t$ = Cost Index in the current/required year $t$
- $C_t$ = Cost of the standard construction basket in year $t$
- $C_0$ = Cost of the same standard construction basket in the base year
- $100$ = Conventional index value of the base year

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a simple analogy: your father bought **1 kg of rice in 2010 for ₹30**. The same 1 kg of rice in **2024 costs ₹60**. To express this change as a single number, you set the 2010 price as 100 and compute:

$$CI_{2024} = \frac{60}{30} \times 100 = 200$$

So, the cost index for rice in 2024 is **200** — meaning things cost "twice" as much as in 2010. The **Cost Index in construction works exactly the same way**, except the "rice" is replaced by a standardized mix of **cement, steel, bricks, labor, sand, aggregates**, and other construction inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use Case in Civil Engineering:**
> If a building was constructed in 2015 for ₹50 Lakhs and the Cost Index has risen from 120 (in 2015) to 240 (in 2024), the equivalent present-day replacement cost becomes:
> $$\text{Present Cost} = 50,00,000 \times \frac{240}{120} = ₹1,00,00,000$$

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| **Base Year Index Value** | **100** (Universal convention) |
| **Reference Organizations** | CPWD, NBCC, RBI, BIS, State PWDs |
| **Standard Basket Composition** | Materials + Labor + Plant + Overheads |
| **Reporting Frequency** | Quarterly / Annually (varies by publisher) |
| **Unit** | Dimensionless (Pure Number / Percentage) |
| **Rounding Convention** | Nearest whole number (usually) |

## 1.4 Classification of Cost Indices

Cost Indices are broadly classified into the following categories used in Indian valuation practice:

1. **Building Cost Index (BCI)** – Tracks the cost of constructing a standard residential/non-residential building.
2. **Material Cost Index** – Tracks price changes of key materials (cement, steel, bitumen) individually.
3. **Labor Cost Index (Wage Index)** – Tracks daily wages of skilled/unskilled workers.
4. **Plant and Machinery Cost Index** – Tracks hire charges of construction equipment.
5. **Wholesale Price Index (WPI)** for construction materials – Published by the Office of the Economic Adviser, GoI.
6. **Consumer Price Index (CPI)** – Used for inflation-related adjustments in long-term contracts.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 Board Examinations, focus primarily on the **Building Cost Index (BCI)** and its direct application in **Valuation of Buildings**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic Behind Cost Index Construction

The construction of a Cost Index follows a **systematic, five-step methodology** recognized by KTU syllabus and CPWD/NBCC guidelines:

### Step 1 — Selection of the Base Year
A "Base Year" is chosen where the economic conditions are considered **normal** (no major recession, war, or hyperinflation). The index of this base year is set to **100**.

### Step 2 — Selection of the Representative Basket
A standard list of materials, labor categories, and their quantities is fixed. This basket represents a "model building" or "model project". The basket remains constant over time; only the **prices** change.

### Step 3 — Collection of Current Prices
Periodically, market prices for each item in the basket are collected from a defined sample of markets/states.

### Step 4 — Weighted Aggregation
Each item is assigned a **weight ($w_i$)** based on its relative importance in total project cost. The weighted aggregate cost is then compared to the base year.

### Step 5 — Index Number Computation
The Cost Index is computed using one of the standard index number formulas (see Section 3 for derivations).

## 2.2 Methods of Computing Cost Index

The KTU syllabus recognizes three principal methods:

### Method 1 — Simple Aggregative Method (Unweighted)
$$CI = \frac{\sum P_n}{\sum P_0} \times 100$$

Where $P_n$ = current prices, $P_0$ = base year prices. This method ignores weights.

### Method 2 — Weighted Aggregative Method (Laspeyres' Formula)
$$CI_L = \frac{\sum (P_n \times q_0)}{\sum (P_0 \times q_0)} \times 100$$

Where $q_0$ = base year quantities. This is the most widely used method.

### Method 3 — Weighted Aggregative Method (Paasche's Formula)
$$CI_P = \frac{\sum (P_n \times q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \times q_n)} \times 100$$

Where $q_n$ = current year quantities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Cost Index** | $CI = \frac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100$ | Direct computation of index |
| **Laspeyres Index** | $CI_L = \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_0)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0)} \times 100$ | Most common weighted index |
| **Paasche Index** | $CI_P = \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_n)} \times 100$ | Current-quantity weighted |
| **Fisher's Ideal Index** | $CI_F = \sqrt{CI_L \times CI_P}$ | Geometric mean of L and P |
| **Present Value by CI** | $V_n = V_0 \times \frac{CI_n}{CI_0}$ | Valuation of buildings |
| **Annual Escalation Rate** | $r = \left[\left(\frac{CI_n}{CI_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right] \times 100$ | Average annual rate of cost rise |
| **Quantity Variation (Ratio)** | $\rho = \frac{q_n}{q_0}$ | Used in Paasche formula |
| **Price Relative** | $R_i = \frac{P_{n,i}}{P_{0,i}} \times 100$ | Individual item price change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Cost Index is indispensable in **insurance valuation, expropriation compensation, replacement cost accounting, tender escalation clauses, PPP/Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT) contract pricing**, and **arbitration disputes** involving time delays in construction projects.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Civil Engineering

1. **Replacement Valuation:** Estimating the cost to rebuild a structure destroyed by fire/natural calamity at current market rates.
2. **Escalation Payments:** Adjusting contractor running-account bills when contracts have an escalation clause.
3. **Insurance Settlements:** Computing the "sum insured" for fire and allied perils insurance policies.
4. **Acquisition Compensation:** When government acquires private property under the Land Acquisition Act, the cost index is used to escalate historical costs.
5. **Bookkeeping & Depreciation:** Adjusting the historical cost of capital assets in company balance sheets for inflation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Cost Index Formula

The cost index is essentially a **relative measure**. We start with the fundamental concept: an index measures the ratio of a value at a given time to the same value at a reference time.

**Step 1 — Define the value of the standard construction basket at the base year.**
$$V_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{0,i} \times q_{0,i})$$

This represents the total cost of the basket using base-year prices ($P_{0,i}$) and base-year quantities ($q_{0,i}$). The summation runs over $n$ items in the basket (cement, steel, labor, etc.).

**Step 2 — Define the value of the same basket at the current year, but using base-year quantities (Laspeyres approach).**
$$V_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{n,i} \times q_{0,i})$$

Here, $P_{n,i}$ are the **current prices** of each item $i$, but the **quantities $q_{0,i}$ are kept fixed** at the base-year level. This is the core assumption that allows meaningful comparison.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of the two values to obtain the index.**
$$CI = \frac{V_n}{V_0}$$

This is the raw (decimal) ratio of current cost to base cost. To convert it into a percentage/index form, multiply by 100.

**Step 4 — Final derived expression of the Laspeyres Cost Index.**
$$\boxed{CI_L = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{n,i} \times q_{0,i})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{0,i} \times q_{0,i})} \times 100}$$

This is the most rigorous and widely accepted form of the cost index in valuation practice.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A contractor prepares a "standard construction basket" consisting of 4 items with the following base-year (2018) and current-year (2023) data:

| Item | Quantity (constant) | Price in 2018 (₹) | Price in 2023 (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cement (50 kg bag) | 100 bags | 350 | 480 |
| Steel (per kg) | 500 kg | 45 | 75 |
| Sand (per cum) | 10 cum | 1200 | 1800 |
| Labor (per day) | 200 days | 500 | 700 |

**Required:** Compute the **Laspeyres Cost Index** for 2023 with 2018 as the base year.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute $P_{0,i} \times q_{0,i}$ for each item.**

$$\text{Cement: } 350 \times 100 = 35{,}000$$

$$\text{Steel: } 45 \times 500 = 22{,}500$$

$$\text{Sand: } 1200 \times 10 = 12{,}000$$

$$\text{Labor: } 500 \times 200 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

**Sum of base year value ($V_0$):**

$$V_0 = 35{,}000 + 22{,}500 + 12{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}69{,}500$$

**Step 2: Compute $P_{n,i} \times q_{0,i}$ for each item.**

$$\text{Cement: } 480 \times 100 = 48{,}000$$

$$\text{Steel: } 75 \times 500 = 37{,}500$$

$$\text{Sand: } 1800 \times 10 = 18{,}000$$

$$\text{Labor: } 700 \times 200 = 1{,}40{,}000$$

**Sum of current year value ($V_n$):**

$$V_n = 48{,}000 + 37{,}500 + 18{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 = ₹2{,}43{,}500$$

**Step 3: Compute the Laspeyres Cost Index.**

$$CI_L = \frac{V_n}{V_0} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}43{,}500}{1{,}69{,}500} \times 100$$

$$CI_L = 1.4366 \times 100 = 143.66 \approx 144$$

**Conclusion:** The Cost Index in 2023 (with 2018 = 100) is **144**, meaning construction costs have risen by **44%** over 5 years.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Application in Building Valuation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important application of Cost Index in KTU valuation problems is the **Escalation of Historical Cost to Present Cost**.

**Derivation of the Valuation Formula using Cost Index:**

We have a building whose original construction cost was $C_0$ in year 0 (when the Cost Index was $CI_0$). We wish to find its present-day equivalent cost $C_n$ when the current Cost Index is $CI_n$.

**Step 1 — Express the relationship between cost and index linearly.**

By definition of the cost index, the cost per unit of construction is **directly proportional** to the cost index number:

$$\frac{C_0}{CI_0} = \frac{C_n}{CI_n} = k \text{ (constant of proportionality)}$$

This is because the cost index is *defined* as the relative cost, and the unit "basket" cost scales linearly with the index value.

**Step 2 — Solve for the present cost $C_n$.**

$$C_n = C_0 \times \frac{CI_n}{CI_0}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Valuation Problem.**

A building was constructed in **2010** for **₹25,00,000**. The Cost Index in 2010 was **120** and in **2024** is **270**. Find the present-day replacement cost (ignoring depreciation).

$$C_{2024} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{270}{120}$$

$$C_{2024} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 2.25 = ₹56{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual average escalation rate.**

$$r = \left[\left(\frac{CI_{2024}}{CI_{2010}}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right] \times 100$$

$$r = \left[\left(\frac{270}{120}\right)^{1/14} - 1\right] \times 100$$

$$r = \left[(2.25)^{0.0714} - 1\right] \times 100$$

$$r = [1.0593 - 1] \times 100 = 5.93\%$$

So the cost has been rising at an average compounded rate of **5.93% per annum**.

## 3.4 Fisher's Ideal Index — Derivation

The Fisher's Ideal Index is the **geometric mean** of the Laspeyres and Paasche indices. It is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Take the square root of the product of the two indices.**

$$CI_F = \sqrt{CI_L \times CI_P}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions for Laspeyres and Paasche indices.**

$$CI_F = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_0)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0)} \times \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_n)}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — This is the final expression of Fisher's Ideal Index.**

$$\boxed{CI_F = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_0) \cdot \sum (P_n \cdot q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0) \cdot \sum (P_0 \cdot q_n)}} \times 100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Ideal"?** Fisher's index satisfies the **Time Reversal Test** and the **Factor Reversal Test**, making it mathematically consistent. It is rarely used in KTU problems but is a high-yield concept.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cost Index Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Purpose of Cost Index] --> B[Select Base Year]
    B --> C[Set Base Year Index = 100]
    C --> D[Define Standard Construction Basket]
    D --> E[Materials: Cement, Steel, Bricks, Sand, Aggregate]
    D --> F[Labor: Skilled, Unskilled, Semiskilled Categories]
    D --> G[Plant: Machinery Hire Charges]
    D --> H[Overheads: Supervision, Contingencies]
    E --> I[Assign Weights wi to Each Item]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Collect Base Year Prices P0i]
    J --> K[Collect Current Year Prices Pni]
    K --> L{Choose Index Method}
    L -->|Unweighted| M[Simple Aggregative Method]
    L -->|Weighted| N[Laspeyres Method]
    L -->|Alternative| O[Paasche Method]
    L -->|Combined| P[Fishers Ideal Method]
    M --> Q[Compute Sum Pn and Sum P0]
    N --> R[Compute Sum Pn times q0 and Sum P0 times q0]
    O --> S[Compute Sum Pn times qn and Sum P0 times qn]
    P --> T[Geometric Mean of L and P]
    Q --> U[Final Cost Index Value]
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Apply in Valuation, Escalation, Insurance]
    V --> W[End: Decision Support Ready]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Application Architecture of Cost Index in Valuation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Construction Year] --> B[Historical Cost C0]
    A1[Cost Index in Base Year CI0] --> C
    B --> C[Historical Cost Adjusted by Index]
    A1 --> C
    C --> D[Present Day Equivalent Cost Cn]
    A2[Current Cost Index CIn] --> D
    D --> E{Application Domain}
    E --> F[Insurance Valuation]
    E --> G[Replacement Cost Estimation]
    E --> H[Government Acquisition Compensation]
    E --> I[Contract Escalation Bills]
    E --> J[Depreciation and Balance Sheet Adjustments]
    F --> K[Final Valuation Report]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between Different Cost Indices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cost Index Family] --> B[Building Cost Index BCI]
    A --> C[Material Cost Index MCI]
    A --> D[Labor Cost Index LCI]
    A --> E[Plant and Machinery Index PMI]
    B --> F[Composite of MCI plus LCI plus PMI]
    C --> G[Cement, Steel, Bitumen Sub-indices]
    D --> H[Skilled vs Unskilled Wage Sub-indices]
    F --> I[Used for Total Building Valuation]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for Students:** These block diagrams summarize the entire workflow of a Cost Index study — from data collection to final application. KTU examiners often award marks for **flowchart-style explanations** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define Cost Index. What is its significance in valuation?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Cost Index is a numerical measure that expresses the relative change in the cost of a standardized construction basket over time, with the base year fixed at 100.
>
> **Significance in Valuation:**
> 1. It is used to **escalate** historical construction costs to present-day values for valuation, insurance, and acquisition.
> 2. It accounts for **inflation** in long-term contracts and helps in fair compensation.
> 3. It enables **comparison** of project costs across different time periods.
> 4. The formula used is: $V_n = V_0 \times \frac{CI_n}{CI_0}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for significance)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Laspeyres and Paasche Cost Index methods.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Laspeyres Method | Paasche Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantities Used** | Base year quantities ($q_0$) | Current year quantities ($q_n$) |
| **Formula** | $CI_L = \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_0)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0)} \times 100$ | $CI_P = \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_n)} \times 100$ |
| **Data Requirement** | Easier (only base quantities needed) | Difficult (current quantities needed) |
| **Use** | Most common in practice | Used in dynamic studies |
| **Bias** | Overstates inflation (substitution effect ignored) | Understates inflation |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for formulas, 1 mark for practical note)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**(a)** Explain in detail the **methods of preparing a Cost Index**. Discuss the **Laspeyres method** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A building was constructed at a cost of **₹18,00,000 in 2012** when the Cost Index was **150**. Find the **present cost in 2024** when the Cost Index is **320**. Also compute the **average annual rate of cost escalation**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
A Cost Index measures the relative change in construction cost. Preparing it requires:
1. Selection of base year (set at 100).
2. Selection of representative items and quantities (basket).
3. Collection of prices for the basket in the base year and the current year.
4. Application of an index number formula.

**Step 2 — List of Methods:**
- **Simple Aggregative Method** (unweighted)
- **Laspeyres Method** (weighted, base-year quantities)
- **Paasche Method** (weighted, current-year quantities)
- **Fisher's Ideal Index** (geometric mean of L and P)

**Step 3 — Laspeyres Method with Example:**

Suppose the base year is 2018 and the current year is 2023. The standard basket has 3 items:

| Item | $q_0$ (units) | $P_0$ (₹) | $P_n$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cement bag | 200 | 350 | 480 |
| Steel kg | 1000 | 45 | 75 |
| Labor day | 300 | 500 | 700 |

**Step 4 — Apply Laspeyres Formula:**

$$\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0) = (350 \times 200) + (45 \times 1000) + (500 \times 300)$$

$$= 70{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}65{,}000$$

$$\sum (P_n \cdot q_0) = (480 \times 200) + (75 \times 1000) + (700 \times 300)$$

$$= 96{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 2{,}10{,}000 = 3{,}81{,}000$$

$$CI_L = \frac{3{,}81{,}000}{2{,}65{,}000} \times 100 = 143.77 \approx 144$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The cost index in 2023 is **144** with 2018 as the base year (100). Construction costs have risen by **44%** over 5 years.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Original cost in 2012: $V_0 = ₹18{,}00{,}000$
- Cost Index in 2012: $CI_0 = 150$
- Cost Index in 2024: $CI_n = 320$
- Number of years: $n = 2024 - 2012 = 12$ years

**Step 1 — Apply the Valuation Formula:**

$$V_n = V_0 \times \frac{CI_n}{CI_0}$$

$$V_n = 18{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{320}{150}$$

$$V_n = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 2.1333 = ₹38{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Annual Escalation Rate:**

$$r = \left[\left(\frac{CI_n}{CI_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right] \times 100$$

$$r = \left[\left(\frac{320}{150}\right)^{1/12} - 1\right] \times 100$$

$$r = \left[(2.1333)^{0.0833} - 1\right] \times 100$$

$$r = [1.0651 - 1] \times 100 = 6.51\%$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer:**
- Present cost in 2024 = **₹38,40,000**
- Average annual escalation rate = **6.51% per annum**

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*
**(a)** Define Cost Index. Explain the **Fisher's Ideal Index method** with its formula and properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data refers to a **standard construction basket** for a residential building. Compute the **Laspeyres** and **Paasche Cost Indices** for 2024, taking 2019 as the base year. **(7 marks)**

| Item | $q_0$ (2019) | $q_n$ (2024) | $P_0$ (2019) | $P_n$ (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cement (bags) | 400 | 380 | 350 | 460 |
| Steel (kg) | 800 | 850 | 50 | 80 |
| Bricks (Nos.) | 5000 | 4500 | 8 | 12 |

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** (as stated in Section 1) — Cost Index is a relative measure of construction cost change over time, with the base year fixed at 100.

**Fisher's Ideal Index — Derivation:**

Fisher's Ideal Index is the **geometric mean** of the Laspeyres and Paasche indices:

$$CI_F = \sqrt{CI_L \times CI_P}$$

Substituting the standard definitions:

$$CI_F = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_0)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0)} \times \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_n)}} \times 100$$

**Properties / Tests Satisfied by Fisher's Index:**
1. **Time Reversal Test:** $CI_{01} \times CI_{10} = 1$ ✔
2. **Factor Reversal Test:** $P \times Q$ index = $P$ index $\times$ $Q$ index ✔
3. It is **dimensionless** and lies between Laspeyres and Paasche values.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Laspeyres Index:**

$$\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0) = (350 \times 400) + (50 \times 800) + (8 \times 5000)$$
$$= 1{,}40{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 2{,}20{,}000$$

$$\sum (P_n \cdot q_0) = (460 \times 400) + (80 \times 800) + (12 \times 5000)$$
$$= 1{,}84{,}000 + 64{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 3{,}08{,}000$$

$$CI_L = \frac{3{,}08{,}000}{2{,}20{,}000} \times 100 = 140.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute Paasche Index:**

$$\sum (P_0 \cdot q_n) = (350 \times 380) + (50 \times 850) + (8 \times 4500)$$
$$= 1{,}33{,}000 + 42{,}500 + 36{,}000 = 2{,}11{,}500$$

$$\sum (P_n \cdot q_n) = (460 \times 380) + (80 \times 850) + (12 \times 4500)$$
$$= 1{,}74{,}800 + 68{,}000 + 54{,}000 = 2{,}96{,}800$$

$$CI_P = \frac{2{,}96{,}800}{2{,}11{,}500} \times 100 = 140.38$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer:**
- **Laspeyres Cost Index** = **140.00**
- **Paasche Cost Index** = **140.38**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $q_0$ and $q_n$** — In Laspeyres, **always use $q_0$** in BOTH numerator and denominator. In Paasche, **always use $q_n$** in BOTH numerator and denominator. Mixing them gives wrong marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** — The result is a ratio; multiplication by 100 converts to index form.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in valuation problems** — Always verify whether cost is in Lakhs, Crores, or Rupees. CI is dimensionless, so unit cancellation must be consistent.
> 4. **Forgetting depreciation** — When the problem asks for "present value of building", Cost Index gives the **replacement cost**, not the **depreciated value**. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cost Index**

- **Cost Index (CI)** is a **dimensionless number** that measures the **relative cost** of a standard construction basket over time, with the **base year = 100**.
- The **general formula** is: $CI = \frac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100$.
- The **valuation formula** is: $V_n = V_0 \times \frac{CI_n}{CI_0}$ — the most asked formula in KTU valuation problems.
- **Laspeyres Index** uses **base-year quantities** ($q_0$); formula: $CI_L = \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_0)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_0)} \times 100$.
- **Paasche Index** uses **current-year quantities** ($q_n$); formula: $CI_P = \frac{\sum (P_n \cdot q_n)}{\sum (P_0 \cdot q_n)} \times 100$.
- **Fisher's Ideal Index** = $\sqrt{CI_L \times CI_P}$; satisfies **Time Reversal** and **Factor Reversal** tests.
- **Annual escalation rate**: $r = \left[\left(\frac{CI_n}{CI_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right] \times 100$ percent.
- **Base year is always 100** by international convention; never assume another value.
- **CPI, WPI, BCI, MCI, LCI** are different types of indices; BCI is the most relevant for KTU valuation.
- Cost Index is used in **replacement valuation, insurance, government acquisition, contract escalation, and inflation accounting**.
- The Cost Index gives the **gross replacement cost**; subtract **depreciation** (straight-line or written-down-value) to get **net present value** of an existing building.
- In **14-mark problems**, always show the **formula, substitution, and final answer with units** for full credit.
- **Avoid mixing quantities and prices across years incorrectly** — this is the #1 reason for losing marks in KTU valuation numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
