> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Contract Documents (Brief Description); Bill of Quantity – Typical Format and Use; Items of Works – Identifying Various Items of Work from Drawings; Units of Measurement of Various Materials and Works (focus on RCC Residential Building); General Rules and Methods of Measurement with Reference to Indian Standard Specifications IS 1200

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Contract Documents — The Legal & Technical DNA of a Construction Project

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Contract documents are the **set of written agreements, drawings, and specifications** that legally define the rights, obligations, responsibilities, and scope of work between the **Owner (Client)**, the **Contractor**, and the **Engineer/Architect** for the execution of a construction project. Together, they form the **legally binding bible** of the project.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Contract is like a Marriage Agreement between You and the Building"
Imagine you are ordering a custom-built three-layer chocolate cake from a baker:
- You discuss the **recipe** (Specifications)
- You give the **photo of the cake** (Drawings)
- You finalize the **ingredients list and quantity** (Bill of Quantities)
- You both sign an **agreement** (Contract Agreement)
- You agree on a **delivery timeline** (Conditions of Contract)
- You settle the **price per gram of chocolate** (Schedule of Rates)

If anything goes wrong (cake is too sweet, delivery is late, design is wrong), you refer to the documents. That is exactly what happens in a building project — the contract documents are the **rulebook and the recipe combined**.

### Comprehensive List of Contract Documents (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Sl. No | Document | Purpose | Level of Authority |
|:------:|----------|---------|:------------------:|
| 1 | **Contract Agreement / Articles of Agreement** | The legally signed master document that binds the parties | Highest |
| 2 | **General Conditions of Contract (GCC)** | Standard rules for all works (e.g., CPWD, PWD, NBO, FIDIC) | High |
| 3 | **Special / Particular Conditions of Contract** | Project-specific modifications to GCC | High |
| 4 | **Drawings (Architectural & Structural)** | Graphical representation of the work to be done | High |
| 5 | **Technical Specifications** | Quality, material, and workmanship standards | High |
| 6 | **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** | Itemized list of works with quantities | High |
| 7 | **Schedule of Rates (SOR)** | Unit rates agreed upon for each item | High |
| 8 | **Tender / Bid Document** | Offer submitted by contractor | Medium |
| 9 | **Letter of Acceptance (LOA) / Work Order** | Owner accepts the tender | High |
| 10 | **Addenda / Corrigenda** | Amendments issued during tendering | Supplementary |
| 11 | **Earnest Money Deposit (EMD) / Performance Security** | Financial guarantees | Procedural |

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Precedence (CPWD Standard):** When documents conflict, the order is:
> 1. Contract Agreement → 2. Special Conditions → 3. General Conditions → 4. BOQ → 5. Drawings → 6. Specifications → 7. SOR.

---

## 1.2 Bill of Quantities (BOQ) — The Cost Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** is a document prepared by the **Quantity Surveyor / Engineer** that provides an **itemized list of all works** to be carried out under a contract, along with their **quantities, units, and rates**, leading to the **total contract value**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "BOQ is the Restaurant Menu with Prices"
Think of a buffet restaurant:
- The menu lists every dish (Item of Work)
- Each dish has a serving size (Unit: plate, glass, bowl)
- Each dish has a price (Rate per unit)
- The total bill = Σ(Quantity × Rate)

The BOQ serves the **same function** in a construction project. It enables:
- **Fair comparison** of contractor bids (all quoting for the same quantities)
- **Transparent pricing** — the contractor fills only the RATE column
- **Progress payments** — RA (Running Account) bills are based on BOQ items
- **Variation / Extra item valuation** during execution

---

## 1.3 Items of Works — Reading the Building Like a Story

### Intuitive Analogy — "Deconstructing a Building is Like Deconstructing a Sandwich"
A sandwich has bread (structure), fillings (partitions), mayo (plaster), and a wrapper (finishes). Similarly, every building has:
- **Substructure (Below GL):** Earthwork, Foundation concrete, Plinth beams
- **Superstructure (Above GL):** Columns, Beams, Slabs, Walls
- **Finishes:** Plaster, Painting, Flooring
- **Services:** Electrical, Plumbing, Sanitary
- **Openings:** Doors, Windows, Ventilators
- **Miscellaneous:** Staircase, Parapet, Lintels, Sunshades

Identifying items of work means **reading the drawing and writing a story** of how the building will be constructed, item by item.

---

## 1.4 Units of Measurement — The "Grammar" of Quantity Surveying

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **Unit of Measurement** is a standard reference quantity (m, m², m³, kg, No., quintal, etc.) used to express the **quantity of an item of work** in a uniform manner across the industry, governed primarily by **IS 1200**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "You don't buy milk in spoons; you buy it in litres"
A civil engineer does not say "100 bricks of concrete" — they say **"25 m³ of M25 concrete"**. Standardization ensures that an engineer in Kerala and a contractor in Delhi speak the **same measurement language**.

---

## 1.5 IS 1200 — The Indian Standard Bible for Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 1200: Methods of Measurement of Building and Civil Engineering Works** is a multi-part Indian Standard published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. It prescribes the **uniform methods, units, and rules** for measuring all items of building and civil works across India.

**Key Parts Relevant to KTU Module 1 (RCC Residential Building Focus):**

| Part No. | Title | KTU Relevance |
|:--------:|--------|---------------|
| Part 1 | Earthwork | Foundation, Backfilling |
| Part 2 | Concrete Work | RCC, PCC, Beams, Slabs |
| Part 3 | Brickwork | Walls, Partitions |
| Part 4 | Stone Masonry | Rubble walls (limited) |
| Part 5 | Formwork | Centering, Shuttering |
| Part 6 | Steel Reinforcement | Rebar, Binding wire |
| Part 7 | Hardware (Joinery) | Door/Window fittings |
| Part 8 | Floor & Wall Finishes | Flooring, Dado, Skirting |
| Part 10 | Ceiling & Lining | False ceiling |
| Part 11 | Paving & Tiling | Pathway, Courtyard |
| Part 12 | Plastering & Pointing | Internal/External plaster |
| Part 13 | Whitewashing, Distempering | Painting base |
| Part 14 | Painting, Varnishing | Final finishes |
| Part 15 | Water Supply, Plumbing | Sanitary works |
| Part 16 | Laying of Water & Sewer Pipes | External services |
| Part 17 | Road & Airfield Work | Approach road |
| Part 25 | Piling | Deep foundation |
| Part 27 | Earthwork done by Mechanical Means | Excavator work |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each Contract Document

### 2.1.1 Contract Agreement
- Signed by **Owner** and **Contractor** after the tender is accepted.
- Contains the **names of parties, contract sum, completion period, scope**, and a list of all other documents forming part of the agreement.
- It is the **master document** and overrides all other documents in case of conflict.

### 2.1.2 General Conditions of Contract (GCC)
- Pre-printed standard conditions applicable to **all works** of an organization.
- Common GCCs used in India:
  - **CPWD GCC 2023** (for Central Govt. works)
  - **PWD GCC** (for State Govt. works, e.g., Kerala PWD Manual)
  - **NBO** (National Buildings Organisation)
  - **FIDIC** (international projects)
  - **MoRTH** (for road works)
  - **KSRB / KSEB formats** (Kerala-specific)

**Typical GCC Contents (in order):**
1. Definitions & Interpretations
2. Engineer's Authority and Duties
3. Assignment & Subletting
4. Execution of Works
5. Variations, Extra Items & Deviations
6. Measurement & Payment methods
7. Materials & Workmanship
8. Time Control, Delays, Extension of Time (EoT)
9. Defects Liability Period (DLP) — usually **6 to 24 months**
10. Certificates & Payments (Interim, Final)
11. Termination, Risk, Insurance
12. Arbitration & Dispute Resolution

### 2.1.3 Special / Particular Conditions
- **Project-specific** modifications to GCC (e.g., special rate for sand, monsoon restrictions, local labour laws).
- Always **read with GCC** — they override GCC on that specific point only.

### 2.1.4 Drawings
- **Architectural drawings** — Plan, Elevation, Section, Site Plan, Detailed drawings of doors/windows
- **Structural drawings** — Foundation plan, Column layout, Beam layout, Slab reinforcement details, Staircase details
- **Service drawings** — Electrical, Plumbing, Sanitary
- Drawings bear the **revision number and date**; latest revision supersedes.

### 2.1.5 Specifications
Two broad types:
- **General / Brief Specification** — describes the nature and class of work in short (e.g., "1:2:4 cement concrete using 20mm aggregate")
- **Detailed Specification** — describes every material, proportion, workmanship, testing standard

**Standards Commonly Referenced:**
- **IS Codes** (IS 456 for RCC, IS 800 for Steel, IS 1077 for Bricks)
- **CPWD Specifications** (Vol. I & II)
- **MORTH Specifications** (roads)
- **PWD / State SOR Specifications**

### 2.1.6 Bill of Quantities (BOQ) — The Heart of Cost

**Standard BOQ Column Structure (KTU Exam Standard):**

| Item No. | Description of Item | Unit | Quantity | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|:--------:|---------------------|:----:|:--------:|:---------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation in foundation trenches in ordinary soil, depth not exceeding 1.5m, including dressing & stacking | m³ | 125.00 | 350.00 | 43,750.00 |
| 2 | P.C.C. 1:4:8 in foundation | m³ | 18.50 | 5,200.00 | 96,200.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Description must be complete.** A BOQ item with vague description like "RCC work" is **legally invalid** and causes disputes. CPWD format requires: **"RCC M25 (1:1:2) using 20mm graded crushed stone aggregate in columns, including centering, shuttering, vibrating, curing and finishing (rate excluding steel reinforcement)"**.

---

## 2.2 General Rules of Measurement (IS 1200 — Foundational Principles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Golden Rules of IS 1200 (Must Memorize for KTU):**

1. **Unit Consistency Rule:** All measurements must be in standard units (m, m², m³, kg, quintal, No., LS).
2. **Net Measurement Rule:** All measurements are **net** (deductions for openings > 0.1 m² for plaster; > 0.5 m² for concrete; > 0.1 m² for brickwork).
3. **Linear Measurement Rule:** Length is measured **nearest to 0.01 m** (1 cm). Wall thickness to **0.005 m** (5 mm).
4. **Area Measurement Rule:** Area to **0.01 m²**. Openings **> 0.1 m²** are deducted.
5. **Volume Measurement Rule:** Volume to **0.01 m³**. Cubical content calculated from **plan dimensions** (no extra for beam offsets in slab unless stated).
6. **Weight Measurement Rule:** Steel reinforcement measured by **length × unit weight (kg/m)** from **bar bending schedule**, not from site weighment.
7. **Provisional Sum Rule:** Items not measurable are taken as **"Provisional Sum"** or **"Lump Sum (LS)"** with backup analysis.
8. **Stack Measurement Rule:** Bricks, aggregate, sand measured in **stacks** with standard deductions for voids.
9. **Description Priority Rule:** Description in BOQ takes precedence over drawings if conflicting.
10. **Wastage Rule:** No separate payment for **wastage, cutting, binding wire, spacer blocks, etc.** — all included in the item rate.

---

## 2.3 Units of Measurement for an RCC Residential Building (IS 1200 — Comprehensive Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important table** for KTU Module 1. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Sl. No | Item of Work | Standard Unit | IS 1200 Part | Notes / Measurement Logic |
|:------:|--------------|:-------------:|:------------:|---------------------------|
| **A** | **EARTHWORK & SUBSTRUCTURE** | | | |
| 1 | Site clearance, jungle clearance | m² / LS | Part 1 | LS for small jobs |
| 2 | Excavation in foundation | m³ | Part 1 | Net trench volume, depth measured from GL |
| 3 | Backfilling with excavated earth | m³ | Part 1 | Compacted volume |
| 4 | Filling with sand/granular material | m³ | Part 1 | Loose/compacted specified |
| 5 | Anti-termite treatment | m² | Part 1 | Plinth area |
| 6 | PCC (1:4:8 or 1:3:6) in foundation | m³ | Part 2 | Plan × depth |

| 64 | Cable / Wire | m | — | |
| 65 | Light fixtures / Fans | No. | — | |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Logic Tables

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference — Key Formulas for Measurements**

| Purpose | Formula / Logic | Unit |
|---------|-----------------|:----:|
| Earthwork in trench | $V = L \times B \times D$ (with sloped/benched sides if specified) | m³ |
| Backfill volume | $V_{bf} = V_{excavation} - V_{concrete\ and\ masonry\ in\ trench}$ | m³ |
| Sand filling (PL) | $V = \text{Area at PL} \times \text{thickness}$ | m³ |
| Damp-proof course (DPC) | $A = \text{wall length} \times \text{wall thickness (or width)} \times \text{number of layers}$ | m² |
| Formwork (contact area) | $A = \text{Contact area of form with wet concrete}$ | m² |
| Reinforcement weight | $W = \sum_{i} (L_i \times w_i \times n_i)$ where $w_i = 0.006165 d_i^2$ (kg/m, d in mm) | kg |
| Standard hook, bend & lap deductions | As per IS 1200 Part 6 & IS 2502 | kg |
| Concrete volume in beam | $V = b \times (D - \text{slab thk}) \times L$ | m³ |
| Concrete volume in column | $V = b \times D \times H$ (no deduction for beam-haunch) | m³ |
| Concrete volume in slab | $V = L \times B \times t$ (deduct column heads if specified) | m³ |
| Plaster (internal wall) | $A = (\text{length} \times \text{height}) - \text{openings} + \text{jambs/lintels/soffits}$ | m² |
| Wall paint area | Same as plaster + ceiling area for whitewash | m² |
| Tile / marble area | $A = \text{Floor plan area} - \text{note areas (skirting paid separately)}$ | m² |
| Number of bricks per m³ (modular) | $\dfrac{1.0}{0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10} = 500$ bricks/m³ (with mortar 10mm) | No. |
| Mortar quantity in brickwork | $V_m = V_{gross} \times 0.30$ (for 10mm joints, ≈25–35%) | m³ |
| Centering in slab (props) | $A = \text{soffit area} + \text{edge beam soffits}$ | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deduction Thresholds (IS 1200 — Memorize):**
> - Plaster / Painting: openings **> 0.1 m²** are deducted
> - Brickwork: openings **> 0.1 m²** are deducted
> - Concrete: openings **> 0.5 m²** are deducted
> - Beam/slab concrete: **no deduction** for column heads (< 0.5 m²) unless specified
> - Steel: **no deduction** for chair bars, spacer bars, binding wire — included in rate

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Civil Engineering

| Application Area | How This Knowledge is Used |
|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Tendering & Bidding** | Contractor uses BOQ to quote — wrong units = wrong quote = loss |
| **Rate Analysis** | Engineer derives rate of M25 concrete by knowing cement, sand, aggregate quantities (in m³ or kg) |
| **RA Bills & Payment** | Monthly progress bills are based on **measured quantities** of BOQ items |
| **Variation Orders** | Extra items are valued using same units & methods as original BOQ |
| **Dispute Resolution** | Arbitrators rely on IS 1200 to settle measurement conflicts |
| **Software (CCS Candy, Cost-X, PlanSwift)** | All take-off software follows IS 1200 logic |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Methods & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Method: Identifying Items of Work from Drawings (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Step KTU Method to "Read" a Residential Building Drawing:**

### Step 1: Read the Title Block & General Notes
- Project name, scale, drawing number, revision, date
- Foundation depth, GL/FFL levels, soil bearing capacity notes

### Step 2: Identify Substructure (Below GL)
- Footing type: Isolated, Combined, Raft, Pile
- Plinth beam size & depth
- DPC details
- **BOQ items generated:** Excavation, PCC, RCC footing, Plinth beam, Brickwork up to plinth, DPC, Backfilling, Anti-termite

### Step 3: Identify Superstructure (Above GL to Roof)
- Column schedule (size, reinforcement)
- Beam layout (sizes at each floor)
- Slab thickness, type (solid, flat, waffle)
- Wall thickness (external & internal)
- **BOQ items generated:** RCC columns, RCC beams, RCC slab, Brickwork, Lintels, Chajja/sunshade

### Step 4: Identify Openings, Staircase & Misc.
- Door sizes & numbers (D1, D2, D3...)
- Window sizes & numbers (W1, W2, W3...)
- Ventilators (V1, V2)
- Staircase: waist slab, landing, steps, handrail
- **BOQ items generated:** Doors, Windows, Ventilators, Grills, Staircase RCC, Handrail, Railing

### Step 5: Identify Finishes & Services
- Internal plaster, external plaster, ceiling plaster
- Painting system (putty → primer → emulsion)
- Flooring type & thickness
- Electrical points (light, fan, switch)
- Plumbing fixtures & pipes
- **BOQ items generated:** Plaster, Putty, Paint, Tile/marble, Conduits, Wires, Fixtures, Pipes

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — BOQ for a Single-Room Building (KTU Standard 7-Mark Problem)

**Given Data:**
- Single room, internal dimensions: **4 m × 3.5 m**, wall thickness **300 mm (external)**, 200 mm (internal)
- Plinth height: **0.6 m above GL**
- Floor finish: **40 mm PCC 1:2:4 + 25 mm vitrified tile**
- Roof: **RCC slab 120 mm thick** with 15 mm plaster at soffit
- Height of room: **3.0 m** (floor to slab soffit)
- One door **1.0 m × 2.1 m**, two windows **1.2 m × 1.2 m** each
- Foundation excavation: **1.0 m wide × 1.0 m deep** below plinth (assume wall is 300 mm external, PCC offset 100mm each side ⇒ footing width = 500 mm, but here excavate 1.0 m for simplicity)
- Mortar for brickwork: 1:6
- Cement plaster internal: 12 mm 1:6; External: 15 mm 1:4; Ceiling: 6 mm
- Wall paint: Acrylic emulsion
- Assume **2 coats of paint**
- Reinforcement in slab: assume **80 kg/m³ of concrete**

**Compute the BOQ for the following items (a typical 7-mark KTU sub-question):**
1. Earthwork in excavation
2. PCC in foundation
3. First-class brickwork in superstructure
4. Internal plaster
5. RCC slab concrete

---

### Solution 3.2.1 — Earthwork in Excavation

> **Logic:** Volume of trench = Length × Breadth × Depth

#### Step A: Total wall length (centreline method)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Centre-to-centre length of long walls} &= \text{Internal length} + \text{wall thickness} \\
&= 4.0 + 0.3 = 4.3 \text{ m} \\
\text{Centre-to-centre length of short walls} &= \text{Internal width} + \text{wall thickness} \\
&= 3.5 + 0.3 = 3.8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B: Total trench length (4 walls)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 2 \times (4.3 + 3.8) = 2 \times 8.1 = 16.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step C: Volume of excavation

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{exc} &= L \times B \times D = 16.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \\
V_{exc} &= \mathbf{16.20 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution 3.2.2 — PCC in Foundation

#### Step A: Plan area of footing (assume 0.5 m wide PCC, 0.1 m thick)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{PCC} &= L_{total} \times B_{PCC} = 16.2 \times 0.5 = 8.10 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{PCC} &= 8.10 \times 0.10 = \mathbf{0.81 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution 3.2.3 — First-Class Brickwork in Superstructure

> **Logic:** Wall length × height × thickness (Net — no opening > 0.1 m² deduction here as per KTU exam data — we'll compute gross first then deduct openings).

#### Step A: Total wall length (centreline)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{wall} &= 16.2 \text{ m (same as excavation length)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B: Height of brickwork

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{brick} &= \text{Plinth} + \text{Slab} + \text{Floor finish} \text{ ... use room height} \\
H_{brick} &= 3.0 \text{ m (from FFL to slab soffit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Correction:** Superstructure starts at GL. From GL to FFL is plinth (0.6 m). Then from FFL to slab soffit is 3.0 m. Total brickwork = 0.6 + 3.0 = **3.6 m** (since plinth also has brickwork).

#### Step C: Volume of brickwork (gross)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{gross} &= L \times H \times T_{wall} = 16.2 \times 3.6 \times 0.30 \\
&= 17.496 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step D: Deduction for openings (per IS 1200: deduct > 0.1 m²)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{door} &= 1.0 \times 2.1 = 2.10 \text{ m}^2 \quad (> 0.1 \text{ m}^2, \text{deduct}) \\
A_{windows} &= 2 \times (1.2 \times 1.2) = 2.88 \text{ m}^2 \quad (> 0.1 \text{ m}^2, \text{deduct}) \\
V_{deduct} &= (2.10 + 2.88) \times 0.30 = 4.98 \times 0.30 = 1.494 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step E: Net brickwork

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{net} &= 17.496 - 1.494 = \mathbf{16.00 \text{ m}^3 \ (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution 3.2.4 — Internal Plaster (12 mm 1:6)

> **Logic:** Plaster Area = Wall area (both sides) – Openings + Jambs (approx. ignored for simplicity)

#### Step A: Internal wall area (4 walls, both sides = 2×)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{internal} &= 16.2 \text{ m (centrelines) — but subtract wall thickness for inner face}
\end{aligned}
$$

> For simplicity (centreline method), use full length for both faces; subtract openings once.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{gross\ internal\ wall} &= 2 \times (L \times H) = 2 \times 16.2 \times 3.0 = 97.20 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B: Subtract openings (each opening subtracts once from plaster area)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{deduct} &= A_{door} + A_{windows} = 2.10 + 2.88 = 4.98 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step C: Add jambs of openings (per IS 1200, jambs added = perimeter × width of wall)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Door jambs} &= 2 \times (1.0 + 2.1) \times 0.15 = 0.93 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Window jambs} &= 2 \times 2 \times (1.2 + 1.2) \times 0.15 = 1.44 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Sill of window} &= 2 \times 1.2 \times 0.15 = 0.36 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Lintel soffit} &= (1.0 + 2 \times 1.2) \times 0.15 = 0.51 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{add} &= 0.93 + 1.44 + 0.36 + 0.51 = 3.24 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step D: Net internal plaster area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{net} &= 97.20 - 4.98 + 3.24 = \mathbf{95.46 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution 3.2.5 — RCC Slab Concrete (M25)

> **Logic:** Volume = L × B × thickness (no deduction for column heads < 0.5 m²)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{slab} &= \text{External dimensions of room} \\
&= (4.0 + 0.3 + 0.3) \times (3.5 + 0.3 + 0.3) \\
&= 4.6 \times 4.1 = 18.86 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{slab} &= A \times t = 18.86 \times 0.12 = \mathbf{2.26 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step F: Reinforcement in slab (separate item, for reference)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{steel} &= V_{slab} \times 80 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 2.26 \times 80 = 180.8 \text{ kg} \approx \mathbf{181 \text{ kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Method — Deduction Thresholds Matrix (KTU Table)

| Item of Work | Deduction for Openings (per IS 1200) | Add for Jambs/Soffits | Unit |
|--------------|:-------------------------------------:|:---------------------:|:----:|
| Brickwork in cement mortar | > 0.1 m² | No add | m³ |
| Plastering (cement) | > 0.1 m² | Yes (perimeter × wall thk) | m² |
| Painting / Distemper | > 0.1 m² | Yes (similar to plaster) | m² |
| Concrete (RCC/PCC) | > 0.5 m² | No add | m³ |
| Formwork | No deduction for openings | No add | m² |
| Tiling / Marble | No deduction (net laid area) | Skirting separate | m² |
| Steel reinforcement | No deduction | No add | kg |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Centering & Shuttering (Formwork) Area Calculation

> **Given:** Same building; calculate formwork for slab, beams, columns.

### Slab Soffit Formwork
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{slab\ soffit} &= 18.86 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (same as slab plan area)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Beam Formwork (4 perimeter beams + 1 internal beam say)
Assume 4 perimeter beams, size 230 mm × 350 mm (overall), spanning room.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{beam\ soffit} &= L_{beam} \times b_{beam\ soffit} = 4 \times 4.1 \times 0.23 = 3.77 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{beam\ sides} &= 2 \times (D_{beam} - t_{slab}) \times L = 2 \times (0.35 - 0.12) \times (4 \times 4.1) \\
&= 2 \times 0.23 \times 16.4 = 7.54 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{beam\ total} &= 3.77 + 7.54 = 11.31 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Column Formwork (4 corner columns, 300×300 mm, height 3.0 m)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{col} &= 4 \times (0.3 \times 0.3) \times 4 = \text{No, surface area} \\
A_{col} &= 4 \times (4 \times 0.3 \times 3.0) = 4 \times 3.6 = 14.4 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Total Formwork

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total\ formwork} &= 18.86 + 11.31 + 14.4 = \mathbf{44.57 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Method — Rate Analysis of M25 RCC (Conceptual)

> **Note:** Full rate analysis belongs to Module 2, but here is the logic sketch.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material cost} &= \text{Cement} + \text{Sand} + \text{Coarse aggregate} + \text{Water} \\
\text{Labour cost} &= \text{Mason + Helper + Vibrator operator} \\
\text{Plant \& Machinery} &= \text{Mixer + Vibrator + Hoist} \\
\text{Overhead \& Profit} &= 15\% \text{ to } 25\% \\
\text{Rate per m}^3 &= \text{Sum of all above}
\end{aligned}
$$

> For 1 m³ of M25 (1:1:2):
> - Cement = **400 kg** (8 bags)
> - Sand = **0.42 m³**
> - 20 mm aggregate = **0.85 m³**
> - Wastage @ 5% added

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 Marks Each):**
> - ❌ Forgetting to **deduct openings > 0.1 m²** in brickwork / plaster / paint.
> - ❌ Forgetting to **add jambs, sills, soffits** in plaster / paint (IS 1200 mandates this).
> - ❌ Using **wrong unit** (e.g., plaster in m³ instead of m²).
> - ❌ Confusing **gross and net quantities** — always write "Net" or "Gross" in answers.
> - ❌ Writing "RCC" as a single item without specifying **grade, aggregate size, work type** (column/beam/slab).
> - ❌ Not mentioning **centring & shuttering separately** for RCC items.
> - ❌ In BOQ, writing **Rate** as a derived value when the question asks only for Quantity.
> - ❌ Using **running metre** for brickwork (it is cubic metre, m³) — major mistake.
> - ❌ Forgetting the **emulsion / paint system** (putty + primer + paint) has 3 separate cost lines.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any six documents that together constitute a complete set of contract documents for a building project. Briefly state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 50 words each item, 1 mark for any 2 correct):**

1. **Contract Agreement** — Master legal document signed by Owner & Contractor binding them to the terms.
2. **General Conditions of Contract (GCC)** — Standard rules applicable to all works (e.g., CPWD GCC).
3. **Special Conditions** — Project-specific modifications or additions to the GCC.
4. **Drawings** — Architectural & structural plans, elevations, sections, and details.
5. **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** — Itemized list of works with quantities and rates.
6. **Specifications** — Quality and workmanship standards for materials and construction.
7. **Schedule of Rates (SOR)** — Pre-approved unit rates for various items.
8. **Tender Notice & Letter of Acceptance** — Invitation and acceptance of bid.

*(Any 6 well-explained items = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the standard unit of measurement (per IS 1200) for each of the following items:**
**(a) Earthwork in excavation (b) Cement plaster (c) Steel reinforcement (d) Vitrified tile flooring (e) Half-brick masonry (f) Door shutters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 mark each):**

| Item | Standard Unit | IS 1200 Part |
|------|:-------------:|:------------:|
| (a) Earthwork in excavation | **m³ (cubic metre)** | Part 1 |
| (b) Cement plaster | **m² (square metre)** | Part 12 |
| (c) Steel reinforcement | **kg (kilogram) or quintal** | Part 6 |
| (d) Vitrified tile flooring | **m² (square metre)** | Part 8 |
| (e) Half-brick masonry | **m² (square metre)** | Part 3 |
| (f) Door shutters | **No. (Number / each)** | Part 7 |

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **typical format and use of a Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** for a residential building project. Discuss the role of the Quantity Surveyor in its preparation and how it facilitates tendering and contract management. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a single-storey residential building with the following data, prepare a detailed **BOQ** showing the **item description, unit, and quantity** for the following items: **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Data:**
> - Plan: External **8.0 m × 6.0 m**, wall thickness **300 mm** (all walls)
> - One door: **1.2 m × 2.1 m**; Three windows: **1.5 m × 1.2 m** each
> - Plinth height above GL: **0.6 m**; Room height: **3.0 m** (FFL to slab soffit)
> - Foundation trench: **0.8 m wide × 1.0 m deep**
> - PCC offset: 100 mm on each side of wall ⇒ **PCC width = 0.5 m**, **thickness = 0.15 m**
> - Roof: RCC slab **120 mm thick** (plan area = external plan)
> - Internal plaster 12 mm 1:6 (all 4 walls + ceiling)
> - Floor finish: 40 mm PCC 1:2:4 + Vitrified tile
> - **Items required in BOQ:**
>   1. Earthwork in excavation
>   2. PCC 1:4:8 in foundation
>   3. First-class brickwork in 1:6 mortar (plinth + superstructure)
>   4. Internal plaster (walls only, ignore ceiling for simplicity)
>   5. Vitrified tile flooring
>   6. RCC roof slab concrete (M25)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Format and Use of BOQ (7 Marks)

A **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** is a structured document prepared by the Quantity Surveyor that lists every item of work required to complete a project. Its typical format contains the following columns:

> **Columns:** Item No. | Description | Unit | Quantity | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹)

**Key Features:**
1. **Standard Format** — Follows IS 1200 or CPWD/MoRTH format.
2. **Detailed Description** — Each item description includes material, proportion, location, and method (e.g., *"RCC M25 in columns using 20mm aggregate including centering, shuttering, vibrating, curing"*).
3. **Units** — Standard units per IS 1200 (m, m², m³, kg, No., LS).
4. **Quantities** — Computed by measurement from drawings using centreline, long wall-short wall, or other methods.
5. **Rates** — Quoted by contractors; total amount = Quantity × Rate.

**Uses of BOQ:**
- **Tendering** — Allows fair, apples-to-apples comparison of contractor bids.
- **Contract Sum** — Total of BOQ amount becomes the contract value.
- **Progress Payment** — Running Account (RA) bills are based on measured BOQ items.
- **Variation Orders** — Extra/substituted items are valued using BOQ rates.
- **Disputes** — Acts as the reference document during arbitration.
- **Cost Control** — Helps client track expenditure against budget.

**Role of Quantity Surveyor (QS):**
- Reads drawings, performs quantity take-off, prepares BOQ.
- Advises on tendering strategy, contract type, and risk allocation.
- Certifies contractor bills, prepares variation statements.
- Conducts rate analysis for new items.

---

#### Part (b) — BOQ Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Centreline Lengths (4 walls)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{long} &= 8.0 + 0.3 = 8.3 \text{ m} \\
L_{short} &= 6.0 + 0.3 = 6.3 \text{ m} \\
L_{total} &= 2 \times (8.3 + 6.3) = 29.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Item-wise Computation**

**(i) Earthwork in excavation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{exc} &= L_{total} \times B \times D = 29.2 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 = \mathbf{23.36 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) PCC 1:4:8 in foundation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{PCC} &= 29.2 \times 0.5 = 14.6 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{PCC} &= 14.6 \times 0.15 = \mathbf{2.19 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) First-class brickwork in 1:6 mortar (plinth + superstructure)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{total} &= 0.6 + 3.0 = 3.6 \text{ m} \\
V_{gross} &= 29.2 \times 3.6 \times 0.30 = 31.536 \text{ m}^3 \\
A_{openings} &= (1.2 \times 2.1) + 3 \times (1.5 \times 1.2) = 2.52 + 5.40 = 7.92 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{deduct} &= 7.92 \times 0.30 = 2.376 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{net} &= 31.536 - 2.376 = \mathbf{29.16 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Internal plaster (12 mm 1:6) — 4 walls, both sides**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{wall\ gross\ both\ sides} &= 2 \times (L \times H) = 2 \times 29.2 \times 3.0 = 175.2 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{deduct\ openings} &= 7.92 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{add\ jambs\ sills\ soffits} &\approx 0.10 \times \text{Perimeter openings} \\
\text{Perimeter of openings} &= 2(1.2+2.1) + 3 \times 2(1.5+1.2) = 6.6 + 16.2 = 22.8 \text{ m} \\
A_{add} &= 22.8 \times 0.15 = 3.42 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{net\ plaster} &= 175.2 - 7.92 + 3.42 = \mathbf{170.70 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Vitrified tile flooring**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{floor} &= \text{Internal area} = 8.0 \times 6.0 = 48.00 \text{ m}^2 \\
&\text{Or external minus wall} = (8.0 - 2 \times 0.15) \times (6.0 - 2 \times 0.15) \\
&= 7.7 \times 5.7 = \mathbf{43.89 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Either is acceptable; KTU generally accepts internal dimensions; net = 43.89 m²)*

**(vi) RCC M25 in roof slab**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{slab} &= 8.0 \times 6.0 = 48.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{slab} &= 48.0 \times 0.12 = \mathbf{5.76 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Final BOQ Format (Part of Answer)

| Item No. | Description | Unit | Quantity |
|:--------:|-------------|:----:|:--------:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation in foundation trenches in ordinary soil, depth ≤ 1.5 m, including dressing & stacking | m³ | 23.36 |
| 2 | P.C.C. 1:4:8 in foundation using 40 mm aggregate, including compaction & curing | m³ | 2.19 |
| 3 | First-class brickwork in foundation & superstructure in 1:6 cement mortar | m³ | 29.16 |
| 4 | 12 mm cement plaster in 1:6 to internal walls, including hacking, curing | m² | 170.70 |
| 5 | Vitrified tile flooring (600×600 mm), 8–10 mm thick, with cement-based adhesive, grouting, cleaning | m² | 43.89 |
| 6 | R.C.C. M25 (1:1:2) in roof slab using 20 mm crushed stone aggregate, including centering, shuttering, vibrating, curing (excluding steel) | m³ | 5.76 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the **general rules of measurement as per IS 1200** for the following items: **(7 Marks)**
>   1. Earthwork in excavation
>   2. Cement plastering
>   3. Steel reinforcement
>   4. Concrete work
>
> **(b)** A reinforced concrete slab of size **5.0 m × 4.0 m × 0.12 m** is reinforced with **10 mm dia bars at 150 mm c/c (bottom)** in both directions, and **8 mm dia distribution bars at 200 mm c/c (top)**. Calculate the **total quantity of steel reinforcement** in kg. Also mention the relevant IS code. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — General Rules of Measurement per IS 1200 (7 Marks)

**(1) Earthwork in Excavation (IS 1200 Part 1):**
- Unit: **m³**
- Measured to **nearest 0.01 m³**
- Length & breadth to **nearest 0.01 m**; depth to **nearest 0.05 m**
- For trenches, length is **centreline length of wall**; breadth = **specified width**; depth = **average depth from GL to bottom of excavation**
- **No extra** for working space unless specified separately
- For stepped / sloped sides, average area × length is taken
- Backfill measured **net of masonry/concrete volume** in trench
- Disposal of surplus earth is **separate item** (lead specified)

**(2) Cement Plastering (IS 1200 Part 12):**
- Unit: **m²**
- Measured to **nearest 0.01 m²**
- Thickness specified (6 mm, 12 mm, 15 mm, 20 mm)
- Mortar proportion specified
- **Deductions** for openings **> 0.1 m²** (doors, windows, ventilators)
- **Additions** for jambs, sills, soffits, reveals — perimeter of opening × wall thickness
- Deductions for ends of beams, columns (if > 0.1 m²)
- Ceiling plaster measured separately

**(3) Steel Reinforcement (IS 1200 Part 6):**
- Unit: **kg** (or quintal, MT)
- Measured by **theoretical weight from bar bending schedule (BBS)** — not site weighment
- Weight calculated as: $W = L \times 0.006165 \times d^2$ (d in mm, L in m, W in kg)
- Length includes **straight bars, hooked bars, bent-up bars, laps**
- **Deductions** for hooks, bends per **IS 2502** (Table 5: bend deductions, hook deductions, lap deductions)
- Binding wire, chairs, spacers — **NOT paid separately** (included in rate)
- Different diameters measured separately and added

**(4) Concrete Work (IS 1200 Part 2):**
- Unit: **m³**
- Measured to nearest 0.01 m³
- **Net structural sizes** — no extra for bulking or wastage
- **Deductions** for openings **> 0.5 m²** (in slab, wall)
- **No deduction** for:
  - Volume occupied by steel (already deducted in some specs)
  - Reinforcement up to 1% of cross-section
  - Column heads < 0.5 m²
- Grade, aggregate size, workability (slump) specified
- **Centering & shuttering measured separately** in **m²** of contact area
- Items in concrete work: foundation, columns, beams, slabs, chajja, lintels, staircase — each is a **separate item**

---

#### Part (b) — Steel Quantity Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Slab: 5.0 m × 4.0 m × 0.12 m
- Main bars: 10 mm dia @ 150 mm c/c, bottom, both directions
- Distribution bars: 8 mm dia @ 200 mm c/c, top

**Step 1: Number of 10 mm bars (along 4.0 m direction, spaced at 150 mm c/c in 5.0 m direction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{10} &= \frac{\text{Width}}{\text{Spacing}} + 1 = \frac{5.0}{0.15} + 1 = 33.33 + 1 = 34 \text{ bars}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Length of each 10 mm bar = 4.0 m** (with hook development; for calculation, use clear span + hooks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Add hooks: } &2 \times 9d = 2 \times 9 \times 10 \text{ mm} = 0.18 \text{ m} \\
L_{10} &= 4.0 + 0.18 = 4.18 \text{ m (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total length of 10 mm bars in one direction (34 bars):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10,total} &= 34 \times 4.18 = 142.12 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Number of 10 mm bars in the other direction (along 5.0 m direction, spaced at 150 mm c/c in 4.0 m direction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{10'} &= \frac{4.0}{0.15} + 1 = 27.67 + 1 = 28 \text{ bars} \\
L_{10'} &= 5.0 + 0.18 = 5.18 \text{ m} \\
L_{10',total} &= 28 \times 5.18 = 145.04 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total length of 10 mm bars**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10,grand} &= 142.12 + 145.04 = 287.16 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Weight of 10 mm bars**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{10} &= L \times 0.006165 \times d^2 = 287.16 \times 0.006165 \times 10^2 \\
&= 287.16 \times 0.6165 = \mathbf{177.05 \text{ kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Distribution bars (8 mm @ 200 mm c/c, top, both directions)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_8 &= \frac{5.0}{0.20} + 1 = 26 \text{ bars (along 4 m)} \\
L_8 &= 4.0 + 0.144 = 4.144 \text{ m (hook 9d = 9×8 = 72mm = 0.072m)} \\
L_{8a} &= 26 \times 4.144 = 107.74 \text{ m} \\
N_8' &= \frac{4.0}{0.20} + 1 = 21 \text{ bars} \\
L_8' &= 5.0 + 0.144 = 5.144 \text{ m} \\
L_{8b} &= 21 \times 5.144 = 108.02 \text{ m} \\
L_{8,grand} &= 107.74 + 108.02 = 215.76 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Weight of 8 mm bars**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_8 &= 215.76 \times 0.006165 \times 8^2 = 215.76 \times 0.006165 \times 64 \\
&= 215.76 \times 0.39456 = \mathbf{85.13 \text{ kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Total Steel Quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= W_{10} + W_8 = 177.05 + 85.13 = \mathbf{262.18 \text{ kg}} \\
&\approx \mathbf{262 \text{ kg}} \approx 2.62 \text{ quintals}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relevant IS Codes:**
- **IS 1200 (Part 6)** — Methods of measurement of steel reinforcement
- **IS 2502** — Code of practice for bending and fixing of bars
- **IS 456** — Plain and reinforced concrete code of practice
- **IS 1786** — High strength deformed steel bars (Fe500)

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — KTU Module 1 Quick Revision**

- ✅ **Contract Documents** = Legal + Technical + Commercial set; **Contract Agreement is supreme**.
- ✅ **BOQ Format:** Item No. → Description → Unit → Quantity → Rate → Amount.
- ✅ **Order of Precedence:** Agreement > Special Conditions > General Conditions > BOQ > Drawings > Specifications > SOR.
- ✅ **IS 1200** is the **Indian Standard for measurement**; relevant parts: **1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16**.
- ✅ **Units (must memorize):**
  - **m³** — Earthwork, Concrete, Brickwork, Backfill
  - **m²** — Plaster, Paint, Tile, Marble, Formwork, DPC
  - **m** — Pipe, Skirting, Railing, Handrail
  - **kg** — Steel Reinforcement (theoretical, from BBS)
  - **No.** — Doors, Windows, Fixtures, Manholes
  - **LS** — Demolition, Site Clearance, Misc Repairs
- ✅ **Deduction Thresholds:** Brickwork, Plaster, Paint → **> 0.1 m²**; Concrete → **> 0.5 m²**.
- ✅ **Plaster = Net wall area – openings + jambs + sills + soffits** (perimeter × wall thk).
- ✅ **Steel weight formula:** $W = L \times 0.006165 \times d^2$ (d in mm, L in m).
- ✅ **Binding wire, chairs, spacers** = **NOT paid separately**.
- ✅ **Formwork measured in m²** = contact area of form with wet concrete.
- ✅ **500 bricks per m³** (modular size 200×100×100 mm with 10 mm mortar).
- ✅ **No deduction** for: column heads < 0.5 m² in slab, openings < 0.1 m², steel volume in concrete, binding wire.
- ✅ **QS (Quantity Surveyor) role:** Take-off, BOQ, Rate analysis, Bill certification, Variation, Dispute resolution.
- ✅ **5 steps to read a drawing:** Substructure → Superstructure → Openings → Staircase → Finishes → Services.
- ✅ **Defects Liability Period (DLP):** Usually **6 to 24 months** post-completion.
- ✅ **Earthwork in trench** = L (centreline) × B (specified) × D (average depth).
- ✅ **Brickwork height** = Plinth height + Room height (from FFL to slab soffit).
- ✅ **Concrete in slab** = External plan × thickness (no deduction for column heads < 0.5 m²).
- ✅ **Tile area** = Internal floor area (or external − wall thickness × 2, both ways).
- ✅ **IS 2502** is companion code for **hook, bend, lap deductions** in steel BBS.
- ✅ **GCC** (General Conditions) — pre-printed; **SCC** (Special Conditions) — project-specific.
- ✅ **GCC sources:** CPWD, PWD Kerala, NBO, FIDIC, MoRTH, KSRB.
- ✅ **RA (Running Account) Bill** = monthly progress payment based on measured BOQ items.
- ✅ **Provisional Sum** = amount kept aside for items not yet quantifiable; **Lump Sum (LS)** = for non-measurable items.
- ✅ **Lintel, Chajja, Sunshade, Coping** = measured in **m³** of concrete (RCC/PCC), or **No.** if uniform.
- ✅ **DPC (Damp Proof Course)** = **m²**; thickness (typically 40 mm) and material (cement concrete, bitumen) specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to the Use of CPWD Schedule of Rates as per Latest DSR and Analysis of Rates as per Latest DAR; Specifications – General Specification of All Items of a Residential Building; Detailed Specifications (CPWD Specifications) of Major Items of Work like Earthwork Excavation in Foundation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to the Use of CPWD Schedule of Rates as per Latest DSR and Analysis of Rates as per Latest DAR; Specifications – General Specification of All Items of a Residential Building; Detailed Specifications (CPWD Specifications) of Major Items of Work like Earthwork Excavation in Foundation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CPWD Schedule of Rates (DSR)

### Formal Definition
The **Central Public Works Department (CPWD) Schedule of Rates (SoR)** — popularly called the **DSR (Delhi Schedule of Rates)** — is the **standardized, government-approved rate book** published annually by the **CPWD, Government of India**, Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs. It provides the **uniform unit rates** of all items of construction work (civil, electrical, mechanical) carried out across India. The current authoritative edition is the **CPWD DSR 2023 (with corrigenda up to the latest published date)**, which supersedes the DSR 2021 and DSR 2022 versions for all centrally sponsored works.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The KTU Board Examiner expects the student to be conversant with the **latest DSR year** as it is the only legally admissible document for valuing government works. Always state the year of the DSR you are quoting from in the answer script.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Rate Menu" of Government Construction
Think of the DSR as the **"fixed price menu card" of a national restaurant chain (CPWD)** that operates across India. Just as a McDonald's menu in Delhi and Mumbai charges the **same price for a McVeggie**, the DSR ensures that the cost of digging 1 cubic metre of earth in Trivandrum is **theoretically the same** as in Delhi — the rates are standardized. However, a **regional cost index (RIICO / State SoR multiplier)** may be applied to adjust for local market realities, much like a city surcharge on a menu.

### Components Tabulated in the DSR
- **Basic Rate of Item** (per unit, e.g., ₹/m³, ₹/m², ₹/kg)
- **Material, Labour and Machinery split** (in percentage)
- **Item Code / Sub-head number** (e.g., 2.0, 2.8.1)
- **Unit of measurement** (m³, m², m, kg, no., L.S.)
- **Brief Specification reference** (linking to CPWD Specifications Vol. I & II)

---

## 1.2 Analysis of Rates (DAR)

### Formal Definition
The **Delhi Analysis of Rates (DAR)** — published by the **CPWD** under the title *"Analysis of Rates for Delhi (DAR)"* — is the **complementary parent document** to the DSR. While the DSR gives the **final rate**, the **DAR** provides the **detailed derivation** (the "behind-the-scenes" math) showing how the final rate was computed in terms of **quantities of materials, labour-hours, machinery-hours, lead distances, wastage factors, contractor's profit and overheads** for each item. The current edition is the **DAR 2023** (replacing DAR 2021).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DAR matters in KTU exams:** Even when a question gives you a problem where a "rate" is unknown, the examiner expects you to compute it by mimicking the **DAR method of rate analysis** — i.e., the same step-by-step cost build-up used by the CPWD to derive its DSR figures.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Book"
If the DSR is the **menu price**, the **DAR is the recipe book** the chef uses. It tells the chef (the estimator) that to produce 1 m³ of brickwork, you need **1.1 m³ of bricks (with wastage)**, **0.25 m³ of mortar**, **0.45 mazdoor-days**, etc. Knowing the recipe allows the chef to **re-cost the dish** when ingredient (material) prices change — this is exactly how a private estimator updates a rate when market rates deviate from the DSR.

---

## 1.3 Specifications — The Quality Bible

### Formal Definition
A **Specification** is a **written, detailed description of the materials, workmanship, quality, proportions, methods of execution, and the standard to which a particular item of construction work must be carried out.** Specifications are of two principal types:
1. **General Specifications** — short, broad, work-specific descriptions of *all* items in a building (used in the Estimate and in NITs).
2. **Detailed Specifications** — exhaustive, item-wise technical descriptions (as per **CPWD Specifications Vol. I & II**) used in tender documents, contracts and site execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The KTU valuation pattern awards **separate marks for specifications**. A student who provides the correct material, proportion, workmanship and IS Code reference for an item is awarded full marks, while a student who only writes the rate often loses 2–3 marks out of 14.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Project Instruction Manual"
Imagine you order a **customised dining table from Flipkart**. The **General Specification** says *"wooden table, 6-seater, polished"* (brief description). The **Detailed Specification** says *"18 mm BWR plywood base, 25 mm solid Sheesham wood top, 3 coats of melamine polish, 4 coats of PU lacquer, CNC routed edges, IS 303 grade, moisture content < 12%"* (engineering-grade instruction). A tender is incomplete without the latter.

---

## 1.4 Earthwork Excavation in Foundation — Context Setting

### Formal Definition
**Earthwork Excavation in Foundation** is the **first physical item of work** executed on a construction site. As per CPWD item no. **2.1 / 2.2 / 2.8 (depending on depth and soil type)**, it involves the **dressing of loose earth, digging of trenches/pits to the required depth and width, keeping the sides vertical, shoring strutting if required, dewatering if required, refilling, watering and ramming**, all measured in **m³**. The standard unit of measurement is **cubic metre (m³)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The depth slabs in the DSR for earthwork are:
> - **2.1** — Excavation in ordinary soil up to 1.5 m depth
> - **2.2** — Excavation in ordinary soil 1.5 m to 3.0 m depth
> - **2.3** — Hard soil (DGBC)
> - **2.4 / 2.5** — Soft rock / Hard rock
> - **2.8** — Earthwork in surface excavation
>
> The student **must specify the item number, depth, and soil type** to score full marks in a rate analysis problem.

### Key Standard Metrics (CPWD DSR 2023)
| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Unit of measurement | **m³** (cubic metre) |
| Standard depth (ordinary soil) | **Up to 1.5 m** (no surcharge) |
| Depth surcharge (per metre beyond 1.5 m) | **+10% per metre** (as per DAR 2023) |
| Bulking of soil | **20%–25%** (loose) |
| Lead (free) | **50 m** (CPWD standard) |
| Lift (free) | **1.5 m** (CPWD standard) |
| Shrinkage (refill) | **10%** (allowance) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Relationship Between DSR, DAR & Specifications

The three documents form a **3-tier hierarchy** that every Government of India construction project must follow:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 1: CPWD SPECIFICATIONS (Vol. I & II)              │
│  "WHAT to build" + "HOW to build"                       │
│  → Quality, materials, proportions, workmanship         │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │
                     ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 2: DAR — Delhi Analysis of Rates                   │
│  "WHY the rate is what it is"                           │
│  → Quantities × Unit costs of materials, labour, plant  │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │
                     ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 3: DSR — Delhi Schedule of Rates                   │
│  "WHAT is the final price"                              │
│  → Final adopted rate per unit (₹/m³, ₹/m², ₹/kg)      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Memory Trick — "S-D-A" hierarchy:** **S**pecification tells *what*, **D**AR tells *how the price was derived*, **DSR** tells the *final price*. From top to bottom → increasing condensation of information.

---

## 2.2 Use of the CPWD DSR (Schedule of Rates) — Operational Steps

The DSR is used by the **Assistant Engineer (AE), Executive Engineer (EE), Superintending Engineer (SE)** and the contractor for the following purposes:

1. **Preparation of Estimates:** The AE calculates quantities from drawings, multiplies by DSR rates to obtain the **Abstract of Cost**.
2. **Preparation of Tenders / NIT:** The DSR rate is the **upper ceiling**; the contractor quotes a **percentage above/below/At Par** the DSR.
3. **Valuation of Excess Quantities / Extra Items:** Any item *not* in the DSR is valued using the **DAR methodology**.
4. **Settlement of Disputes:** The DSR is the **legal reference** in arbitration proceedings for government works.
5. **Escalation Calculations:** The DSR year-on-year variation is used to compute **Price Index (PI)** for escalation.

### How to Read a DSR Item — Standard Layout

```
┌────┬──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────┬─────────┐
│Sr. │  Item    │           Description                   │  Unit    │ Rate (₹)│
│No. │  Code    │                                         │          │ (2023)  │
├────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────┤
│1.  │  2.1     │ Earthwork in surface excavation n.ex.    │  m³      │  154    │
│    │          │ 50% 1.5m thick, disposal within 50m     │          │         │
└────┴──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────┴─────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** When a question asks *"Find the cost of X m³ of earthwork excavation in foundation in ordinary soil up to 1.5 m depth, using DSR 2023"*, you **must** show: (i) DSR item number, (ii) Description, (iii) Unit, (iv) Adopted rate, and (v) Multiplication by quantity to get total cost. Marks are lost when only the final figure is written.

---

## 2.3 DAR — The Standard Methodology of Rate Analysis

The **DAR (Analysis of Rates)** derives the rate of *each* item as the sum of the following **six cost heads** (per unit quantity of the item):

$$R = C_m + C_l + C_p + C_o + C_c + C_pf$$

Where:
- $C_m$ = Cost of **Materials** consumed (including wastage + lead charge)
- $C_l$ = Cost of **Labour** (skilled + unskilled, hours × wage)
- $C_p$ = Cost of **Plant & Machinery / Equipment** hire
- $C_o$ = **Overhead charges** (10% of $C_m + C_l + C_p$, as per CPWD)
- $C_c$ = **Contingency** charges (3% of $C_m + C_l + C_p$)
- $C_{pf}$ = **Contractor's Profit** (10% of $C_m + C_l + C_p$)

The **total cost build-up** in a standard DAR sheet is therefore:

$$R = (C_m + C_l + C_p) \times \left(1 + \frac{10 + 3 + 10}{100}\right) = (C_m + C_l + C_p) \times 1.23$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **23% composite** on direct cost is the **CPWD standard** in DSR 2023 / DAR 2023. Some private estimators use 15%–20%; the student **must state the standard percentage** used when solving KTU problems.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic of DAR

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Identify the **item** and the **unit** | e.g., Earthwork excavation in foundation, m³ |
| 2 | List the **materials** required per unit (from DAR chapter) | Usually **NIL** for earthwork |
| 3 | List **labour categories** with hours (or "day-work") | Mate, Mazdoor, Beldar, Coolie |
| 4 | List **machinery** with hours (if any) | e.g., 1% mechanical + 99% manual in ordinary soil |
| 5 | Apply **Lead & Lift** rules | Free lead 50 m, Free lift 1.5 m; extra @ given rate |
| 6 | Compute **direct cost** = $C_m + C_l + C_p$ | ₹/m³ |
| 7 | Add 23% (overheads + contingency + profit) | Final ₹/m³ |
| 8 | Compare with **DSR** to validate | Should match within ±5% |

---

## 2.4 Specifications — Distinction Between General & Detailed

### General Specification (Work-Specific, Used in Estimates)
The **General Specification of all items of a Residential Building** is a short, crisp, paragraph-style document attached to the **Estimate Abstract** of a project. It conveys the **material, proportion, brand, and IS Code** in a single sentence. It is used during the *sanctioning* stage of the project.

### Detailed Specification (CPWD Specifications, Used in Contracts)
The **Detailed Specifications** are voluminous, chapter-wise documents (CPWD Specifications Vol. I for Civil Works, Vol. II for Electrical; recent editions of 2019 with corrigenda up to 2023). They cover *every possible work* of construction with sub-clauses on **Materials, Workmanship, Testing, Tolerances, and Mode of Measurement**. These are part of the **tender document** and are legally binding.

### Tabular Comparison for KTU Exams

| Aspect | General Specification | Detailed Specification (CPWD) |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Purpose | Estimate preparation | Tender / Contract |
| Volume | 1–2 pages per project | 1000+ pages (Vol. I) |
| Style | Narrative paragraphs | Clause-wise (sub-clauses) |
| Coverage | All items of one project | All items of all government works |
| IS Code | Mentioned | Detailed cross-references |
| Authority | AE / Architect | CPWD (Govt. of India) |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Value (CPWD 2023) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lead charge (extra beyond 50 m)** | $L_c = Q_{mat} \times \text{Lead rate} \times \text{Extra km}$ | ₹ per m³ per km (varies by item) | ₹ |
| 2 | **Lift charge (extra beyond 1.5 m)** | $L_{ft} = Q_{mat} \times \text{Lift rate} \times \text{Extra m}$ | ₹ per m³ per m (DSR 2.0.20) | ₹ |
| 3 | **Surcharge for depth > 1.5 m** | $\Delta R = 10\% \times R_{1.5} \times (d - 1.5)$ | 10% per additional metre | ₹ |
| 4 | **Refill volume = excavated − foundation volume** | $V_r = V_e - V_f$ | — | m³ |
| 5 | **Shrinkage allowance (refill)** | $V_{r,\text{actual}} = 1.10 \times V_r$ | 10% | m³ |
| 6 | **Bulking of soil (loose)** | $V_{l} = 1.25 \times V_e$ | 20–25% (soil type) | m³ |
| 7 | **Total rate with 23% build-up** | $R = (C_m + C_l + C_p) \times 1.23$ | 23% composite | ₹/unit |
| 8 | **Number of bricks per m³ brickwork** | $500 \text{ bricks (nominal)}$ | ± 5% wastage | nos. |
| 9 | **Volume of mortar per m³ brickwork** | $0.25 \text{ to } 0.30 \text{ m³}$ | 1:6 / 1:4 mix | m³ |
| 10 | **Cement bags per m³ mortar (1:6)** | $1.20 \text{ bags (approx.)}$ | 50 kg bag | nos. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of every government estimate** in India. Whether the project is a PM Awas Yojana house in Kerala, a NHAI road in Gujarat, or a Metro Rail pier in Chennai, the DSR–DAR–Specifications trio governs how the public money is budgeted, tendered, and audited.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Composite 23% Rate Build-Up (CPWD Methodology)

Let the direct cost of an item be $D$ = Cost of materials + Cost of labour + Cost of plant & machinery. Per CPWD DAR 2023 norms, three charges are applied **sequentially on the direct cost**:

### Step 1 — Overhead Charges ($C_o$)
The contractor's **office expenses, site establishment, supervision, temporary sheds, electricity, water, telephone, transport of personnel, watch & ward, insurance, etc.** are bundled into overheads. CPWD fixes this at:

$$C_o = 0.10 \times D$$

### Step 2 — Contingency Charges ($C_c$)
To cover **unforeseen minor items, variations, escalations within the project duration**, the CPWD adds a contingency:

$$C_c = 0.03 \times D$$

### Step 3 — Contractor's Profit ($C_{pf}$)
A **fair profit margin** for the contractor is allowed:

$$C_{pf} = 0.10 \times D$$

### Step 4 — Summation
The total rate $R$ is the sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= D + C_o + C_c + C_{pf} \\
&= D + 0.10D + 0.03D + 0.10D \\
&= D (1 + 0.10 + 0.03 + 0.10) \\
&= D \times 1.23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** For every ₹100 of direct cost, the final DSR rate is **₹123** — i.e., a **23% composite addition** over the direct cost.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Rate Analysis of Earthwork Excavation in Foundation (Ordinary Soil, Depth ≤ 1.5 m)

**Problem Statement:**
> *Analyse the rate per cubic metre of earthwork excavation in foundation trenches in ordinary soil, depth not exceeding 1.5 m, including dressing, refilling, watering, ramming and disposal of surplus earth within a lead of 50 m. Use the CPWD DAR 2023 methodology.*

### Step 1 — Identify Materials
Earthwork in ordinary soil up to 1.5 m depth uses **no manufactured materials** (no cement, no sand, no aggregate). The only material entry in the DAR is **NIL**:

$$C_m = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic exam trap: students often *additionally* include sand, bricks, or water as materials. Water for compaction is **charged under Labour (Bhisti)**, not as a material.

### Step 2 — Identify Labour (Per 10 m³ of excavation, as per DAR convention)
The DAR specifies labour per **10 m³** of work. Per DAR Chapter 2, the labour requirement for 1 m³ of earthwork in ordinary soil is:

| Category | Number per 10 m³ | Daily wage (₹) (assume) | Cost (₹) |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| Mate (supervisor) | 0.05 | ₹ 700 | 35.00 |
| Mazdoor (skilled) | 0.50 | ₹ 600 | 300.00 |
| Coolie (unskilled) | 1.20 | ₹ 500 | 600.00 |
| Bhisti (water carrier) | 0.10 | ₹ 550 | 55.00 |
| **Total per 10 m³** | — | — | **₹ 990.00** |
| **Total per 1 m³** | — | — | **₹ 99.00** |

> [!NOTE]
> Wage figures used here are indicative for academic illustration. KTU students should use the **latest DAR 2023 wage rates** published by CPWD, which are revised annually based on the All-India Consumer Price Index (AICPI).

Therefore:

$$C_l = 99.00 \; \text{₹/m³}$$

### Step 3 — Identify Plant & Machinery
In ordinary soil up to 1.5 m depth, **manual labour dominates** (≥ 99%); only minor tools are used (pickaxe, shovel, basket, rope). As per DAR, this is treated as a **1% surcharge on labour** (or a flat hire charge):

$$C_p = 0.01 \times C_l = 0.01 \times 99 = 0.99 \; \text{₹/m³} \approx 1.00 \; \text{₹/m³}$$

### Step 4 — Direct Cost

$$
D = C_m + C_l + C_p = 0 + 99.00 + 1.00 = 100.00 \; \text{₹/m³}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply 23% Composite Build-Up

$$
R = D \times 1.23 = 100.00 \times 1.23 = 123.00 \; \text{₹/m³}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare with DSR 2023
The CPWD DSR 2023 entry for item 2.1 is **₹ 154 / m³** (indicative; actual varies). The minor deviation is due to regional labour wage differences (DAR 2023 uses Delhi wages; Kerala's minimum wages are slightly lower). The student must mention this in the answer.

### Step 7 — Final Cost for Given Quantity
If the **volume of excavation is 250 m³**, total cost = $250 \times 154 = $ **₹ 38,500/-** (using DSR) or $250 \times 123 = $ **₹ 30,750/-** (using student's own analysis). In a KTU answer, **both are acceptable**, but the DSR value is preferred as the reference.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Effect of Depth Surcharge (> 1.5 m)

**Problem Statement:**
> *Estimate the rate of excavation in foundation in ordinary soil at a depth of 3.0 m, given the rate for 1.5 m depth is ₹ 123.00 / m³.*

### Step 1 — Identify Excess Depth

$$\Delta d = 3.0 - 1.5 = 1.5 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 2 — Apply 10% Surcharge per Additional Metre

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R &= 10\% \times R_{1.5} \times \Delta d \\
&= 0.10 \times 123.00 \times 1.5 \\
&= 18.45 \; \text{₹/m³}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Rate

$$
R_{3.0} = 123.00 + 18.45 = 141.45 \; \text{₹/m³}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Some students erroneously apply **10% on the *new* depth** (i.e., 30% on 3.0 m). The correct CPWD rule is **10% per additional *metre*** beyond 1.5 m, calculated on the base 1.5 m rate. Writing this rule clearly earns full marks.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Effect of Lead and Lift

**Problem Statement:**
> *For the earthwork in Worked Example 1, compute the additional cost if the disposal lead is 150 m and the lift is 3.0 m. Use the following hypothetical rates:*
> - Lead charge beyond 50 m: ₹ 4.50 per m³ per 50 m
> - Lift charge beyond 1.5 m: ₹ 3.00 per m³ per m

### Step 1 — Extra Lead

$$
\text{Extra lead} = 150 - 50 = 100 \; \text{m} = 2 \times 50 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
C_{lead} = 4.50 \times 2 = 9.00 \; \text{₹/m³}
$$

### Step 2 — Extra Lift

$$
\text{Extra lift} = 3.0 - 1.5 = 1.5 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
C_{lift} = 3.00 \times 1.5 = 4.50 \; \text{₹/m³}
$$

### Step 3 — Revised Total Rate

$$
R_{revised} = 123.00 + 9.00 + 4.50 = 136.50 \; \text{₹/m³}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Lead and lift are **added *before* applying the 23% build-up** in actual CPWD practice, because they are direct cost heads. For KTU exam simplicity, they may be added at the end if the problem statement permits. Always read the question carefully.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Specification — Earthwork Excavation in Foundation (CPWD Spec.)

The **CPWD Detailed Specification** for earthwork excavation in foundation (Sub-head 2.0 of CPWD Specifications, 2019 edition with corrigenda) reads (paraphrased for KTU exam):

> **Materials:** N.A. (earth at site).
> **Workmanship:**
> (a) Excavation shall be to the exact lines, levels and widths shown on the drawing.
> (b) Sides shall be kept vertical; if the soil is loose, **shoring and strutting** shall be done using approved timber.
> (c) Foundation trenches shall be inspected and approved by the Engineer-in-Charge **before** any PCC / soling is laid.
> (d) The bottom of the trench shall be **levelled and watered, then rammed**.
> (e) All **loose earth, roots, organic matter** shall be removed from the trench bottom.
> (f) **Surrounding areas** shall be cleared of slush, and access ramps provided.
> (g) **Refilling** shall be done in layers of **15–20 cm**, each layer watered and rammed.
> (h) Surplus earth shall be **disposed of** within the specified lead, levelled neatly, or stacked as directed.
> (i) Dewatering with pumps (5 HP / 10 HP) shall be done if water table is within 1.5 m of the trench bottom.
> **Mode of Measurement:** Length × breadth × depth (as per drawing), measured in **m³**. Excavation in slush/water/marl, hard rock, soft rock, etc., shall be measured and paid **separately**.

### Tabular Specification Matrix (For KTU Exam Writing)

| Item | General Specification (one-line) | Detailed Specification (CPWD) |
|------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Earthwork in foundation (ordinary soil) | Excavation in foundation trenches in ordinary soil, depth not exceeding 1.5 m, including refilling, watering, ramming and disposal of surplus earth within 50 m lead, as per CPWD item 2.1. | As above — full clause-wise description |
| PCC (1:4:8) | Providing and laying in foundation bed plain cement concrete 1:4:8 (1 cement : 4 coarse sand : 8 graded stone aggregate 40 mm nominal size), including compaction and curing, IS 456:2000, measured in m³. | Mix proportion, w/c ratio ≤ 0.6, hand-mix or machine-mix, curing 7 days minimum |
| Brickwork in foundation | Brickwork with F.P.S. bricks of class designation 75 in foundation in cement mortar 1:6 (1 cement : 6 coarse sand), IS 2212, m³. | Brick soaking 2 h, frog up, joints ≤ 10 mm, curing 7 days |
| D.P.C. | Providing and laying damp-proof course 40 mm thick cement concrete 1:2:4 with water-proofing compound, IS 2645, m². | Surface preparation, two coats of hot bitumen 80/100 grade, curing 3 days |
| Anti-termite treatment | Pre-constructional anti-termite treatment using chloropyriphos 0.5% emulsion, IS 6313 Part II, m². | 2 L/m² chemical, drilling holes 30 cm c/c, post-construction stage separately measured |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State the purpose of the CPWD Schedule of Rates (DSR) and the Delhi Analysis of Rates (DAR).**

#### Model Answer
The **CPWD Schedule of Rates (DSR)** is the official rate book published annually by the Central Public Works Department (CPWD), Government of India. It provides **standardized unit rates (₹/m³, ₹/m², ₹/kg) for all items of civil, electrical and mechanical works** carried out in government construction projects. Its primary purposes are:
1. **Preparation of estimates** of government works
2. **Preparation of NIT / tender documents** — the contractor quotes a percentage above or below DSR
3. **Valuation of variations and extra items**
4. **Settlement of disputes** during arbitration
5. **Computation of escalation** through the Price Index (PI)

The **Delhi Analysis of Rates (DAR)** is the *parent document* of the DSR. It provides the **detailed derivation** of each DSR rate by analysing the **quantities of materials, labour-hours, machinery-hours, lead, lift, wastage and contractor's profit**. The DAR is used to (a) verify DSR rates, (b) compute rates for *new* items not in the DSR, and (c) update rates when market prices fluctuate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse DSR with DAR and write *both* as the same document. The examiner deducts 1 mark for this. Always write: **DSR = final rate, DAR = derivation of the rate.**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between General Specification and Detailed Specification with one example each.**

#### Model Answer
| Aspect | General Specification | Detailed Specification |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Scope | Project-specific, short | Standard, voluminous |
| Use | In the Estimate Abstract | In the Tender / Contract |
| Style | Paragraph form | Clause-wise (numbered) |
| Example | *"Earthwork in foundation in ordinary soil, depth not exceeding 1.5 m, including refilling and disposal within 50 m lead"* (used in KTU estimate problems) | *CPWD Specifications 2019, Sub-head 2.1, Clause 2.1.1 to 2.1.12* — covering excavation, dressing, shoring, dewatering, refilling, watering, ramming, disposal, mode of measurement, IS code references (IS 1200 Part 1) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Writing a *detailed* specification for a *general* specification question (or vice versa) costs you at least 1 mark. Read the question stem carefully — "general" vs "detailed" must be matched correctly.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchy and inter-relationship** between **CPWD Specifications, DAR and DSR** with a neat flowchart. State the role of each in the **lifecycle of a government construction project**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Analyse the **rate per cubic metre** of **earthwork excavation in foundation in ordinary soil, depth not exceeding 1.5 m, including refilling, watering, ramming and disposal of surplus earth within 50 m lead**, using the **CPWD DAR 2023 methodology**. Compare your result with the DSR 2023 published rate and discuss any deviation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchy and Inter-Relationship**

The three documents form a **3-tier hierarchy** that governs every public construction project in India:

1. **Tier 1 — CPWD Specifications (Vol. I & II):** Define the *quality* and *workmanship* standards. They specify the *materials* (with IS Code references), *proportions*, *method of execution*, *tolerances*, and *mode of measurement* (as per IS 1200).
2. **Tier 2 — DAR (Delhi Analysis of Rates):** Translates the *quality* into a *cost* by computing the quantities of materials, labour and machinery required per unit of the item, and summing them with 23% composite build-up.
3. **Tier 3 — DSR (Delhi Schedule of Rates):** The *final published rate per unit*, used directly in estimates, tenders, and bills.

**Role in project lifecycle:**
- **Concept / DPR stage:** CPWD Specifications guide the *design* (e.g., choice of brick class, concrete grade).
- **Estimate preparation:** DSR rates × quantities = estimated cost.
- **Tender stage:** Specifications are part of the agreement; DSR is the *schedule of rates* against which percentage tenders are invited.
- **Execution / Billing:** DSR rates are used for the *on-account* and *final* bills; DAR is invoked for *extra / substituted* items.
- **Arbitration:** DSR/DAR are the legal reference for valuation of disputes.

> A neat flowchart (already shown in Section 4.1) earns full marks.

**(b) Rate Analysis — Earthwork Excavation**

**Step 1 — Item Identification:** CPWD Item 2.1 — Earthwork in foundation in ordinary soil, depth ≤ 1.5 m, disposal within 50 m lead. Unit: m³.

**Step 2 — Materials:** NIL.

**Step 3 — Labour (per 10 m³, as per DAR 2023):**

| Category | Number | Wage (₹) | Cost (₹) |
|----------|--------|----------|----------|
| Mate | 0.05 | 700 | 35.00 |
| Mazdoor | 0.50 | 600 | 300.00 |
| Coolie | 1.20 | 500 | 600.00 |
| Bhisti | 0.10 | 550 | 55.00 |
| **Total / 10 m³** | — | — | **990.00** |
| **Per 1 m³** | — | — | **99.00** |

**Step 4 — Plant & Machinery:** ~1% of labour cost = **₹ 1.00 / m³**.

**Step 5 — Direct Cost:** $D = 0 + 99 + 1 = $ **₹ 100 / m³**.

**Step 6 — 23% Build-Up:** $R = 100 \times 1.23 = $ **₹ 123 / m³**.

**Step 7 — Comparison with DSR 2023:** The DSR 2023 entry is **₹ 154 / m³** (indicative). The deviation of ₹ 31 is explained by:
- DAR 2023 uses *Delhi* minimum wages; Kerala wages are *lower* → our computed rate is *lower*.
- DAR 2023 excludes GST; DSR 2023 is *inclusive of GST* (18%) → DSR is *higher*.
- Regional cost index for Kerala is 0.92 to 0.96.

**Conclusion:** The analysed rate (₹ 123/m³) is consistent with DSR 2023 after applying the Kerala Regional Cost Index and GST adjustment. The minor variation is acceptable within the 5% tolerance permitted in CPWD practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2** (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Write the **General Specification of all major items of work of a Residential Building** in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **Detailed Specification (as per CPWD)** of **Earthwork Excavation in Foundation** and the **Detailed Specification of P.C.C. in Foundation (1:4:8)**. State the relevant **IS codes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) General Specification Table — Residential Building**

| Sl. No. | Item | General Specification (one-line) |
|---------|------|----------------------------------|

| 2 | P.C.C. (1:4:8) | P.C.C. in foundation bed using 1:4:8 (cement:coarse sand:stone aggregate 40 mm), including compaction, curing 7 days. |
| 3 | Brickwork in foundation | Brickwork in F.P.S. bricks class 75, in C.M. 1:6, including soaking, raking joints, curing 7 days. |
| 4 | D.P.C. | 40 mm thick D.P.C. in C.C. 1:2:4 with water-proofing compound, two coats of hot bitumen 80/100. |
| 5 | Anti-termite treatment | Pre-constructional anti-termite treatment using chloropyriphos 0.5% EC, IS 6313 (Part II). |
| 6 | Plastering (internal) | 12 mm C.M. plaster 1:6 to walls, finished smooth, curing 7 days. |
| 7 | Flooring (vitrified) | Providing and laying 600×600 mm vitrified tiles, 8 mm thick, in C.M. 1:3 bedding, joints filled with white cement + pigment. |
| 8 | Painting (external) | Acrylic smooth exterior paint, 2 coats over 1 coat of primer, IS 15489, shade as approved. |
| 9 | Doors & windows | Flush door shutters 35 mm with commercial ply, seasoned Sal wood frame, IS 2202. |
