
### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Cost Components in Rate Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RC[Net Rate per Unit] --> MA[Material Cost M]
    RC --> LB[Labour Cost L]
    RC --> SU[Sundries S]
    RC --> PR[Contractor Profit 10 percent]
    RC --> OH[Overhead Charges 10 percent]
    MA --> CMT[Cement]
    MA --> SND[Sand and Aggregates]
    MA --> STL[Steel]
    MA --> FIN[Finishing Materials Tiles Paint]
    LB --> SKL[Skilled Labour Mason Carpenter]
    LB --> USK[Unskilled Labour Beldar]
    SU --> TP[Tools and Plants]
    SU --> SCF[Scaffolding]
    SU --> WTR[Water and Curing]
    style RC fill:#ffe082,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style PR fill:#ffab91,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style OH fill:#ffab91,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> *Define "Rate Analysis" and list any four factors that affect the rate of an item of work.*

**Model Answer:**
Rate Analysis is the systematic process of determining the cost per unit of a construction item by breaking it down into the cost of materials, labour, equipment, contractor's profit, and overheads.
**Factors affecting rate:**
1. Specification and quality of materials
2. Wages of skilled and unskilled labour
3. Location of site (lead and lift)
4. Market rate fluctuations
5. Profit margin and overheads of the contractor

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *What are the standard units of measurement for: (a) Earthwork in excavation, (b) Plastering, (c) Pointing?*

**Model Answer:**
- (a) Earthwork in excavation: **m³ (Cubic Metre)**
- (b) Plastering: **m² (Square Metre)**
- (c) Pointing: **m² (Square Metre)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> *(a) [7 Marks] Explain the procedure of "Analysis of Rates" with a suitable example. What are the standard values of contractor's profit and overhead charges adopted by Kerala PWD?*
>
> *(b) [7 Marks] Prepare a detailed rate analysis for 20 mm cement plaster (1:6) on external walls. Given: Cement = ₹ 380/bag, Sand = ₹ 1,900/m³, Mason's output = 10 m²/day, Helper's output = 50 m²/day, Mason's wage = ₹ 750/day, Helper's wage = ₹ 500/day. Apply 10% profit and 10% OH.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure of Rate Analysis:**
1. **Identify the item** and its unit (m², m³, etc.)
2. **Quantify materials** required per unit, applying wastage factor.
3. **Quantify labour** (skilled + unskilled) required per unit.
4. **Add sundries** (T&P, scaffolding, water) ≈ 2–3% of (M+L).
5. **Compute basic cost** = M + L + S.
6. **Add contractor's profit** (10%) on basic cost.
7. **Add overhead charges** (10%) on (basic + profit).
8. **Obtain net rate** per unit.
Kerala PWD standard: **10% Profit + 10% Overhead** (Total multiplier = 1.21).

**(b) Numerical Solution — 20 mm Cement Plaster (1:6):**

Wet volume = $1 \times 0.020 = 0.020$ m³/m²

Dry volume = $0.020 \times 1.33 = 0.0266$ m³/m²

Sum of ratio = $1 + 6 = 7$ parts

Cement vol = $0.0266 \times \frac{1}{7} = 0.0038$ m³/m²

With 2% wastage: $0.0038 \times 1.02 = 0.003876$ m³/m²

Cement bags = $\frac{0.003876}{0.035} = 0.111$ bags/m²

Sand vol = $0.0266 \times \frac{6}{7} = 0.0228$ m³/m²

With 5% wastage: $0.0228 \times 1.05 = 0.02394$ m³/m²

**Material Cost:**
- Cement: $0.111 \times 380 = 42.18$ ₹/m²
- Sand: $0.02394 \times 1900 = 45.49$ ₹/m²
- Water & misc.: $1.00$ ₹/m²
- **Total M = 88.67 ₹/m²**

**Labour Cost:**
- Mason: $\frac{750}{10} = 75.00$ ₹/m²
- Helper: $\frac{500}{50} = 10.00$ ₹/m²
- **Total L = 85.00 ₹/m²**

**Sundries:** $0.02 \times (88.67 + 85.00) = 3.47$ ₹/m²

**Basic Cost:** $88.67 + 85.00 + 3.47 = 177.14$ ₹/m²

**Net Rate:** $177.14 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 = 177.14 \times 1.21 = \mathbf{214.34 \text{ ₹/m²}}$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> *(a) [7 Marks] List and explain the various types of "Finishing" works in a building with their units of measurement.*
>
> *(b) [7 Marks] Analyse the rate for "Excavation in Foundation Trenches" in ordinary soil, depth 1.0 m, lead 50 m, lift 2.0 m. Given: gang output = 30 m³/day (1 skilled + 2 beldars), Skilled wage = ₹ 600/day, Beldar wage = ₹ 450/day. Lead charge = ₹ 40 per 30 m per 100 m³ beyond first 30 m. Lift charge = ₹ 8 per 1.5 m per 100 m³ beyond first 1.5 m. Add 10% profit + 10% OH.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Finishing Works:**

| Type | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Plastering | Smooth protective/decorative layer on walls/ceilings | m² |
| Pointing | Finishing of exposed masonry joints | m² |
| Whitewashing | Lime wash with adhesive | m² |
| Colour washing | Pigment-mixed lime wash | m² |
| Distempering | Water-based decorative paint | m² |
| Painting (enamel/emulsion) | Durable washable paint | m² |
| Varnishing | Transparent wood finish | m² |
| Polishing (French) | High-gloss wood finish | m² |
| Flooring (tiles/marble) | Finished floor surface | m² |
| Dado/Skirting | Lower wall protection | m² |
| False ceiling | Suspended decorative ceiling | m² |

**(b) Numerical Solution — Excavation Analysis:**

**Labour Cost per m³:**

Given gang output = 30 m³/day; 1 skilled + 2 beldars.

- Skilled: $\frac{1}{30} \times 600 = 20.00$ ₹/m³
- Beldars: $\frac{2}{30} \times 450 = 30.00$ ₹/m³
- **Total L = 50.00 ₹/m³**

**Sundries (T&P, watchman) ≈ 2% of labour:**

$S = 0.02 \times 50 = 1.00$ ₹/m³

**Material = 0** (no material consumed in plain excavation)

**Lead Charge Calculation:**
- Free lead = first 30 m.
- Beyond 30 m: extra lead = $50 - 30 = 20$ m.
- Charge: $\frac{40}{30} \times 20 = 26.67$ ₹ per 100 m³ = **0.27 ₹/m³**

(Note: Some schedules quote ₹/m³ directly. We follow the standard KTU formula.)

Let me recompute: $26.67 / 100 = 0.27$ ₹/m³ (rounded).

**Lift Charge Calculation:**
- Free lift = first 1.5 m.
- Beyond 1.5 m: extra lift = $2.0 - 1.5 = 0.5$ m.
- Charge: $\frac{8}{1.5} \times 0.5 = 2.67$ ₹ per 100 m³ = **0.027 ₹/m³**

**Total Lead + Lift per m³ = 0.27 + 0.027 = 0.30 ₹/m³**

**Basic Cost:** $0 + 50.00 + 1.00 + 0.30 = 51.30$ ₹/m³

**Net Rate:** $51.30 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 = 51.30 \times 1.21 = \mathbf{62.07 \text{ ₹/m³}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add Wastage %** for cement and sand (2% and 5% respectively). **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert dry volume to wet volume** using the 1.33 multiplier. **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Incorrect bag conversion**: Some students use 0.0347, others 0.035. Use 0.035 as per PWD.
> 4. **Applying profit & overhead incorrectly**: It is a *multiplicative* chain (1.10 × 1.10 = 1.21), NOT additive (10% + 10% = 20%).
> 5. **Ignoring lead/lift free limits**: Always state the *first* 30 m and 1.5 m are free; only the *excess* attracts charges.
> 6. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for missing units.
> 7. **Confusing the ratio** (e.g., 1:4 means 1 part cement + 4 parts sand by volume, NOT by weight).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Rate** = Cost of (Material + Labour + Sundries) × Profit% × Overhead%
- [ ] Standard **Profit + OH** for KTU/PWD = **10% + 10%** (multiplier = **1.21**)
- [ ] **Wet to Dry volume multiplier** for mortar = **1.33**
- [ ] **1 cement bag** = **50 kg = 0.035 m³ = 1.25 ft³**
- [ ] **Wastage factors**: Cement **2%**, Sand **5%**, Bricks **5%**, Steel **5%**
- [ ] **Free Lead** = first **30 m**; **Free Lift** = first **1.5 m**
- [ ] **Finishing units**: Plastering/Painting/Distempering/Pointing = **m²**
- [ ] **Earthwork units**: Excavation/Backfilling = **m³**
- [ ] **Order of work**: Earthwork → Structure → Plastering → Whitewashing/Colour washing → Distempering → Painting/Varnishing
- [ ] **Curing time** for cement plaster = **7 days minimum**
- [ ] For **1:4 plaster (12 mm)**, cement consumed ≈ **0.10 bags/m²** (memorize for quick solving)
- [ ] Always **specify units** in the final rate (e.g., ₹/m², ₹/m³)
- [ ] **Hacking** of RCC surface is required before plastering for proper bond
- [ ] **Plastering** is measured in m²; deductions are made for openings > 0.5 m² (as per IS 1200)
- [ ] **Pointing** is preferred over plastering in heritage/stone masonry for aesthetic reasons
- [ ] **Distemper** is water-based (low cost, internal use); **Emulsion paint** is acrylic-based (washable, durable)
- [ ] **Varnishing** protects wood from moisture, UV, insects; **French polish** gives a high-gloss mirror finish
- [ ] **E-in-C's Schedule of Rates (SOR)** is the authoritative reference for Kerala PWD projects
- [ ] **1 m³ = 35.315 ft³** — always show both metric and imperial conversions in AoR sheets
- [ ] **Contractor's profit** is NOT a fixed 10% globally — confirm with tender conditions (sometimes 7.5% or 12%)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mortars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mortars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Mortar

**Mortar** is a workable paste prepared by mixing a binding material (such as cement or lime), fine aggregate (sand/surkhi), water, and sometimes special admixtures, which hardens upon curing to bind masonry units (bricks, stones, blocks) into a monolithic structural mass and to fill the joints uniformly.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, mortar is classified as a **homogeneous mixture** of binder, fine aggregate, and water, used in the construction industry as a **jointing material** to bind building units and distribute loads evenly across the masonry structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mortar constitutes a primary item under the "Materials and Specifications" module of Quantity Surveying because the **dry volume, wet volume, wastage factor, and ratio of mix** directly determine the **material consumption statement (MCS)** in the Abstract of Cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sandwich**. The bread slices are bricks/stones, but without a spread (butter, mayo, or jam) holding them together, the sandwich falls apart. **Mortar is the "spread" of masonry construction** — it binds individual units into one solid wall.

A closer engineering analogy: Think of mortar as the **"structural glue"** of a building. While steel and concrete form the skeleton, mortar acts as the connective tissue ensuring every brick acts as part of a unified load-bearing system rather than an individual loose unit.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Mortar

The key functions of mortar in civil engineering practice are:

- **Binding Action:** Holds bricks, stones, or blocks together to form a strong, durable structural unit.
- **Load Distribution:** Evenly distributes superimposed loads across the masonry surface, preventing point-stress concentrations.
- **Joint Filling:** Fills the gaps between masonry units, ensuring a uniform, water-tight, and aesthetically finished surface.
- **Bonding with Plaster:** Provides a coarse, rough surface for plastering and rendering operations to adhere properly.
- **Accommodating Minor Movements:** Acts as a cushion to absorb minor movements due to thermal expansion, contraction, and structural settlement.
- **Decorative Finish:** Provides neat, even joints that enhance the visual appeal of exposed masonry walls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The **characteristic compressive strength of mortar** for ordinary brick masonry in residential construction typically ranges between **3 N/mm² to 5 N/mm²** (M1 to M3 grade as per IS 2250).

## 1.4 Ingredients of Mortar

The ingredients required for preparing mortar in KTU 2024 syllabus framework are:

1. **Binding Material (Cement/Lime):** Provides adhesive strength and binding property. Common binders include Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC), Pozzolana Portland Cement (PPC), hydrated lime, and gypsum.
2. **Fine Aggregate (Sand/Surkhi):** Acts as a filler material and bulking agent. Sand must be clean, sharp, well-graded, and free from organic impurities, silt, and clay lumps. As per IS 2116, sand should be **well-graded** with particle size passing through a **4.75 mm IS sieve** and retained on **75 micron IS sieve**.
3. **Water:** Used for hydration of cement and workability of the mix. Potable water with **pH value not less than 6** is recommended (as per IS 456).
4. **Admixtures:** Special chemicals added to enhance specific properties such as workability, water resistance, setting time, and bonding strength. Examples: plasticizers, accelerators, retarders, air-entraining agents, and waterproofing compounds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water-Cement Ratio Rule:** A lower water-cement ratio increases strength but reduces workability. For mortar mixes, a practical **w/c ratio of 0.40 to 0.60** is generally adopted for optimum results.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Mortars

Mortars are systematically classified based on the type of binding material, application, and bulk density. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following major categories:

### 2.1.1 Cement Mortar
- **Composition:** Portland cement + sand + water
- **Ratio Range:** 1:2 to 1:6 (cement:sand) for different applications
- **Properties:** High compressive strength, fast setting, durable, suitable for heavy loads and damp conditions
- **Applications:** Load-bearing masonry, foundation walls, DPC (Damp Proof Course), plastering, pointing

### 2.1.2 Lime Mortar
- **Composition:** Lime (fat lime or hydraulic lime) + sand + water
- **Ratio Range:** 1:2 to 1:3 (lime putty:sand) for brickwork
- **Properties:** Excellent workability, high water retentivity, slow setting, self-healing of minor cracks
- **Applications:** Historical restoration, ornamental work, low-rise buildings, plastering in heritage structures

### 2.1.3 Surkhi Mortar
- **Composition:** Lime + surkhi (finely ground burnt clay) + water
- **Properties:** Hydraulic action, moderate strength, economical in regions with abundant burnt clay
- **Applications:** Masonry in ordinary buildings, plastering of internal walls

### 2.1.4 Mud Mortar
- **Composition:** Mud (clayey soil) + water ± straw/cow dung
- **Properties:** Very low strength, highly porous, temporary nature, eco-friendly
- **Applications:** Temporary structures, rural constructions, low-cost housing

### 2.1.5 Gypsum Mortar
- **Composition:** Gypsum (Calcium Sulphate) + sand + water
- **Properties:** Quick setting (within 3–4 minutes), fire-resistant, smooth finish
- **Applications:** Plaster work, false ceiling, ornamental work, fireproofing

### 2.1.6 Special Mortars
- **a) Fire-Resistant Mortar:** Made with aluminous cement and refractory aggregates (chamotte, crushed brick)
- **b) Lightweight Mortar:** Made with sawdust, cork, or lightweight aggregates
- **c) Sound-Absorbing Mortar:** Made with cement, lime, and asbestos fibers
- **d) X-ray Shielding Mortar:** Contains barium sulphate and lead glass
- **e) Polymer-Modified Mortar:** Cement mortar enriched with polymers for enhanced bonding

## 2.2 Properties of Good Mortar (The Six Quality Pillars)

A good mortar must satisfy the following six essential properties as per KTU/IS standards:

1. **Workability:** The mortar should be easily spreadable with a trowel and adhere well to the masonry surface. Measured using the **flow test** (typical flow value: **100% to 115%** as per IS 2250).
2. **Water Retentivity:** The capacity to retain water against absorption by porous masonry units, preventing premature drying. Should be **≥ 70%** for good quality mortar.
3. **Consistency:** The mortar should be plastic and uniform without bleeding or segregation.
4. **Strength:** Adequate compressive strength to handle design loads. Mortar strength should ideally match the masonry unit strength.
5. **Durability:** Resistance to weathering, chemical attack, freeze-thaw cycles, and sulphate attack.
6. **Bond Strength:** Strong adhesion between mortar and masonry units to ensure monolithic behavior.

## 2.3 Recommended Mortar Proportions for Various Applications

| **Application** | **Mortar Type** | **Mix Ratio (Binder:Sand)** | **Grade of Mortar** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load-bearing brickwork in foundations | Cement Mortar | 1:3 to 1:4 | M5–M7.5 |
| Load-bearing brickwork in superstructure | Cement Mortar | 1:4 to 1:6 | M3–M5 |
| Partition walls (non-load bearing) | Cement Mortar / Lime Mortar | 1:6 / 1:2 | M1–M3 |
| Damp Proof Course (DPC) | Cement Mortar | 1:2 (with waterproofing admixture) | M7.5–M10 |
| Plastering (external walls) | Cement Mortar | 1:4 to 1:6 | M3–M5 |
| Plastering (internal walls) | Cement Mortar / Lime Mortar | 1:5 to 1:6 / 1:2 | M2–M3 |
| Pointing work | Cement Mortar | 1:1 to 1:3 | M7.5–M10 |
| Stone masonry (rubble) | Cement Mortar / Lime Mortar | 1:4 / 1:2 | M3–M5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For QS purposes, remember that **1 bag of cement = 50 kg = 0.0353 m³ = 1.226 ft³** (in loose volume terms for estimation).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Equation / Value** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dry Volume of Mortar | $V_{dry} = V_{wet} \times 1.33$ | m³ | Quantity estimation |
| Volume of Cement (per m³ wet mortar) | $V_{cement} = \dfrac{1}{(1 + n)} \times V_{dry}$ | m³ | Material take-off |
| Volume of Sand (per m³ wet mortar) | $V_{sand} = \dfrac{n}{(1 + n)} \times V_{dry}$ | m³ | Material take-off |
| Number of Cement Bags | $B = \dfrac{V_{cement}}{0.0353}$ | bags | Procurement |
| Weight of Cement | $W = B \times 50$ | kg | Procurement |
| Volume of Sand (in brass) | $V_{sand(brass)} = \dfrac{V_{sand}(m^3)}{2.83}$ | brass | Local market unit (Kerala) |
| Mortar per 1 m³ Brickwork | Approx. 0.25 to 0.30 m³ | m³ | Brickwork estimate |
| Mortar per 1 m³ Stone Masonry | Approx. 0.30 to 0.40 m³ | m³ | Stone masonry estimate |
| Wastage Allowance (Cement) | 2% to 3% | % | Overhead in MCS |
| Wastage Allowance (Sand) | 5% to 7.5% | % | Overhead in MCS |

Where: **$n$** = sand coefficient in mix ratio (e.g., for 1:6, $n = 6$); **$V_{dry}$** = dry volume; **$V_{wet}$** = wet/finished volume.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Quantity Surveying practice, the **mortar mix ratio directly drives the cost of masonry work** in the Bill of Quantities (BoQ). A 1:4 mix consumes **33.3% more cement** than a 1:6 mix, significantly impacting the project budget for large-scale housing or commercial developments. Civil engineers and QS professionals must therefore select the **lowest acceptable mortar grade** that satisfies structural and durability requirements — a balance between **safety, performance, and economy**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Dry-to-Wet Volume Conversion

When cement and sand are mixed dry and water is added, the final wet volume is **less** than the sum of dry volumes due to the filling of voids between sand grains by cement particles. This phenomenon is known as the **bulking factor of sand** and **void filling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The standard dry-to-wet conversion factor for cement-sand mortar is empirically determined to be **1.33** (i.e., wet volume is 33% greater than dry volume for the same finished product).

$$
V_{dry} = 1.33 \times V_{wet}
$$

**Logical Steps:**

Step 1: Take 1 m³ of wet mortar (fully compacted and finished state).

Step 2: When this is dried, the volume reduces to $\frac{1}{1.33}$ m³ ≈ 0.75 m³ due to water evaporation.

Step 3: Therefore, the dry volume of materials required to produce 1 m³ of wet mortar = 1.33 m³.

## 3.2 Derivation of Material Quantities for a Given Mix Ratio

For a mortar mix of ratio **1 : n** (1 part cement : $n$ parts sand), the total parts = $1 + n$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Total dry volume required (for 1 m³ wet mortar):

$$
V_{dry} = 1.33 \times 1 = 1.33 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Volume of cement required:

$$
V_{cement} = \frac{\text{Part of cement}}{\text{Total parts}} \times V_{dry}
$$

$$
V_{cement} = \frac{1}{(1 + n)} \times 1.33
$$

**Step 3:** Volume of sand required:

$$
V_{sand} = \frac{\text{Part of sand}}{\text{Total parts}} \times V_{dry}
$$

$$
V_{sand} = \frac{n}{(1 + n)} \times 1.33
$$

**Step 4:** Number of cement bags (since 1 bag = 0.0353 m³):

$$
B = \frac{V_{cement}}{0.0353}
$$

**Step 5:** Weight of cement in kg:

$$
W = B \times 50 \text{ kg}
$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Calculate the quantity of cement (in bags) and sand (in m³ and brass) required for 10 m³ of cement mortar in 1:4 ratio. Assume dry volume factor = 1.33.

**Step 1:** Compute the total dry volume required:

$$
V_{dry} = 1.33 \times 10 = 13.30 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the volume of cement:

$$
V_{cement} = \frac{1}{(1 + 4)} \times 13.30 = \frac{13.30}{5} = 2.66 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the number of cement bags:

$$
B = \frac{2.66}{0.0353} = 75.35 \text{ bags} \approx 76 \text{ bags}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the weight of cement:

$$
W = 76 \times 50 = 3800 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the volume of sand:

$$
V_{sand} = \frac{4}{5} \times 13.30 = 10.64 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Convert sand volume to brass (1 brass = 2.83 m³, Kerala local unit):

$$
V_{sand(brass)} = \frac{10.64}{2.83} = 3.76 \text{ brass}
$$

### 3.3.1 Analytical Matrix (Summary Table)

| **Material** | **Quantity (Raw)** | **Wastage** | **Net Quantity for Order** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cement | 75.35 bags | 3% | $75.35 \times 1.03 = 77.61$ | bags |
| Sand | 10.64 m³ | 7.5% | $10.64 \times 1.075 = 11.44$ | m³ |
| Water | $3800 \times 0.5 = 1900$ | — | 1900 | litres |

## 3.4 Cost Estimation Component (Quantity Surveying Linkage)

Using Kerala PWD / Schedule of Rates (SOR) unit rates (assumed for academic illustration):

- Cement (per bag, 50 kg): ₹ 420
- Sand (per m³): ₹ 1850
- Water (per 1000 L): ₹ 250
- Labour for mixing (per m³): ₹ 350

**Cost Computation:**

$$
C_{cement} = 77.61 \times 420 = \text{₹ } 32,596.20
$$

$$
C_{sand} = 11.44 \times 1850 = \text{₹ } 21,164.00
$$

$$
C_{water} = 1.9 \times 250 = \text{₹ } 475.00
$$

$$
C_{labour} = 10 \times 350 = \text{₹ } 3,500.00
$$

$$
C_{total} = 32596.20 + 21164.00 + 475.00 + 3500.00 = \text{₹ } 57,735.20
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always show **dry volume factor application**, **wastage allowance**, and **unit conversions** explicitly in the answer sheet. Examiners award marks for these systematic steps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Mortar Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[MORTAR - Binding Material + Fine Aggregate + Water]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Based on Binding Material]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Based on Application]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Based on Bulk Density]
    
    nodeB --> nodeE[Cement Mortar]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Lime Mortar]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Gypsum Mortar]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Surkhi Mortar]
    nodeB --> nodeI[Mud Mortar]
    
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Plain Mortar]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Special Mortar]
    nodeK --> nodeK1[Fire Resistant]
    nodeK --> nodeK2[Lightweight]
    nodeK --> nodeK3[X-ray Shielding]
    nodeK --> nodeK4[Polymer Modified]
    
    nodeD --> nodeL[Heavy Mortar - density greater than 15 kN per m3]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Light Mortar - density less than 15 kN per m3]
    
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Mix 1:2 to 1:6]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Fat Lime Mortar 1:2 to 1:3]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Hydraulic Lime Mortar 1:2]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mortar Preparation Process (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Selection of Materials - Cement, Sand, Water]
    step2[Proportioning by Volume or Weight]
    step3[Dry Mixing of Cement and Sand]
    step4[Addition of Water in Required Quantity]
    step5[Wet Mixing on Non-Absorbent Platform]
    step6[Consistency Check - Workability Test]
    step7[Use within Initial Setting Time - 30 min for OPC]
    step8[Application on Masonry Surface]
    step9[Curing for 3 to 7 Days]
    
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 -->|Pass| step7
    step6 -->|Fail| step5
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Six Quality Properties of Good Mortar

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1[Workability - Easy to Spread]
    p2[Water Retentivity - Holds Water]
    p3[Consistency - Uniformity]
    p4[Strength - Compressive Load]
    p5[Durability - Weather Resistance]
    p6[Bond Strength - Adhesion to Bricks]
    
    p1 --> qm[Good Quality Mortar]
    p2 --> qm
    p3 --> qm
    p4 --> qm
    p5 --> qm
    p6 --> qm
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Material Quantity Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Mortar Volume V in m cube]
    input2[Mix Ratio 1 colon n]
    input3[Dry Factor 1.33]
    
    calc1[Compute V dry = V x 1.33]
    calc2[Volume of Cement = 1 over 1 plus n times V dry]
    calc3[Volume of Sand = n over 1 plus n times V dry]
    calc4[Bags = V cement divided by 0.0353]
    
    out1[Material Take-Off Sheet]
    
    input1 --> calc1
    input2 --> calc2
    input3 --> calc1
    calc1 --> calc2
    calc1 --> calc3
    calc2 --> calc4
    calc2 --> out1
    calc3 --> out1
    calc4 --> out1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In valuation and QS problems, examiners often present a flowchart to test whether students can identify the right sequence of operations. Practice reading such diagrams from top-to-bottom and left-to-right.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define mortar and list any four functions of mortar in brick masonry.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Mortar is a homogeneous workable mixture of a binding material (cement or lime), fine aggregate (sand), and water, used to bind masonry units like bricks and stones into a unified structural mass.

**Four important functions of mortar:**

1. **Binding Action:** It holds bricks/stones together, ensuring monolithic behavior of the wall.
2. **Load Distribution:** It evenly distributes superimposed loads over the masonry surface.
3. **Joint Filling:** It fills the gaps between units, providing a uniform and weather-tight surface.
4. **Accommodating Movements:** It acts as a cushion to absorb minor movements due to thermal expansion and structural settlement.

*(Alternative: Bonding for plastering, decorative finishing, damp resistance with admixtures.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Cement Mortar and Lime Mortar.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Cement Mortar** | **Lime Mortar** |
|---|---|---|
| **Binder** | Portland cement | Lime (fat or hydraulic) |
| **Strength** | High (3–10 N/mm²) | Low to moderate (1–3 N/mm²) |
| **Setting Time** | Fast (30 min initial) | Very slow (days to weeks) |
| **Water Retentivity** | Moderate | High |
| **Durability** | High | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Higher | Economical |
| **Use** | Load-bearing, DPC, foundation | Heritage, ornamental, plaster |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Estimate the Quantity of Materials for Mortar

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a) Calculate the quantities of cement (in bags) and sand (in m³) required for 25 m³ of cement mortar in the ratio 1:5. Apply a dry volume factor of 1.33 and wastage allowance of 3% for cement and 7.5% for sand. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the dry volume of mortar:

$$
V_{dry} = 1.33 \times 25 = 33.25 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the volume of cement:

$$
V_{cement} = \frac{1}{1 + 5} \times 33.25 = \frac{33.25}{6} = 5.542 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the number of cement bags (before wastage):

$$
B_{raw} = \frac{5.542}{0.0353} = 157.0 \text{ bags}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply 3% wastage:

$$
B_{net} = 157.0 \times 1.03 = 161.7 \text{ bags} \approx 162 \text{ bags}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the volume of sand:

$$
V_{sand} = \frac{5}{6} \times 33.25 = 27.708 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Apply 7.5% wastage:

$$
V_{sand(net)} = 27.708 \times 1.075 = 29.79 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Final Answer:**

- **Cement required = 162 bags (8100 kg)**
- **Sand required = 29.79 m³**

**(b) If the unit rates are: Cement = ₹430/bag, Sand = ₹1900/m³, Labour for mixing = ₹400/m³, compute the total cost of mortar. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Cost of cement:

$$
C_{cement} = 162 \times 430 = \text{₹ } 69,660
$$

**Step 2:** Cost of sand:

$$
C_{sand} = 29.79 \times 1900 = \text{₹ } 56,601
$$

**Step 3:** Cost of labour (per m³ of wet mortar = 25 m³):

$$
C_{labour} = 25 \times 400 = \text{₹ } 10,000
$$

**Step 4:** Total cost:

$$
C_{total} = 69660 + 56601 + 10000 = \text{₹ } 1,36,261
$$

**Final Answer:** **Total cost of mortar = ₹ 1,36,261/-**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Properties, Selection, and Applications of Mortar

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a) Explain the six essential properties of good mortar. Mention the test/method to determine at least three of these properties. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A good mortar must possess the following six properties:

1. **Workability** — The mortar must be easily spreadable with a trowel and adhere well to the masonry units.
   - *Test:* **Flow Test** as per IS 2250. A standard truncated cone of mortar is placed on a glass plate, given 25 jolts in a flow table, and the spread is measured. A flow of **100% to 115%** is considered good.

2. **Water Retentivity** — The mortar should retain sufficient water during laying, preventing premature drying and loss of bond.
   - *Test:* **Water Retentivity Test** as per IS 2250. A mortar sample is subjected to suction, and the water retained is measured. A value of **≥ 70%** is desirable.

3. **Consistency** — The mortar should be uniform, plastic, and free from segregation or bleeding.
   - *Test:* **Penetration Test** and visual inspection.

4. **Strength** — The mortar should develop adequate compressive strength to resist design loads.
   - *Test:* **Compression Test** on 70.6 mm × 70.6 mm × 70.6 mm cubes at 28 days.

5. **Durability** — Resistance to weathering, chemical attack, freeze-thaw, and sulphate action.
   - *Test:* Long-term exposure tests, sulphate resistance test as per IS 4031.

6. **Bond Strength** — The mortar should develop strong adhesion to the masonry units.
   - *Test:* **Bond Wrench Test** (cross-coupling test) on brick couplets.

**(b) Discuss the selection criteria for mortar for different applications. Provide a recommended mortar table for at least five distinct applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of mortar depends on the following criteria:

1. **Type of Masonry Unit:** Strength of brick/stone should be matched with mortar strength.
2. **Nature of Structure:** Load-bearing vs. non-load-bearing.
3. **Exposure Conditions:** Interior vs. exterior, normal vs. severe (damp, marine, sulphate-rich).
4. **Type of Loading:** Static vs. dynamic.
5. **Availability and Cost of Materials:** Local economics influence choice.
6. **Workability Requirement:** Laying ease, time available, weather conditions.

**Recommended Mortar Table:**

| **S.No.** | **Application** | **Type** | **Ratio (Binder:Sand)** | **Mortar Grade** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Foundation brickwork (load-bearing) | Cement Mortar | 1:3 | M7.5 |
| 2 | Superstructure brickwork | Cement Mortar | 1:5 | M4 |
| 3 | Damp Proof Course (DPC) | Cement Mortar + WP admixture | 1:2 | M10 |
| 4 | External Plastering | Cement Mortar | 1:4 | M5 |
| 5 | Internal Plastering | Lime Mortar | 1:2 | M2 |
| 6 | Pointing Work | Cement Mortar | 1:2 | M7.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dry volume factor (1.33)** is the most common mistake — it causes a 33% underestimation of materials, leading to a significant loss of marks (typically **3–4 marks deducted**).
> 2. **Skipping the wastage allowance** is another frequent error. Always apply **3% for cement** and **7.5% for sand** in your final answer.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions:** Remember **1 bag = 50 kg = 0.0353 m³** and **1 brass = 2.83 m³** (Kerala standard). Examiners check this carefully.
> 4. **Not specifying the mortar grade (M1 to M10)** in the final answer leads to loss of 1 mark.
> 5. **For QS problems, missing the labour or overhead component** when computing cost is a common pitfall.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mortar = Binder + Fine Aggregate + Water** (and optionally admixtures).
- **Cement Mortar** is the most widely used modern mortar; **Lime Mortar** is used in heritage/restoration works.
- **Standard dry-to-wet conversion factor = 1.33** for all cement-mortar volume calculations.
- **Mix ratio 1:n** means 1 part cement and $n$ parts sand by volume.
- **Formula for cement volume:** $V_{cement} = \frac{1}{1+n} \times V_{dry}$.
- **Formula for sand volume:** $V_{sand} = \frac{n}{1+n} \times V_{dry}$.
- **1 bag of cement = 50 kg = 0.0353 m³.**
- **1 brass (Kerala local unit) = 2.83 m³** of sand.
- **Wastage allowance:** 3% for cement, 7.5% for sand.
- **Mortar per 1 m³ brickwork** ≈ 0.25–0.30 m³; for **stone masonry** ≈ 0.30–0.40 m³.
- **Six quality properties:** Workability, Water Retentivity, Consistency, Strength, Durability, Bond Strength.
- **Higher mix ratio (1:6)** = more sand, less cement = lower cost but reduced strength — a QS optimization decision.
- **DPC and pointing** require rich mix (1:2 to 1:3) for water resistance.
- **Mortar grade classification** (M1 to M10) based on compressive strength as per IS 2250.
- **Initial setting time of OPC = 30 minutes**; mortar must be used before this.
- **Curing period for mortar joints** = 3 to 7 days depending on type and exposure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforced Cement Concrete Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reinforced Cement Concrete Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 / IS 1200 Part 5 Compliant):**
> **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** is a composite structural material formed by combining **concrete** (a mixture of cement, fine aggregate, coarse aggregate, and water in specified proportions) with **steel reinforcement bars (rebars)** strategically embedded to resist tensile and shear stresses. For quantity surveying purposes, RCC works include the measurement of concrete volume, reinforcement steel weight, and associated **formwork (centering and shuttering)** as three distinct payable items.

## 1.1 The Three Components of an RCC Item Rate

Every RCC work item in a KTU-aligned estimate is composed of three separately measured and paid sub-items:

1. **Concrete (Wet Volume → Dry Volume → Structural Volume):** The hardened matrix. Unit: **cubic metre (m³)**.
2. **Reinforcement Steel:** Cold-worked ribbed/Tor steel bars, measured by weight. Unit: **kilogram (kg) or quintal (100 kg)**.
3. **Formwork / Centering & Shuttering:** Temporary mould to hold wet concrete. Unit: **square metre (m²)** of contact area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Code for Measurement:** All RCC quantity surveying in India follows **IS 1200 (Part 5) – Method of Measurement of Civil Engineering Works: Concrete and Reinforced Concrete**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Skeleton and Flesh

Imagine the human body. The **skeleton (steel rebars)** gives shape and bears all the pulling and bending forces, while the **flesh and skin (concrete)** protect the skeleton, distribute compressive loads, and bind everything into one rigid unit. Concrete on its own is exceptionally strong in **compression** (like pressing a brick between your palms) but weak in **tension** (it snaps if you pull it). Steel is the opposite — weak in compression (it buckles), strong in tension. RCC exploits this *complementary duality*, much like a Velcro strap where one side alone fails, but together they are inseparable.

## 1.3 Classification of RCC Structural Elements (KTU Module Scope)

| Sl. | Element | Typical Location | Primary Load Role |
|-----|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | RCC **Footings** | Below ground, base of column/wall | Transfer load to soil |
| 2 | RCC **Columns** | Vertical members | Axial compression + bending |
| 3 | RCC **Beams** | Horizontal, spanning openings | Bending + shear |
| 4 | RCC **Slabs** | Floor / roof | Bending across short span |
| 5 | RCC **Lintels** | Above door/window openings | Support masonry over opening |
| 6 | RCC **Chajjas** | Projecting sun-shades | Cantilever bending |
| 7 | RCC **Staircases** | Stepped access | Combined bending + axial |
| 8 | RCC **Retaining Walls** | Earth-retaining structures | Lateral earth pressure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Why and How of RCC Quantity Take-Off

The estimator's task is to convert a structural drawing into three measured quantities per element. The "why" behind every formula is rooted in **IS 1200 (Part 5)** rules of measurement, designed to ensure uniform, fair payment across the industry.

### 2.1.1 Concrete Volume Calculation — "Why" Each Multiplier Exists

**Step 1: Read the structural dimensions from the drawing (Length × Breadth × Depth).**

**Step 2: Apply the "Net vs Gross" principle.** Gross dimensions (e.g., beam outer-to-outer) overestimate; net dimensions (clear span + bearing) reflect actual concrete poured. The estimator must decide based on the *bill of quantities clause* — most KTU standard schedules adopt *clear span + bearing* for beams.

**Step 3: Convert wet volume to dry volume** (when computing material cost from mixer ratio):
- Dry Volume = Wet Volume × **1.54**
- *Why 1.54?* Because voids in aggregates and cement inflate the dry bulk by roughly 54% when water is added and air escapes.

**Step 4: Account for wastage** in BOQ:
- Concrete wastage allowance = **2% to 5%** (unavoidable spillage, pump residue, formwork leakage).

### 2.1.2 Reinforcement Steel — The "Why" Behind the d²/162 Formula

The weight of any round steel bar per running metre is derived from its **cross-sectional area × density**:

$$W = A \times \rho = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times 7850 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}$$

Where $d$ is diameter in **mm** and $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is steel density. Simplifying:

$$\frac{\pi \times 7850}{4 \times 10^6} = \frac{24649}{10^6} \approx \frac{1}{162}$$

Hence the universally used KTU formula:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{d^2}{162} \text{ kg/m}}$$

This compact relation lets an estimator convert "linear metres of bar" to "kilograms of steel" without a calculator.

### 2.1.3 Hook, Bend, and Lap Deductions — "Why" the Bar Length Reduces

A bar bent into shape loses some length at every bend (the outer arc is longer than the straight chord). Standard deductions per IS 2502 / SP 34:

- 45° bend → deduct **1d** (where d = bar diameter)
- 90° bend → deduct **2d**
- 135° bend → deduct **3d**
- 180° bend → deduct **2d** (seamless U-turn)

Hook length is added per IS 2502:
- For ≤ Ø25 mm bars → **9d** (with min 100 mm)
- For > Ø25 mm bars → **6d** (with min 125 mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1.8% Reinforcement Heuristic:** For quick preliminary estimates, RCC slabs in buildings require approximately **0.8% to 1.5%** of concrete volume as steel weight, while beams need **1.5% to 2.0%** and columns **2.0% to 4.0%**. These percentages multiplied by concrete volume in m³ × 7850 kg/m³ give a fast cross-check.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Governing Rule |
|-----|-----------|--------------------------|------|----------------|
| 1 | Steel weight per metre | $d^2 / 162$ | kg/m | IS 1786 (Fe 415/500) |
| 2 | Dry volume factor | Wet volume × **1.54** | m³ | Empirical, IS 456 |
| 3 | Concrete wastage | **2% to 5%** | % | Site/contract spec |
| 4 | Steel wastage | **3% to 5%** (cutting, binding) | % | SP 34 / BBS practice |
| 5 | Hook length (≤ Ø25) | **9d** | mm | IS 2502 |
| 6 | Hook length (> Ø25) | **6d** | mm | IS 2502 |
| 7 | 90° bend deduction | **2d** | mm | IS 2502 |
| 8 | 135° bend deduction | **3d** | mm | IS 2502 |
| 9 | Lap length (tension) | **50d** | mm | IS 456 |
| 10 | Lap length (compression) | **40d** | mm | IS 456 |
| 11 | Development length (L_d) | **45d to 60d** | mm | IS 456, Cl. 26.2 |
| 12 | Min. clear cover (slab) | **15 mm** (mild), **20 mm** (moderate) | mm | IS 456, Table 16 |
| 13 | Min. clear cover (beam) | **25 mm** | mm | IS 456 |
| 14 | Min. clear cover (column) | **40 mm** | mm | IS 456 |
| 15 | Slab steel (% of volume) | **0.8% to 1.5%** | % | Empirical |
| 16 | Beam steel (% of volume) | **1.5% to 2.0%** | % | Empirical |
| 17 | Column steel (% of volume) | **2.0% to 4.0%** | % | Empirical |
| 18 | Formwork cost (typical) | **6% to 8%** of concrete cost | % | Market trend |
| 19 | No-deduction threshold (openings) | **≤ 0.1 m²** | m² | IS 1200 Part 5 |
| 20 | Stirrup spacing (beam) | **min (d/4, 16Ø, 300 mm)** | mm | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance — Vertical Pipes Inside Columns:** As per IS 1200 (Part 5), the volume occupied by **structural steel encasement** in RCC columns is deducted, but the volume of **reinforcement steel** is **NOT** deducted (it is measured separately by weight and paid). Mixing these two is a common valuation error.

## 2.3 Rules of Deduction (IS 1200 Part 5 — High-Yield)

| Element | Deduction Rule | Note for KTU 2024 |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|
| RCC Beams | Cross-section × clear span + bearing | Openings > 0.1 m² → full deduction |
| RCC Columns | Height × full cross-section | No deduction for steel inside |
| RCC Slabs | Area × thickness | Openings > 0.1 m² → full deduction |
| RCC Footings | Per drawing dimensions | No deduction for chamfers ≤ 50 mm |
| RCC Chajjas | L × B × thickness (avg.) | Includes edge thickening |

> [!NOTE]
> **"±10 mm" Tolerance:** Nominal dimensions on drawings are assumed accurate; field variations of ±10 mm are absorbed by the contractor and **not** re-measured. This is a KTU valuation shortcut worth memorising.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Tendering:** Contractors use these formulas to bid competitive rates without owning the BOQ.
- **Rate Analysis:** The Karnataka/Kerala PWD *Standard Schedule of Rates (SSR)* is built on these unit conversions.
- **Project Auditing:** Government auditors use d²/162 to verify the *Bills of Quantities* submitted by contractors, catching over-claims within minutes.
- **Pre-engineered Buildings:** Even in steel-prefabricated projects, the foundation is invariably RCC, making these formulas universally applicable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Volume of Concrete for a Simply Supported Beam

### Given Data
A residential building has a simply supported RCC beam:
- Clear span = **4.0 m**
- Bearing on wall on each side = **0.3 m**
- Cross-section = **230 mm (width) × 450 mm (overall depth)**

### Step 1: Determine the Effective Length of the Beam

Effective length is the sum of the clear span plus the bearings on both sides. This is the standard convention for KTU and IS 1200 (Part 5).

$$L_{eff} = \text{Clear span} + \text{Left bearing} + \text{Right bearing}$$

$$L_{eff} = 4.0 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 4.6 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Convert Cross-Section Dimensions to Metres

$$b = 230 \text{ mm} = 0.230 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 450 \text{ mm} = 0.450 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Compute Wet Volume of Concrete

$$V_{wet} = L_{eff} \times b \times D$$

$$V_{wet} = 4.6 \times 0.230 \times 0.450$$

$$V_{wet} = 0.4761 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4: Convert Wet Volume to Dry Volume (for Material Estimation)

This conversion is essential when the estimator needs to order **cement, sand, and aggregate** in dry bulk (before water addition). The factor 1.54 accounts for the void-filling and bulking that occurs as cement and aggregate occupy dry space and then hydrate with water.

$$V_{dry} = V_{wet} \times 1.54$$

$$V_{dry} = 0.4761 \times 1.54 = 0.7332 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5: Add 5% Wastage Allowance

$$V_{order} = V_{dry} \times 1.05 = 0.7699 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Final Concrete Quantity for One Beam:** $V_{wet} = 0.4761 \text{ m}^3$ (paid to contractor); $V_{order} = 0.7699 \text{ m}^3$ (used to procure raw materials).

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Reinforcement Steel Calculation & Bar Bending Schedule (BBS)

### Given Data
Continuing with the same beam (4.6 m effective length, 230 mm × 450 mm cross-section):
- Main bars: **4 numbers of 16 mm Ø** at top and bottom corners
- Stirrups: **8 mm Ø @ 150 mm centre-to-centre spacing**
- Clear cover: **25 mm on all sides** (standard for beams)
- Hook length: **9d** (since 16 mm < 25 mm)
- Bend deduction: **2d for 90°, 3d for 135°**

### Step 1: Length of One Main Bar (16 mm Ø)

The straight-line length is the effective span plus hooks at both ends, minus the cover that doesn't contribute to anchorage.

$$L_{straight} = L_{eff} - 2 \times \text{cover} = 4600 - 2 \times 25 = 4550 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{hooks} = 2 \times 9d = 2 \times 9 \times 16 = 288 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{main} = L_{straight} + L_{hooks} = 4550 + 288 = 4838 \text{ mm} = 4.838 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Total Length of Main Steel

$$L_{main,total} = 4 \text{ bars} \times 4.838 = 19.352 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Weight of Main Steel

Using the d²/162 formula, weight per metre of 16 mm bar:

$$W_{16} = \frac{16^2}{162} = \frac{256}{162} = 1.5802 \text{ kg/m}$$

$$W_{main} = L_{main,total} \times W_{16} = 19.352 \times 1.5802 = 30.58 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4: Cutting Length of One Stirrup (8 mm Ø)

A stirrup is a closed loop with two 135° hooks (seismic-style) and two 90° bends.

- Width of stirrup centreline: $a = b - 2 \times \text{cover} - \text{stirrup Ø}$
$$a = 230 - 2 \times 25 - 8 = 172 \text{ mm}$$
- Height of stirrup centreline: $h = D - 2 \times \text{cover} - \text{stirrup Ø}$
$$h = 450 - 2 \times 25 - 8 = 392 \text{ mm}$$
- Perimeter of stirrup (before hooks & bends):
$$P = 2(a + h) = 2(172 + 392) = 1128 \text{ mm}$$
- Hook length addition (two 135° hooks of length 9d each):
$$L_{hook} = 2 \times 9 \times 8 = 144 \text{ mm}$$
- Bend deduction (two 90° bends + two 135° bends, treated as 3d each for the 135°):
$$L_{bend ded} = (2 \times 2d)_{90°} + (2 \times 3d)_{135°} = 4d + 6d = 10d = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, the cutting length of one stirrup is:

$$L_{stirrup} = P + L_{hook} - L_{bend ded} = 1128 + 144 - 80 = 1192 \text{ mm} = 1.192 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5: Number of Stirrups

The number of spacings is the effective length divided by centre-to-centre spacing; we add 1 to get the number of stirrups.

$$N_{stirrups} = \left(\frac{L_{eff}}{\text{spacing}}\right) + 1 = \left(\frac{4600}{150}\right) + 1 = 30.67 + 1 = 31.67$$

Rounding **up** to the next whole number (we never round down a stirrup — it leaves a gap), $N_{stirrups} = 32$.

### Step 6: Total Length of Stirrup Steel

$$L_{stirrup,total} = 32 \times 1.192 = 38.144 \text{ m}$$

### Step 7: Weight of Stirrup Steel

$$W_8 = \frac{8^2}{162} = \frac{64}{162} = 0.3951 \text{ kg/m}$$

$$W_{stirrup} = 38.144 \times 0.3951 = 15.07 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 8: Total Steel Weight for One Beam

$$W_{total} = W_{main} + W_{stirrup} = 30.58 + 15.07 = 45.65 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 9: Add 5% Wastage and 3% Binding Allowances

In KTU estimates, allowances of **3% for binding wire** and **2–3% for cutting wastage** are standard.

$$W_{payable} = 45.65 \times 1.05 = 47.93 \text{ kg} \approx 0.48 \text{ quintal}$$

### Step 10: Verification via Reinforcement Percentage Cross-Check

$$\text{Steel \%} = \frac{W_{total}}{V_{wet} \times 7850} \times 100$$

$$\text{Steel \%} = \frac{45.65}{0.4761 \times 7850} \times 100 = \frac{45.65}{3737.4} \times 100 = 1.22\%$$

This lies within the expected **1.5% to 2.0%** band for beams. The slight under-shoot indicates this is a lightly loaded residential beam — **cross-check successful**.

---

## 3.3 Tabular Bar Bending Schedule (BBS) — Final Output

| Bar Mark | Bar Type | Ø (mm) | Shape | No. of Bars | Cutting Length (m) | Total Length (m) | kg/m | Total Weight (kg) |
|----------|----------|--------|-------|-------------|--------------------|-------------------|------|-------------------|
| A1 | Main bar (bottom) | 16 | Straight with hooks | 2 | 4.838 | 9.676 | 1.5802 | 15.29 |
| A2 | Main bar (top) | 16 | Straight with hooks | 2 | 4.838 | 9.676 | 1.5802 | 15.29 |
| B1 | Stirrup | 8 | Rectangular with 135° hooks | 32 | 1.192 | 38.144 | 0.3951 | 15.07 |
| **Total** | — | — | — | **36** | — | **57.496** | — | **45.65** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 135° Hooks in Stirrups?** KTU 2024-aligned drawings specify **seismic-friendly 135° hooks** (instead of legacy 90°) to prevent stirrup end-opening during cyclic earthquake loading. The 3d deduction (instead of 2d) reflects the longer arc that the bar travels around the 135° bend.

---

## 3.4 Formwork Calculation for the Same Beam

Formwork (centering & shuttering) is the area of **wet concrete surface** that needs temporary support moulding.

For a rectangular beam, the side and bottom surfaces require shuttering; the top is open and not shuttered.

$$A_{formwork} = (\text{2 sides} \times D + b) \times L_{eff}$$

$$A_{formwork} = (2 \times 0.450 + 0.230) \times 4.6$$

$$A_{formwork} = 1.130 \times 4.6 = 5.198 \text{ m}^2 \approx 5.20 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Formwork Quantity for One Beam:** $5.20 \text{ m}^2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of RCC Quantity Take-Off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Structural Drawing] --> B{Identify RCC Element}
    B --> C[Read Dimensions from Drawing]
    C --> D[Compute Concrete Volume m3]
    C --> E[Compute Formwork Area m2]
    C --> F[Compute Steel Weight kg]
    D --> G[Apply Dry Volume Factor 1.54]
    G --> H[Add Wastage 2 to 5%]
    E --> I[Apply IS 1200 Deduction Rules]
    F --> J[Prepare Bar Bending Schedule]
    J --> K[Compute Cutting Length per Bar Shape]
    K --> L[Apply d2 by 162 Formula]
    L --> M[Add Lap and Wastage Allowance]
    H --> N[Consolidated BOQ Entry]
    I --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Rate Analysis per KTU SSR]
    O --> P[Final Abstract of Cost]
```

## 4.2 Logical Block Schematic — RCC Element Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RCC Structural Element] --> B[Concrete Component]
    A --> C[Steel Component]
    A --> D[Formwork Component]
    B --> B1[Volume in m3]
    B --> B2[Grade M20 M25 M30]
    C --> C1[Main Bars Longitudinal]
    C --> C2[Distribution Bars Transverse]
    C --> C3[Stirrups Shear Links]
    D --> D1[Beam Soffit Slab]
    D --> D2[Column Sides]
    D --> D3[Wall Shutters]
```

## 4.3 Workflow Logic — Bar Bending Schedule Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start BBS Process] --> S2[Identify Bar Diameter from Drawing]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Theoretical Length]
    S3 --> S4[Add Hook Length 9d or 6d]
    S4 --> S5[Deduct Bend Losses 2d or 3d]
    S5 --> S6[Get Cutting Length]
    S6 --> S7[Multiply by Number of Bars]
    S7 --> S8[Convert Linear m to Weight kg using d2/162]
    S8 --> S9[Add 5 percent Wastage]
    S9 --> S10[Tabulate Final BBS]
    S10 --> S11[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-to-Reality Mapping:** These flowcharts mirror the exact procedure an estimator follows on a KTU 2024 Scheme project. The first diagram shows the *Three-Pillar Take-Off* (concrete, formwork, steel). The second unpacks each pillar into measurable sub-items. The third isolates the *BBS pipeline* — a standalone deliverable often demanded by the KTU Board as a Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the standard rules of deduction for RCC works as per IS 1200 (Part 5).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per IS 1200 (Part 5), the following rules govern deductions while measuring RCC works:

1. **Openings ≤ 0.1 m²:** No deduction is made for small openings in slabs, beams, or walls. *(1 mark)*
2. **Openings > 0.1 m²:** Full deduction is made for the volume of the opening, and formwork deduction follows the perimeter of the opening. *(1 mark)*
3. **Embedded steel sections in RCC:** Where RCC encases structural steel (e.g., composite columns), the volume of the encased steel is **deducted** from concrete volume. However, the volume of **reinforcement bars is NOT deducted** since steel is measured and paid separately by weight (kg). *(1 mark)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the formula $W = d^2 / 162$ used for computing the weight of steel reinforcement? Show its derivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The formula $W = d^2 / 162$ expresses the weight (in kg) of **one running metre** of a round steel bar of diameter $d$ (in mm). It is derived as follows:

**Starting from the physical definition of weight:**

$$W = A \times \rho = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Convert diameter from mm to m:** $d$ in mm means the cross-section area uses $d^2$ in mm²; density 7850 kg/m³ must be multiplied by $10^{-6}$ to convert mm² to m².

$$W = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times 7850 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}$$

**Simplify the constant:**

$$\frac{\pi \times 7850}{4 \times 10^6} = \frac{24649.0}{4000000} \approx 0.006162 = \frac{1}{162}$$

**Therefore:**

$$W = \frac{d^2}{162} \text{ kg/m}$$

The "162" emerges from the mathematical identity $\pi \times 7850 / 4 \times 10^{-6}$, which is the reciprocal of approximately 162.25, rounded to 162 for engineering convenience. *(3 marks: 1 for stating the formula's purpose, 1 for the constant derivation, 1 for final form)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A simply supported RCC beam in a residential building has the following details:**
- Clear span: **5.0 m**
- Bearing on each support: **300 mm**
- Cross-section: **230 mm wide × 500 mm deep**
- Reinforcement: **4 numbers of 20 mm Ø bars** as main bars at corners
- Stirrups: **8 mm Ø @ 150 mm c/c**
- Cover: **25 mm on all sides**
- Concrete grade: **M20 (1 : 1.5 : 3)**

**Compute:**
**(a)** The volume of concrete in m³ for the beam. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** The total quantity of steel reinforcement in kg, with a full Bar Bending Schedule. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Effective Length of Beam**

The effective length is the clear span plus the bearings on both sides. This is the convention followed in IS 1200 (Part 5) and KTU BOQ items.

$$L_{eff} = 5.0 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 5.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Cross-Section Conversion to Metres**

The drawing dimensions are in millimetres; they must be converted to metres for direct multiplication into cubic metres.

$$b = 230 \text{ mm} = 0.23 \text{ m}, \quad D = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Wet Volume**

The wet volume is the product of effective length, width, and overall depth. This is the volume that is billed.

$$V_{wet} = 5.6 \times 0.23 \times 0.50 = 0.6440 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{(7 marks completed)}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Cutting Length of One Main Bar (20 mm Ø)**

The straight length spans the effective length minus the cover, plus hooks at both ends (each hook = 9d since Ø20 < 25 mm).

$$L_{straight} = 5600 - 2(25) = 5550 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{hooks} = 2 \times 9 \times 20 = 360 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{main,one} = 5550 + 360 = 5910 \text{ mm} = 5.910 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Total Main Steel Length and Weight**

$$L_{main,total} = 4 \times 5.910 = 23.640 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{20} = \frac{20^2}{162} = \frac{400}{162} = 2.4691 \text{ kg/m}$$

$$W_{main} = 23.640 \times 2.4691 = 58.37 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Cutting Length of One Stirrup (8 mm Ø)**

- Width of stirrup centreline: $a = 230 - 2(25) - 8 = 172 \text{ mm}$
- Height of stirrup centreline: $h = 500 - 2(25) - 8 = 442 \text{ mm}$
- Perimeter: $P = 2(172 + 442) = 1228 \text{ mm}$
- Hooks: $2 \times 9d = 2 \times 9 \times 8 = 144 \text{ mm}$
- Bend deductions: $10d = 80 \text{ mm}$

$$L_{stirrup,one} = 1228 + 144 - 80 = 1292 \text{ mm} = 1.292 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Number of Stirrups and Weight**

$$N_{stirrups} = \frac{5600}{150} + 1 = 37.33 + 1 = 38.33 \rightarrow 39 \text{ (round up)}$$

$$L_{stirrup,total} = 39 \times 1.292 = 50.388 \text{ m}$$

$$W_8 = \frac{8^2}{162} = 0.3951 \text{ kg/m}$$

$$W_{stirrup} = 50.388 \times 0.3951 = 19.91 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Total Steel with 5% Wastage**

$$W_{total} = (58.37 + 19.91) \times 1.05 = 78.28 \times 1.05 = 82.19 \text{ kg}$$

| Bar Mark | Ø (mm) | Shape | No. | Cut Length (m) | Total Length (m) | kg/m | Weight (kg) |
|----------|--------|-------|-----|----------------|-------------------|------|-------------|
| A1 | 20 | Straight + 2 hooks | 4 | 5.910 | 23.640 | 2.4691 | 58.37 |
| B1 | 8 | Rectangular stirrup + 135° hooks | 39 | 1.292 | 50.388 | 0.3951 | 19.91 |
| | | | | | | **Total** | **78.28** |

**Final Answer with 5% wastage:** **Total steel = 82.19 kg ≈ 0.822 quintal.** *(7 marks completed)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the procedure for preparing a **Bar Bending Schedule (BBS)** with the help of a neat labelled format. Why is the d²/162 formula universally used in BBS preparation? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An RCC column of size **300 mm × 450 mm** has a clear height of **3.0 m** with **6 numbers of 20 mm Ø longitudinal bars** and **8 mm Ø lateral ties @ 200 mm c/c**. Compute:
- (i) Volume of concrete in m³
- (ii) Total weight of steel in kg (including 5% wastage)

Assume cover = 40 mm, effective length = clear height + 1.0 m for footing embedment and lap. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Procedure for Preparing a Bar Bending Schedule (BBS):**

1. **Read structural drawings** to identify each bar's location, diameter, and shape. *(1 mark)*
2. **List each bar mark** (A1, A2, B1, etc.) corresponding to one unique shape/dia combination. *(1 mark)*
3. **Compute the cutting length** of each unique bar using the formula:
   $$\text{Cutting Length} = \text{Straight Length} + \text{Hook Lengths} - \text{Bend Deductions}$$
   Hooks = 9d (for ≤25 mm) or 6d (for >25 mm). Bend deductions = 2d (90°) or 3d (135°). *(2 marks)*
4. **Count the number of bars** of each mark from the drawing. *(1 mark)*
5. **Calculate total length** = number of bars × cutting length. *(1 mark)*
6. **Convert to weight** using $W = d^2 / 162$ kg/m. Sum to obtain total steel. *(1 mark)*

**Why d²/162 is universally used:** The formula arises directly from the geometry of a round bar (cross-section area = $\pi d^2 / 4$) multiplied by steel density (7850 kg/m³). The constant 162 makes manual calculation easy — a 16 mm bar weighs ~1.58 kg/m, a 20 mm bar weighs ~2.47 kg/m, memorised instantly. This convenience, combined with its accuracy, has made it the standard across all Indian engineering estimates. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Volume of Concrete:**

- Effective height = $3.0 + 1.0 = 4.0 \text{ m}$
- Cross-section = $0.300 \times 0.450 = 0.135 \text{ m}^2$
- Volume = $4.0 \times 0.135 = 0.540 \text{ m}^3$

**(ii) Steel Calculation:**

**Longitudinal bars (20 mm Ø, 6 nos):**
- Cutting length per bar = effective height + hooks (9d × 2) − cover reduction
- $L_{straight} = 4000 - 2(40) = 3920 \text{ mm}$
- $L_{hooks} = 2 \times 9 \times 20 = 360 \text{ mm}$
- $L_{one} = 3920 + 360 = 4280 \text{ mm} = 4.280 \text{ m}$
- Total longitudinal length = $6 \times 4.280 = 25.680 \text{ m}$
- $W_{20} = 2.4691 \text{ kg/m}$
- $W_{long} = 25.680 \times 2.4691 = 63.40 \text{ kg}$

**Lateral ties (8 mm Ø @ 200 mm c/c):**
- Tie centreline dimensions: $a = 300 - 2(40) - 8 = 212 \text{ mm}$; $h = 450 - 2(40) - 8 = 362 \text{ mm}$
- Perimeter = $2(212 + 362) = 1148 \text{ mm}$
- Hooks = $2 \times 9 \times 8 = 144 \text{ mm}$
- Bend deduction = $80 \text{ mm}$
- Cutting length = $1148 + 144 - 80 = 1212 \text{ mm} = 1.212 \text{ m}$
- Number of ties = $(4000/200) + 1 = 21$ nos
- Total tie length = $21 \times 1.212 = 25.452 \text{ m}$
- $W_{ties} = 25.452 \times 0.3951 = 10.06 \text{ kg}$

**Total steel before wastage:**

$$W = 63.40 + 10.06 = 73.46 \text{ kg}$$

**With 5% wastage:**

$$W_{final} = 73.46 \times 1.05 = 77.13 \text{ kg} \approx 0.77 \text{ quintal}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in RCC Questions — Read Before Writing!**
>
> 1. **The "162" Sign Error:** Students often write $d^2 \times 162$ instead of $d^2 / 162$. The result is ~25,000× larger, instantly flagging the answer as wrong. **Always** use the **division** form: $W = d^2 / 162$.
>
> 2. **Bearing vs Clear Span Confusion:** A frequent 2-mark loss is using *only the clear span* (e.g., 4.0 m) instead of *clear span + bearings* (4.6 m) for a beam's effective length. Always add bearings to clear span.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Add Hooks:** Many students compute bar length as $L_{eff} - 2 \times \text{cover}$ only, forgetting the 9d hooks at both ends. Hooks typically add ~290 mm for 16 mm bars — a non-trivial 6% of bar length.
>
> 4. **Rounding Down Stirrup Count:** You must **always round UP** the number of stirrups. Rounding down leaves a length of beam without shear reinforcement — a structural failure.
>
> 5. **Confusing 90° and 135° Bend Deductions:** 90° hook = 2d deduction; 135° hook = 3d deduction. Seismic-zone drawings specify 135°; mixing these gives wrong stirrup cutting length.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Wastage Allowance:** KTU standard rate analysis always includes 2–5% for concrete and 3–5% for steel. Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks despite the math being correct.
>
> 7. **Mixing Up Units:** Concrete in m³, formwork in m², steel in kg. Writing "kg" for concrete or "m³" for steel is a unit error worth a 1-mark penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RCC = Concrete + Steel + Formwork** — three separate payable items in any KTU BOQ.
- **Concrete volume** = effective length × cross-section; remember to add bearings for beams; convert wet to dry volume using ×1.54 for material ordering.
- **Steel weight** = $(d^2 / 162) \times$ total bar length; the constant 162 is a non-negotiable memory item.
- **Hook lengths:** 9d for bars ≤ 25 mm Ø; 6d for bars > 25 mm Ø.
- **Bend deductions:** 2d (90°), 3d (135°), 1d (45°).
- **Lap lengths:** 50d in tension, 40d in compression (IS 456).
- **Stirrup count:** Always round **up** to next whole number; never round down.
- **IS 1200 Part 5 Deductions:** Openings ≤ 0.1 m² ignored; openings > 0.1 m² fully deducted; **reinforcement steel volume NOT deducted** from concrete.
- **Reinforcement cross-check percentages:** Slab 0.8–1.5%, Beam 1.5–2.0%, Column 2.0–4.0% (by concrete volume). If your calculated % falls outside, re-verify.
- **Formwork area** for a beam = (2 × depth + width) × effective length; for a column = perimeter × height.
- **Wastage allowances** to remember: Concrete 2–5%, Steel 3–5%, Binding wire 1%.
- **KTU-specific hooks** (135° stirrups, seismic detailing) are now part of the 2024 Scheme syllabus — expect at least one question on seismic-friendly detailing per paper.
- **Quick mental check:** Volume of M20 concrete per 100 kg of cement ≈ 0.7 m³; 1 m³ of M20 needs ~7 bags of cement.
- **BBS sequence (mnemonic: R-L-C-H-B-W):** Read drawing → List marks → Compute cutting length → Compute Hooks → Bend deductions → tabulate Weights.
- **Formwork cost heuristic:** Add 6–8% of total concrete cost to account for shuttering in any preliminary estimate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finishing Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finishing Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Finishing Work** in Quantity Surveying refers to all construction activities carried out **after the structural carcass of a building is completed**, primarily aimed at protecting, decorating, and enhancing the **functional and aesthetic performance** of exposed surfaces (walls, floors, ceilings, joinery, and external façades). It is measured as a **separate trade item** under the *Standard Schedule of Rates (SSR)* and is governed by the method-of-measurement rules laid down in **IS 1200 (Parts relevant to finishing works)**.

In a KTU valuation problem, *finishing work* is a non-structural item that is billed either:
- **In percentage rates** (for lump-sum tendering – e.g., 8% to 12% of building cost), or
- **In detail-item rates** (for item-rate tenders – each activity measured in m², m³, kg, or litres and multiplied by its unit rate).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Skin and Suit" of a Building

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a building the same way you think of a **human body**:
> - **Structure (RCC, masonry)** = the **bones and muscles** — gives shape, but is rough and bare.
> - **Finishing Work** = the **skin, clothes, and cosmetics** — protects the body from weather, gives comfort, and decides how the person (building) *looks* to the world.
>
> Just as a tailor measures every metre of cloth, every button, and every stitch separately, a Quantity Surveyor measures every square metre of plaster, every litre of paint, and every metre of skirting **independently** before pricing the building.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **Wall plaster thickness (internal)** : **12 mm** (single coat) or **15 mm** (two-coat)
- **Ceiling plaster thickness** : **6 mm** to **12 mm**
- **External plaster (sand-face / rough-cast)** : **15 mm** to **20 mm**
- **Standard deduction for openings (Doors/Windows) for plastering** : **> 3 m²** — full deduction, plus **50% of opening area** added back for jambs/soffits (as per IS 1200 convention)
- **Standard deduction for openings for whitewashing/painting** : **> 0.5 m²** — full deduction; no re-addition.
- **Deduction for floor finishing** : **Openings > 0.5 m²** are fully deducted.
- **Putty thickness (POP / acrylic)** : **1.0 mm to 1.5 mm** per coat.
- **Paint coverage (emulsion, 2 coats on plastered surface)** : **≈ 10 to 12 m² per litre**.
- **Varnish coverage** : **≈ 10 to 12 m² per litre** per coat.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics must be memorised — they form the basis of almost every 14-mark valuation problem in Module 2.

### 1.4 Classification of Finishing Work (As Per KTU SSR Practice)

| S.No. | Category | Example Activities |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wall Finishing** | Plastering (internal, external), pointing, distempering, painting, wall putty, wall paper |
| 2 | **Floor & Floor Finish** | IPS (Indian Patent Stone), mosaic, marble, granite, vitrified/ceramic tiles, skirting/dado |
| 3 | **Ceiling Finish** | POP, false ceiling (gypsum/PVC), distemper, painting |
| 4 | **Joinery Finish** | Polishing, varnishing, painting of doors/windows, French polishing |
| 5 | **Waterproofing & Damp-Proof** | Terrace waterproofing, coba treatment, chemical DPC |
| 6 | **External Façade Finish** | Cladding, texture paint, weather-proof coating |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Why Finishing Work is Measured Separately

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a separate bill item?**
> 1. **Heterogeneous rate** — A mason laying bricks is paid `₹/m³`, but a painter is paid `₹/m²` with different overheads.
> 2. **Variable quality** — Finishes are **visible & auditable**; clients can complain easily. Hence, item-rate measurement is preferred for transparency.
> 3. **Maintenance** — Finishes are periodically renewed; their cost must be **separable** from the structure (which is not renewed).
> 4. **Wastage & labour** — Each finish has a different wastage percentage (paint: 5–10%, tile: 5%, marble: 10–15%).

### 2.2 Standard Methods of Measurement (IS 1200 — Relevant Parts)

| IS Code | Title | Key Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 1200 (Pt 12)** | *Plastering & Pointing* | Measured in **m²**. Deductions for openings > 3 m². |
| **IS 1200 (Pt 13)** | *White-washing, Colour-washing, Distempering* | Measured in **m²**. No addition for jambs. |
| **IS 1200 (Pt 15)** | *Painting, Varnishing, Oiling* | Measured in **m²** for walls; **per leaf** for doors/windows. |
| **IS 1200 (Pt 11)** | *Floor Finishes (Pavements, Skirting)* | Floor in m²; Skirting in **running metre (m)** at specified height. |
| **IS 1200 (Pt 7)** | *Woodwork* (joinery finish) | Door/window paint measured by **number of leaves** (one coat = 1.0 leaf, two coats = 2.0 leaves). |

### 2.3 Deduction Rules (Most Exam-Significant)

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **#1 scoring area** in KTU valuation problems. Memorise the table below word-for-word.

| Activity | Opening Deduction Threshold | Treatment of jambs/soffits | Measured In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plastering | **> 3 m²** (any single opening) | Add **50% of opening area** | m² |
| White-washing / Distempering | **> 0.5 m²** | **No addition** | m² |
| Painting (wall) | **> 0.5 m²** | **No addition** | m² |
| Painting (door/window) | No deduction | Measured by leaves (not area) | number of leaves |
| Floor finish | **> 0.5 m²** | Not applicable (floor is below) | m² |
| Skirting | No deduction | — | running metre |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{\text{plaster}} = L \times H - \text{Large openings} + 0.5 \times \text{Large openings}$ | Net plastered area | m² |
| 2 | $A_{\text{plaster}} = L \times H - \text{Small openings}$ (no addition) | When all openings ≤ 3 m² | m² |
| 3 | $A_{\text{paint}} = L \times H - \text{All openings} > 0.5 \text{ m}^2$ | Painted area (no jamb addition) | m² |
| 4 | $A_{\text{ceiling}} = L \times B$ | Ceiling plaster/paint | m² |
| 5 | $V_{\text{plaster}} = A_{\text{plaster}} \times t$ (t = thickness, in m) | Volume of plaster (for cement/sand calc.) | m³ |
| 6 | $n_{\text{leaves}} = N_{d} + 2 N_{w}$ | Painting quantity for joinery (1 leaf = 1 coat) | leaves |
| 7 | $A_{\text{skirting}} = L \times h_s$ | Skirting area (h = 100 mm typical) | m² or m |
| 8 | $W_{\text{cement}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{plaster}} \times \text{Ratio}_\text{cement}}{\text{Total ratio}} \times 1440$ | Dry cement quantity in kg | kg |
| 9 | $W_{\text{sand}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{plaster}} \times \text{Ratio}_\text{sand}}{\text{Total ratio}} \times \text{loose vol. factor}$ | Sand quantity | m³ |
| 10 | $C_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{A}{\text{Coverage per litre}}$ | Litres of paint required (per coat) | litre |
| 11 | $C_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{coats}} \times C_{\text{coat}} \times (1 + \text{wastage})$ | Total paint with wastage | litre |
| 12 | $A_{\text{tile}} = A_{\text{floor}} \times (1 + \text{wastage\%})$ | Tile order quantity | m² |
| 13 | $\text{Cost} = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Rate from SSR}$ | Item cost | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Loose volume factor** for sand ≈ **1.333** (i.e., 1 m³ wet mortar needs ~1.333 m³ loose sand). **Density of cement** ≈ **1440 kg/m³** (loose) or **1500 kg/m³** (compacted in bag).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Tender preparation** — Finishing items form **20–35%** of total building cost; even small measurement errors cause large cost overruns.
- **Dispute resolution** — A correctly measured deduction table avoids site disputes between contractor and client.
- **Material procurement** — Painters, tile-setters, and POP workers order materials based on QS quantities; under-measurement leads to project delay.
- **Life-cycle costing** — Finishes are renewed every 5–10 years; isolating their cost helps estimate *maintenance budgets* accurately.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Plastering Quantity (Mortar + Cement + Sand)

> **Problem:** A room of internal dimensions **4.5 m × 3.5 m** with a height of **3.2 m** has one door **1.0 m × 2.1 m** and one window **1.2 m × 1.2 m**. Internal plaster is **12 mm thick** in **1 : 4 (cement : sand)** mortar. Calculate:
> (a) Net plastered area, (b) Volume of wet mortar, (c) Quantity of cement and sand required.

**Step 1 — Total wall area (internal face):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Perimeter} &= 2 \times (L + B) = 2 \times (4.5 + 3.5) = 16.0 \text{ m} \\
A_{\text{wall}} &= \text{Perimeter} \times H = 16.0 \times 3.2 = 51.2 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{ceiling}} &= L \times B = 4.5 \times 3.5 = 15.75 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{internal plaster}} &= A_{\text{wall}} + A_{\text{ceiling}} = 51.2 + 15.75 = 66.95 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Deductions for openings (IS 1200 Pt 12 rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{door}} &= 1.0 \times 2.1 = 2.10 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\le 3 \text{ m}^2, \text{so NO deduction}) \\
A_{\text{window}} &= 1.2 \times 1.2 = 1.44 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\le 3 \text{ m}^2, \text{so NO deduction}) \\
\therefore A_{\text{deduct}} &= 0 \\
\boxed{A_{\text{net plaster}} = 66.95 - 0 = 66.95 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic explanation:** Because both openings are **≤ 3 m²**, the deduction rule says we deduct **nothing** for plastering. The 50% jamb addition rule is **only** triggered when an opening individually exceeds 3 m² (e.g., a large shop shutter or garage door).

**Step 3 — Volume of wet mortar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{wet}} &= A_{\text{net}} \times t = 66.95 \times 0.012 = 0.8034 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Dry volume of mortar (with shrinkage factor 1.33):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{dry}} &= V_{\text{wet}} \times 1.33 = 0.8034 \times 1.33 = 1.0685 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cement and sand quantities (1 : 4 ratio, total parts = 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{cement}} &= \frac{1}{1+4} \times V_{\text{dry}} = \frac{1}{5} \times 1.0685 = 0.2137 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{\text{sand}} &= \frac{4}{5} \times V_{\text{dry}} = \frac{4}{5} \times 1.0685 = 0.8548 \text{ m}^3 \\
W_{\text{cement}} &= V_{\text{cement}} \times 1440 = 0.2137 \times 1440 = 307.7 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Bags of cement} &= \frac{307.7}{50} = 6.15 \approx 7 \text{ bags} \\
V_{\text{sand (loose)}} &= 0.8548 \times 1.333 = 1.1394 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why 1.33?** Wet mortar shrinks on drying as water evaporates. 1 m³ wet needs 1.33 m³ dry mix of cement + sand to be fully compacted in the wet state.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Painting Quantity for Walls + Joinery

> **Problem:** A hall of size **6.0 m × 4.0 m**, height **3.6 m**, has **2 doors (1.0 m × 2.1 m)** and **3 windows (1.5 m × 1.2 m)**. Apply **plastic emulsion paint (2 coats)** on walls and ceiling. Apply **enamel paint (2 coats)** on door/window joinery. Find:
> (a) Painted wall area, (b) Litres of emulsion needed, (c) Joinery painting leaves.

**Step 1 — Wall + ceiling area (gross):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{wall}} &= 2 \times (6.0 + 4.0) \times 3.6 = 72.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{ceiling}} &= 6.0 \times 4.0 = 24.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{gross}} &= 72.0 + 24.0 = 96.0 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Deductions (IS 1200 Pt 15: deduct openings > 0.5 m², no jamb addition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{doors}} &= 2 \times (1.0 \times 2.1) = 4.20 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{both} > 0.5, \text{deduct}) \\
A_{\text{windows}} &= 3 \times (1.5 \times 1.2) = 5.40 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{all} > 0.5, \text{deduct}) \\
A_{\text{deduct}} &= 4.20 + 5.40 = 9.60 \text{ m}^2 \\
\boxed{A_{\text{net painted}} = 96.0 - 9.60 = 86.40 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Litres of emulsion paint (coverage ≈ 10 m²/L/coat, wastage 10%):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{per coat}} &= 86.40 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{1 \text{ coat}} &= \frac{86.40}{10} = 8.64 \text{ L} \\
V_{2 \text{ coats}} &= 2 \times 8.64 = 17.28 \text{ L} \\
V_{\text{with wastage}} &= 17.28 \times 1.10 = 19.01 \text{ L} \\
\boxed{V_{\text{emulsion}} \approx 19.0 \text{ L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Joinery painting (enamel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Doors} &: 2 \text{ leaves} \times 2 \text{ coats} = 4.0 \text{ leaves} \\
\text{Windows} &: 3 \text{ leaves} \times 2 \text{ coats} = 6.0 \text{ leaves} \\
\boxed{\text{Total} = 10.0 \text{ leaves of enamel}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction** — Walls are measured in **m²**, but door/window joinery is measured in **"leaves per coat"**. One leaf = one full painting operation of one side of one shutter or one window. This is the **IS 1200 (Pt 15)** convention.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Floor Finish + Skirting

> **Problem:** A room **5.0 m × 3.5 m** is to be finished with **vitrified tiles (600 mm × 600 mm)** and **100 mm skirting** in same material. Calculate (a) Tile order quantity (5% wastage), (b) Skirting in m² and m run.

**Step 1 — Floor area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{floor}} &= 5.0 \times 3.5 = 17.50 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Tile order quantity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{order}} &= 17.50 \times 1.05 = 18.375 \approx 18.4 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Number of tiles} &= \frac{18.4}{0.6 \times 0.6} = \frac{18.4}{0.36} = 51.11 \approx 52 \text{ tiles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Skirting (running metre × height):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{perimeter}} &= 2 \times (5.0 + 3.5) = 17.0 \text{ m} \\
A_{\text{skirting}} &= 17.0 \times 0.10 = 1.70 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Tile area required (5% wastage)} &= 1.70 \times 1.05 = 1.785 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU SSR practice, skirting is often billed by the **running metre (Rmt)** directly with the height mentioned (e.g., 100 mm high skirting — `₹/m`).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Rule to Apply?

| If the Question Asks… | Use This Rule | Watch Out For |
|---|---|---|
| Plaster quantity for *internal* 12 mm | IS 1200 Pt 12 | Openings > 3 m² trigger 50% jamb addition |
| Distemper for *walls* | IS 1200 Pt 13 | Openings > 0.5 m² deducted, **no** jamb addition |
| Enamel paint for *wooden door* | IS 1200 Pt 15 | Count by **leaves**, not by m² |
| Skirting in *vitrified tile* | SSR practice | Billed in m run (with height 100 mm) |
| Cement quantity for plaster | Ratio method | Use **dry vol. = wet vol. × 1.33** |
| Floor tile order | Quantity + wastage % | Add 5% (cutting) to 10% (breakage) |
| Waterproofing (terrace) | m² | Treated as continuous surface, no deduction for pipe crossings |
| French polish for wood | Per leaf per coat | Same convention as enamel paint |
