List any four flooring items that appear in the latest DSR along with their item numbers.

**Model Answer (sample):**
1. Item 11.1 – Cement concrete 1:2:4 flooring, 40 mm thick.
2. Item 11.5 – Kota stone slab 22 mm thick, machine cut.
3. Item 11.13 – Vitrified ceramic tile 600×600 mm, 10 mm thick.
4. Item 11.21 – Polished granite slab, 18 mm thick.
*(Note: actual numbers should be verified against the DSR edition provided in the question paper.)* *(2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

> **Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer any ONE from the two alternatives.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Analyse the rate per $m^2$ for **50 mm thick cement concrete flooring 1:1.5:3 (1 cement : 1.5 coarse sand : 3 graded stone aggregate 20 mm nominal size)** with a floating coat of neat cement. Use the latest DAR data given in the supplementary sheet.

**OR (Internal Choice)**

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Calculate the rate per $m^2$ for **Kota stone flooring, 22 mm thick, machine cut, laid over 20 mm thick CM 1:4 mortar bed**, including skirting 100 mm high. Assume standard DAR labour and material constants.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Data Table (assumed from question):**

| Item | Constant/Rate |
|------|---------------|
| Cement bag (50 kg) | ₹ 380 |
| Sand ($m^3$) | ₹ 1,800 |
| 20 mm aggregate ($m^3$) | ₹ 2,100 |
| Mason 1st class/day | ₹ 800 |
| Beldar/day | ₹ 500 |
| Wastage: cement 2 %, others 10 % |
| OH = 15 %, CP = 10 % |

**Step 2 — Wet volume of CC 1:1.5:3 for 1 $m^2$ at 50 mm:**

$$
V_{\text{wet}} = 1 \times 1 \times 0.050 = 0.050 \, m^3
$$

**Step 3 — Dry volume:**

$$
V_{\text{dry}} = 0.050 \times 1.33 = 0.0665 \, m^3
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of ratios = 1 + 1.5 + 3 = 5.5**

**Step 5 — Cement:**

$$
V_c = \frac{1}{5.5} \times 0.0665 = 0.01209 \, m^3 \Rightarrow 17.41 \, \text{kg} = 0.3552 \, \text{bags}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = 0.3552 \times 1.02 \times 380 = \text{₹ } 137.62
$$

**Step 6 — Sand (1.5 parts, 10 % wastage):**

$$
V_s = \frac{1.5}{5.5} \times 0.0665 \times 1.10 = 0.01995 \, m^3 \Rightarrow 0.01995 \times 1800 = \text{₹ } 35.91
$$

**Step 7 — Aggregate (3 parts, 10 % wastage):**

$$
V_a = \frac{3}{5.5} \times 0.0665 \times 1.10 = 0.03990 \, m^3 \Rightarrow 0.03990 \times 2100 = \text{₹ } 83.79
$$

**Step 8 — Neat cement floating coat (2 kg/$m^2$ at ₹ 7.6/kg):**

$$
\text{Cost} = 2.0 \times 7.60 = \text{₹ } 15.20
$$

**Step 9 — Sundries 1 % of material:**

$$
M = (137.62 + 35.91 + 83.79 + 15.20) \times 1.01 = \text{₹ } 275.27
$$

**Step 10 — Labour (for 0.05 $m^3$, scaling from 1 $m^3$ basis of 0.12 day for mason, 0.48 day for beldar):**

$$
L = (1 \times 0.12 \times 800 + 4 \times 0.12 \times 500) \times 0.05 = (96 + 240) \times 0.05 = \text{₹ } 16.80
$$

**Step 11 — Final Rate:**

$$
R = (275.27 + 16.80) \times 1.265 = 292.07 \times 1.265 = \text{₹ } 369.47 \text{ per } m^2
$$

> **Final Answer: ₹ 369.47 per $m^2$** *(7 marks: 2 for data, 3 for material, 1 for labour, 1 for OH+CP)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Analyse the rate per $m^2$ for **18 mm thick polished marble flooring (white)** laid over **20 mm thick CM 1:4 mortar bed** including **skirting 100 mm high**, using latest DAR data.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare the total cost of flooring a hall of size **10 m × 8 m** using (i) Vitrified tile flooring, and (ii) Marble flooring, using the rates you have computed. Which is more economical and why?

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Outline:**

**Step 1 — Marble slab + Mortar (CM 1:4, 20 mm):**

- Mortar dry vol = 0.020 × 1.33 = 0.0266 $m^3$
- Cement = $\frac{1}{5} \times 0.0266 \times 1440 \times 1.02/50 = 0.1563$ bags → ₹ 59.39
- Sand = $\frac{4}{5} \times 0.0266 \times 1.10 = 0.02341 \, m^3$ → ₹ 42.14

**Step 2 — Marble slab 18 mm, 5 % wastage:** 1.05 × ₹ 1200 = ₹ 1,260

**Step 3 — Polishing & finishing labour:** ₹ 80

**Step 4 — Skirting 100 mm high (per m² of floor, skirting = 0.1 m of wall per m² floor):** ₹ 35

**Step 5 — Sundries 1 %:** ~₹ 14.77

**Step 6 — Total $M + L \approx ₹ 1,491$**

**Step 7 — Apply multiplier 1.265:**

$$
R \approx \text{₹ } 1,886 \text{ per } m^2
$$

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

Total floor area = 10 × 8 = 80 $m^2$

| Flooring Type | Rate ($/m^2$) | Total Cost (₹) |
|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Vitrified tile | 657 | **52,560** |
| Marble (18 mm polished) | 1,886 | **1,50,880** |

**Vitrified tile is more economical** (difference = ₹ 98,320, ~65 % cheaper). However, marble is preferred in heritage/commercial buildings for durability, aesthetics, and resale value. The choice depends on **functional requirement, life cycle, and aesthetic intent** — a concept of value engineering, not just initial cost. *(7 marks: 2 for area, 2 for comparison, 2 for discussion, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in flooring rate analysis:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.33 dry-volume factor** — instant 30 % cost error. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Not adding wastage** — cement 2 %, sand/aggregate 10 %, tiles 5 %. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Omitting Overhead & Contractor's Profit (OH + CP = 26.5 %)** — answer looks 27 % too low. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not converting kg → bags** of cement correctly (÷ 50, not ÷ 1.0). (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping skirting/dado** when the DSR item description mentions "including skirting." (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not stating the data in a clear table at the start** — examiners expect a "Data Should Be Given" table. (–1 mark)
> 7. **Confusing $m^3$ with $m^2$** units in the final answer. (–0.5 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **DSR 2023** is the latest CPWD Schedule of Rates; **DAR 2023** is the latest Analysis of Rates.
- [ ] Always start the answer with a **clear "Data Table"** — this is the first thing an examiner looks for.
- [ ] **Wet-to-dry factor = 1.33** is sacred — apply it for all mortar/concrete volume calculations.
- [ ] **Wastage factors:** Cement 2 %, Sand 10 %, Aggregate 10 %, Tiles/Stone 5 %.
- [ ] **Cement density = 1440 kg/$m^3$**, **1 bag = 50 kg**.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $R = (M + L + P) \times 1.265$ (where 1.265 = 1.15 × 1.10).
- [ ] **Standard OH = 15 %**, **Standard CP = 10 %** (CPWD norms).
- [ ] Don't forget **carriage/lead charges** for sand and aggregate using the DAR Lead Statement.
- [ ] **Sundries** = 1 %–2 % of (M + L) to cover water, tools, curing, etc.
- [ ] Unit is always **₹ per $m^2$** for flooring items (and ₹/m for skirting/dado).
- [ ] For **flooring** → measure floor area; for **skirting** → measure along wall length × height.
- [ ] The **flooring cross-section** has 4–5 layers: sub-base → PCC base → mortar bed → finish layer → skirting.
- [ ] **Skirting rate** ≈ 10 %–20 % of the flooring rate (approximate thumb rule).
- [ ] **Floor finishes comparison:** cheapest = IPS → Ceramic → Kota → Vitrified → Marble → Granite → Wooden.
- [ ] When asked for a "**valuation**" question, combine DSR unit rate with measured quantities to find **Total Cost = Rate × Quantity × Floor Area**.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2: Flooring with Reference to Latest DSR and Latest DAR**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detailed Estimate – Preparation of Detailed Measurement using Centre Line Method and Short Wall-Long Wall (Separate Wall) Method for RCC Single-Storeyed Building (Flat Roof) including Stair Cabin – Residential/Office/School Building; BOQ Preparation of a Single-Storeyed RCC Building Work; Material Quantity Calculation of the Items of Work (Rubble)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Detailed Estimate – Preparation of Detailed Measurement using Centre Line Method and Short Wall-Long Wall (Separate Wall) Method for RCC Single-Storeyed Building (Flat Roof) including Stair Cabin – Residential/Office/School Building; BOQ Preparation of a Single-Storeyed RCC Building Work; Material Quantity Calculation of the Items of Work (Rubble)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Detailed Estimate – Formal Academic Definition

A **Detailed Estimate** is an exhaustive, item-wise costing document prepared prior to the execution of any civil engineering project. It is prepared by working out the **quantities of all items of work** required to complete the structure, multiplied by their respective **prevailing schedule of rates (SOR)** to arrive at the total probable project cost.

As per **IS 1200 (Methods of Measurement of Building Works)** and the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCCET601*, a detailed estimate comprises:

- **Detailed Measurements (Dimensions Book)**: Length, breadth, and depth/height recorded against each item.
- **Abstract of Estimate (BOQ)**: A summary sheet listing the quantities and rates of each item.
- **Material Statements**: Quantities of cement, sand, aggregate, bricks/blocks, steel, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Favourite**: A detailed estimate question is a guaranteed **14-mark question** in End Semester Examinations (ESE) under Module 3. Mastery of *Centre Line Method* AND *Long Wall–Short Wall Method* is non-negotiable.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Module 3 Estimation

### Pillar 1 – Centre Line Method (CLM)

The **Centre Line Method** calculates quantities by measuring the **total running length of the centre line of each type of wall/footing** and then multiplying it by the corresponding cross-sectional dimensions (width × height/depth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea**: Measure *once* the centre-to-centre perimeter of the building, and apply it to all subsequent items like excavation, PCC, foundation, DPC, brickwork, plastering, etc. This dramatically reduces duplication of work in the Dimension Book.

### Pillar 2 – Short Wall–Long Wall Method (SWLW)

The **Short Wall–Long Wall Method (Separate Wall Method)** is the traditional, classical approach. Here, the internal plan dimensions of the building are taken, and the **outer (long) walls** and **inner (short) walls** are measured separately.

- **Long Wall** = Wall whose length (centre-to-centre) is along the *longer* direction of the building.
- **Short Wall** = Wall whose length (centre-to-centre) is along the *shorter* direction of the building.

### Pillar 3 – Bill of Quantities (BOQ)

A **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** is a standardised tabular document that lists **every pay item**, its **quantity**, its **unit**, and its **rate**, summing up to a grand total. It forms the **legal and contractual basis** for tendering and payment.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Cake-Baking" Analogy

Imagine you are a baker asked to estimate the cost of icing a **rectangular birthday cake**:

- **Centre Line Method** is like running a single thread around the *middle* of the cake's edge and measuring it once. You then multiply this thread length by the height and thickness of icing. *One length measurement serves all cross-sections*.
- **Long Wall–Short Wall Method** is like carefully measuring the icing needed on **each of the four side faces separately** – the two long faces (length × height) and the two short faces (breadth × height). Then you add them. *More measurements, more chance of error, but more transparent*.
- **BOQ** is the final *invoice* given to the customer listing "Icing – X grams @ Rs.Y/g = Rs.Z" along with other items.

Both methods must give the **same final quantity**; the difference is purely in the **measurement workflow**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Units and Conventions (KTU Board Norms)

| Parameter | Standard Unit | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Length / Breadth / Height | **metre (m)** | m |
| Earthwork excavation | **cubic metre (cum / m³)** | m³ |
| PCC / RCC / Brickwork | cubic metre (m³) | m³ |
| Plastering / Painting / Flooring | square metre (m²) | m² |
| Centering, shuttering, formwork | square metre (m²) | m² |
| Reinforcement steel | **kilogram (kg) or quintal (qtl)** | kg |
| DPC thickness | **millimetre (mm) / metre (m)** | mm or m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board answers, always show the *unit* next to every quantity and write the final answer in the **standard unit** (e.g., 12.65 m³, not 12,650 litres).

---

## 1.5 Why RCC Single-Storeyed Flat-Roof Buildings?

A **RCC single-storeyed building with a flat roof** is the most frequently asked KTU exam case because:

1. The structural components (footing → plinth → wall → lintel → roof slab) form a **clean, repetitive vertical sequence**.
2. The *flat roof* removes the complexity of sloped/trussed roofing, allowing students to focus on measurement logic.
3. A **stair cabin** introduces an extra offset/recess, which is a *favourite examiner's trick* to test whether students can handle **non-rectangular plan adjustments** in the centre line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Centre Line Method (CLM) – Step-by-Step Logic

The Centre Line Method rests on one fundamental principle:

> **Total Quantity = (Total Centre Line Length of that type) × (Cross-Sectional Dimension)**

The procedure to apply CLM is:

1. Prepare a **plan of the building** showing all walls, footings, and offsets.
2. Draw the **centre lines** of all walls (i.e., the line running through the *mid-thickness* of each wall).
3. Calculate the **total running length of the centre line** for each category of work. For a simple closed rectangular building:
   - **Sum of all long wall centre lines** = 2 × (long centre-to-centre length)
   - **Sum of all short wall centre lines** = 2 × (short centre-to-centre length)
   - **Total Centre Line** = Sum of all individual centre line segments (around the closed loop).
4. For each item, multiply this total centre line length by the appropriate **breadth and depth/height** of that item.
5. For special features (junctions, T-junctions, stair cabin offsets), the centre line *does not* reduce automatically – the cross-sectional area at junctions is counted only once, so a small **adjustment** is needed at the end (explained in Section 3).

### Mathematical Foundation of CLM

For a closed rectangular building with internal dimensions $L \times B$ and wall thickness $t$:

$$L_{c} = L + t$$

$$B_{c} = B + t$$

where $L_c$ and $B_c$ are the **centre-to-centre lengths** of the long and short walls respectively.

**Total Centre Line Length (Closed Loop):**

$$L_{total} = 2 \, (L_c + B_c) = 2 \, (L + B + 2t)$$

This $L_{total}$ is then multiplied by the cross-section of the item (e.g., for excavation: $L_{total} \times \text{width of excavation} \times \text{depth}$).

---

## 2.2 Short Wall–Long Wall Method (SWLW) – Step-by-Step Logic

This method works on the **internal plan dimensions** of the building and handles each wall independently.

### Procedure:

1. Note the **internal dimensions** of the building (length $L$ and breadth $B$).
2. Note the **wall thickness** $t$.
3. Compute the **centre-to-centre length** of each type of wall:
   - **Long Wall (Centre-to-Centre):** $L_c = L + t$
   - **Short Wall (Centre-to-Centre):** $B_c = B + t$
4. For each item of work (e.g., brickwork in foundation, brickwork in superstructure, plastering), measure the **long walls** and **short walls separately**:
   - **Long Wall Quantity** = $L_c \times \text{(height of that item)} \times \text{(thickness of that item)}$
   - **Short Wall Quantity** = $B_c \times \text{(height of that item)} \times \text{(thickness of that item)}$
5. **Total** = Long Wall Quantity + Short Wall Quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Long Wall" always refers to walls running along the longer side of the building, and "Short Wall" refers to walls along the shorter side. In a *square* building, the distinction is arbitrary, but the calculation rule is the same.

---

## 2.3 Comparison of the Two Methods

| Aspect | Centre Line Method | Short Wall–Long Wall Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | One total centre line length × cross-section | Long walls and short walls calculated separately |
| **Time & Effort** | Faster, less repetitive | More time-consuming, more repetitive |
| **Error Rate** | Lower (less repetition) | Higher (more chances of arithmetic error) |
| **Handling T-Junctions** | Requires careful adjustment | Automatically handled by separate walls |
| **Best Suited For** | Simple rectangular buildings with no offsets | Any shape, including T, L, U plans |
| **KTU Board Preference** | Frequently asked for 7- or 14-mark problems | Frequently asked as the base method |
| **Output Quantity** | Identical for the same building | Identical for the same building |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

### A. General Centre Line Formulas

| Item / Concept | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Long wall centre length (rectangular plan) | $L_c = L + t$ | m |
| Short wall centre length (rectangular plan) | $B_c = B + t$ | m |
| Total CL for closed rectangle | $L_{total} = 2(L_c + B_c)$ | m |
| Earthwork excavation (CLM) | $V_{exc} = L_{total} \times B_{exc} \times D_{exc}$ | m³ |
| PCC in foundation (CLM) | $V_{pcc} = L_{total} \times B_{pcc} \times D_{pcc}$ | m³ |
| RCC footing (CLM) | $V_{foot} = L_{total} \times B_{foot} \times D_{foot}$ | m³ |
| Brickwork in foundation (CLM) | $V_{bm\_fdn} = L_{total} \times t \times H_{fdn}$ | m³ |
| Brickwork in superstructure (CLM) | $V_{bm\_ss} = L_{total} \times t \times H_{ss}$ | m³ |
| Plastering (CLM) | $A_{plaster} = L_{total} \times H_{total}$ | m² |
| DPC (CLM) | $A_{dpc} = L_{total} \times t_{dpc}$ | m² |

### B. Long Wall–Short Wall Method Formulas

| Item | Long Wall (LW) | Short Wall (SW) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brickwork in superstructure | $L_c \times H \times t$ | $B_c \times H \times t$ | LW + SW |
| Plastering (outer) | $L_c \times H$ | $B_c \times H$ | LW + SW |
| Plastering (inner) | $L \times H$ | $B \times H$ | LW + SW |

### C. Standard Wall Thicknesses Used in KTU Problems

| Wall Type | Thickness (m) |
|---|---|
| Outer load-bearing wall (230 mm brick) | **0.230 m** |
| Outer load-bearing wall (1st class brick) | **0.200 m** |
| RCC frame with brick infill | **0.115 m / 0.230 m** |
| Partition wall (half brick) | **0.115 m** |
| Stone masonry (rubble) | **0.30 m to 0.45 m** |

### D. Deductions for Openings (KTU Standard Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **IS 1200 (Part 1)**, for plastering/painting, deductions for openings are made when the area of *one opening* exceeds **0.5 m²**. For brickwork, deductions are made for *all* openings regardless of size.

| Item | Deduction Rule |
|---|---|
| Door / Window in brickwork | Deduct full opening area (no lintel deduction) or deduct lintel bearing area if lintel is over opening |
| Door / Window in plastering | Deduct full opening area, but **add back 30%** of the opening area to account for jambs/soffits |
| Lintel bearing in plastering | Lintel is not deducted separately if it is a thin RCC band |

### E. Material Quantity for Rubble Masonry (Per 1 m³)

> [!NOTE]
> For *dry rubble masonry* or *rubble masonry in CM 1:6*, the following constants are used (as per KTU SOR):

| Material | Quantity per 1 m³ of Rubble Masonry (CM 1:6) |
|---|---|
| Rubble stone | **1.25 m³** (≈ 35 cu ft) |
| Cement | **0.143 bags (≈ 6.43 kg)** or as per ratio |
| Sand | **0.27 m³** (varies with mix) |
| Labour | Per SOR |

> The **sum of ratio parts** for CM 1:6 = 1 + 6 = **7 parts**.
> Volume of dry mortar = (Wet mortar volume) × 1.33 (dry-to-wet factor).
> Cement = $\frac{1}{7} \times \text{dry mortar vol.} \times \text{density of cement} / 50$ (bags)
> Sand = $\frac{6}{7} \times \text{dry mortar vol.}$ (in m³)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional practice, the **Centre Line Method is the industry standard** because:

- It allows direct **replication across multiple floors** (multi-storey buildings) with minimal re-measurement.
- It integrates seamlessly with **BIM (Building Information Modelling)** and **digital quantity take-off software** like Revit, CostX, and Planswift.
- It forms the **foundation of Rate Analysis**, where the cost of *one metre* of a particular item is calculated and then multiplied by total length.

The **Long Wall–Short Wall Method** remains vital in:
- Academic teaching and **competitive exams** (SSC JE, GATE, KTU).
- Manual estimation in small firms that do not use BIM.
- **Cross-verification** of digital take-offs in quality audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Master Worked Example – RCC Single-Storeyed Building (Flat Roof) with Stair Cabin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical KTU 14-mark type problem.** Work through every line. Understand *why* each step is taken, not just *what* to write.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Plan & Sections)

**Plan:** A rectangular RCC single-storeyed building with an attached **stair cabin** projection on one side.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal dimensions of main building (L × B) | **10.00 m × 6.00 m** |
| Wall thickness (all walls) | **0.30 m** |
| Height of plinth above GL | **0.60 m** |
| Height of superstructure (plinth top to roof bottom) | **3.30 m** |
| Depth of foundation below GL | **1.20 m** |
| Stair cabin size (external) | **3.00 m × 3.00 m** (add-on, with 0.30 m walls) |
| Stair cabin roof at same level as main roof | Yes (flat RCC roof) |

**Cross-Sectional Details (Trench Excavation):**

| Layer | Width (m) | Depth (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Earthwork in excavation | **0.90 m** | **1.20 m** |
| PCC (1:4:8) bed | **0.80 m** | **0.10 m** |
| Rubble masonry in CM 1:6 (footing) | **0.70 m** | **0.60 m** (from bottom of PCC) |
| Brickwork in CM 1:6 (footing/step) | **0.50 m** | up to GL |
| Brickwork in CM 1:6 (plinth) | **0.30 m** | **0.60 m** (plinth height) |
| Damp Proof Course (DPC) | **0.30 m** | **0.075 m** (75 mm) |
| Brickwork in CM 1:4 (superstructure) | **0.30 m** | **3.30 m** |
| RCC roof slab (over main building + stair cabin) | — | **0.12 m** thick |

**Openings (Main Building):**

| Opening | Size (m) | Number | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Door (D) | 1.20 × 2.10 | 2 | One on each short wall (front & back) |
| Window (W) | 1.50 × 1.50 | 4 | Two on each long wall |
| Ventilator (V) | 0.60 × 0.60 | 4 | One above each window |

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1 – Derivation of Centre-to-Centre Dimensions

For the **main rectangular building**:

$$L_c = L + t = 10.00 + 0.30 = 10.30 \text{ m}$$

$$B_c = B + t = 6.00 + 0.30 = 6.30 \text{ m}$$

For the **stair cabin** (projection on the long wall):

- External size = 3.00 m × 3.00 m
- Internal size = 3.00 − 2 × 0.30 = **2.40 m × 2.40 m**

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2 – Total Centre Line Length (For Centre Line Method)

**Part A: Main Building Centre Line Loop**

The total perimeter of the centre line (closed loop):

$$L_{cl,\,main} = 2 \, (L_c + B_c) = 2 \, (10.30 + 6.30) = 2 \times 16.60 = 33.20 \text{ m}$$

**Part B: Stair Cabin Centre Line**

The stair cabin has 3 new free walls + 1 wall shared with the main building. The shared wall's centre line is *already counted* in the main loop.

New free walls of stair cabin (centre-to-centre):
- Two walls of length = 3.00 m (external) − 0.15 m (half of shared wall thickness) − 0.15 m (half of free wall thickness) = **2.70 m each**
- One wall of length = 3.00 − 0.30 = **2.70 m**

$$L_{cl,\,stair} = 2 \, (2.70) + 2.70 = 8.10 \text{ m}$$

> **Verification:** Alternatively, the stair cabin outer centre-to-centre length is $3.00 - 0.30 = 2.70$ m, and the perpendicular walls are 2.70 m each. Three free walls: $2.70 + 2.70 + 2.70 = 8.10$ m. ✓

**Total Centre Line Length:**

$$L_{total} = 33.20 + 8.10 = 41.30 \text{ m}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3 – Long Wall–Short Wall Method Setup (For Main Building)

For the main building:
- **Long wall (centre-to-centre length)** = 10.30 m, **number** = 2
- **Short wall (centre-to-centre length)** = 6.30 m, **number** = 2

For the stair cabin (treated as a *separate unit* in SWLW method):
- The wall shared with the main building is *not* separately measured (avoiding double counting).
- Three new free walls: each of centre length 2.70 m. Two are "long" (parallel to long wall), one is "short" (parallel to short wall).

---

### 3.1.5 Step 4 – Earthwork Excavation Quantity

#### Using Centre Line Method:

$$V_{exc} = L_{total} \times B_{exc} \times D_{exc}$$

$$V_{exc} = 41.30 \times 0.90 \times 1.20$$

$$V_{exc} = 44.604 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Using Long Wall–Short Wall Method (for cross-verification):

**Main building:**

- Long walls: $2 \times 10.30 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 = 22.248$ m³
- Short walls: $2 \times 6.30 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 = 13.608$ m³

**Stair cabin free walls (centre lengths × full excavation width):**

- 2 walls × 2.70 × 0.90 × 1.20 = 5.832 m³
- 1 wall × 2.70 × 0.90 × 1.20 = 2.916 m³

**Subtotal stair cabin** = 5.832 + 2.916 = 8.748 m³

**Total** = 22.248 + 13.608 + 8.748 = **44.604 m³** ✓ (Matches CLM)

> **Cross-check passed**: Both methods give 44.604 m³. This is a critical KTU board examiner expectation.

---

### 3.1.6 Step 5 – PCC (1:4:8) Bed Quantity

#### Using Centre Line Method:

$$V_{pcc} = L_{total} \times B_{pcc} \times D_{pcc}$$

$$V_{pcc} = 41.30 \times 0.80 \times 0.10 = 3.304 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the main building walls, the PCC width is 0.80 m (i.e., 0.10 m offset on each side of the 0.60 m wide footing masonry above). For the stair cabin walls, the same cross-section is assumed.

---

### 3.1.7 Step 6 – Rubble Masonry in Foundation (CM 1:6) – The KEY Item

This is the "rubble masonry" item highlighted in your syllabus.

#### Using Centre Line Method:

$$V_{rubble} = L_{total} \times B_{rubble} \times H_{rubble}$$

$$V_{rubble} = 41.30 \times 0.70 \times 0.60 = 17.346 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Long Wall–Short Wall Verification (Main Building Only for Foundation):

Often in KTU problems, the **foundation depth and width differ** for the main building and the stair cabin. Let's assume the stair cabin has the same footing (0.70 m × 0.60 m):

- Main building LW: $2 \times 10.30 \times 0.70 \times 0.60 = 8.652$ m³
- Main building SW: $2 \times 6.30 \times 0.70 \times 0.60 = 5.292$ m³
- Stair cabin walls: $3 \times 2.70 \times 0.70 \times 0.60 = 3.402$ m³
- **Total** = 8.652 + 5.292 + 3.402 = **17.346 m³** ✓

#### Material Calculation for 17.346 m³ of Rubble Masonry (CM 1:6):

**Step 6a – Volume of Rubble Stone:**

$$V_{stone} = V_{rubble} \times 1.25 = 17.346 \times 1.25 = 21.683 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6b – Volume of Wet Mortar Required:**

$$V_{wet\,mortar} = V_{rubble} \times 0.25 = 17.346 \times 0.25 = 4.337 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6c – Volume of Dry Mortar (Wet × 1.33):**

$$V_{dry\,mortar} = 4.337 \times 1.33 = 5.768 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6d – Cement Quantity (Ratio 1:6, sum = 7):**

$$V_{cement\,dry} = \frac{1}{7} \times 5.768 = 0.824 \text{ m}^3$$

Density of cement (loose) = 1440 kg/m³:

$$\text{Mass of cement} = 0.824 \times 1440 = 1186.6 \text{ kg}$$

Number of bags (50 kg/bag):

$$\text{Bags} = \frac{1186.6}{50} = 23.73 \approx 24 \text{ bags}$$

**Step 6e – Sand Quantity (Ratio 6/7):**

$$V_{sand} = \frac{6}{7} \times 5.768 = 4.944 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Summary for Rubble Masonry (17.346 m³):**
> - Rubble stone = **21.683 m³**
> - Cement = **24 bags** (≈ 1187 kg)
> - Sand = **4.944 m³**

---

### 3.1.8 Step 7 – Brickwork in Foundation (Below GL, Step Footing)

#### Using Centre Line Method:

The brickwork in foundation has stepped offsets. Let's assume a single step from 0.50 m width (at top of rubble) to 0.30 m (at GL).

$$V_{bm\_fdn} = L_{total} \times \text{average width} \times \text{height}$$

Average width (if one step): (0.70 + 0.30)/2 = 0.50 m, OR if using a single offset of 0.50 m uniform, then width = 0.50 m.

Assume uniform width 0.50 m, height from top of rubble (0.60 m below GL) to GL = 0.60 m:

$$V_{bm\_fdn} = 41.30 \times 0.50 \times 0.60 = 12.39 \text{ m}^3$$

---

### 3.1.9 Step 8 – Brickwork in Plinth (Above GL, Below DPC)

$$V_{bm\_plinth} = L_{total} \times t \times H_{plinth}$$

$$V_{bm\_plinth} = 41.30 \times 0.30 \times 0.60 = 7.434 \text{ m}^3$$

---

### 3.1.10 Step 9 – Damp Proof Course (DPC) Quantity

$$A_{dpc} = L_{total} \times t = 41.30 \times 0.30 = 12.39 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V_{dpc} = 12.39 \times 0.075 = 0.929 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (if measured volumetrically)}$$

---

### 3.1.11 Step 10 – Brickwork in Superstructure (Above DPC to Roof Bottom)

Using **Long Wall–Short Wall Method** (commonly used at this stage so we can deduct openings easily):

**Long Walls (height = 3.30 m, deductions for windows and doors):**
- Length of each long wall (centre-to-centre) = 10.30 m
- Area of each long wall = 10.30 × 3.30 = 33.99 m²
- 2 walls → 67.98 m²
- Deduction for 2 windows on each long wall: $2 \times (1.50 \times 1.50) = 4.50$ m² per long wall
- Net area of long walls (brickwork) = $67.98 - 2 \times 4.50 = 58.98$ m²

**Short Walls (height = 3.30 m, deduction for door):**
- Length of each short wall (centre-to-centre) = 6.30 m
- Area of each short wall = 6.30 × 3.30 = 20.79 m²
- 2 walls → 41.58 m²
- Deduction for 1 door per short wall: $1.20 \times 2.10 = 2.52$ m²
- Net area of short walls (brickwork) = $41.58 - 2 \times 2.52 = 36.54$ m²

**Stair cabin walls (no openings):**
- 3 free walls × 2.70 m × 3.30 m = 26.73 m²

**Total brickwork area in superstructure (net):**
$$A_{ss,\,net} = 58.98 + 36.54 + 26.73 = 122.25 \text{ m}^2$$

**Volume of Brickwork in Superstructure:**
$$V_{ss} = 122.25 \times 0.30 = 36.675 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Cross-check using CLM** (gross, then deduct openings):
> - Gross area = 41.30 × 3.30 = 136.29 m²
> - Total opening area = 4 × (1.50×1.50) + 2 × (1.20×2.10) = 9.00 + 5.04 = 14.04 m²
> - Net area = 136.29 − 14.04 = 122.25 m² ✓
> - Volume = 122.25 × 0.30 = 36.675 m³ ✓

---

### 3.1.12 Step 11 – Plastering Quantities (Inside & Outside)

**Outside Plastering (gross area, no opening deduction per IS 1200 simplified rule for residential KTU problems):**

$$A_{plaster,\,out} = L_{total} \times H_{ss} = 41.30 \times 3.30 = 136.29 \text{ m}^2$$

**Inside Plastering (using internal plan dimensions, deducting openings fully):**

For the main building:
- Long walls (internal length = 10.00 m, height = 3.30 m): 2 × 10.00 × 3.30 = 66.00 m²
- Short walls (internal breadth = 6.00 m, height = 3.30 m): 2 × 6.00 × 3.30 = 39.60 m²
- Deductions: 4 windows + 2 doors = 14.04 m²
- Net main building inside = 105.60 − 14.04 = 91.56 m²

For stair cabin (internal dimensions 2.40 m × 2.40 m):
- 2 walls × 2.40 × 3.30 = 15.84 m²
- 1 wall × 2.40 × 3.30 = 7.92 m²
- (No openings in stair cabin)
- Total stair cabin inside = 23.76 m²

**Total Inside Plastering = 91.56 + 23.76 = 115.32 m²**

**Total Plastering (Out + In) = 136.29 + 115.32 = 251.61 m²**

---

### 3.1.13 Step 12 – RCC Roof Slab (1:2:4) for Main Building + Stair Cabin

**Area of roof slab:**

- Main building external area = (10.00 + 0.30) × (6.00 + 0.30) = 10.30 × 6.30 = **64.89 m²**
- Stair cabin external area = 3.00 × 3.00 = **9.00 m²**
- Total roof area = 64.89 + 9.00 = **73.89 m²**

**Volume of RCC Slab:**

$$V_{slab} = 73.89 \times 0.12 = 8.867 \text{ m}^3$$

**Steel Reinforcement (typically 80 kg per m³ of RCC for slabs):**

$$\text{Steel} = 8.867 \times 80 = 709.4 \text{ kg} \approx 7.09 \text{ quintals}$$

---

### 3.1.14 Step 13 – Centering & Shuttering (Formwork) for Slab

For the *bottom* of the slab:

$$A_{shuttering} = 73.89 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Side shuttering of beams** is usually extra; in KTU basic problems, only the slab soffit is asked.

---

### 3.1.15 Step 14 – Flooring (1:2:4 with IPS finish, 50 mm thick)

**Area = Internal floor area of main building + stair cabin**

- Main building internal = 10.00 × 6.00 = 60.00 m²
- Stair cabin internal = 2.40 × 2.40 = 5.76 m²
- **Total** = **65.76 m²**

---

|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation | m³ | 44.604 |
| 2 | PCC (1:4:8) in foundation | m³ | 3.304 |
| 3 | Rubble masonry in CM 1:6 (footing) | m³ | 17.346 |

> [!IMPORTANT]

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Detailed Estimation Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *complete process* a Quantity Surveyor follows from drawing receipt to final BOQ.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Receive Architectural Drawings Plan Sections Elevations"] --> B["Identify All Items of Work Earthwork PCC Masonry DPC RCC Plastering Flooring"]
    B --> C{"Choose Estimation Method"}
    C -->|Method 1| D["Centre Line Method CL Total Length Cross Section"]
    C -->|Method 2| E["Long Wall Short Wall Method Internal Dimensions Wall-wise"]
    D --> F["Calculate Quantities for Each Item Apply IS 1200 Rules"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Deduct Openings Doors Windows Ventilators as per IS 1200"]
    G --> H["Prepare Dimensions Book Detailed Measurements Sheet"]
    H --> I["Prepare Abstract of Estimate BOQ Item Quantity Unit Rate Amount"]
    I --> J["Calculate Material Quantities Cement Sand Aggregate Steel from Concrete and Mortar Ratios"]
    J --> K["Final BOQ with Grand Total Cost"]
```

> The diagram above illustrates the universal workflow irrespective of method chosen. Note the critical branch point – the choice between Centre Line Method and Long Wall–Short Wall Method – and the convergence at quantity calculation, ensuring **identical results**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Application of Centre Line Method on a Rectangular Plan with Stair Cabin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Main Building Centre Line Loop Perimeter 33.20 m"] --> P3["Add Stair Cabin Free Walls 3 walls of 2.70 m each 8.10 m"]
    P3 --> P4["Total Centre Line Length Ltotal 41.30 m"]
    P4 --> P5["Apply to Earthwork Ltotal 0.90 1.20 equals 44.604 m3"]
    P4 --> P6["Apply to PCC Ltotal 0.80 0.10 equals 3.304 m3"]
    P4 --> P7["Apply to Rubble Masonry Ltotal 0.70 0.60 equals 17.346 m3"]
    P4 --> P8["Apply to Brickwork Ltotal 0.50 0.60 equals 12.39 m3"]
    P4 --> P9["Apply to Plastering Ltotal 3.30 equals 136.29 m2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The total centre line length **41.30 m** is the *single common multiplier* across all the *cross-sectional* items. This visualises why CLM is so efficient: one calculation feeds five items.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Hierarchy of Estimation Items (Vertical Section View of a Wall)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["RCC Roof Slab 120 mm thick"]
    S2["Brickwork in Superstructure CM 1:4 3.30 m high 0.30 m thick"]
    S3["Damp Proof Course 75 mm thick"]
    S4["Brickwork in Plinth CM 1:6 0.60 m high"]
    S5["Ground Level GL"]
    S6["Brickwork in Foundation Step 0.50 m width 0.60 m height below GL"]
    S7["Rubble Masonry in Foundation CM 1:6 0.70 m width 0.60 m height"]
    S8["PCC Bed 1:4:8 0.80 m width 0.10 m thick"]
    S9["Earthwork in Excavation 0.90 m width 1.20 m depth"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This represents a *vertical slice* through the wall. For every item, you measure the **height (or depth)** for that specific layer. This is the same logic whether you use CLM or SWLW.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram – BOQ Sheet Layout (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Column 1 Sl No"] --> H2["Column 2 Item Description"]
    H2 --> H3["Column 3 Quantity"]
    H3 --> H4["Column 4 Unit"]
    H4 --> H5["Column 5 Rate Rs per unit"]
    H5 --> H6["Column 6 Amount Rs"]
    H6 --> H7["Add Contingencies 3 to 5 percent"]
    H7 --> H8["Add Contractors Profit 10 percent"]
    H8 --> H9["Add GST 18 percent as applicable"]
    H9 --> H10["Grand Total Estimated Cost"]
```

> The BOQ must list every item separately. The total cost is obtained by applying *contingencies*, *contractor profit*, and *taxes* in sequence as per the relevant *Schedule of Rates (SOR)* in force.
