**Q: What is the purpose of preparing a Bar Bending Schedule (BBS)? Mention the standard hook length for main bars and stirrups as per IS 2502.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Purpose of BBS (2 marks):**
   - To arrive at the total quantity of steel required (procurement and billing).
   - To provide cutting length and shape sketches to bar-benders at site.
   - To verify design quantity with actual placement and prevent wastage.
2. **Hook lengths per IS 2502 (1 mark):**
   - Main longitudinal bar: **12d** at 90°.
   - Stirrups / ties: **9d** at 135°.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: A single-storey residential building has the following data:**
- Outer dimensions (long × short wall): 10.00 m × 7.00 m
- Wall thickness: 230 mm
- Floor-to-floor height: 3.10 m
- Plinth height: 0.45 m (above GL)
- Doors (D1): 2 nos. — 1.00 m × 2.10 m (internal)
- Windows (W1): 3 nos. — 1.20 m × 1.20 m
- Ventilators (V1): 1 no. — 0.60 m × 0.60 m

**Compute the following:**
**(a)** The quantity of internal cement plaster (12 mm thick, 1:4 mix). Also calculate the **cement and sand** required using DAR data (per 100 m²: cement = 14 bags, sand = 1.86 m³). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The quantity of external cement plaster (20 mm thick, 1:5 mix). Calculate the **cement and sand** using DAR (per 100 m²: cement = 19 bags, sand = 3.20 m³). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Internal Plaster (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Internal wall lengths:
- $L_{long,\,inner} = 10.00 - 2(0.23) = 9.54$ m
- $L_{short,\,inner} = 7.00 - 2(0.23) = 6.54$ m

**Step 2:** Internal wall height (assume slab thickness 0.15 m):
- $H_{int} = 3.10 - 0.15 = 2.95$ m

**Step 3:** Gross internal plaster area:
- $A_{gross} = 2 \times (9.54 + 6.54) \times 2.95 = 2 \times 16.08 \times 2.95 = 94.872$ m²

**Step 4:** Deductions:
| Opening | Nos. | Area Each (m²) | Total (m²) | Treatment | Deduction |
|---------|------|----------------|------------|-----------|-----------|
| D1 | 2 | 2.10 | 4.20 | > 0.5 | 50% = 2.10 |
| W1 | 3 | 1.44 | 4.32 | > 0.5 | 50% = 2.16 |
| V1 | 1 | 0.36 | 0.36 | ≤ 0.5 | 0% = 0.00 |

**Total deduction** = 4.26 m²

**Step 5:** Net internal plaster area:
$$A_{net} = 94.872 - 4.26 = \boxed{90.61 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 6:** Material:
$$\text{Cement} = \frac{90.61}{100} \times 14 = 12.685 \approx \boxed{12.69 \text{ bags}}$$
$$\text{Sand} = \frac{90.61}{100} \times 1.86 = 1.685 \approx \boxed{1.69 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

**Part (b) — External Plaster (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** External gross area (outer dimensions used):
- $A_{gross,\,ext} = 2 \times (10.00 + 7.00) \times 3.10 = 107.80$ m²
(Use floor-to-floor height; subtract lintel projection if needed — KTU standard: ignore chajja separately)

**Step 2:** Deductions for external plaster openings (all > 0.5 m², deduct 50%):
- D1: 2 × 2.10 = 4.20 → 50% = 2.10
- W1: 3 × 1.44 = 4.32 → 50% = 2.16
- V1: 1 × 0.36 = 0.36 → 50% = 0.18

**Total deduction** = 4.44 m²

**Step 3:** Net external plaster area:
$$A_{net,\,ext} = 107.80 - 4.44 = \boxed{103.36 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 4:** Material:
$$\text{Cement} = \frac{103.36}{100} \times 19 = 19.638 \approx \boxed{19.64 \text{ bags}}$$
$$\text{Sand} = \frac{103.36}{100} \times 3.20 = 3.308 \approx \boxed{3.31 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Summary Answer (A):**
| Item | Area (m²) | Cement (bags) | Sand (m³) |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| Internal Plaster (12 mm, 1:4) | 90.61 | 12.69 | 1.69 |
| External Plaster (20 mm, 1:5) | 103.36 | 19.64 | 3.31 |
| **Total** | **193.97** | **32.33** | **5.00** |

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: Prepare the Bar Bending Schedule (BBS) for an RCC rectangular beam with the following data:**
- Clear span: 5.00 m (face-to-face of columns)
- Column size: 250 mm × 250 mm
- Beam size: 250 mm × 500 mm (b × D)
- Effective cover: 40 mm
- Main bars at bottom: **3 nos. of 12 mm dia., straight with end hooks**
- Extra top bars (cranked from bottom): **2 nos. of 12 mm dia., cranked at 45°**
- Top straight bars: **2 nos. of 10 mm dia.**
- Stirrups: **8 mm dia., 2-legged, 100 mm c/c**
- Concrete: M20; Steel: Fe 500
- Adopt IS 2502 provisions.

**Required:**
**(a)** Cutting lengths of all bars and total BBS tabulated for the beam (7 marks).
**(b)** Total weight of steel required (in kg) for the beam (7 marks).

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Cutting Lengths and BBS (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute centre-to-centre dimensions of stirrup:
- $a = 250 - 2(25) = 200$ mm
- $h = 500 - 2(25) = 450$ mm

**Step 2:** Cutting lengths:

**(i) Straight 12 mm bar (bottom, 3 nos.)** with two 90° hooks of 12d:
$$L_{cut,\,12,\,str} = L_{clear} - 2(\text{cover}) + 2(12d) - 2(2d)$$
$$= 5000 - 80 + 2(144) - 2(24) = 5000 - 80 + 288 - 48 = 5160 \text{ mm} = 5.160 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Cranked 12 mm bar (2 nos., extra top):**
$$L_{cut,\,12,\,crank} = L_{clear} - 2(\text{cover}) + 2(0.42d) + 2(12d) - 2(2d)$$
$$= 5000 - 80 + 2(5.04) + 288 - 48 = 5000 - 80 + 10.08 + 288 - 48 = 5170.08 \text{ mm} \approx 5.170 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Straight 10 mm bar (top, 2 nos.):**
$$L_{cut,\,10,\,str} = 5000 - 80 + 2(120) - 2(20) = 5000 - 80 + 240 - 40 = 5120 \text{ mm} = 5.120 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Stirrup 8 mm (rectangular 2-legged):**
$$L_{stirrup} = 2(200 + 450) + 2(9 \times 8) - 3(2 \times 8)$$
$$= 1300 + 144 - 48 = 1396 \text{ mm} = 1.396 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Number of stirrups:
$$N = \frac{5000}{100} + 1 = 51 \text{ nos.}$$

**Step 4:** Tabulated BBS:

| Bar Mark | Description | Dia (mm) | Shape | Cut Length (m) | Nos. | Total Length (m) |
|----------|-------------|----------|-------|----------------|------|-------------------|
| A1 | Bottom straight + hooks | 12 | L-Hook | 5.160 | 3 | 15.480 |
| A2 | Cranked + hooks | 12 | Crank + Hook | 5.170 | 2 | 10.340 |
| B1 | Top straight + hooks | 10 | L-Hook | 5.120 | 2 | 10.240 |
| C1 | Stirrup 2-legged | 8 | Rectangle | 1.396 | 51 | 71.196 |

---

**Part (b) — Total Weight (7 marks)**

Using $W = \frac{d^2}{162} \times L_{total}$:

- 12 mm bars: $W_{12} = \frac{144}{162} \times (15.480 + 10.340) = 0.8889 \times 25.820 = \mathbf{22.95 \text{ kg}}$
- 10 mm bars: $W_{10} = \frac{100}{162} \times 10.240 = 0.6173 \times 10.240 = \mathbf{6.32 \text{ kg}}$
- 8 mm bars: $W_{8} = \frac{64}{162} \times 71.196 = 0.3951 \times 71.196 = \mathbf{28.13 \text{ kg}}$

$$\boxed{W_{total} = 22.95 + 6.32 + 28.13 = 57.40 \text{ kg}}$$

Additional summary metrics:
- Steel per m³ of concrete = $57.40 / (0.25 \times 0.50 \times 5.25) = 57.40 / 0.656 = 87.5$ kg/m³ (within IS 456 limit of 40 kg/m³ for slabs/beams of mild exposure — pass).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU PCCET601 Plastering & BBS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Applying wrong opening deduction thresholds** — students often deduct 100% for ALL openings. Correct rule: ≤ 0.5 m² → no deduction; 0.5–3 m² → 50 %; > 3 m² → 100 %. *KTU exam tips: when in doubt, write the rule explicitly before computing.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in stirrup count** — `(L_clear/spacing) + 1`. A common error yields `(L_clear/spacing)`, missing one stirrup.
>
> 3. **Crank extra factor confusion** — Students use `2d` per crank instead of `0.42d`. For a 45° crank, the *horizontal projection added* per end is `d` but the *extra length consumed beyond straight bar* is `0.42d` per end.
>
> 4. **Bend deduction confusion** — A 90° bend is `2d`, not `1d`. A 135° bend is `3d`, not `2d`. KTU valuation is strict here.
>
> 5. **Unit of DAR data** — DAR is per **100 m²**, not per m². Students forget to divide by 100 → answer off by factor 100.
>
> 6. **Mix ratio 1:M** — *cement is 1*. For 1:4 mix, total parts = 5, cement fraction = 1/5, sand fraction = 4/5. Don't transpose.
>
> 7. **No cover subtraction in DAR method** — DAR already includes realistic wastage; do *not* further reduce by 5 %.
>
> 8. **Showing unit weight derivation in exam** — for full marks, briefly write $W = d^2/162$ derivation once, then tabulate; examiners reward clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print-ready, rapid-fire revision. Pin to your study wall before KTU exams.**

### ✅ Plastering
- [x] **Unit:** m²; **DAR consumption:** per 100 m².
- [x] **Internal plaster:** 12 mm, 1:3 / 1:4.
- [x] **External plaster:** 20 mm, 1:4 / 1:5 (2 coats often).
- [x] **Deductions (IS 1200):** ≤ 0.5 m² → 0 %; 0.5–3 m² → 50 %; > 3 m² → 100 %.
- [x] **Soffit of chajja, jambs, sills:** NOT separately paid.
- [x] **Projection deductions:** 0.5 × cross-section of beam/column ends.
- [x] **Dry volume factor:** 1.33 (for manual derivation, not required when using DAR).
- [x] **Bulk density of cement:** 1440 kg/m³; **bag weight:** 50 kg.
- [x] **DAR multiplier:** Area ÷ 100 × DAR value.

### ✅ Bar Bending Schedule
- [x] **Unit weight:** $d^2 / 162$ kg/m (d in mm).
- [x] **Hook length main bar:** 12d (90°); **stirrup:** 9d (135°).
- [x] **Bend deduction:** 45° = 0.5d; 90° = 2d; 135° = 3d; 180° = 4d.
- [x] **Crank extra (45°):** 0.42d per crank end → 0.84d per bar.
- [x] **Lap length (tension, HYSD):** 50d; (compression): 24d.
- [x] **Stirrup number:** `(L_clear / spacing) + 1`.
- [x] **Stirrup deduction:** 3 × 2d = 6d for 2-legged rectangular stirrup.
- [x] **Clear cover (typical beam):** 25–50 mm; deduct from overall depth for stirrup dimension.
- [x] **Total steel per m³ of concrete (beam):** typically 80–120 kg/m³.
- [x] **BBS Format columns:** Bar Mark | Description | Dia | Shape | Cut Length | Nos. | Total Length | Unit Wt | Total Wt.

### ✅ Examination Strategy
- [x] Always **write the formula first**, then substitute values.
- [x] Always **state the IS code clause** for any rule cited.
- [x] Always **show unit weight derivation** at least once.
- [x] **Neat tabular BBS presentation** — KTU examiners reward clarity.
- [x] In plastering, **separately compute internal vs external** with clear sub-headings.
- [x] **Round off cement bags** to 2 decimal places; sand to 2 decimal m³.

---

### 🔚 End of Module 3 Notes — Plastering & BBS
*Prepared in compliance with KTU 2024 Scheme PCCET601 syllabus, IS 1200 (Part 12), IS 2502, IS 456:2000, and Kerala PWD DAR.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slabs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slabs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **slab** is a flat, horizontal structural element of a building, typically of uniform thickness, whose primary function is to transfer the superimposed dead and live loads to the supporting beams, columns, or walls. In the context of **Quantity Surveying (QS)**, slabs represent a critical **reinforced cement concrete (RCC)** component whose volume of concrete, weight of reinforcement steel, and area of formwork/shuttering must be precisely quantified to generate a **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** and arrive at a realistic project cost estimate.

Slabs are broadly classified under KTU Module 3 into the following categories:
- **One-Way Slabs** – bending primarily in one direction (ly/Lx ≥ 2).
- **Two-Way Slabs** – bending in both orthogonal directions (ly/Lx < 2).
- **Cantilever Slabs** – fixed at one end and free at the other (e.g., chajjas, balcony projections).
- **Continuous Slabs** – monolithic with multiple supports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The valuation aspect of a slab requires estimation of **three primary items** — (1) Concrete volume in m³, (2) Reinforcement steel in kg, and (3) Shuttering/formwork area in m². Each item has a distinct unit rate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a slab as a **flat wooden table top** resting on legs (columns) and edges (beams). When you place a heavy book on the table, the table bends slightly — the load travels along the shortest path to the supports. If the table is long and narrow (like a corridor), the bending happens mainly along the short span → **One-Way Slab**. If the table is nearly square, the load splits and travels in **two perpendicular directions** → **Two-Way Slab**. From a QS perspective, you are essentially counting the **cubic meters of "table top concrete"**, the **kilograms of hidden "skeleton" steel bars** inside it, and the **square meters of "wooden mold"** required to shape the wet concrete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Defaults for Slab Design (IS 456:2000):**
> - Unit weight of RCC = **25 kN/m³**
> - Unit weight of PCC (1:2:4) = **24 kN/m³**
> - Grade of concrete (default residential) = **M20 (1:1.5:3)**
> - Grade of steel = **Fe415 or Fe500**
> - Live load on residential slab = **2 to 3 kN/m²**
> - Floor finish load = **0.5 to 1.5 kN/m²**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – Quantification of a Slab
The QS workflow for a slab follows a **4-step logical chain**:
1. **Geometric Step:** Extract length (L), breadth (B), and overall thickness (D) of the slab from architectural/structural drawings.
2. **Effective Span Step:** Compute the effective span (lex or ley) as the **clear span + effective depth** (as per IS 456, cl. 22.2).
3. **Reinforcement Step:** Identify main bars, distribution bars, crank bars, and chair bars from BBS (Bar Bending Schedule).
4. **Rate Application Step:** Multiply each quantity by the corresponding **Schedule of Rates (SOR)** unit rate to obtain the total cost.

### 2.2 Why "Effective Depth" Matters in QS
The slab thickness is given as **overall depth (D)** in drawings, but reinforcement is placed with a **clear cover of 20 mm** (for mild exposure) above the bottom of the slab. The remaining depth available for lever arm calculations is the **effective depth (d)**:

$$d = D - \text{clear cover} - \frac{\phi_{\text{main bar}}}{2}$$

For a **standard 150 mm thick slab** with 12 mm main bars and 20 mm cover, the effective depth is:

$$d = 150 - 20 - 6 = 124 \text{ mm} \approx 0.124 \text{ m}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Slab Quantity Estimation (KTU Module 3)**

| Sl. No. | Quantity to Estimate | Formula / Thumb Rule | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Volume of Concrete | $V_c = L \times B \times D$ | m³ |
| 2 | Area of Shuttering (Soffit) | $A_s = L \times B$ | m² |
| 3 | Area of Shuttering (Edges) | $A_e = 2(L + B) \times D$ | m² |
| 4 | Total Shuttering Area | $A_{\text{total}} = A_s + A_e$ | m² |
| 5 | Steel Weight (kg/m) | $W = 0.006165 \times \phi^2$ | kg/m |
| 6 | No. of Bars | $n = \dfrac{L_{\text{required}}}{S_{\text{spacing}}} + 1$ | nos. |
| 7 | Total Steel Length | $L_{\text{steel}} = n \times l_{\text{bar}}$ | m |
| 8 | Total Steel Weight | $W_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{steel}} \times 0.006165 \times \phi^2$ | kg |
| 9 | % Steel Thumb Rule (Slab) | $70 \text{ to } 90 \text{ kg/m}^3$ of concrete | kg/m³ |
| 10 | Effective Span (IS 456) | $l_{\text{eff}} = \min(L_n + d,\ L_c)$ | m |
| 11 | Crank Bar Length | $\text{Crank length} = 0.42 \times d$ (each side) | m |
| 12 | L-Bend (Hook) Length | $9\phi$ (for Fe415) or $12\phi$ (for Fe500) | mm |

> **Notation Note:** $\phi$ = diameter of bar in mm. To avoid LaTeX pipe conflicts, **absolute value and "such that" symbols are written using \vert and \mid** within equations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
In **practice**, civil engineering contractors in Kerala rely on these QS formulas to:
- **Tender Preparation:** Generate accurate competitive bids.
- **Material Procurement:** Order the exact quantity of cement, aggregate, sand, and TMT bars.
- **Project Scheduling:** Estimate labor days for bar bending, centering, and concrete pouring.
- **Valuation for Insurance/Resale:** Determine the depreciated value of a structure using the **PWD Schedule of Rates (SOR)** combined with these quantities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bar Weight per Running Meter

The weight of any reinforcement bar depends on its **cross-sectional area** and the **density of steel** (7850 kg/m³).

**Step 1: Area of a circular bar of diameter $\phi$ (in mm).**
Convert $\phi$ to meters: $\dfrac{\phi}{1000}$ m.

$$A_{\text{bar}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \left(\frac{\phi}{1000}\right)^2 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Volume per meter length (1 m = 1 m).**
$$V_{\text{per m}} = A_{\text{bar}} \times 1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \frac{\phi^2}{10^6} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Weight per meter (multiply by density $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³).**
$$W = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \frac{\phi^2}{10^6} \times 7850$$

**Step 4: Numerical simplification.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{3.1416 \times 7850}{4 \times 10^6} \times \phi^2 \\
&= \frac{24649.0}{4 \times 10^6} \times \phi^2 \\
&= 0.0061625 \times \phi^2 \\
&\approx 0.006165 \times \phi^2 \text{ kg/m}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The constant **0.006165** is the **single most used constant** in a QS office for steel estimation. It is also called the *"unit weight constant"* for HYSD bars.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Span of a Slab (IS 456:2000, Clause 22.2)

The effective span $l_{\text{eff}}$ is the smaller of:
- **Case A:** Clear span ($L_n$) + effective depth ($d$)
- **Case B:** Center-to-center distance between supports ($L_c$)

Formally stated:

$$l_{\text{eff}} = \min \left( L_n + d,\ L_c \right)$$

> **Example:** A simply supported slab has clear span $L_n = 3.5$ m, center-to-center span $L_c = 3.7$ m, and effective depth $d = 0.124$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Case A: } L_n + d &= 3.5 + 0.124 = 3.624 \text{ m} \\
\text{Case B: } L_c &= 3.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Since Case A is smaller, the **effective design span = 3.624 m**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Bars in a Slab

Bars are placed at a specified **center-to-center spacing (S)**. The number of bars required across a width $B$ is:

$$n = \frac{B}{S} + 1$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- The first bar starts at the **cover distance** from the slab edge.
- Each subsequent bar is spaced $S$ mm away.
- The last bar is at the **cover distance** from the opposite edge.
- Total width = $(n-1) \times S$ = $B - 2 \times \text{cover}$.
- Solving gives $n = \dfrac{B - 2 \times \text{cover}}{S} + 1$, which is approximated to $\dfrac{B}{S} + 1$ when cover is small relative to $B$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:** A residential one-way slab has the following specifications:
- Clear span = 3.0 m, Width = 2.5 m, Overall thickness = 120 mm.
- Main bars = 10 mm $\phi$ @ 150 mm c/c, Distribution bars = 8 mm $\phi$ @ 200 mm c/c.
- Cover = 20 mm, Fe415 steel, M20 concrete.
- Crank bars provided at supports.

**Required:** Estimate (a) Concrete volume, (b) Shuttering area, (c) Main steel weight, (d) Distribution steel weight.

---

#### (a) Volume of Concrete

$$V_c = L \times B \times D = 3.0 \times 2.5 \times 0.120$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_c &= 3.0 \times 2.5 \times 0.120 \\
&= 0.900 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

#### (b) Shuttering Area (Soffit + Edges)

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{soffit}} &= L \times B = 3.0 \times 2.5 = 7.50 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{edges}} &= 2(L + B) \times D = 2(3.0 + 2.5) \times 0.120 \\
&= 2 \times 5.5 \times 0.120 = 1.32 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{total}} &= 7.50 + 1.32 = 8.82 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

#### (c) Main Steel (10 mm $\phi$ @ 150 mm c/c)

Unit weight of 10 mm bar:
$$w_{10} = 0.006165 \times 10^2 = 0.6165 \text{ kg/m}$$

Number of main bars across 2.5 m width:
$$n_{\text{main}} = \frac{2500}{150} + 1 = 16.67 + 1 \approx 17.67 \rightarrow 18 \text{ bars}$$

Length of each main bar (with crank + hook allowances, simplified as straight):
$$l_{\text{bar}} \approx 3.0 + 2 \times (9 \times 0.010) = 3.0 + 0.18 = 3.18 \text{ m}$$

Total length of main steel:
$$L_{\text{main}} = 18 \times 3.18 = 57.24 \text{ m}$$

Total main steel weight:
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{main}} &= 57.24 \times 0.6165 \\
&\approx 35.29 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (d) Distribution Steel (8 mm $\phi$ @ 200 mm c/c)

Unit weight of 8 mm bar:
$$w_{8} = 0.006165 \times 8^2 = 0.3946 \text{ kg/m}$$

Number of distribution bars along 3.0 m length:
$$n_{\text{dist}} = \frac{3000}{200} + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16 \text{ bars}$$

Length of each distribution bar:
$$l_{\text{bar}} \approx 2.5 + 2 \times (9 \times 0.008) = 2.5 + 0.144 = 2.644 \text{ m}$$

Total distribution steel weight:
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{dist}} &= 16 \times 2.644 \times 0.3946 \\
&\approx 16.69 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Summary Table

| Item | Quantity | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Concrete Volume | 0.900 | m³ |
| Shuttering Area | 8.82 | m² |
| Main Steel (10 mm) | 35.29 | kg |
| Distribution Steel (8 mm) | 16.69 | kg |
| **Total Steel** | **51.98** | **kg** |
| **Steel % per m³** | **57.76** | **kg/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> The actual steel percentage (57.76 kg/m³) is **slightly lower** than the empirical thumb rule of 70–90 kg/m³ for slabs, because crank bars and extra cover laps have not been included. In KTU valuation problems, always add **8 to 12% wastage** to the calculated steel weight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Slab Quantity Surveying

The following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the complete QS workflow for a slab, from drawing extraction to final rate application — mimicking the actual workflow followed in a Kerala PWD-approved QS office.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Structural Drawing] --> B[Extract Slab Dimensions: L, B, D]
    B --> C[Identify Slab Type: One-Way, Two-Way, Cantilever]
    C --> D{Compute Effective Span per IS 456}
    D --> E[Step 1: Concrete Volume = L x B x D]
    E --> F[Step 2: Shuttering Area = Soffit + Edges]
    F --> G[Step 3: Steel Estimation from BBS]
    G --> G1[Identify Main Bars: dia, spacing, length]
    G1 --> G2[Identify Distribution Bars: dia, spacing, length]
    G2 --> G3[Identify Crank Bars and Chairs]
    G3 --> H[Apply Unit Weight: 0.006165 x phi-squared]
    H --> I[Sum Steel Quantities in kg]
    I --> J[Add Wastage Allowance: 8 to 12 percent]
    J --> K[Apply SOR Unit Rates: Concrete, Steel, Shuttering]
    K --> L[Generate BOQ Line Item for Slab]
    L --> M[End: Cost Aggregation in Abstract Sheet]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Layout of a One-Way Slab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SlabCrossSection["One-Way Slab Cross-Section (Not to Scale)"]
        W1[Wall / Beam Support] --- S1[Slab D = 120 mm]
        S1 --- W2[Wall / Beam Support]
        MB[Main Bar 10 mm @ 150 c/c] -.-> S1
        DB[Distribution Bar 8 mm @ 200 c/c] -.-> S1
        CV[Clear Cover 20 mm] --- S1
    end
    SlabCrossSection --> LoadPath[Load Path: Span Direction only, ly by lx greater than 2]
```

### 4.3 Bar Bending Schedule (BBS) Format Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Member: Slab Panel S1] --> P2[Bar Mark: A1]
    P2 --> P3[Type: Main Bar Straight]
    P3 --> P4[Dia: 10 mm]
    P4 --> P5[Spacing: 150 mm c/c]
    P5 --> P6[Length each: 3.18 m]
    P6 --> P7[Nos: 18]
    P7 --> P8[Total Length: 57.24 m]
    P8 --> P9[Total Weight: 35.29 kg]

    Q1[Member: Slab Panel S1] --> Q2[Bar Mark: B1]
    Q2 --> Q3[Type: Distribution Bar]
    Q3 --> Q4[Dia: 8 mm]
    Q4 --> Q5[Spacing: 200 mm c/c]
    Q5 --> Q6[Length each: 2.64 m]
    Q6 --> Q7[Nos: 16]
    Q7 --> Q8[Total Length: 42.30 m]
    Q8 --> Q9[Total Weight: 16.69 kg]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid diagrams are **functional architecture schematics**, not physical scale drawings. They map the **logical dependencies** between estimation steps — exactly what is required for a KTU viva or a QS audit trail.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
> **Differentiate between a one-way slab and a two-way slab. State the IS 456 criterion used to classify them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | One-Way Slab | Two-Way Slab |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IS 456 Criterion** | $l_y / l_x \geq 2$ | $l_y / l_x < 2$ |
| Load Transfer | Along shorter span only | Along both spans |
| Main Reinforcement | Provided along shorter span | Provided along both spans |
| Distribution Bars | Placed perpendicular to main bars | Not provided as such; bars in both directions are main |
| Typical Example | Corridor, verandah slab | Floor slab of a square room |

(1 mark for criterion, 1 mark for load path, 1 mark for reinforcement distinction)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
> **List any three thumb rules used in the preliminary estimation of steel in an RCC slab.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Steel in slab = **70 to 90 kg per m³** of concrete (1 mark).
2. Steel in beams = **100 to 130 kg per m³** of concrete (1 mark).
3. Steel in columns = **120 to 180 kg per m³** of concrete (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> 
> A simply supported RCC slab for a residential room has the following data:
> - **Clear span (shorter) $l_x$ = 3.5 m, Clear span (longer) $l_y$ = 4.5 m**
> - **Overall slab thickness D = 150 mm**
> - **Main bars: 12 mm $\phi$ @ 130 mm c/c (along short span)**
> - **Distribution bars: 10 mm $\phi$ @ 180 mm c/c (along long span)**
> - **Clear cover = 20 mm, Fe415 steel, M20 concrete**
> - **Crank bars provided at both supports (45° bend)**
> 
> **Estimate the following as per KTU standard practice:**
> **(a)** Classify the slab and justify. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the volume of concrete, area of shuttering, and total weight of reinforcement steel. **(7 marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Check the aspect ratio.**
$$\frac{l_y}{l_x} = \frac{4.5}{3.5} = 1.286$$

**Step 2: Apply IS 456 criterion.**
Since $\dfrac{l_y}{l_x} = 1.286 < 2$, the slab is classified as a **Two-Way Slab**. (2 marks for ratio, 1 mark for classification)

**Step 3: Justify structural behavior.**
In a two-way slab, the load is transferred to all four supporting edges in both directions. Consequently, **main reinforcement is provided in BOTH directions** — along the shorter span ($l_x$ direction) to resist primary bending, and along the longer span ($l_y$ direction) to resist secondary bending moments arising from two-way action. (2 marks)

**Step 4: State design implications.**
- The maximum bending moment coefficients are taken from **IS 456 Table 12** (or SP-16 charts) for two-way slabs.
- Torsion reinforcement must be provided at the corners if the slab is simply supported on both edges meeting at that corner. (2 marks)

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Volume of concrete.**
$$V_c = l_x \times l_y \times D = 3.5 \times 4.5 \times 0.150 = 2.3625 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Shuttering area.**
- Soffit area = $3.5 \times 4.5 = 15.75 \text{ m}^2$
- Edge area = $2(l_x + l_y) \times D = 2(3.5 + 4.5) \times 0.150 = 2 \times 8.0 \times 0.150 = 2.40 \text{ m}^2$
- Total shuttering = $15.75 + 2.40 = 18.15 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 3: Main steel (12 mm @ 130 mm c/c, along $l_y$ direction = 4.5 m direction).**
- Unit weight of 12 mm bar: $w_{12} = 0.006165 \times 12^2 = 0.8878 \text{ kg/m}$
- Number of main bars across 4.5 m width: $n = \dfrac{4500}{130} + 1 = 34.6 + 1 \approx 36 \text{ bars}$
- Length of each main bar: $l_{\text{bar}} = 3.5 + 2 \times (9 \times 0.012) = 3.5 + 0.216 = 3.716 \text{ m}$ (with 90° hooks at both ends)
- Total main steel length: $L_{\text{main}} = 36 \times 3.716 = 133.78 \text{ m}$
- Total main steel weight: $W_{\text{main}} = 133.78 \times 0.8878 \approx 118.77 \text{ kg}$

**Step 4: Distribution steel (10 mm @ 180 mm c/c, along $l_x$ direction = 3.5 m direction).**
- Unit weight of 10 mm bar: $w_{10} = 0.6165 \text{ kg/m}$
- Number of distribution bars across 3.5 m: $n = \dfrac{3500}{180} + 1 = 19.44 + 1 \approx 20 \text{ bars}$
- Length of each: $l_{\text{bar}} = 4.5 + 2 \times (9 \times 0.010) = 4.5 + 0.18 = 4.68 \text{ m}$
- Total length: $L_{\text{dist}} = 20 \times 4.68 = 93.6 \text{ m}$
- Total weight: $W_{\text{dist}} = 93.6 \times 0.6165 = 57.70 \text{ kg}$

**Step 5: Crank bars (extra).**
Assume 2 extra crank bars at each end: $2 \times 2 = 4$ crank bars of 12 mm.  
Each crank bar additional length = $0.42 \times d$ on each side (where $d = 150 - 20 - 6 = 124$ mm = 0.124 m).
Extra length per crank = $2 \times 0.42 \times 0.124 = 0.104 \text{ m}$.
Total extra weight = $4 \times 0.104 \times 0.8878 \approx 0.37 \text{ kg}$ (negligible in this case).

**Step 6: Add 10% wastage.**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{raw}} &= 118.77 + 57.70 + 0.37 = 176.84 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{final}} &= 176.84 \times 1.10 = 194.52 \text{ kg} \approx 195 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Summary Table.**

| Item | Quantity |
| :--- | :--- |
| Concrete Volume | 2.3625 m³ |
| Shuttering Area | 18.15 m² |
| Total Steel (with wastage) | 195 kg |
| Steel per m³ | 82.5 kg/m³ (within 70–90 kg/m³ thumb rule ✓) |

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**  
> 
> An office building has **6 identical one-way RCC slabs**, each with the following details:
> - **Effective span = 4.0 m, Width = 3.0 m, Slab thickness = 130 mm**
> - **Main bars: 10 mm $\phi$ @ 120 mm c/c**
> - **Distribution bars: 8 mm $\phi$ @ 150 mm c/c**
> - **Clear cover = 20 mm, Fe500 steel**
> 
> **Required:**  
> **(a)** Compute the **total volume of concrete** and the **total area of formwork** for all 6 slabs. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Prepare a **Bar Bending Schedule (BBS)** for one slab and compute the **total quantity of steel in kg** for the entire floor. **(7 marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Volume of concrete for one slab.**
$$V_1 = 4.0 \times 3.0 \times 0.130 = 1.56 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Total volume for 6 slabs.**
$$V_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 1.56 = 9.36 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Shuttering area for one slab (soffit).**
$$A_{\text{soffit}} = 4.0 \times 3.0 = 12.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Edge shuttering for one slab.**
$$A_{\text{edge}} = 2 \times (4.0 + 3.0) \times 0.130 = 14.0 \times 0.130 = 1.82 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Total shuttering per slab.**
$$A_{\text{slab}} = 12.0 + 1.82 = 13.82 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6: Total shuttering for 6 slabs.**
$$A_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 13.82 = 82.92 \text{ m}^2$$

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Bar Bending Schedule for one slab.**

| Bar Mark | Type | Dia (mm) | Spacing (mm c/c) | Length/bar (m) | Nos | Total Length (m) | Unit Wt (kg/m) | Weight (kg) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A1 | Main | 10 | 120 | 4.18 | 26 | 108.68 | 0.6165 | 67.00 |
| B1 | Distribution | 8 | 150 | 3.14 | 21 | 65.94 | 0.3946 | 26.02 |
| | | | | | | | **Subtotal** | **93.02** |

**Step 2: Bar count derivations.**
- Main bars: $n = \dfrac{3000}{120} + 1 = 25 + 1 = 26$ bars. Length with hooks = $4.0 + 2(9 \times 0.010) = 4.18$ m.
- Distribution bars: $n = \dfrac{4000}{150} + 1 = 26.67 + 1 \approx 28$ bars. (Corrected: 28 bars; length = $3.0 + 2(9 \times 0.008) = 3.144$ m).

**Step 3: Recomputed total length of distribution steel.**
$$L_{\text{dist}} = 28 \times 3.144 = 88.03 \text{ m}$$
$$W_{\text{dist}} = 88.03 \times 0.3946 = 34.74 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Revised BBS for one slab.**

| Bar Mark | Type | Dia (mm) | Length/bar (m) | Nos | Total Length (m) | Unit Wt (kg/m) | Weight (kg) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A1 | Main | 10 | 4.18 | 26 | 108.68 | 0.6165 | 67.00 |
| B1 | Distribution | 8 | 3.144 | 28 | 88.03 | 0.3946 | 34.74 |
| | | | | | | **Subtotal** | **101.74** |

**Step 5: Add 10% wastage.**
$$W_{\text{one slab}} = 101.74 \times 1.10 = 111.91 \text{ kg} \approx 112 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Total steel for 6 slabs.**
$$W_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 112 = 672 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7: Verification with thumb rule.**
Steel per m³ = $\dfrac{672}{9.36} \approx 71.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$ → within the 70–90 kg/m³ range for slabs. **Result verified.** ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add hook length:** The hook length ($9\phi$ for Fe415, $12\phi$ for Fe500) is **not optional** in BBS. Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Confusing main vs. distribution direction:** In a one-way slab, main bars are along the **SHORTER span**, not the longer one. Many students invert this.
> 3. **Ignoring cover while computing effective depth:** Always use $d = D - \text{cover} - \phi/2$, never $d = D$.
> 4. **Omitting the wastage/overlap percentage:** Real-world steel always includes 8–12% extra. A BBS without this looks incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong unit for shuttering:** Shuttering is **m²**, NOT m³. Examiners deduct full marks if the unit is wrong.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classification of slabs** depends on the **aspect ratio $l_y / l_x$**: ≥ 2 is one-way, < 2 is two-way (IS 456).
- **Volume of concrete** = $L \times B \times D$ (in m³).
- **Shuttering area** = soffit + edges = $L \times B + 2(L + B) \times D$ (in m²).
- **Steel unit weight formula:** $W = 0.006165 \times \phi^2$ kg/m (memorize this constant).
- **Number of bars:** $n = \dfrac{\text{width}}{\text{spacing}} + 1$.
- **Hook length** = $9\phi$ for Fe415 and $12\phi$ for Fe500.
- **Crank bar extra length** ≈ $0.42 \times d$ on each side of the bend.
- **Effective span** (IS 456) = $\min(L_n + d, L_c)$.
- **Thumb rule for steel in slab** = 70 to 90 kg per m³ of concrete.
- **Always add 8–12% wastage** in final steel quantity.
- **Unit weight of RCC** = 25 kN/m³ (remember this for dead load calculation).
- **Clear cover for slab** = 20 mm (mild exposure) or 30 mm (moderate exposure).
- **M20 concrete mix** = 1 : 1.5 : 3 (cement : sand : aggregate by volume).
- **Default slab thickness** for residential = 100–150 mm; for commercial = 150–200 mm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Footings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Column Footings # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Column Footing** (also called a *pad foundation* or *spread footing*) is a structural element constructed beneath a reinforced cement concrete (RCC) column to distribute the concentrated superstructure load over a larger area of supporting soil, ensuring that the **safe bearing capacity (SBC)** of the soil is never exceeded. In the context of *Quantity Surveying and Valuation*, the footing represents a critical *below-ground (sub-structure)* item that must be measured systematically for **earthwork excavation, plain cement concrete (PCC) bedding, RCC, shuttering, and reinforcement steel**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student is expected to compute the **quantities of concrete, PCC, steel, and earthwork** for isolated, combined, stepped, and sloped footings using the relevant standard formulas, and apply **IS 1200 (Method of Measurement of Building Works)** deduction rules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine pressing your thumb firmly against a soft sponge. Your thumb will sink in and tear the sponge. Now press with your **flat palm** — the same force spreads across a much wider area, and the sponge stays intact. A **column** is like the thumb — it carries the entire building load concentrated on a tiny area. The **soil** below is the sponge. The **footing** is the *palm* — a wider, thicker RCC slab that spreads the load gently and uniformly into the earth so the soil does not fail in **shear or settlement**. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Thumb Rules > [!NOTE] > **Standard Dimensional Rules Used in KTU Board Valuation:** > - **Projection of footing beyond column face:** **75 mm to 150 mm** (commonly adopted as **150 mm** in board problems). > - **Thickness of PCC bed (lean concrete):** **75 mm to 100 mm** (usually taken as **100 mm** in estimation). > - **Minimum depth of isolated footing slab:** **300 mm** (so that the punching shear cone is contained). > - **Standard cover to main footing reinforcement:** **50 mm** (for footings resting directly on soil). > - **Sloped footings:** Critical depth at edge is kept **≥ 150 mm** to permit proper concrete pouring. ## 1.4 Types of Footings | Type | When Used | Load Transfer | |------|-----------|---------------| | **Isolated / Spread Footing** | Single column, soil SBC adequate | One column → one pad | | **Combined Footing** | Two columns very close, soil weak | Two columns → one mat | | **Strap (Cantilever) Footing** | Two columns near property line | Independent pads connected by a beam | | **Raft / Mat Footing** | Very low SBC, heavy loads | All columns → one continuous slab | | **Pile Footing / Pile Cap** | Very weak top soil | Column → pile cap → deep piles | > [!TIP] > For KTU Module-3 numericals, **isolated stepped footings** and **sloped footings** are the most frequently tested variants. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Geometry of a Stepped Footing A typical isolated stepped footing is composed of the following **vertical layers (from top to bottom):** 1. **Column neck / pedestal** — of size equal to the column, projecting into the footing. 2. **Top step (Footing slab top step)** — of size equal to footing base in plan. 3. **Bottom step (Footing slab bottom step)** — same base, but thicker section near column. 4. **PCC bed (Plain Cement Concrete / Lean Concrete)** — **100 mm thick** leveling course. 5. **Earthwork excavation** — extending on all sides to allow formwork placement. > The depth of the footing is **not uniform**; the section is **thicker directly under the column** and **thinner at the edges** to economize on concrete. ## 2.2 Why Stepped and Sloped Footings? - **Material Economy:** Concrete at the edges carries less bending moment. A constant thick slab is wasteful. - **Shear Cone Containment:** The depth under the column is sufficient to resist **punching shear** in a 45° cone pattern. - **Cost Optimization:** A sloped/stepped profile reduces the volume of concrete (and hence cost) by **15%–30%** compared to a uniform-depth footing. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Volume of **rectangular slab** (single step) | $V = L \times B \times D$ | m³ | L, B = plan dimensions; D = depth | | 2 | Volume of **stepped footing** (2 steps) | $V = L_1 B_1 D_1 + L_2 B_2 D_2$ | m³ | Sum of each step's volume | | 3 | Volume of **sloped footing** (Frustum of pyramid) | $V = \dfrac{h}{3} \left[ A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2} \right]$ | m³ | $A_1$ = top area; $A_2$ = bottom area; h = vertical depth | | 4 | Volume of **PCC bed** | $V_{PCC} = (L+0.3) \times (B+0.3) \times 0.10$ | m³ | Extend 150 mm on each side | | 5 | Volume of **earthwork excavation** | $V_{exc} = (L+0.6) \times (B+0.6) \times D_{total}$ | m³ | Extend 300 mm on each side (working space) | | 6 | **Weight of steel** | $W = D^2 / 162 \times L_{total}$ | kg | D = dia in mm, L in m; gives kg/m of bar | | 7 | Total bar length from one kg | $L = 162 / D^2$ | m/kg | Used in reverse calculation | | 8 | Deduction for column in footing | **No deduction** for column volume passing through footing (as per IS 1200) | — | Board exam rule | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Convention:** While measuring RCC footing, **do NOT deduct** the volume of reinforcement steel. Similarly, the **column passing through the footing is NOT deducted** from the footing concrete volume. This is a recurring marking scheme trap. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Accurate footing quantity estimation directly impacts: - **Contractor's bidding price** — over/under-estimation can cause contract loss or financial loss. - **Billing of substructure work** — measured as running account (RA) bills in stage-wise construction. - **Valuation of existing buildings** — for resale, insurance, or property tax assessment. - **Cost optimization** — selecting stepped vs. sloped vs. flat slab footing based on concrete saving. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Sloped Footing Volume (Frustum Formula) The footing slab is treated as a **frustum of a rectangular pyramid** (or a wedge). The derivation uses the *principle of geometric mean area*. **Given:** Top area = $A_1$, Bottom area = $A_2$, vertical height = $h$. For a pyramid frustum, the volume is obtained by integrating the cross-sectional area as a function of height: $$V = \int_{0}^{h} A(x)\,dx$$ For a linear taper, the area varies as a function of the square of the linear dimension. Solving the integral for a square/rectangular frustum gives the standard prismoidal formula: $$V = \frac{h}{3} \left[ A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2} \right]$$ > **Interpretation:** The middle term $\sqrt{A_1 A_2}$ is the *geometric mean* of the top and bottom areas — this is what differentiates the frustum formula from a simple average. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Step-by-Step Quantity Estimation (KTU Standard Problem) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU 2024 Pattern):** > Estimate the quantities of (i) Earthwork excavation, (ii) PCC bed, (iii) RCC for stepped footing, and (iv) Steel reinforcement, for an isolated column footing with the following data: > - **Column size:** 300 mm × 300 mm > - **Footing size (base):** 1500 mm × 1500 mm > - **Footing depth at column face:** 450 mm > - **Footing depth at edge:** 150 mm (with a 150 mm step at the column) > - **Top step size:** 900 mm × 900 mm, depth = 300 mm (above the bottom step) > - **Bottom step size:** 1500 mm × 1500 mm, depth = 150 mm (at edges) > - **PCC thickness:** 100 mm, with 150 mm projection on all sides. > - **Working space for excavation:** 300 mm on all sides beyond PCC. > - **Reinforcement:** 12 mm dia bars @ 120 mm c/c both ways in bottom step; 10 mm dia bars @ 150 mm c/c both ways in top step (in two layers). > - **Assume clear cover = 50 mm** at bottom, **hook & bend allowance** already included in spacing. --- ### **Step (i) — Earthwork Excavation** Length of pit = $1.500 + 2 \times 0.300 = 2.100$ m Breadth of pit = $1.500 + 2 \times 0.300 = 2.100$ m Total depth = PCC + Footing = $0.100 + 0.450 = 0.550$ m $$V_{exc} = 2.100 \times 2.100 \times 0.550$$ $$V_{exc} = 4.41 \times 0.550 = 2.4255 \text{ m}^3$$ --- ### **Step (ii) — PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) Bed** Length of PCC = $1.500 + 2 \times 0.150 = 1.800$ m Breadth of PCC = $1.500 + 2 \times 0.150 = 1.800$ m Thickness = 0.100 m $$V_{PCC} = 1.800 \times 1.800 \times 0.100 = 0.3240 \text{ m}^3$$ --- ### **Step (iii) — RCC for Stepped Footing** The footing has **two distinct rectangular steps**: **Bottom Step (larger, thinner at edges):** - Dimensions: 1.500 m × 1.500 m × 0.150 m $$V_{bottom} = 1.500 \times 1.500 \times 0.150 = 0.3375 \text{ m}^3$$ **Top Step (smaller, thicker at column):** - Dimensions: 0.900 m × 0.900 m × 0.300 m $$V_{top} = 0.900 \times 0.900 \times 0.300 = 0.2430 \text{ m}^3$$ **Total RCC Footing Volume:** $$V_{RCC} = 0.3375 + 0.2430 = 0.5805 \text{ m}^3$$ > As per **IS 1200**, no deduction is made for the column embedded in the footing and no deduction for reinforcement. --- ### **Step (iv) — Steel Reinforcement (Using $W = D^2/162 \times L$)** **Bottom mesh (12 mm dia @ 120 mm c/c, both ways):** - Number of bars along 1.5 m direction (with 50 mm cover on both sides, length available = 1.500 − 0.100 = 1.400 m): $$N_1 = \frac{1400}{120} + 1 \approx 12.67 \rightarrow \text{take } 13 \text{ bars}$$ - Total length along X = $13 \times 1.400 = 18.20$ m - By symmetry, same length in Y direction = 18.20 m - **Total length (12 mm)** = $18.20 + 18.20 = 36.40$ m $$W_{12} = \frac{12^2}{162} \times 36.40 = \frac{144}{162} \times 36.40 = 0.8889 \times 36.40 \approx 32.36 \text{ kg}$$ **Top mesh (10 mm dia @ 150 mm c/c, both ways, two layers):** - Effective length = 0.900 − 0.100 = 0.800 m $$N_2 = \frac{800}{150} + 1 \approx 6.33 \rightarrow \text{take } 7 \text{ bars per layer}$$ - Length per layer (X-dir) = $7 \times 0.800 = 5.60$ m - Two layers in X = $5.60 \times 2 = 11.20$ m - By symmetry in Y = 11.20 m - **Total length (10 mm)** = $11.20 + 11.20 = 22.40$ m $$W_{10} = \frac{10^2}{162} \times 22.40 = \frac{100}{162} \times 22.40 = 0.6173 \times 22.40 \approx 13.83 \text{ kg}$$ **Total Steel:** $$W_{steel} = 32.36 + 13.83 \approx 46.19 \text{ kg}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Quantities | Item | Quantity | Unit | Billed as | |------|----------|------|-----------| | Earthwork excavation | **2.4255** | m³ | Earthwork in foundation | | PCC (1:4:8) bed | **0.3240** | m³ | Plain concrete in foundation | | RCC (1:2:4) footing | **0.5805** | m³ | RCC in footing | | Steel (10 mm + 12 mm) | **46.19** | kg | TMT bars / Fe-500 | > [!TIP] > **Cross-Check:** Steel percentage = $\frac{46.19 / 7850}{0.5805} \times 100 \approx 1.01\%$ — this falls within the typical **0.8% to 1.2%** range for isolated footings. A valid sanity check! # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Footing Component Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["ABOVE GROUND"] A1["COLUMN 300 mm x 300 mm
Carries superstructure load"] end subgraph B["FOOTING - SUB STRUCTURE LAYERS"] B1["TOP STEP 900 x 900 x 300 mm
RCC 1:2:4
Contains column pedestal"] B2["BOTTOM STEP 1500 x 1500 x 150 mm
RCC 1:2:4
Spreads load to PCC"] end subgraph C["FOUNDATION BED"] C1["PCC BED 1800 x 1800 x 100 mm
Plain Concrete 1:4:8
Leveling course"] C2["EARTHWORK EXCAVATION
2100 x 2100 x 550 mm
Working space 300 mm"] end subgraph D["SOIL"] D1["BEARING STRATUM
Safe Bearing Capacity SBC"] end subgraph E["REINFORCEMENT DETAILS"] E1["BOTTOM MESH 12 mm at 120 c/c
Both ways single layer"] E2["TOP MESH 10 mm at 150 c/c
Both ways double layer"] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> D1 E1 -.reinforces.-> B2 E2 -.reinforces.-> B1 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — Load Path & Quantity Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["LOAD FROM
COLUMN"] --> L2["TOP STEP
900 x 900 x 300"] L2 --> L3["BOTTOM STEP
1500 x 1500 x 150"] L3 --> L4["PCC BED
1800 x 1800 x 100"] L4 --> L5["SOIL SBC
Settlement Check"] Q1["Quantity Surveyor Inputs:
Column size, Footing dims,
Step depth, Cover, Bar dia"] --> Q2["Compute V_exc"] Q2 --> Q3["Compute V_PCC"] Q3 --> Q4["Compute V_RCC stepped"] Q4 --> Q5["Compute Steel kg using D2/162"] Q5 --> Q6["Abstract in BOQ with
IS 1200 item codes"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The left branch shows the *physical load transfer path*; the right branch shows the *quantity surveyor's computational workflow*. Both culminate at a measurable, billable item in the **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)**. ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Cross Section of a Stepped Footing ``` |<-- 300 mm -->| | COLUMN 300x300 | |___________________| | | | | | | TOP STEP | | <-- 300 mm deep | | 900 x 900 | | (RCC) |__|_______________|__| | | | BOTTOM STEP | <-- 150 mm deep | 1500 x 1500 | (RCC) |___________________| | | | PCC BED 100 mm | <-- 100 mm (1:4:8) |___________________| | NATURAL SOIL SBC | |___________________| ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List the various types of column footings used in building construction. Under what condition is a combined footing preferred over an isolated footing?* **Model Answer:** The common types are: (1) Isolated/Spread footing, (2) Combined footing, (3) Strap/Cantilever footing, (4) Raft/Mat footing, and (5) Pile footing/Pile cap. A **combined footing** is preferred when **two columns are very close to each other** such that their individual isolated footings would **overlap**, or when the **soil safe bearing capacity (SBC) is low**, requiring a larger bearing area that a single pad cannot provide. *(3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the formula used to estimate the volume of a sloped (trapezoidal) footing. What is the practical purpose of providing a 100 mm PCC bed beneath the RCC footing?* **Model Answer:** The volume of a sloped footing is calculated using the **frustum of pyramid (prismoidal) formula**: $$V = \frac{h}{3} \left[ A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2} \right]$$ where $A_1$ and $A_2$ are the top and bottom plan areas respectively, and $h$ is the vertical depth of the sloped section. The **100 mm PCC bed** (1:4:8 lean concrete) serves three purposes: 1. Provides a **level, uniform working surface** for the RCC footing. 2. **Prevents direct contact** of structural RCC with soil (avoids water absorption from fresh concrete). 3. Acts as a **shuttering base** and prevents mud-mixing during placement. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks) > **ESE Module Internal Choice — Typical KTU Pattern (7 + 7 Marks)** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **An isolated column footing is to be constructed with the following details:** - Column size: **400 mm × 400 mm** - Footing base: **1800 mm × 1800 mm** - Stepped footing with two steps: - **Bottom step:** 1800 mm × 1800 mm × 150 mm (depth at edges) - **Top step:** 1000 mm × 1000 mm × 350 mm (depth at column face) - PCC bed: **100 mm thick**, projecting **150 mm** beyond RCC on all sides. - Working space for excavation: **300 mm** on all sides beyond PCC. - **Reinforcement (bottom mesh):** 12 mm dia bars @ 130 mm c/c, both ways. Clear cover = 50 mm. **Calculate the quantities of:** - **(a)** Earthwork excavation, PCC (1:4:8) and RCC (1:2:4) for the footing. - **(b)** Steel reinforcement (in kg) for the bottom mesh, using $W = D^2/162 \times L$. --- #### **Model Solution (a) — Concrete & Earthwork** **Earthwork Excavation:** Total depth = PCC + Footing = $0.100 + (0.150 + 0.350) = 0.600$ m Pit dimensions = $1.800 + 2(0.300 + 0.150) = 2.700$ m $1.800 + 2 \times 0.450 = 2.700$ m $(2.700)^2 = 7.29$ m² $$V_{exc} = 2.700 \times 2.700 \times 0.600 = 7.29 \times 0.600 = 4.374 \text{ m}^3$$ **PCC Bed:** PCC dimensions = $1.800 + 0.300 = 2.100$ m $$V_{PCC} = 2.100 \times 2.100 \times 0.100 = 0.4410 \text{ m}^3$$ **RCC Footing (Stepped):** Bottom step: $1.800 \times 1.800 \times 0.150 = 0.4860$ m³ Top step: $1.000 \times 1.000 \times 0.350 = 0.3500$ m³ $$V_{RCC} = 0.4860 + 0.3500 = 0.8360 \text{ m}^3$$ --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Steel Reinforcement** Effective length of bars = $1.800 - 2 \times 0.050 = 1.700$ m Number of bars per direction = $\dfrac{1700}{130} + 1 \approx 14.08 \rightarrow$ take **15 bars** Length in X-direction = $15 \times 1.700 = 25.50$ m Length in Y-direction = $15 \times 1.700 = 25.50$ m Total length of 12 mm bars = $25.50 + 25.50 = 51.00$ m $$W_{steel} = \frac{12^2}{162} \times 51.00 = \frac{144}{162} \times 51.00 = 0.8889 \times 51.00 \approx 45.33 \text{ kg}$$ > **Answer:** Earthwork = **4.374 m³**, PCC = **0.441 m³**, RCC = **0.836 m³**, Steel = **45.33 kg**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **A sloped isolated footing has the following data:** - Column size: **300 mm × 300 mm** - Top of footing: **900 mm × 900 mm** (at column face) - Bottom of footing: **1500 mm × 1500 mm** (at base) - Total vertical depth of sloped section: **400 mm** - PCC bed: **100 mm thick**, with 150 mm projection on all sides. - Working space for excavation: 300 mm beyond PCC. - **Reinforcement:** 12 mm dia bars @ 125 mm c/c both ways (single mesh) at the bottom; clear cover 50 mm. **Calculate the quantities of:** - **(a)** Earthwork, PCC, and RCC (sloped section using prismoidal formula). - **(b)** Steel reinforcement in kg. --- #### **Model Solution (a) — Concrete & Earthwork** **Earthwork:** Total depth = $0.100 + 0.400 = 0.500$ m Pit size = $1.500 + 2(0.300 + 0.150) = 2.400$ m $$V_{exc} = 2.400 \times 2.400 \times 0.500 = 2.880 \text{ m}^3$$ **PCC Bed:** PCC size = $1.500 + 0.300 = 1.800$ m $$V_{PCC} = 1.800 \times 1.800 \times 0.100 = 0.3240 \text{ m}^3$$ **RCC Sloped Footing (Prismoidal Formula):** $A_1 = 0.900 \times 0.900 = 0.81$ m² $A_2 = 1.500 \times 1.500 = 2.25$ m² $h = 0.400$ m $$V = \frac{0.400}{3} \left[ 0.81 + 2.25 + \sqrt{0.81 \times 2.25} \right]$$ $$V = \frac{0.400}{3} \left[ 3.06 + \sqrt{1.8225} \right] = \frac{0.400}{3} \left[ 3.06 + 1.350 \right]$$ $$V = \frac{0.400}{3} \times 4.410 = 0.5880 \text{ m}^3$$ --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Steel Reinforcement** Effective length = $1.500 - 2 \times 0.050 = 1.400$ m Number of bars per direction = $\dfrac{1400}{125} + 1 \approx 12.2 \rightarrow$ take **13 bars** Length per direction = $13 \times 1.400 = 18.20$ m Total length (both ways) = $18.20 \times 2 = 36.40$ m $$W_{steel} = \frac{12^2}{162} \times 36.40 = 0.8889 \times 36.40 \approx 32.36 \text{ kg}$$ > **Answer:** Earthwork = **2.880 m³**, PCC = **0.324 m³**, RCC = **0.588 m³**, Steel = **32.36 kg**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These!):** > 1. **Forgetting the working space** (300 mm) in earthwork calculation — causes a 15-20% under-estimation and **loss of 1–2 marks**. > 2. **Not extending PCC by 150 mm** beyond RCC on all sides — examiners specifically check this. > 3. **Deducting the column volume** from footing RCC — this is **WRONG** as per IS 1200; no deduction is applicable. Some students wrongly deduct it. > 4. **Forgetting the cover** (50 mm) in steel calculation — leads to an over-estimation of bar length. > 5. **Using $L/125$ directly** for number of bars without the **"+1" correction** — this is the most common error. > 6. **Wrong unit** in final answer — always write **m³** for concrete and **kg** (or quintal/MT) for steel. Marks are cut for missing units. > 7. **Rounding off mid-calculation** — round only the **final answer**; intermediate values must retain 3-4 decimals. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Column Footings** > - **Five types of footings:** Isolated, Combined, Strap, Raft, Pile. Know the trigger condition for each. > - **Standard PCC thickness = 100 mm** with 150 mm projection beyond RCC. > - **Standard working space for excavation = 300 mm** beyond PCC. > - **Cover at bottom of footing = 50 mm** (because footing is in direct contact with PCC/soil). > - **Stepped footing volume** = sum of volumes of each rectangular step. > - **Sloped footing volume** = $\dfrac{h}{3}\left[A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\right]$ (prismoidal / frustum formula). > - **Steel weight** = $\dfrac{D^2}{162} \times L$ (D in mm, L in m → result in kg). > - **Number of bars** in a mesh = $\dfrac{\text{Effective length}}{\text{spacing}} + 1$ (don't forget the +1). > - **IS 1200 deductions:** **No deduction** for column embedded in footing; **no deduction** for reinforcement steel. > - **Units to write:** Earthwork / PCC / RCC in **m³**; Steel in **kg** (or convert to quintal/MT in BOQ). > - **Sanity check on steel %:** For isolated footings, $W_{steel}/V_{RCC}$ in kg/m³ should lie between **60 kg/m³ and 100 kg/m³** (≈ 0.8% to 1.2% by volume). > - **Most-tested formulas** in KTU exams: (1) prismoidal formula for sloped footing, (2) D²/162 for steel, (3) addition of working space and PCC projection in excavation.

topics.title: Retaining Wall; Road Estimation – Estimation of Earthwork from Longitudinal Section for Metaled Road; Estimation of Sanitary and Water Supply Work – Water Tank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Retaining Wall; Road Estimation – Estimation of Earthwork from Longitudinal Section for Metaled Road; Estimation of Sanitary and Water Supply Work – Water Tank # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Retaining Wall > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A *retaining wall* is a structure designed and constructed to resist the lateral pressure of soil or other backfill material when there is a desired change in ground elevation that exceeds the angle of repose of the soil. In Quantity Surveying, the estimation of a retaining wall involves the detailed measurement of all construction components: earthwork in excavation, Plain Cement Concrete (PCC) in foundation, Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) footing, RCC stem (vertical wall), and the backfill behind the wall. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine you are trying to hold a mound of sand piled up at an angle steeper than it naturally wants to rest. Gravity keeps pulling the sand down. A retaining wall acts like a rigid, vertical "bookend" that fights this lateral push from the soil, water, or surcharge load. From the estimator's perspective, we are essentially breaking this bookend into simple geometric pieces (rectangles and trapezoids) and calculating how much concrete, steel, and excavation each piece requires. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Units in Estimation (KTU Convention):** > - Earthwork excavation: **Cubic metre (m³)** > - Concrete (PCC/RCC): **Cubic metre (m³)** > - Reinforcement steel: **Kilogram (kg)** or **Quintal (q)** > - Plaster / Pointing: **Square metre (m²)** > - Weep holes: **Number (No.)** ### Classification of Retaining Walls | Type | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Gravity Retaining Wall** | Relies on its own massive weight to resist earth pressure. Made of plain concrete or masonry. | Low height walls (< 3 m) | | **Cantilever Retaining Wall** | An L-shaped or T-shaped RCC structure using a stem, heel, and toe slab. | Medium height (3–6 m) | | **Counterfort Retaining Wall** | RCC wall with vertical supports (counterforts) connecting stem and heel slab at intervals. | High walls (> 6 m) | | **Buttress Wall** | Similar to counterfort, but supports are on the front (visible) face. | High walls with space constraints | --- ## 1.2 Road Estimation – Earthwork from Longitudinal Section > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** Estimation of earthwork for a metaled road involves calculating the volume of earth required to be cut (in cutting zones) or filled (in embankment zones) along the proposed road alignment, using the **Longitudinal Section (L-Section)** running along the centerline and the design formation level. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a road like a ribbon stretched over a hilly terrain. The natural ground is uneven, but the road surface must be smooth and gently sloping. Earthwork estimation is the process of figuring out how much soil must be *added* (embankment) or *removed* (cutting) to make the terrain match the desired road profile — just like sculpting clay to a flat top, but stretched over many kilometers. **Components of a Metaled Road Cross-Section:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metaled Road Cross-Section (KTU Standard):** > - **Formation Width (W):** Total width of the prepared surface, typically **7.0 m to 10.0 m** for rural roads. > - **Side Slopes:** Slope of the embankment edges, given as ratio (e.g., **2:1** horizontal:vertical). > - **Metaled Surface (WBM / BM / SDBC):** Top crust, typically **3.75 m to 7.0 m** wide. > - **Shoulders:** Earthen sides providing lateral support. > - **Side Slopes of Cutting/Embankment:** Define the trapezoidal cross-section shape. --- ## 1.3 Estimation of Sanitary and Water Supply Work – Water Tank > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU):** Estimation of a water tank involves detailed measurement of an RCC (or masonry) storage structure including the **base slab, vertical side walls, top dome/slab, column supports, access manhole, and pipe connections** for inlet, outlet, overflow, and washout pipes. Sanitary work estimation includes the quantities of pipes (CI, PVC, GI), sanitary fittings (WC, washbasin, urinals), and associated masonry chambers. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** A water tank is essentially a "concrete box" designed to safely hold water without leaking. From an estimation standpoint, we treat it as a combination of simple solids — like a giant rectangular "bucket" with a lid. We compute how much concrete is needed for the bottom (slab), the four sides (walls), and the lid (roof), and add the steel reinforcement based on standard bar-bending schedules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Water Tank Components (KTU Pattern):** > - **Base / Floor Slab:** RCC, typically **150 mm to 200 mm** thick. > - **Side Walls:** RCC walls, **150 mm to 200 mm** thick. > - **Top Slab / Dome:** RCC flat slab or spherical dome. > - **Columns & Braces:** For overhead tanks (intze-type). > - **Pipes:** CI/PVC pipes for inlet, outlet, scour, overflow. > - **Fittings:** Valves, foot valves, manhole covers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Retaining Wall – Components & Estimation Logic A retaining wall is broken down into the following components for measurement purposes: 1. **Earthwork in Excavation for Foundation** - Trapezoidal pit excavation - Includes working space allowance (**0.30 m to 0.50 m** on each side) 2. **PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) Bed / Levelling Course** - Thickness: **75 mm to 100 mm** below footing - Provides level platform and prevents direct contact of structural concrete with soil 3. **RCC Footing Slab (Base Slab)** - Includes the heel slab (under backfill) and toe slab (in front) - Usually **300 mm to 500 mm** thick 4. **RCC Stem (Vertical Wall)** - Thickness varies: more at the base, less at the top (often tapered) 5. **RCC Counterforts** (if applicable) - Vertical T-shaped supports at regular spacing (**1/3 to 1/2 of height** apart) 6. **Backfill with Selected Earth** - Well-compacted earth behind the wall with proper drainage layer 7. **Weep Holes** - **100 mm to 150 mm** diameter PVC pipes through the stem - Spaced at **1 m to 2 m** horizontally and **0.5 m to 1.0 m** vertically > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' of Each Component:** Each layer performs a structural or functional role — PCC distributes load evenly, RCC footing resists overturning and sliding, the stem resists lateral earth pressure, weep holes relieve hydrostatic pressure, and backfill provides drainage. --- ## 2.2 Road Earthwork – Conceptual Logic The earthwork calculation from a longitudinal section follows this logic: 1. **Plot L-Section**: Natural Ground Level (NGL) vs Proposed Formation Level (FL) along chainage. 2. **Identify Cut / Fill**: Where FL > NGL → Embankment (Fill). Where FL < NGL → Cutting. 3. **Compute Depth (h)** at each chainage point. 4. **Compute Cross-Sectional Area (A)** at each chainage: - For embankment with side slope *s:1* (s horizontal, 1 vertical): - Area = $\left(B + s \cdot h\right) \cdot h$ - Where *B* = formation width, *h* = depth of fill/cut. 5. **Compute Volume** between two successive chainages using the chosen formula. --- ## 2.3 Water Tank Estimation Logic For an RCC rectangular water tank, estimation involves: 1. **Excavation in Foundation** 2. **PCC Bed** (75 mm–100 mm thick) 3. **RCC Base Slab** 4. **RCC Vertical Walls** (4 sides) 5. **RCC Top Slab** (or dome) 6. **Reinforcement Steel** (calculated as **80–120 kg/m³ of RCC**) 7. **Plaster / Waterproofing** (inside surfaces) 8. **Pipes & Fittings** 9. **Manhole & Cover** --- ## KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Formulas for KTU 2024 Examinations** | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | Trapezoidal cross-sectional area of embankment | $A = (B + s \cdot h) \cdot h$ | B = formation width, s = side slope ratio, h = height | | Trapezoidal cross-sectional area of cutting | $A = (B + s \cdot h) \cdot h + 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot s \cdot h$ | With side drains of depth *d* | | **Mean Depth Method** | $V = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2) \cdot L$ | Quick method; assumes average of areas | | **Trapezoidal Rule (Average End Area)** | $V = \tfrac{L}{2} \cdot [A_1 + A_2]$ | Standard; same as mean depth | | **Prismoidal Formula** | $V = \tfrac{L}{6} \cdot [A_1 + 4A_m + A_2]$ | Most accurate; uses mid-area $A_m$ | | Volume of earthwork in embankment (Trapezoidal) | $V = \left( B + \tfrac{4}{3} s \bar{h} \right) \cdot \bar{h} \cdot L$ | Where $\bar{h}$ = mean depth | | Retaining wall stem (tapered) volume | $V = \tfrac{H}{3} \cdot (A_{\text{bottom}} + A_{\text{top}} + \sqrt{A_{\text{bottom}} \cdot A_{\text{top}}})$ | Frustum of pyramid | | Quantity of steel reinforcement | $Q_{\text{steel}} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{RCC}}$ | $\rho \approx 80$–$120 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | | Volume of PCC bed | $V_{\text{PCC}} = L_{\text{foundation}} \cdot B_{\text{foundation}} \cdot t_{\text{PCC}}$ | t = thickness of PCC | | Water tank capacity | $V_{\text{tank}} = L \cdot B \cdot H$ | Rectangular tank | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Trapezoidal Cross-Sectional Area for Embankment Consider an embankment with: - Formation width (top) = $B$ - Side slope = $s:1$ (s horizontal, 1 vertical) - Height of embankment = $h$ The cross-section is a trapezoid with: - Top width = $B$ - Bottom width = $B + 2 \cdot s \cdot h$ (slopes extend outward by $s \cdot h$ on each side) - Height = $h$ By the area formula for a trapezoid: $$ A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\text{Top width} + \text{Bottom width}) \cdot h $$ $$ A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ B + (B + 2sh) \right] \cdot h $$ $$ A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ 2B + 2sh \right] \cdot h $$ $$ A = (B + sh) \cdot h $$ > **Conversion Logic Explanation:** We add the top and bottom widths, divide by 2 (because a trapezoid is the average of two parallel sides), and multiply by the perpendicular height. The "bottom width" exceeds the top width by $2sh$ because each side slopes outward by $s \cdot h$ on either edge of the embankment. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Volume of Earthwork using Prismoidal Formula Between two successive chainages $D_1$ and $D_2$ separated by distance $L$: - Let $A_1$ = cross-sectional area at chainage 1 - Let $A_2$ = cross-sectional area at chainage 2 - Let $A_m$ = cross-sectional area at the midpoint (using mean depth $\bar{h}$) By Simpson's rule (prismoidal formula): $$ V = \frac{L}{6} \cdot (A_1 + 4 A_m + A_2) $$ **Derivation Steps:** $$ V = \int_0^L A(x) \, dx $$ For linear variation of area with distance, the second-degree polynomial interpolation gives: $$ A(x) = A_1 + \frac{(A_2 - A_1)}{L} \cdot x $$ But for prismatic accuracy with varying side slope, we account for the parabolic nature: $$ V = \frac{L}{6} \cdot (A_1 + 4 A_m + A_2) $$ > **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The Trapezoidal rule assumes the cross-section varies linearly between two points, but real terrain has a slight parabolic variation. Simpson's formula corrects this by adding the **mid-section area weighted by 4**, which mathematically captures the parabolic curvature of the actual ground profile. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Volume of Retaining Wall (Tapered Stem) For an RCC retaining wall stem of height $H$ with: - Bottom thickness = $b_1$, length $L$ - Top thickness = $b_2$, length $L$ (assuming constant length) The stem is a **frustum of a rectangular pyramid**. The volume formula: $$ V = \frac{H}{3} \cdot (A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2}) $$ where: - $A_1 = b_1 \cdot L$ (bottom cross-section area) - $A_2 = b_2 \cdot L$ (top cross-section area) **Step-by-Step Derivation:** The frustum volume = Volume of large pyramid − Volume of small pyramid cut from top: $$ V = \frac{1}{3} \cdot A_1 \cdot H_1 - \frac{1}{3} \cdot A_2 \cdot H_2 $$ By similar triangles, $H_1 / H_2 = b_1 / b_2$, leading to: $$ V = \frac{H}{3} \cdot (A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}) $$ > **Conversion Logic Explanation:** A tapered wall is not a prism; both the cross-section area and the "pyramid height" change with elevation. The frustum formula elegantly combines the two end areas with their geometric mean to capture this non-linear shape. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Road Earthwork Estimation > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A road has a formation width of **8 m** and side slopes of **2:1**. The depths of embankment at three successive chainages 0, 30, 60 m are **0.5 m, 0.7 m, 0.6 m** respectively. Calculate the volume of earthwork using (a) Trapezoidal rule and (b) Prismoidal formula. **Given:** $B = 8 \text{ m}$, $s = 2$, $h_1 = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $h_2 = 0.7 \text{ m}$, $h_3 = 0.6 \text{ m}$, $L = 30 \text{ m}$. ### Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Areas At each chainage, $A = (B + sh) \cdot h = (8 + 2h) \cdot h$ $$ A_1 = (8 + 2 \cdot 0.5) \cdot 0.5 = 9 \cdot 0.5 = 4.50 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_2 = (8 + 2 \cdot 0.7) \cdot 0.7 = 9.4 \cdot 0.7 = 6.58 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_3 = (8 + 2 \cdot 0.6) \cdot 0.6 = 9.2 \cdot 0.6 = 5.52 \text{ m}^2 $$ ### Step 2: Apply Trapezoidal Rule $$ V_{\text{trap}} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2) + \frac{L}{2} \cdot (A_2 + A_3) $$ $$ V_{\text{trap}} = \frac{30}{2} \cdot (4.50 + 6.58) + \frac{30}{2} \cdot (6.58 + 5.52) $$ $$ V_{\text{trap}} = 15 \cdot 11.08 + 15 \cdot 12.10 $$ $$ V_{\text{trap}} = 166.20 + 181.50 = 347.70 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 3: Apply Prismoidal Formula Mid-depth: $\bar{h} = (0.5 + 0.6)/2 = 0.55 \text{ m}$ (between chainages 0 and 30 m, using $h_1$ and $h_2$) $$ A_m = (8 + 2 \cdot 0.55) \cdot 0.55 = 9.1 \cdot 0.55 = 5.005 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ V_{\text{prism}} = \frac{30}{6} \cdot (A_1 + 4 A_m + A_2) $$ $$ V_{\text{prism}} = 5 \cdot (4.50 + 4 \cdot 5.005 + 6.58) $$ $$ V_{\text{prism}} = 5 \cdot (4.50 + 20.02 + 6.58) = 5 \cdot 31.10 $$ $$ V_{\text{prism}} = 155.50 \text{ m}^3 $$ > **Result:** Trapezoidal ≈ **347.70 m³**; Prismoidal ≈ **155.50 m³** (for the first segment). --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Retaining Wall Estimation > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** Estimate the quantities for a cantilever retaining wall with the following data: > - Total height above PCC = 5.0 m > - Stem thickness: 0.40 m at top, 0.60 m at bottom > - Stem length = 10 m > - Base slab: 4.5 m × 10 m × 0.50 m (toe + heel combined) > - PCC bed: 100 mm thick, 0.50 m extra on all sides ### Step 1: PCC Quantity PCC dimensions = $(4.5 + 1.0) \times (10 + 1.0) \times 0.10 = 5.5 \times 11.0 \times 0.10$ $$ V_{\text{PCC}} = 6.05 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 2: RCC Base Slab $$ V_{\text{base}} = 4.5 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.50 = 22.50 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 3: RCC Stem (Tapered Frustum) $$ V_{\text{stem}} = \frac{H}{3} \cdot (A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}) $$ - $A_1 = 0.60 \cdot 10 = 6.00 \text{ m}^2$ (bottom) - $A_2 = 0.40 \cdot 10 = 4.00 \text{ m}^2$ (top) $$ V_{\text{stem}} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot (6 + 4 + \sqrt{6 \cdot 4}) $$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot (10 + \sqrt{24}) $$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot (10 + 4.899) $$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 14.899 = 24.83 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 4: Reinforcement Steel (using $\rho = 100 \text{ kg/m}^3$) Total RCC volume = $22.50 + 24.83 = 47.33 \text{ m}^3$ $$ Q_{\text{steel}} = 100 \cdot 47.33 = 4733 \text{ kg} \approx 47.33 \text{ quintals} $$ --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Quick Decision Table | Problem Scenario | Recommended Formula | Reason | |---|---|---| | Flat ground, uniform depth | Trapezoidal Rule | Variation is linear | | Hilly terrain, varying depth | Prismoidal Formula | Captures parabolic variation | | Volume between many sections | Cumulative sum | Use Trapezoidal over each pair | | Tapered wall / column | Frustum formula | Two unequal parallel faces | | Long uniform embankment | Mean depth method | Fast approximation | | Mass haul diagram | Cumulative volume | For economic earthwork distribution | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Retaining Wall – Cross-Sectional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FoundationLayer["FOUNDATION ZONE"] A1["EARTHWORK EXCAVATION
Trapezoidal pit
0.30 m to 0.50 m working space"] A2["PCC BED
75 mm to 100 mm thick
1 : 4 : 8 nominal mix"] A3["RCC BASE SLAB
300 mm to 500 mm thick
L shape with heel and toe"] end subgraph StemZone["STEM ZONE"] B1["RCC STEM
Tapered
Top 0.40 m, Bottom 0.60 m"] B2["COUNTERFORT
Vertical T beam
Spacing 1/3 of height"] B3["WEEP HOLES
100 mm dia PVC
1 m to 2 m spacing"] end subgraph TopZone["BACKFILL ZONE"] C1["DRAINAGE LAYER
100 mm gravel"] C2["SELECTED BACKFILL
Compacted in layers"] C3["TOP SURFACE
Coping beam or earth"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 A3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** This flow shows the structural hierarchy of a cantilever retaining wall. The foundation zone (bottom) carries loads, the stem zone (middle) resists lateral earth pressure, and the backfill zone (top) provides drainage and load distribution. --- ## 4.2 Road Earthwork – Cross-Section Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EmbankmentSection["EMBANKMENT CROSS SECTION"] D1["FORMATION WIDTH B
Top width 8.0 m"] D2["SIDE SLOPES
2 : 1 ratio
s horizontal : 1 vertical"] D3["HEIGHT h
Design depth"] end subgraph MetalingSection["METALED SURFACE"] E1["WBM LAYER
75 mm to 100 mm thick"] E2["BITUMINOUS MACADAM
50 mm to 75 mm"] E3["WEARING COURSE
SDBC 25 mm"] end subgraph DrainageSection["DRAINAGE"] F1["SIDE DRAIN
Earthen trapezoidal"] F2["CULVERTS
At stream crossings"] end D1 --> D2 --> D3 D1 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 D3 --> F1 --> F2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The metaled road consists of three layers stacked on a properly shaped earthwork subgrade. The earthwork volume (embankment or cutting) is calculated separately from the surfacing material quantity. --- ## 4.3 Water Tank (RCC) – Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BaseZone["BASE ZONE"] G1["EXCAVATION
Pit depth 1.0 m to 1.5 m"] G2["PCC BED
100 mm thick"] G3["RCC BASE SLAB
150 mm to 200 mm"] end subgraph WallZone["WALL ZONE"] H1["RCC VERTICAL WALL
150 mm to 200 mm
4 sides"] H2["PLASTER INSIDE
12 mm cement plaster
Waterproof coating"] H3["PIPES
Inlet 50 mm CI
Outlet 75 mm CI
Overflow 100 mm
Scour 50 mm"] end subgraph TopZone2["TOP ZONE"] I1["RCC TOP SLAB
120 mm to 150 mm"] I2["MANHOLE
600 mm x 600 mm
CI cover"] I3["ACCESS LADDER
MS rungs"] end G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> H1 H1 --> H2 H1 --> H3 H1 --> I1 I1 --> I2 I1 --> I3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The water tank estimation follows a bottom-to-top approach — excavation → PCC → base slab → walls → top slab → fittings. Each layer is measured independently and summed for the total cost. --- ## 4.4 Longitudinal Section (L-Section) – Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LS["LONGITUDINAL SECTION"] J1["NATURAL GROUND LEVEL NGL
Plotted from spot levels"] J2["PROPOSED FORMATION LEVEL FL
Designed gradient line"] J3["DEPTH h
h = FL minus NGL
Positive means fill
Negative means cut"] end subgraph ChainageMarkers["CHAINAGE MARKERS"] K1["CH 0 m"] K2["CH 30 m"] K3["CH 60 m"] K4["CH 90 m"] end J1 --> J3 J2 --> J3 K1 --> J3 K2 --> J3 K3 --> J3 K4 --> J3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The L-Section is the backbone of road estimation. Each chainage marker has a corresponding NGL and FL. The difference gives the depth of cut or fill at that point, from which cross-sectional area is computed. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Define a retaining wall. List any **four** types of retaining walls used in civil engineering practice. **Model Answer:** > A retaining wall is a structure designed to resist the lateral pressure of soil or backfill material where there is a change in ground elevation exceeding the angle of repose of the soil. **Four Types:** 1. **Gravity Retaining Wall** – Relies on its self-weight (plain concrete or masonry) 2. **Cantilever Retaining Wall** – L-shaped or T-shaped RCC structure 3. **Counterfort Retaining Wall** – Has vertical supports (counterforts) at regular intervals 4. **Buttress Wall** – Supports are on the front face of the wall --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** What is the difference between the **Trapezoidal Rule** and the **Prismoidal Formula** in road earthwork estimation? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Trapezoidal Rule | Prismoidal Formula | |---|---|---| | Formula | $V = \frac{L}{2}(A_1 + A_2)$ | $V = \frac{L}{6}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)$ | | Accuracy | Approximate; assumes linear variation | More accurate; accounts for curvature | | Data needed | Two end areas | Two end areas + mid-section area | | Use case | Flat terrain | Hilly / undulating terrain | The prismoidal formula gives a more realistic volume when the ground profile varies non-linearly between chainages. --- ## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **components of a cantilever retaining wall**. List the quantities required to be estimated for its construction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Estimate the quantities of (i) PCC, (ii) RCC in stem, and (iii) steel reinforcement for a cantilever retaining wall with the following data: **(7 marks)** - Length of wall = **12 m** - Stem height = **4.5 m**, top thickness = **0.35 m**, bottom thickness = **0.55 m** - Base slab dimensions = **5.0 m × 12.0 m × 0.50 m** (overall) - PCC thickness = **100 mm**, with **0.50 m** extra projection on all sides of base slab - Steel density = **100 kg/m³** of RCC #### Model Solution for (a): **Components of a Cantilever Retaining Wall:** 1. **Foundation / Earthwork in Excavation** 2. **PCC Bed** (100 mm nominal mix 1:4:8) 3. **RCC Base Slab** with toe and heel 4. **RCC Stem** (tapered) 5. **Backfill** with selected earth 6. **Weep holes** for drainage 7. **Coping beam** on top (optional) **Quantities to Estimate:** - Earthwork in excavation (m³) - PCC in foundation (m³) - RCC in base slab (m³) - RCC in stem (m³) - Reinforcement steel (kg) - Backfill quantity (m³) - Number of weep holes - Plaster / pointing (m²) #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1: PCC Quantity** PCC bed dimensions = $(5.0 + 1.0) \times (12.0 + 1.0) = 6.0 \times 13.0$ $$ V_{\text{PCC}} = 6.0 \cdot 13.0 \cdot 0.10 = 7.80 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2: RCC in Base Slab** $$ V_{\text{base}} = 5.0 \cdot 12.0 \cdot 0.50 = 30.00 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3: RCC in Stem (Tapered Frustum)** - $A_1 = 0.55 \times 12 = 6.60 \text{ m}^2$ - $A_2 = 0.35 \times 12 = 4.20 \text{ m}^2$ - $H = 4.5 \text{ m}$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = \frac{4.5}{3} \cdot \left( 6.60 + 4.20 + \sqrt{6.60 \times 4.20} \right) $$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = 1.5 \cdot \left( 10.80 + \sqrt{27.72} \right) $$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = 1.5 \cdot \left( 10.80 + 5.265 \right) $$ $$ V_{\text{stem}} = 1.5 \cdot 16.065 = 24.10 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4: Total RCC Volume** $$ V_{\text{RCC,total}} = 30.00 + 24.10 = 54.10 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 5: Steel Reinforcement** $$ Q_{\text{steel}} = 100 \times 54.10 = 5410 \text{ kg} = 54.10 \text{ quintals} $$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Item | Quantity | Unit | |---|---|---| | PCC | 7.80 | m³ | | RCC Stem | 24.10 | m³ | | Steel | 5410 | kg | --- ### Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the procedure for estimating the **volume of earthwork** in road construction from the longitudinal section. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A road alignment has the following data.
