Calculate the volume of earthwork between chainage **0 m and 60 m** using the **Trapezoidal Rule**: **(7 marks)** - Formation width = **9 m** - Side slopes = **1.5 : 1** - Depths at chainages: at 0 m → **0.8 m**; at 30 m → **1.0 m**; at 60 m → **0.6 m** #### Model Solution for (a): **Procedure for Estimating Earthwork from L-Section:** 1. **Survey & Data Collection:** Take spot levels at regular intervals (typically 30 m) along the proposed centerline. 2. **Plot Longitudinal Section:** Plot NGL (Natural Ground Level) and proposed FL (Formation Level) versus chainage. 3. **Calculate Depth (h) at each chainage:** $h = \text{FL} - \text{NGL}$ (positive for fill, negative for cut). 4. **Calculate Cross-Sectional Area (A):** Use $A = (B + sh) \cdot h$ for embankment. 5. **Apply Earthwork Formula:** Use Trapezoidal Rule or Prismoidal Formula between successive chainages. 6. **Sum Volumes:** Add volumes across all segments for the total earthwork quantity. 7. **Deduct for Shrinkage/Bulking:** Adjust for soil swell factor (typically 10–20% for cut earth used as fill). #### Model Solution for (b): **Given Data:** - $B = 9 \text{ m}$, $s = 1.5$, $h_1 = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $h_2 = 1.0 \text{ m}$, $h_3 = 0.6 \text{ m}$, $L = 30 \text{ m}$ **Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Areas at Each Chainage** $$ A_1 = (9 + 1.5 \cdot 0.8) \cdot 0.8 = (9 + 1.2) \cdot 0.8 = 10.2 \cdot 0.8 = 8.16 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_2 = (9 + 1.5 \cdot 1.0) \cdot 1.0 = (9 + 1.5) \cdot 1.0 = 10.5 \cdot 1.0 = 10.50 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_3 = (9 + 1.5 \cdot 0.6) \cdot 0.6 = (9 + 0.9) \cdot 0.6 = 9.9 \cdot 0.6 = 5.94 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Apply Trapezoidal Rule Between Successive Chainages** Segment 1 (0 m to 30 m): $$ V_1 = \frac{30}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2) = 15 \cdot (8.16 + 10.50) = 15 \cdot 18.66 = 279.90 \text{ m}^3 $$ Segment 2 (30 m to 60 m): $$ V_2 = \frac{30}{2} \cdot (A_2 + A_3) = 15 \cdot (10.50 + 5.94) = 15 \cdot 16.44 = 246.60 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3: Total Volume** $$ V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 = 279.90 + 246.60 = 526.50 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Final Answer:** $V = \mathbf{526.50 \text{ m}^3}$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong Side Slope Interpretation:** Students often confuse *s:1* as *1:s*. Always read it as **s horizontal : 1 vertical**. > 2. **Forgetting to Add 2sh to the Width:** The bottom width is $B + 2sh$, not $B + sh$. The *2* is for both sides of the embankment. > 3. **Mixing Up Trapezoidal and Prismoidal:** Trapezoidal uses $\frac{L}{2}(A_1+A_2)$; Prismoidal uses $\frac{L}{6}(A_1+4A_m+A_2)$. > 4. **PCC Bed Calculation Error:** Always add the working space (0.30–0.50 m) to **all four sides** of the foundation footprint. > 5. **Tapered Wall Volume:** Use the **frustum formula**, not a simple average of top and bottom areas. > 6. **Unit Mistakes:** Steel in **kg**, concrete in **m³**, plaster in **m²**. Converting 1 quintal = **100 kg** is a frequent error. > 7. **Forgetting Reinforcement:** Always estimate steel as a percentage of RCC volume (**80–120 kg/m³** standard). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** ### Retaining Wall - [ ] A retaining wall resists **lateral earth pressure**. - [ ] **Cantilever type** is most common in KTU syllabus. - [ ] Components: Excavation → PCC → Base Slab → Stem → Backfill → Weep holes. - [ ] Stem volume uses the **frustum formula** because of tapering. - [ ] Steel reinforcement ≈ **80–120 kg/m³** of RCC. - [ ] Weep holes are **100 mm dia**, spaced **1–2 m**. ### Road Earthwork - [ ] Cross-sectional area: $A = (B + sh) \cdot h$ for embankment. - [ ] **Trapezoidal Rule:** $V = \frac{L}{2}(A_1 + A_2)$ — fast but approximate. - [ ] **Prismoidal Formula:** $V = \frac{L}{6}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)$ — most accurate. - [ ] Apply shrinkage factor (**10–20%**) for cut earth used as embankment fill. - [ ] Side slope is given as ratio of **horizontal : 1 vertical**. - [ ] L-Section gives depth at each chainage; cross-section gives area; volume = area × length. ### Water Tank Estimation - [ ] Standard components: Base slab, walls, top slab, columns (overhead). - [ ] Standard thicknesses: PCC = 100 mm, RCC base = 150–200 mm, walls = 150–200 mm. - [ ] Pipes: Inlet (50 mm), Outlet (75 mm), Overflow (100 mm), Scour (50 mm). - [ ] Manhole: **600 mm × 600 mm** with CI cover. - [ ] Waterproofing: 12 mm cement plaster + chemical coat. - [ ] Always estimate reinforcement separately for base, walls, and top slab. ### Universal KTU Estimation Rules - [ ] Always work in **SI units**: m, m², m³, kg. - [ ] Show the formula, substitution, and final answer in steps. - [ ] Sketch before calculation (this fetches partial marks even if calculation is wrong). - [ ] Use the **correct shrinkage / bulking factor** for earthwork. - [ ] **Subhead numbering** follows KTU SSR pattern (Earthwork, PCC, RCC, Steel, etc.). - [ ] Always mention the **wastage allowance** for materials (typically **2.5–5%**).

topics.title: Septic Tank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Septic Tank # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Septic Tank (as per IS 2470: Part 1 & Part 2, Bureau of Indian Standards):** > A **septic tank** is a horizontal, continuous-flow, **sedimentation-cum-digestion** underground chamber constructed of brick masonry, stone masonry, or reinforced cement concrete (RCC), designed to receive and partially treat **domestic sewage (waste water)** through the dual processes of **sedimentation** of settleable solids and **anaerobic digestion** of the accumulated sludge, discharging the clarified effluent to a **sub-surface disposal system** (soak pit / dispersion trench / leach field). In the context of **Quantity Surveying and Valuation**, the term refers not only to the engineering component but to the **detailed estimation of quantities of materials** (earthwork excavation, PCC, RCC, brickwork, plastering, reinforcement steel, manhole covers, pipes, etc.) required for the construction of a septic tank of given capacity, followed by the **rate analysis and abstract cost estimation** as per the KTU 2024 Scheme standard schedule of rates (SSR). ## 1.2 Key Specifications & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value (as per IS 2470) | |---|---| | Minimum liquid depth | **1.2 m to 1.8 m** | | Freeboard (air space above water) | **0.3 m minimum** | | Length to width ratio | **2 : 1 to 3 : 1** | | Detention time | **24 to 48 hours** | | Cleaning interval | **6 to 12 months** | | Per capita sewage flow (assumed) | **90 to 135 litres/day** (typically **135 LPCD** for design) | | Sludge accumulation rate | **0.03 m³/person/year** (approximate) | | Minimum tank capacity formula | $Q = (V_d + V_s) \times 1.3$ (with 30% margin) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the estimation problem, the student is **not required to design** the septic tank in detail. The dimensions (length, width, depth) are usually **given in the question**. The focus is on **abstracting the quantities** of: > 1. Earthwork in excavation > 2. PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) bed > 3. Brickwork / RCC in walls > 4. RCC roof slab / covers > 5. Internal & external plaster > 6. Baffle wall, inlet, outlet, and vent pipe > 7. Manhole & cover ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Stomach" Analogy:** > Think of a septic tank as the **"stomach" of a building's drainage system** — just as the human stomach holds food for some time, breaks it down biologically, and then passes the digested liquid (effluent) to the intestines (soak pit) for final absorption. > > - **Inlet Pipe** → the "mouth" where raw sewage enters > - **Tank Chamber** → the "stomach" where solids settle and bacteria digest them > - **Baffle Wall** → the "valve" preventing scum from escaping > - **Outlet Pipe** → the "small intestine" releasing clarified water > - **Soak Pit** → the "large intestine" absorbing the final liquid into the ground > > In Quantity Surveying, the septic tank is treated as a **composite structure with multiple sub-items** (like a layered recipe), and the surveyor must list and quantify each "ingredient" (material). ## 1.4 Classification of Septic Tanks 1. **Based on Construction Material:** - **Brick masonry septic tank** (most common in residential, low-cost applications) - **RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete) septic tank** (for heavy loads / large capacities) - **Stone masonry septic tank** (rural / traditional) 2. **Based on Shape:** - **Rectangular** (most common, L:B = 2:1 to 3:1) - **Circular** (precast, less common) 3. **Based on Capacity:** - **Small** (up to 10 users) - **Medium** (10–50 users) - **Large** (>50 users, may be multi-chambered) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Septic Tank — Structured Logic A septic tank, as a billable item, is **not a single product** but an **assembly of multiple engineering components**. The QS must be aware of each component to perform an accurate take-off. ### 2.1.1 Structural Components - **Excavation** — pit dug to accommodate the tank, often extending **0.30 m on each side** beyond the outer wall of the tank (working space). - **PCC Bed (1:4:8 or 1:5:10)** — 100 mm to 150 mm thick lean concrete base providing a level, dry working platform. - **Walls** — brick masonry (typically **230 mm thick, 1:6 cement mortar**) or RCC (M20 grade, 150–200 mm thick). - **Floor Slab** — RCC or brick-on-edge paved flooring, finished with a slope toward the sludge outlet. - **Roof / Cover Slab** — RCC slab (M20 grade, 100–150 mm thick) with manhole openings. - **Baffle Wall** — internal partition of brick (typically 230 mm thick, height = 2/3 of liquid depth) to direct flow and prevent scum carry-over. ### 2.1.2 Plumbing / Functional Components - **Inlet Pipe** (100 mm dia. CI/PVC) — submerged **0.30 m below liquid level** with a T-junction or submerged bend. - **Outlet Pipe** (100 mm dia.) — placed at the **liquid surface level** to skim off clarified effluent. - **Ventilation Pipe** (minimum 50 mm dia. CI) — extends **1 m above ground level** to release biogas (methane, CO₂, H₂S). - **Manhole** (0.6 m × 0.6 m or 0.6 m dia. circular) — for inspection and desludging. - **Manhole Cover** (CI / SFRC, 0.6 m dia., heavy duty, 5 T capacity) — at every manhole. ### 2.1.3 Finishing Components - **Internal Plaster** — 12 mm to 20 mm thick, cement mortar **1:3**, finished smooth (some specs require 1:1 waterproof plaster or epoxy coating). - **External Plaster** — 12 mm thick, **1:4** cement mortar, for water-proofing and protection. - **Waterproofing** — bitumen paint or chemical waterproof coating on external surfaces in water-table areas. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they are 80% of any numerical question on Septic Tank estimation in KTU ESE.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Liquid volume (V) | $V = N \times q \times t$ | m³ | | 2 | Tank length (L) | $L = 2B$ to $3B$ (assumed/derived) | m | | 3 | Volume of tank (gross) | $V_g = L \times B \times D$ | m³ | | 4 | Earthwork excavation | $E = (L+2a) \times (B+2a) \times (D+H)$ | m³ | | 5 | PCC bed quantity | $PCC = (L+2a) \times (B+2a) \times t_{pcc}$ | m³ | | 6 | Brickwork walls (4 walls) | $W = 2 \times (L \times H_w \times t) + 2 \times (B \times H_w \times t)$ | m³ | | 7 | Floor RCC (bottom slab) | $F = L_{in} \times B_{in} \times t_{floor}$ | m³ | | 8 | Roof RCC (top cover) | $R = (L_{out} \times B_{out}) - \text{manhole opening} \times t_{roof}$ | m³ | | 9 | Internal plaster | $A_{int} = 2(L_{in}+B_{in}) \times H_w + L_{in} \times B_{in}$ | m² | | 10 | External plaster | $A_{ext} = 2(L_{out}+B_{out}) \times H + (L_{out} \times B_{out})$ | m² | | 11 | Steel reinforcement | $S = (\text{volume of RCC}) \times (\text{steel factor } 80 \text{ kg/m}^3)$ | kg | | 12 | Sludge volume (check) | $V_s = N \times 0.03$ m³/year (per IS 2470) | m³ | Where: - $N$ = number of users, $q$ = per capita sewage (L/day), $t$ = detention time (days) - $a$ = working space allowance on each side (typically **0.30 m**) - $D$ = liquid depth, $H_w$ = wall height (D + freeboard) - $H$ = total excavation depth - $t_{pcc}, t_{floor}, t_{roof}$ = respective thickness - $L_{in}, B_{in}$ = internal dimensions; $L_{out}, B_{out}$ = external dimensions > [!WARNING] > **Critical Distinction in KTU Exams:** > - **Liquid volume** uses **internal** dimensions > - **Brickwork** uses **wall thickness** (subtract from internal to get width × length of brickwork) > - **Excavation** uses **external dimensions + working space** > Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose 4–5 marks per question. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Septic tank estimation is a **mandatory skill** for: - **Site engineers** preparing running bills for residential colonies, schools, and hospitals. - **PWD/CPWD estimators** preparing detailed estimates (DE) and abstract estimates (AE) for public buildings. - **Valuation engineers** assessing depreciation and residual life of septic systems during property valuation. - **Billing engineers** preparing BOQ (Bill of Quantities) for tenders under CPWD DSR or Kerala PWD SSR. The estimation follows the principle: **"Take off the gross quantity, deduct the openings, add the lintels/sills/finishings, multiply by rate."** # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — KTU Standard Septic Tank Estimation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU ESE 14-Mark Style):** > *Estimate the quantities of the following items of work for a septic tank of size 4.0 m × 2.0 m × 1.8 m (internal dimensions, liquid depth 1.5 m, freeboard 0.3 m). The tank is constructed with brick masonry walls (230 mm thick) in CM 1:6, with PCC 1:4:8 bed (150 mm thick), RCC M20 roof slab (120 mm thick) with one manhole 0.6 m × 0.6 m, RCC baffle wall (230 mm thick, 0.9 m height), internal plaster 12 mm thick 1:3, and external plaster 12 mm thick 1:4. Allow 300 mm working space on all sides for excavation. Total depth of excavation = 2.0 m.* > > *Required:* > 1. *Earthwork in excavation (m³)* > 2. *PCC 1:4:8 bed (m³)* > 3. *Brickwork in CM 1:6 (m³)* > 4. *RCC M20 roof slab (m³, deducting manhole)* > 5. *Baffle wall brickwork (m³)* > 6. *Internal plaster (m²)* > 7. *External plaster (m²)* > 8. *Steel reinforcement @ 80 kg/m³ of RCC (kg)* ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify All Dimensions $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Internal length, } L_{in} &= 4.0 \text{ m} \\ \text{Internal width, } B_{in} &= 2.0 \text{ m} \\ \text{Liquid depth, } D &= 1.5 \text{ m} \\ \text{Freeboard} &= 0.3 \text{ m} \\ \text{Wall thickness, } t_w &= 0.23 \text{ m} \\ \text{Total wall height, } H_w &= D + \text{freeboard} = 1.5 + 0.3 = 1.8 \text{ m} \\ \text{External length, } L_{out} &= L_{in} + 2t_w = 4.0 + 2(0.23) = 4.46 \text{ m} \\ \text{External width, } B_{out} &= B_{in} + 2t_w = 2.0 + 2(0.23) = 2.46 \text{ m} \\ \text{Working space, } a &= 0.30 \text{ m on each side} \\ \text{Excavation length, } L_e &= L_{out} + 2a = 4.46 + 0.60 = 5.06 \text{ m} \\ \text{Excavation width, } B_e &= B_{out} + 2a = 2.46 + 0.60 = 3.06 \text{ m} \\ \text{Excavation depth, } H_e &= 2.0 \text{ m (given)} \\ \text{PCC thickness, } t_{pcc} &= 0.15 \text{ m} \\ \text{Roof slab thickness, } t_{roof} &= 0.12 \text{ m} \\ \text{Manhole size} &= 0.6 \text{ m} \times 0.6 \text{ m} \\ \text{Baffle wall height} &= 0.9 \text{ m (given)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Earthwork in Excavation $$ \begin{aligned} V_{exc} &= L_e \times B_e \times H_e \\ &= 5.06 \times 3.06 \times 2.0 \\ &= 30.9672 \text{ m}^3 \\ &\approx 30.97 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The excavation extends 0.3 m beyond the external wall on every side, providing working space for the mason to construct the wall and apply external plaster. ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — PCC 1:4:8 Bed $$ \begin{aligned} V_{pcc} &= L_e \times B_e \times t_{pcc} \\ &= 5.06 \times 3.06 \times 0.15 \\ &= 2.3225 \text{ m}^3 \\ &\approx 2.32 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The PCC bed is laid across the full excavated area, providing a uniform base for the brickwork. ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Brickwork in CM 1:6 (Walls) **Logic:** The walls are 4-sided, each 230 mm thick, height = 1.8 m. $$ \begin{aligned} V_{wall} &= 2 \times t_w \times H_w \times (L_{in} + B_{in}) \\ &= 2 \times 0.23 \times 1.8 \times (4.0 + 2.0) \\ &= 2 \times 0.23 \times 1.8 \times 6.0 \\ &= 4.968 \text{ m}^3 \\ &\approx 4.97 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Alternative cross-check (wall by wall):** - Long walls: $2 \times (L_{in} \times t_w \times H_w) = 2 \times (4.0 \times 0.23 \times 1.8) = 3.312$ m³ - Short walls: $2 \times (B_{in} \times t_w \times H_w) = 2 \times (2.0 \times 0.23 \times 1.8) = 1.656$ m³ - Total = 3.312 + 1.656 = 4.968 m³ ✓ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — RCC M20 Roof Slab (Deducting Manhole) $$ \begin{aligned} V_{roof} &= (L_{out} \times B_{out} - A_{manhole}) \times t_{roof} \\ &= (4.46 \times 2.46 - 0.6 \times 0.6) \times 0.12 \\ &= (10.9716 - 0.36) \times 0.12 \\ &= 10.6116 \times 0.12 \\ &= 1.2734 \text{ m}^3 \\ &\approx 1.27 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The RCC slab covers the full external plan, but a $0.6 \text{ m} \times 0.6 \text{ m}$ opening is left for the manhole, which is later covered with a precast RCC or CI cover slab. ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Baffle Wall Brickwork $$ \begin{aligned} V_{baffle} &= L_b \times t_w \times h_b \\ &= 2.0 \times 0.23 \times 0.9 \\ &= 0.414 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The baffle wall runs across the **internal width** (2.0 m), is 230 mm thick, and 0.9 m high (≈ 2/3 of liquid depth as per IS 2470). ### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Internal Plaster (1:3, 12 mm) $$ \begin{aligned} A_{int} &= \underbrace{2 \times (L_{in} + B_{in}) \times H_w}_{\text{4 walls (inside)}} + \underbrace{L_{in} \times B_{in}}_{\text{floor of tank}} \\ &= 2 \times (4.0 + 2.0) \times 1.8 + (4.0 \times 2.0) \\ &= 2 \times 6.0 \times 1.8 + 8.0 \\ &= 21.6 + 8.0 \\ &= 29.6 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.8 Step 8 — External Plaster (1:4, 12 mm) $$ \begin{aligned} A_{ext} &= \underbrace{2 \times (L_{out} + B_{out}) \times H_w}_{\text{4 walls (outside)}} + \underbrace{L_e \times B_e - L_{out} \times B_{out}}_{\text{exposed PCC top (annular strip)}} \\ &= 2 \times (4.46 + 2.46) \times 1.8 + (5.06 \times 3.06 - 4.46 \times 2.46) \\ &= 2 \times 6.92 \times 1.8 + (15.4836 - 10.9716) \\ &= 24.912 + 4.512 \\ &= 29.42 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why do we add the exposed PCC top area?** > The PCC bed extends **beyond the external wall** (since $L_e > L_{out}$ and $B_e > B_{out}$). The annular strip of PCC between the wall and the excavation edge is **exposed** to backfill moisture and is therefore also plastered for waterproofing. ### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Steel Reinforcement (in Roof Slab) $$ \begin{aligned} W_{steel} &= V_{roof} \times \text{steel factor} \\ &= 1.2734 \times 80 \\ &= 101.87 \text{ kg} \\ &\approx 102 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Final Abstract of Quantities — Analytical Matrix | Item No. | Description of Item | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Earthwork in excavation | **30.97** | m³ | (given) | — | | 2 | PCC 1:4:8 bed | **2.32** | m³ | (given) | — | | 3 | Brickwork in CM 1:6 walls | **4.97** | m³ | (given) | — | | 4 | RCC M20 roof slab (net) | **1.27** | m³ | (given) | — | | 5 | Baffle wall brickwork | **0.41** | m³ | (given) | — | | 6 | Internal plaster 1:3, 12 mm | **29.60** | m² | (given) | — | | 7 | External plaster 1:4, 12 mm | **29.42** | m² | (given) | — | | 8 | Steel reinforcement (80 kg/m³) | **102.00** | kg | (given) | — | | | **Total Estimated Cost** | | | | **₹ __________** | > [!TIP] > **To Complete the Cost:** Multiply each quantity by the **rate per unit** taken from the current KTU/CPWD Schedule of Rates (SSR). The sum of all amounts gives the **Total Estimated Cost** of the septic tank, which is then used for the **Abstract Estimate (AE)**. ## 3.3 Common Arithmetic Verification (Cross-Checks) - **Liquid volume check:** $V = 4.0 \times 2.0 \times 1.5 = 12.0$ m³ → For ~90 users at 135 LPCD, 1-day detention: $V = 90 \times 0.135 \times 1 = 12.15$ m³ ✓ - **Sludge storage check:** $V_s = 90 \times 0.03 = 2.7$ m³ → Fits within the tank's lower 1/3 zone. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Septic Tank Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Domestic Sewage Source
Kitchen Toilet Bathroom] --> B[Inlet Pipe 100mm
Submerged 0.30m below liquid] B --> C[Septic Tank Chamber
L x B x D = 4.0m x 2.0m x 1.5m
Liquid depth 1.5m Freeboard 0.3m] C --> D[Baffle Wall
230mm thick Height 0.9m
Prevents scum carryover] D --> E[Clarified Effluent Zone
Outlet Pipe 100mm at liquid level] E --> F[Soak Pit or Dispersion Trench
Final sub-surface disposal] C --> G[Sludge Accumulation Zone
Bottom third of tank
Anaerobic digestion active] G --> H[Ventilation Pipe 50mm
1m above ground level
Releases biogas CH4 CO2 H2S] C --> I[Manhole 0.6m x 0.6m
Inspection and desludging access] I --> J[CI Manhole Cover
Heavy duty 5T capacity] C --> K[PCC Bed 150mm 1:4:8
Levelling course] C --> L[Brickwork Walls 230mm
Cement mortar 1:6] C --> M[RCC Roof Slab 120mm M20
With reinforcement at 80kg per m3] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow — Estimation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Read Internal Dimensions
L B D from question] --> S2[Step 2: Compute External Dimensions
Add 2 x wall thickness to L and B] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Excavation Dimensions
Add 2 x working space 0.30m] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate Earthwork
L e x B e x H e] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Calculate PCC Bed
L e x B e x thickness] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Calculate Brickwork Walls
2 x t w x H w x L in plus B in] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Calculate Roof Slab
L out x B out minus manhole x thickness] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Calculate Baffle Wall
L b x t w x h b] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Calculate Plaster Areas
Internal and external surfaces] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Steel Qty
RCC volume x 80 kg per m3] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Abstract of Quantities
Multiply by SSR rates for total cost] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section Representation (ASCII) ``` <-- Ground Level --> ___________________________ Vent Pipe | | | | | | | | | | | | Freeboard 0.30m | | | | | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ | | <-- Outlet pipe | | | ~ Liquid effluent ~ | | (at liquid level) | | | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ | | | | | | Baffle Wall | | | | | | | 0.23m x 0.9m | | | | | | | ______________ | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | Sludge / | | | | | | | | | Scum zone | | | | | | | | | (bottom 1/3)| | | | | | | | |______________| | | | | | | |__________________| | | | | | Internal 4.0m x 2.0m | | | | | | | Inlet | | | | Pipe | |________________________| | | | | Brick wall 0.23m thick | | | | Total H = 1.80m | | | |______________________________| | | | PCC 1:4:8 0.15m | V | |______________________________| (submerged) | | Excavation depth = 2.0m | |____|_________________________________| ``` ## 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Septic Tank Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[How many users?] --> Q2{Number of users} Q2 -->|Less than 10| R1[Brick Masonry Tank
Single chamber
Low cost] Q2 -->|10 to 50| R2[Brick or RCC Tank
Single or double chamber
Medium capacity] Q2 -->|Greater than 50| R3[RCC Tank Mandatory
Multi chamber design
High capacity] Q1 --> Q4{Site condition?} Q4 -->|High water table| R4[Use RCC with water proofing] Q4 -->|Normal soil| R5[Brick masonry acceptable] Q4 -->|Rocky terrain| R6[RCC tank with PCC base] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Instructions:** Answer in **2–3 sentences** with a clear definition or a small derivation. ### Question A.1 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *Define a septic tank. State any two important provisions of IS 2470 related to its design.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A septic tank is a **horizontal underground sedimentation-cum-digestion chamber** that receives domestic sewage, retains it for **24 to 48 hours**, and discharges clarified effluent to a sub-surface disposal system. Key IS 2470 provisions: 1. The **length-to-width ratio** of the tank should be between **2:1 and 3:1** for efficient flow. 2. The **freeboard** (air space above water level) should be a **minimum of 0.30 m** to allow scum accumulation and gas venting. *(Any two provisions — 1.5 marks each.)* ### Question A.2 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > *What is the purpose of a baffle wall in a septic tank? Mention its standard height.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **baffle wall** is an internal partition placed near the outlet end of the septic tank. Its purpose is to **prevent the floating scum layer from being carried out** with the effluent and to **direct the flow of sewage downward and upward**, ensuring clarified liquid is drawn only from the middle layer. Its standard height is **approximately 2/3 of the liquid depth** (typically **0.9 m to 1.0 m** for a 1.5 m deep tank), and its thickness is generally **230 mm (one-brick thick)**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Instructions:** Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.** Show all formulas, dimensions, and stepwise calculations. --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > *Estimate the quantities of the following items of work for a **brick masonry septic tank of internal size 3.5 m × 1.75 m × 1.5 m** (liquid depth 1.2 m, freeboard 0.3 m), constructed in CM 1:5, with PCC 1:4:8 bed 100 mm thick, RCC M20 cover slab 100 mm thick, and **one manhole 0.6 m × 0.6 m**.* > *The baffle wall is **0.8 m high × 230 mm thick**, internal plaster is **12 mm thick 1:3**, external plaster is **12 mm thick 1:4**. Allow **300 mm working space** for excavation; total depth of excavation = **1.8 m**. Use a steel factor of **80 kg/m³** of RCC.* > > **(a)** Compute the quantity of **(i)** Earthwork in excavation, **(ii)** PCC 1:4:8 bed, **(iii)** Brickwork in walls, and **(iv)** RCC M20 roof slab (deducting manhole). > > **(b)** Compute the quantity of **(i)** Baffle wall brickwork, **(ii)** Internal plaster, **(iii)** External plaster, and **(iv)** Steel reinforcement in kg. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part A (a) **Step 1 — Identify Dimensions** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{in} &= 3.5 \text{ m}, \quad B_{in} = 1.75 \text{ m} \\ D &= 1.2 \text{ m}, \quad H_w = 1.2 + 0.3 = 1.5 \text{ m} \\ t_w &= 0.23 \text{ m}, \quad t_{pcc} = 0.10 \text{ m}, \quad t_{roof} = 0.10 \text{ m} \\ a &= 0.30 \text{ m} \\ L_{out} &= 3.5 + 2(0.23) = 3.96 \text{ m} \\ B_{out} &= 1.75 + 2(0.23) = 2.21 \text{ m} \\ L_e &= 3.96 + 0.6 = 4.56 \text{ m} \\ B_e &= 2.21 + 0.6 = 2.81 \text{ m} \\ H_e &= 1.8 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — (i) Earthwork in Excavation** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{exc} &= 4.56 \times 2.81 \times 1.8 = 23.063 \approx \mathbf{23.06 \text{ m}^3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — (ii) PCC 1:4:8 Bed** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{pcc} &= 4.56 \times 2.81 \times 0.10 = 1.281 \approx \mathbf{1.28 \text{ m}^3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — (iii) Brickwork in CM 1:5 (Walls)** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{wall} &= 2 \times t_w \times H_w \times (L_{in} + B_{in}) \\ &= 2 \times 0.23 \times 1.5 \times (3.5 + 1.75) \\ &= 2 \times 0.23 \times 1.5 \times 5.25 \\ &= 3.6225 \approx \mathbf{3.62 \text{ m}^3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — (iv) RCC M20 Roof Slab** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{roof} &= (L_{out} \times B_{out} - A_{manhole}) \times t_{roof} \\ &= (3.96 \times 2.21 - 0.6 \times 0.6) \times 0.10 \\ &= (8.7516 - 0.36) \times 0.10 \\ &= 8.3916 \times 0.10 = 0.839 \approx \mathbf{0.84 \text{ m}^3} \end{aligned} $$ #### Model Solution — Part A (b) **Step 6 — (i) Baffle Wall Brickwork** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{baffle} &= B_{in} \times t_w \times h_b = 1.75 \times 0.23 \times 0.8 = \mathbf{0.322 \text{ m}^3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — (ii) Internal Plaster** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{int} &= 2(L_{in} + B_{in}) \times H_w + L_{in} \times B_{in} \\ &= 2(3.5 + 1.75) \times 1.5 + (3.5 \times 1.75) \\ &= 2 \times 5.25 \times 1.5 + 6.125 \\ &= 15.75 + 6.125 = \mathbf{21.875 \text{ m}^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — (iii) External Plaster** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{ext} &= 2(L_{out} + B_{out}) \times H_w + (L_e \times B_e - L_{out} \times B_{out}) \\ &= 2(3.96 + 2.21) \times 1.5 + (4.56 \times 2.81 - 3.96 \times 2.21) \\ &= 2 \times 6.17 \times 1.5 + (12.8136 - 8.7516) \\ &= 18.51 + 4.062 = \mathbf{22.572 \text{ m}^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9 — (iv) Steel Reinforcement** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{steel} &= V_{roof} \times 80 = 0.839 \times 80 = \mathbf{67.12 \text{ kg}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > *Prepare a detailed estimate for a **septic tank with internal size 5.0 m × 2.5 m × 1.8 m** (liquid depth 1.5 m, freeboard 0.3 m). The tank is constructed in **brick masonry 230 mm thick in CM 1:6**, with **PCC 1:4:8 bed 150 mm thick**, **RCC M20 roof slab 120 mm thick**, and **two manholes 0.6 m × 0.6 m each**. Internal plaster is **20 mm thick 1:3** (waterproofed), external plaster is **15 mm thick 1:4**, baffle wall is **1.0 m high × 230 mm thick**, working space 0.3 m, total depth of excavation = 2.2 m. Use the **CPWD DSR 2023 rates** given below and find the **total cost**.* > > *Assumed rates:* > - *Earthwork excavation: ₹ 250/m³* > - *PCC 1:4:8: ₹ 4500/m³* > - *Brickwork CM 1:6: ₹ 6500/m³* > - *RCC M20: ₹ 8500/m³* > - *Internal plaster 1:3 (20mm): ₹ 350/m²* > - *External plaster 1:4 (15mm): ₹ 280/m²* > - *Steel: ₹ 75/kg* > > **(a)** Compute the quantity of all items (7 marks). > **(b)** Prepare the **abstract of cost** and find the **total estimated cost** (7 marks). #### Model Solution — Part B (a) **Dimensions:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{in} &= 5.0, \quad B_{in} = 2.5, \quad D = 1.5, \quad H_w = 1.8 \\ L_{out} &= 5.46, \quad B_{out} = 2.96 \\ L_e &= 5.76, \quad B_e = 3.26, \quad H_e = 2.2 \end{aligned} $$ **Calculations:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{exc} &= 5.76 \times 3.26 \times 2.2 = 41.30 \text{ m}^3 \\ V_{pcc} &= 5.76 \times 3.26 \times 0.15 = 2.82 \text{ m}^3 \\ V_{wall} &= 2 \times 0.23 \times 1.8 \times (5.0 + 2.5) = 6.21 \text{ m}^3 \\ V_{roof} &= (5.46 \times 2.96 - 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.6) \times 0.12 = (16.16 - 0.72) \times 0.12 = 1.85 \text{ m}^3 \\ V_{baffle} &= 2.5 \times 0.23 \times 1.0 = 0.575 \text{ m}^3 \\ A_{int} &= 2(5.0 + 2.5) \times 1.8 + 5.0 \times 2.5 = 27 + 12.5 = 39.5 \text{ m}^2 \\ A_{ext} &= 2(5.46 + 2.96) \times 1.8 + (5.76 \times 3.26 - 5.46 \times 2.96) \\ &= 30.34 + (18.78 - 16.16) = 30.34 + 2.62 = 32.96 \text{ m}^2 \\ W_{steel} &= 1.85 \times 80 = 148 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ #### Model Solution — Part B (b) — Abstract of Cost | Item | Description | Qty | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Earthwork excavation | 41.30 | m³ | 250 | 10,325.00 | | 2 | PCC 1:4:8 bed | 2.82 | m³ | 4,500 | 12,690.00 | | 3 | Brickwork CM 1:6 | 6.21 | m³ | 6,500 | 40,365.00 | | 4 | RCC M20 roof slab | 1.85 | m³ | 8,500 | 15,725.00 | | 5 | Baffle wall brickwork | 0.575 | m³ | 6,500 | 3,737.50 | | 6 | Internal plaster 1:3 (20mm) | 39.50 | m² | 350 | 13,825.00 | | 7 | External plaster 1:4 (15mm) | 32.96 | m² | 280 | 9,228.80 | | 8 | Steel reinforcement | 148.00 | kg | 75 | 11,100.00 | | | **TOTAL** | | | | **₹ 1,16,996.30** | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 3–5 Marks per Question:** > > 1. **Mixing internal vs. external dimensions:** Earthwork uses $(L_{in} + 2t_w + 2a)$; brickwork uses $t_w \times H_w$ with internal L,B; roof slab uses $(L_{out} \times B_{out})$. **Always draw a quick sketch with all three dimensions labeled.** > > 2. **Forgetting the manhole deduction:** The RCC roof slab must have the manhole area (0.6 × 0.6 = 0.36 m² per manhole) **deducted**, not added. Many students add it as a separate item by mistake. > > 3. **Forgetting exposed PCC in external plaster:** The annular strip of PCC beyond the external wall is also plastered. Forgetting it leads to a loss of ~2 m² in the typical problem. > > 4. **Wrong unit for steel:** Steel is calculated in **kg** (or quintals), not m³. Multiplying by ₹/m³ instead of ₹/kg inflates the cost. > > 5. **Baffle wall width:** It runs across the **internal width** $B_{in}$, not $L_{in}$. Wrong direction wastes 1 mark. > > 6. **Concrete bed vs concrete floor slab:** The PCC bed lies **below** the walls (under the entire pit). If the problem mentions an RCC floor slab inside the tank, it lies **inside** the brick walls. These are **two different items** — don't merge them. > > 7. **Not including the ventilation pipe and manhole cover** in the bill of quantities. These are small items but carry **1–2 marks** in the long-form KTU questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] A **septic tank** is a sedimentation-cum-digestion chamber for domestic sewage, governed by **IS 2470 (Parts 1 & 2)**. - [x] Standard proportions: **L : B = 2 : 1 to 3 : 1**; liquid depth = **1.2 to 1.8 m**; freeboard ≥ **0.30 m**. - [x] Detention time = **24 to 48 hours**; per capita flow assumed = **135 LPCD** for design. - [x] Septic tank estimation requires **abstracting 7–8 distinct sub-items** — never treat it as a single cubic-metre item. - [x] Always work with **three sets of dimensions**: - **Internal** (L_in, B_in) — for liquid volume, plaster inside, baffle wall. - **External** (L_out = L_in + 2t_w) — for roof slab. - **Excavation** (L_e = L_out + 2a) — for earthwork and PCC bed. - [x] Working space **a = 0.30 m** on all sides is the KTU standard. - [x] **Brickwork** = $2 \times t_w \times H_w \times (L_{in} + B_{in})$ — always 4 walls. - [x] **Roof slab** = $(L_{out} \times B_{out} - \text{manhole area}) \times t_{roof}$. - [x] **Internal plaster** = $2(L_{in}+B_{in})H_w + L_{in} B_{in}$ (walls + floor). - [x] **External plaster** = $2(L_{out}+B_{out})H_w + (L_e B_e - L_{out} B_{out})$ (walls + exposed PCC top). - [x] **Steel** = Volume of RCC × **80 kg/m³** (standard KTU factor). - [x] **Baffle wall** spans **B_in** (not L_in), height = 2/3 of liquid depth. - [x] The **vent pipe** must extend **1 m above ground level** (this is a frequent viva question). - [x] **Manhole size** = 0.6 m × 0.6 m (rectangular) or 0.6 m dia. (circular); cover = CI/SFRC heavy duty. - [x] The **sub-surface disposal system** (soak pit / dispersion trench) is a **separate item** — not part of the septic tank quantity, but a follow-up estimation. - [x] For **Abstract Estimate (AE)**, multiply each item quantity by the relevant **CPWD DSR / Kerala PWD SSR rate** and sum. - [x] For **Detailed Estimate (DE)**, include quantities of **cement, sand, aggregate, bricks, steel, water** (materials) and **mason, mazdoor, beldar** (labour) — usually done via rate analysis. - [x] Common viva questions: *"What is the function of the baffle wall?"* / *"Why is the outlet pipe placed at the liquid surface and the inlet pipe submerged?"* / *"What is the difference between a septic tank and a soak pit?"*

topics.title: Manhole (No Detailed Estimate Needed – Concept of Item of Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manhole (No Detailed Estimate Needed – Concept of Item of Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition of a Manhole

A **manhole** is a vertical, confined access shaft constructed on a sewer or stormwater drainage line, primarily intended to permit human entry for inspection, cleaning (desilting), maintenance, ventilation, and joining of sewers at varying depths, alignments, or diameters. It is typically built of brick masonry in cement mortar (or RCC/precast) and is covered at the top with a cast iron (CI) or reinforced cement concrete (RCC) cover and frame embedded in a roof slab.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3 – Sanitary and Water Supply Works), a manhole is treated as a **composite item of work**. This means that for estimation purposes, it is **not** taken apart into its individual constituents (excavation, brickwork, plaster, concrete, cover, etc.) and measured separately. Instead, it is measured and paid for as a **single composite unit** — *"one manhole of given size and depth"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Mandate — "No Detailed Estimate Needed":**
> For the KTU board examination, students are **NOT** required to prepare a detailed, item-rate abstract estimate for a manhole (unlike an RCC slab or a building). The expected answer is a clear explanation of the **"concept of item of work"** — i.e., what components are bundled into the manhole rate, the units of measurement, and how the composite rate is built up.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a manhole like a **"subscription box"** in e-commerce.

When you buy a subscription box online (say, a monthly meal kit), you don't get billed separately for the vegetables, the spices, the recipe card, the packaging box, and the delivery. You are charged **one consolidated price** for the whole "box" — and inside that price, the vendor has already accounted for every component (ingredients + packaging + logistics).

A manhole in KTU estimation works **exactly the same way**:

- The **"subscription box"** = the complete manhole structure.
- The **"individual items inside"** = excavation, PCC bed, brickwork, plaster, benching, channel, RCC cover slab, CI frame & cover, footing, etc.
- The **"monthly price"** = the **composite rate per manhole (in ₹/number)**, declared in the schedule of rates (SSR / DSR).

The estimator simply counts **"how many boxes"** (i.e., how many manholes) the project requires, and multiplies by the unit rate. The internal breakup is hidden inside the composite rate.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units (KTU 2024 Convention)

| Parameter | Standard Metric | KTU Unit of Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| Size of manhole (rectangular plan) | $0.9 \text{ m} \times 0.8 \text{ m}$ to $1.5 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m}$ | m (length × breadth) |
| Depth of manhole | $0.9 \text{ m}$ to **2.5 m** (shallow) ; up to **9 m** (deep) | m |
| Brick wall thickness | **200 mm (one brick)** for shallow; **400 mm (1.5 brick)** for deeper | mm |
| Working space at bottom | Min **0.6 m** clear on both sides of channel | m |
| CI cover diameter | **600 mm (circular)** or **900 mm × 600 mm (rectangular)** | mm |
| Unit of payment | Per manhole of specified size and depth | **Number (No.)** |
| Wall thickness in taper portion | **300 mm (1.25 brick)** | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** The unit of measurement for a manhole in the abstract estimate is always **"Number (No.)"** — never cubic metre, never square metre. This is the essence of the "item of work" concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components Bundled Inside the "Manhole Item of Work"

A KTU-style manhole item of work is a **composite package** that includes *every* operation, material, and labour required to deliver a *complete, functional, usable* manhole. The estimator does **NOT** measure these separately — they are deemed included in the unit rate.

The bundled components are:

1. **Earthwork in excavation** — for the pit, in any type of soil (soft / hard / rock), up to the required depth, with side clearance for working.
2. **PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) bed** — typically **1:4:8** or **1:5:10**, **100 mm to 150 mm thick**, laid at the bottom.
3. **Brick masonry in CM** — **1:4** or **1:5** cement mortar, walls of specified thickness (200 mm / 400 mm depending on depth).
4. **Inside plastering** — **12 mm** thick, **1:3 cement mortar**, smooth finish, water-proofing admixed where required.
5. **Outside plastering** — **20 mm** thick, **1:3 or 1:4 cement mortar**, upto the full depth of the manhole.
6. **Benching and channelling** — the semi-circular channel at the bottom (in brick or PCC, plastered smooth) and sloped benching on either side to direct sewage flow.
7. **Footing / offset (offsetting/recess)** — the stepped widening at the base of the brickwork (typically **75 mm to 100 mm** offset on each side).
8. **RCC cover slab** — at the top, generally **150 mm to 200 mm thick**, with reinforcement as per design, including shuttering, concreting, and curing.
9. **Cast Iron (CI) frame and cover** — embedded in the RCC slab, including fixing, painting with bitumen, and sealing.
10. **Steps / rungs** — CI foot rests at **300 mm c/c vertical spacing**, embedded in the brickwork.
11. **Curing, dewatering** (if needed), backfilling, and disposal of surplus earth.
12. **Shoring and strutting** (in deep manholes or loose soils) — at the contractor's discretion, deemed included.

### 2.2 Classification of Manholes (As per KTU 2024 Module 3)

KTU classification is based primarily on **depth**:

| Type of Manhole | Depth Range | Shape (Plan) | Wall Thickness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shallow / Inspection Chamber** | $\le 0.9 \text{ m}$ | Rectangular (0.9 m × 0.8 m) | **100 mm (half-brick)** | At property start / building connection |
| **Normal / Medium Manhole** | $0.9 \text{ m}$ to $1.5 \text{ m}$ | Rectangular (1.2 m × 0.9 m) | **200 mm (one brick)** | Residential / light commercial |
| **Deep Manhole** | $1.5 \text{ m}$ to $2.5 \text{ m}$ | Rectangular (1.5 m × 1.2 m) | **400 mm (1.5 brick)** | Main sewer lines, minor roads |
| **Very Deep Manhole** | $> 2.5 \text{ m}$ (up to 9 m) | Circular ($\phi 1.2$ to $1.5$ m) | **400 mm** tapering to **300 mm** | Trunk sewers, deep nallah crossings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rectangular vs Circular:** Rectangular is preferred for shallow depths (easier construction). Circular is preferred for deep manholes (uniform hoop compression from soil pressure — structurally efficient). The taper (cone) portion at the top reduces the working shaft opening to 600 mm dia to suit the CI cover.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Item | Formula / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manhole quantity in abstract estimate | $Q = N \times 1$ (where $N$ = number of manholes) | Number |
| 2 | Abstract cost | $C = N \times R_d$ | ₹ |
| 3 | Depth classification cut-off (shallow → normal) | $0.90 \text{ m}$ | m |
| 4 | Depth classification cut-off (normal → deep) | $1.50 \text{ m}$ | m |
| 5 | Step (foot rest) spacing | $S_{\text{step}} = 0.30 \text{ m}$ (vertical centre-to-centre) | m |
| 6 | Number of steps per metre depth | $n_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{1}{0.30} \approx 3$ to 4 | nos/m |
| 7 | Minimum working width at bottom | $w_{\min} = 0.6 \text{ m}$ (each side of channel) | m |
| 8 | CI cover clear opening (circular) | $\phi 600 \text{ mm}$ | mm |
| 9 | Channel cross-section | Half-circle, same dia as outgoing sewer | mm |
| 10 | Bed concrete (PCC) thickness | $t_{\text{PCC}} = 100$ to $150 \text{ mm}$ | mm |
| 11 | Taper portion height (top) | Typically $0.45$ to $0.6 \text{ m}$ | m |
| 12 | Wall thickness (deep) | $0.40 \text{ m}$ (1.5 brick) | m |
| 13 | Wall thickness (taper top) | $0.30 \text{ m}$ (1.25 brick) | m |
| 14 | Internal plaster (inside) | $12 \text{ mm}$ thick, CM 1:3 | mm |
| 15 | External plaster (outside) | $20 \text{ mm}$ thick, CM 1:4 | mm |

**Units used (KTU standard):**

- For the manhole as a whole → **Number (No.)**
- For items *inside* the composite (had they been measured separately, which they are NOT for KTU manhole) → m³ (brickwork, PCC), m² (plaster), kg (CI cover), etc.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the "Item of Work" Concept

The "item of work" (or "composite item rate") approach in quantity surveying is **not** unique to manholes. It is the **default rule** in civil engineering estimation whenever:

- The work is **routine, repetitive, and standardized** in dimensions.
- The cost of *measuring each constituent separately* would be **disproportionate** to the total value of the work.
- The **Schedule of Rates (SSR / DSR / PWD SOR)** has already published a fair composite rate.

**Other examples of composite "item of work" in KTU syllabus:**

- Septic tank (number, of given size and depth)
- Soak pit (number)
- Water meter chamber, valve chamber
- Gully trap chamber, inspection chamber
- Flush tank, urinal, wash basin (number, of given type)
- Boundary wall of standard height (running metre)
- Standard door / window (number, of given size)

The estimator's skill lies in **knowing the limit of the composite item** — i.e., understanding *exactly* what is bundled and what is not (e.g., excavation in rock is sometimes a *separate* item even inside a manhole).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantity of a Manhole in the Abstract Estimate

In the **abstract of estimate** (Module 3 — Public Health Engineering works), the manhole is recorded as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Item No.} \quad & 1 \quad \text{— Providing and constructing brick masonry manhole of size} \\
& 0.9 \text{ m} \times 0.8 \text{ m} \text{ (internal), depth } 1.0 \text{ m} \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ m}, \\
& \text{with 200 mm brick walls in CM 1:4, 12 mm internal plaster in CM 1:3,} \\
& \text{20 mm external plaster, 100 mm PCC 1:4:8 bed, semi-circular channel,} \\
& \text{benching, RCC cover slab 150 mm thick, and CI frame and cover 600 mm dia.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Quantity in the abstract = Number of manholes required (N).**

There is **no length, breadth, height multiplication** for the manhole as a whole. The estimator simply counts the locations on the line plan / sewer network drawing where a manhole is to be provided (e.g., at every **30 m** to **45 m** straight run, at every change of direction, at every junction, at every change of gradient, and at the head of the sewer).

**Example derivation (typical layout):**

Consider a 300 m long sewer line. As per IS standards, manholes are placed at:

- $S = 30$ m spacing (for $\phi \le 450$ mm sewer)
- Plus at both ends (start and termination).

Number of manholes:

$$N = \left( \frac{L}{S} \right) + 1$$

Substituting $L = 300 \text{ m}$, $S = 30 \text{ m}$:

$$N = \left( \frac{300}{30} \right) + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11 \text{ nos.}$$

> However, in KTU, a *typical* simple question gives the count directly. The above formula is to be used when spacing is mentioned.

### 3.2 Derivation: Abstract Cost of Manholes

The **abstract cost** of manholes in the estimate is:

$$C_{\text{MH}} = N \times R_d$$

where:

- $C_{\text{MH}}$ = total cost of all manholes (₹)
- $N$ = number of manholes (nos.)
- $R_d$ = composite rate per manhole for the given size and depth (₹/No.) — read from the **Schedule of Rates (SSR/DSR)**.

**KTU exam-style worked example:**

**Question:** A sewer line is 240 m long. Manholes are provided at 30 m spacing, at both ends, and at one additional change-of-direction location. The depth of manholes is 1.2 m (medium type). The composite rate from SOR is ₹ 18,500 per manhole. Calculate the abstract cost of manholes.

**Solution — Step by step:**

**Step 1 — Number of manholes from spacing:**
$$N_1 = \left( \frac{L}{S} \right) + 1 = \left( \frac{240}{30} \right) + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ nos.}$$

**Step 2 — Add the extra manhole at the change of direction:**
$$N_2 = N_1 + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10 \text{ nos.}$$

**Step 3 — Abstract cost:**
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{MH}} &= N_2 \times R_d \\
&= 10 \times 18{,}500 \\
&= 1{,}85{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Statement of the item in the abstract (KTU format):**

| Sl. | Description | No. | Length (m) | Breadth (m) | Depth (m) | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brick masonry manhole of size 0.9 m × 0.8 m, depth 1.0 to 1.5 m, with PCC bed, plaster, RCC cover slab, and CI frame & cover, complete as per SOR | 10 | — | — | — | **10** | No. | 18,500 | **1,85,000** |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "Item of Work" Boundary (What is IN vs OUT)

This is the **single most important table** for KTU 2024 Module 3. The examiner can ask *"List the items covered under the unit rate of a manhole"* or *"Which of the following is NOT included in the manhole rate?"*

| Sl. | Component / Activity | Included in Manhole "Item of Work" Rate? | KTU Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork excavation in ordinary soil | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 2 | Earthwork excavation in **hard rock** (requiring blasting) | ❌ No (or paid extra) | Demanding separate provision |
| 3 | Dewatering (continuous pumping) | ⚠️ Mostly separate item | Project-specific |
| 4 | PCC bed 1:4:8, 100 mm | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 5 | Brickwork in CM 1:4 | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 6 | 12 mm internal plaster in CM 1:3 | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 7 | 20 mm external plaster in CM 1:4 | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 8 | Benching & channel | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 9 | CI foot rests (steps) @ 300 mm c/c | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 10 | RCC cover slab (150 mm thick) | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 11 | CI frame & cover (600 mm dia) | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 12 | Curing of brickwork and concrete | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 13 | Backfilling of pit | ✅ Yes | Bundled |
| 14 | Shoring & strutting | ✅ Yes (deemed) | Bundled |
| 15 | Drop manhole / ramp connection | ❌ No | Separate item |
| 16 | Stormwater gratings / gully traps | ❌ No | Separate item |
| 17 | Sewer pipes (incoming / outgoing) | ❌ No | Measured in m, separate item |
| 18 | Testing of manhole (water test) | ✅ Yes | Bundled |

### 3.4 Composition of the Composite Rate $R_d$

Although KTU 2024 says *"no detailed estimate needed"*, a *conceptual* understanding of how the rate $R_d$ is built up is expected. The composite rate is the sum of:

$$R_d = R_{\text{mat}} + R_{\text{lab}} + R_{\text{plant}} + \text{Contractor's profit \& overheads}$$

where:

- $R_{\text{mat}}$ = material cost of bricks, cement, sand, aggregate, CI cover, reinforcement steel, water.
- $R_{\text{lab}}$ = labour cost of mason, helper, bhisti, mixerman, etc.
- $R_{\text{plant}}$ = mixer, vibrator, dewatering pump (depreciated per manhole).

**Typical order of magnitude (for KTU exam reference, 2024 SSR approx.):**

- Shallow manhole (0.9 m × 0.8 m, 1.0 m deep) → ₹ **12,000 – 15,000** / No.
- Normal manhole (1.2 m × 0.9 m, 1.5 m deep) → ₹ **18,000 – 22,000** / No.
- Deep manhole (1.5 m × 1.2 m, 2.5 m deep) → ₹ **30,000 – 45,000** / No.
- Very deep / circular manhole (2.5 m to 9 m deep) → ₹ **60,000 – 2,50,000+** / No. (depending on depth, dewatering, shoring).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The *exact* rate is **not** to be memorized. The exam will *give* the rate in the question. The student is expected to apply $C = N \times R_d$ correctly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["New Line Item in Estimate"] --> Q1{"Is the work\nstandardized and\nrepetitive in\nsize and shape?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"Is the unit cost\npublished in\nSSR or DSR\nas a composite rate?"}
    Q1 -- NO --> A1["Measure each\nconstituent\nseparately\n(detailed estimate)"]
    Q2 -- YES --> A2["Adopt as ITEM OF WORK\nMeasure by Number\nApply composite rate\nExample: Manhole, Septic tank,\nFlush tank, Standard door"]
    Q2 -- NO --> A3["Build up rate analytically\nfrom materials and labour\nor refer to nearest SSR item\nwith suitable adjustment"]
    A2 --> End["Recorded in Abstract\nas a single line item"]
    A1 --> End
    A3 --> End
```
