C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{old}} \times \frac{CI_{\text{present}}}{CI_{\text{old}}}
$$

This is used by **insurance companies, arbitrators, and valuers** to update historical costs to current market levels.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a) Explain the various methods of cost estimation in building construction. Compare plinth area rate method with the cubical content rate method. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Introduction (1 mark):** Cost estimation is the process of forecasting the expenditure required to complete a construction project. The accuracy depends on the stage of the project and the data available.

**Methods of Cost Estimation (3.5 marks):**

1. **Preliminary/Approximate Method** — Used at concept stage, based on plinth area, cubical content, or unit base.
2. **Detailed/Item-Rate Method** — Most accurate, uses quantities × unit rates.
3. **Approximate Quantity Method** — Intermediate accuracy, uses percentage estimates.
4. **Cube-Bulk Method** — Multiplies building volume by standard cost/m³.

**Comparison Table (2.5 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Plinth Area Method** | **Cubical Content Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Plinth area (m²) | Volume of building (m³) |
| Rate | ₹/m² of plinth | ₹/m³ of volume |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Slightly higher |
| Account for height | No | Yes (considers height) |
| Best for | Single-storey / Residential | Multi-storey / Varied heights |
| KTU Prevalence | Very common in Kerala PWD | Less common now |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Both methods are **approximate methods** suitable for **preliminary estimates**; detailed item-rate is preferred for final tenders.

---

**(b) An existing building was constructed 10 years ago at a cost of ₹ 30,00,000. The cost index at that time was 220. The present cost index is 410. Find the present cost of constructing a similar building. Also estimate the depreciated cost if the building has aged by 10% through straight-line method. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Given Data (0.5 mark):**
- $C_0 = ₹ 30,00,000$
- $CI_0 = 220$
- $CI_1 = 410$
- Depreciation = 10% (straight-line)

**Step 2 — Escalation Formula (1 mark):**

$$
C_1 = C_0 \times \frac{CI_1}{CI_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values (1 mark):**

$$
C_1 = 30,00,000 \times \frac{410}{220}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_1 = 30,00,000 \times 1.8636
$$

$$
C_1 = ₹ 55,90,909.09
$$

**Present Cost ≈ ₹ 55.91 Lakhs**

**Step 5 — Depreciation Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{dep}} = C_1 \times (1 - D/100) = 55,90,909 \times (1 - 0.10)
$$

$$
C_{\text{dep}} = 55,90,909 \times 0.90 = ₹ 50,31,818.18
$$

**Depreciated Cost ≈ ₹ 50.32 Lakhs**

**Final Conclusion (½ mark):** The present replacement cost is **₹ 55.91 Lakhs**, and after accounting for 10% depreciation, the **depreciated cost is ₹ 50.32 Lakhs**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a) Define 'Analysis of Rate'. Prepare the Analysis of Rate for 1 m³ of RCC M25 concrete using standard PWD rates. Assume 8.5 bags of cement per m³, sand = 0.45 m³, aggregate = 0.90 m³, labour = 1 mason + 2 helpers for 1 day. Take overheads = 15% and profit = 10%. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition (1 mark):** **Analysis of Rate (AOR)** is the systematic procedure of computing the **unit rate** of an item of work by breaking it down into components — material, labour, plant, overheads, and profit.

**Standard Rates Assumed (1 mark):**
- Cement bag (50 kg) = ₹ 400
- Sand = ₹ 1,900/m³
- Coarse aggregate (20 mm) = ₹ 1,600/m³
- Mason = ₹ 850/day
- Helper = ₹ 550/day

**Step 1 — Material Cost (1.5 marks):**

| **Item** | **Qty** | **Rate (₹)** | **Cost (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cement | 8.5 bags | 400 | 3,400 |
| Sand | 0.45 m³ | 1,900 | 855 |
| Aggregate | 0.90 m³ | 1,600 | 1,440 |
| **Total Material M** | | | **5,695** |

**Step 2 — Labour Cost (1 mark):**

$$
L = 1 \times 850 + 2 \times 550 = 850 + 1,100 = ₹ 1,950
$$

**Step 3 — Plant Cost — Assume 5% of Materials (0.5 mark):**

$$
P = 5695 \times 0.05 = ₹ 284.75
$$

**Step 4 — Direct Cost (0.5 mark):**

$$
DC = M + L + P = 5695 + 1950 + 284.75 = ₹ 7,929.75
$$

**Step 5 — Overheads @ 15% (0.5 mark):**

$$
O = 7929.75 \times 0.15 = ₹ 1,189.46
$$

**Step 6 — Profit @ 10% on (DC + O) (0.5 mark):**

$$
Pf = (7929.75 + 1189.46) \times 0.10 = ₹ 911.92
$$

**Step 7 — Final Unit Rate (0.5 mark):**

$$
R = 7929.75 + 1189.46 + 911.92 = ₹ 10,031.13 \text{ per m}^3
$$

**Answer: Rate per m³ of RCC M25 = ₹ 10,031 (approx).**

---

**(b) A contractor quotes ₹ 8,000 per m³ for RCC M20 work. He uses 8 bags of cement at ₹ 380 each, 0.45 m³ sand at ₹ 1,800/m³, and 0.90 m³ aggregate at ₹ 1,500/m³. Labour cost is ₹ 1,700 per m³ and plant cost is 4% of material cost. If the overheads are 12% of direct cost and the contractor's profit is 10%, verify whether the quoted rate is justified. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Material Cost (1 mark):**

$$
M = 8 \times 380 + 0.45 \times 1800 + 0.90 \times 1500
$$

$$
M = 3040 + 810 + 1350 = ₹ 5,200
$$

**Step 2 — Labour Cost (0.5 mark):**

$$
L = ₹ 1,700
$$

**Step 3 — Plant Cost — 4% of M (0.5 mark):**

$$
P = 5200 \times 0.04 = ₹ 208
$$

**Step 4 — Direct Cost (0.5 mark):**

$$
DC = 5200 + 1700 + 208 = ₹ 7,108
$$

**Step 5 — Overheads @ 12% (1 mark):**

$$
O = 7108 \times 0.12 = ₹ 852.96
$$

**Step 6 — Profit @ 10% on (DC + O) (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Base} = 7108 + 852.96 = ₹ 7,960.96
$$

$$
Pf = 7960.96 \times 0.10 = ₹ 796.10
$$

**Step 7 — Computed Unit Rate (1 mark):**

$$
R_{\text{calc}} = 7108 + 852.96 + 796.10 = ₹ 8,757.06 \text{ per m}^3
$$

**Step 8 — Comparison & Conclusion (1 mark):**

- Quoted rate: ₹ 8,000/m³
- Computed rate: ₹ 8,757/m³
- **Difference: ₹ 757/m³ (quoted is LOWER)**

> **Conclusion:** The quoted rate is **NOT justified**. The contractor is under-quoting by ₹ 757/m³, and the project will result in a **loss of ₹ 757 × total volume of RCC M20**. The contractor should revise the quote to **at least ₹ 8,757/m³** to ensure break-even with profit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in "Cost" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Direct Cost" with "Total Cost".** Direct cost **excludes** overheads and profit. Many students add them twice and lose **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Profit on Wrong Base.** Profit is calculated on **(DC + Overheads)**, not on **DC alone**. This is a 2-mark error.
> 3. **Forgetting Wastage.** Material cost **must include** 2.5%–5% wastage, especially for cement and steel.
> 4. **Wrong Cost Index Formula.** Some students write $CI = C_{\text{old}}/C_{\text{new}}$ — this inverts the ratio. Always re-derive on the answer sheet.
> 5. **Not Converting Units.** Plinth area in **ft²** and rate in **₹/m²** — many forget to convert. Always work in **SI units** (m², m³, kg, ₹).
> 6. **Skipping Final Sentence in 14-mark Qs.** Examiners award **½ to 1 mark** for a clear **conclusion statement**. Never leave the answer hanging.
> 7. **Confusing AOR with BOQ.** AOR is for **rate analysis** of one unit. BOQ is the **bill of quantities** listing all items. Keep them separate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — Revise before exam:**

- **Cost** = Sum of Money expended on a construction project (Materials + Labour + Plant + Overheads + Profit).
- **Direct Cost (DC)** = M + L + P (site-specific).
- **Indirect Cost (IC)** = Site supervision, head office allocation, insurance, taxes.
- **Fixed Cost** = Independent of project volume (e.g., rent).
- **Variable Cost** = Proportional to work output (e.g., cement).
- **Semi-Variable Cost** = Mixed (e.g., electricity).
- **Total Cost Formula:** $TC = DC \left( 1 + \dfrac{o}{100} \right) \left( 1 + \dfrac{p}{100} \right)$.
- **Plinth Area Method:** $C = A_p \times R_{pl}$ (₹/m²).
- **Cubical Content Method:** $C = V \times R_{cub}$ (₹/m³).
- **Cost Index:** $CI = (C_{\text{current}}/C_{\text{base}}) \times 100$.
- **Escalation Formula:** $C_{\text{new}} = C_{\text{old}} \times (CI_{\text{new}}/CI_{\text{old}})$.
- **AOR Steps:** Identify item → List materials → Add wastage → Compute M, L, P → Add Overheads → Add Profit → Final Unit Rate.
- **Standard Profit %** = 10%–15%; **Standard Overhead %** = 10%–20%.
- **Wastage Allowances:** Cement 2.5%–5%, Steel 5%–10%, Bricks 5%–7.5%.
- **Standard Rent** = (Cost × Interest Rate) / 12 + Sinking Fund + Repairs + Municipal Tax.
- **Depreciation** reduces cost over time; **Straight Line Method** = $D = (C - S)/n$.
- **Cost vs Price vs Value:** Cost = What is paid, Price = What is asked, Value = What it is worth.
- **AOR is NOT a BOQ:** AOR = rate analysis; BOQ = quantity listing.
- **Kerala PWD SSR (Schedule of Rates)** is the **primary reference** for KTU exam rates.
- **Memorise the four key AOR numericals:** RCC M20, Brick masonry, Plaster, Earthwork excavation.
- **Always mention "Per Unit"** in the final AOR answer (e.g., "₹ 9,386 per m³").
- **Convert all units to SI** before plugging into formulas (m, m², m³, kg).
- **Cost Index applications:** Insurance claims, valuation, arbitration, replacement cost estimation.

> **End of Topic: Cost | Module 4 | PCCET601**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Price
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of PRICE

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRICE** is formally defined as the **quantitative monetary expression** (in ₹, $, or any standard currency) assigned to a **unit quantity** of work, material, labour, or service, representing the total consideration exchanged between the buyer (Client/Owner) and the seller (Contractor/Vendor) in a construction contract.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context of *Quantity Surveying and Valuation*, **Price** is one of the three fundamental pillars of commercial construction economics, sitting alongside **Cost** and **Value**. It is the figure that appears in the **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)**, **Schedule of Rates (SOR)**, **Tender Documents**, and **Valuation Reports**.

The standard metric representation for price in engineering economics is:

$$\text{Price per Unit} \; (\text{₹/unit}) = \frac{\text{Total Monetary Consideration}}{\text{Total Quantity Supplied}}$$

where the unit may be **per m³, per m², per kg, per number, per running metre, or per tonne** depending on the item of work.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, "Price" forms the foundational concept upon which *Valuation*, *Standard Rent*, *Market Value*, *Book Value*, and *Depreciation* are built. Mastering the Price concept is essential before tackling any numerical valuation problem.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Vegetable Vendor Model

Imagine you walk to a local vegetable market to buy **tomatoes**.

- The farmer **grew** the tomatoes — what he spent on seeds, water, fertilizer, and his own labour is his **Cost** (≈ ₹20/kg).
- The vendor **bought** them from the farmer at a **wholesale price** and now sells them to you.
- The price you pay at the counter — say **₹40/kg** — is the **Price**.
- This ₹40/kg **includes**:
  - The farmer's cost (₹20)
  - Vendor's transport, shop rent, electricity (Overheads ≈ ₹10)
  - Vendor's profit margin (≈ ₹10)

Now translate this analogy to **construction**:

| Vegetable Market Element | Construction Industry Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Tomatoes | Concrete, Steel, Bricks |
| Farmer's Cost (₹20) | Direct Cost (Material + Labour) |
| Vendor's Overheads (₹10) | Site Overheads + Office Overheads |
| Vendor's Profit (₹10) | Contractor's Profit Margin |
| **Final Counter Price (₹40/kg)** | **Quoted/All-in Price per Unit (₹/m³)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** *Price is what you pay at the counter — it is the final number on the invoice, regardless of what it costs the seller to produce.* In construction, this is exactly how a contractor's **tendered rate** becomes the **price** for the owner, even though the contractor's internal cost may be lower.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard conventions are used universally in KTU board examinations for expressing **Price**:

- **Currency:** Indian Rupee (₹) — assumed constant unless inflation/escalation is given.
- **Rate basis:** Per unit of measurement (₹/m³, ₹/m², ₹/kg, ₹/Rm, ₹/No.)
- **Standard units adopted in CPWD / KWA / PWD Schedules of Rates:**
  - Earthwork → ₹/m³
  - Concrete (M20, M25, etc.) → ₹/m³
  - Brickwork → ₹/m³
  - Plastering → ₹/m²
  - Steel reinforcement → ₹/kg or ₹/tonne
  - Centring & shuttering → ₹/m²
- **Price escalation rate:** typically expressed as **% per annum**.
- **Profit margin in civil contracts:** typically **10% to 15%** of total cost.

---

## 1.4 Types of Price in Civil Engineering Economics

There are several distinct categories of **Price** that the KTU syllabus expects students to differentiate:

| Type of Price | Definition | Who Decides It? |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Price** | The prevailing rate at which an item is commonly bought/sold in the open market | Market forces (supply–demand) |
| **Tendered Price** | The rate quoted by a contractor in a competitive bidding process | Contractor (via competitive bidding) |
| **Estimated Price** | The rate computed by the Quantity Surveyor / Engineer before tendering | Engineer / QS based on SOR |
| **Contract Price** | The final agreed price between owner and contractor, as signed in the agreement | Mutual agreement (legally binding) |
| **Schedule Price (SOR Price)** | Standard rate published in government Schedule of Rates (CPWD/PWD) | Government / Public authority |
| **Book Price** | The price recorded in the organisation's books of accounts | Accounts department |
| **Salvage Price** | Price realised by selling a building/asset at the end of its useful life | Market at end-of-life |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse *Tendered Price* with *Estimated Price*. **Remember:** Estimated price is calculated *before* tendering using SOR, while Tendered price is what the contractor *quotes* during bidding — the two need not be equal!

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Price — The Build-Up Logic

Every unit price in a construction contract is mathematically built up from **five distinct cost layers**. Understanding this layered structure is critical for solving KTU numerical problems on **Rate Analysis** and **Valuation**.

### Layer 1 — Direct Material Cost (DMC)

The actual cost of all raw materials consumed per unit of work. This is computed from:

$$\text{DMC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times M_i)$$

where:
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of the *i*-th material consumed per unit of work
- $M_i$ = Market rate of that material per unit

### Layer 2 — Direct Labour Cost (DLC)

The wages paid to skilled/unskilled workers who physically execute the work.

$$\text{DLC} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} (H_j \times W_j)$$

where:
- $H_j$ = Number of hours/days worked by the *j*-th category of labour per unit of work
- $W_j$ = Wage rate of the *j*-th labour category per hour/day

### Layer 3 — Direct Equipment / Plant Cost (DEC)

Cost of machinery (excavator, mixer, vibrator, scaffolding) deployed for the work.

$$\text{DEC} = \text{Hire Charges} + \text{Fuel} + \text{Operator Charges} + \text{Depreciation}$$

### Layer 4 — Overheads (OH)

Overheads are the **indirect costs** that cannot be directly attributed to a single unit of work but are necessary to run the business. They include:
- Site supervision salaries
- Site office rent, electricity, water
- Insurance, taxes, permits
- Head-office administration costs
- Transport of materials to site

Overheads are usually expressed as a **percentage of direct costs**:

$$\text{OH} = \left( \frac{p}{100} \right) \times (\text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC})$$

where **p** = overhead percentage (typically **10% to 25%**).

### Layer 5 — Contractor's Profit (CP)

The contractor's earning margin, expressed as a percentage of total cost.

$$\text{CP} = \left( \frac{q}{100} \right) \times (\text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC} + \text{OH})$$

where **q** = profit percentage (typically **10% to 15%**).

---

## 2.2 The Master Price Equation

Combining all five layers, the **final all-inclusive Price** per unit of work is:

$$\boxed{\;P \;=\; \underbrace{(\text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC})}_{\text{Prime/Direct Cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{OH}}_{\text{Overheads}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{CP}}_{\text{Profit}}\;}$$

Equivalently, expanding OH and CP:

$$P = \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)(\text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC}) \left(1 + \frac{q}{100}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **universal price-buildup equation** that examiners love to test in Part B questions. Memorise it.

---

## 2.3 Price vs. Cost vs. Value — The KTU Trifecta

This is one of the **most frequently asked conceptual distinctions** in KTU exams:

| Aspect | Price | Cost | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Money exchanged in a transaction | Money spent to produce/acquire | Worth/utility of an item to its owner |
| **Perspective** | Buyer & Seller (agreed) | Seller (internal) | Owner/User (subjective) |
| **Includes Profit?** | **Yes** | **No** (purely expenditure) | Not relevant |
| **Determined by** | Negotiation / Market | Actual expenses + receipts | Utility, scarcity, demand |
| **Example** | Contractor quotes ₹8,500/m³ for M25 concrete | Contractor spends ₹7,000/m³ to produce it | Owner values the finished structure at ₹1.5 Crore |
| **Role in Valuation** | Determines *present-day replacement cost* | Forms the *base* for price | Determines *final valuation figure* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of the Trifecta:**
> $$\text{Price} = \text{Cost} + \text{Profit}$$
> $$\text{Value} \;\neq\; \text{Price} \;\neq\; \text{Cost}$$
> **Value depends on utility, not money exchanged.**

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Price in Construction

KTU examiners often ask students to "list and explain the factors affecting the price of a building/structure." The principal factors are:

### 2.4.1 Supply-Side Factors
1. **Cost of raw materials** — cement, steel, aggregate rates fluctuate daily.
2. **Labour availability and wage rates** — scarcity of skilled masons raises wages.
3. **Equipment and fuel costs** — diesel/petrol price hikes directly impact machinery hire.
4. **Transportation distance** — longer lead distance = higher freight cost.
5. **Interest rates on borrowed capital** — high rates raise contractor's working-capital cost.
6. **Government taxes and duties** — GST, customs, excise.
7. **Site conditions** — rocky soil, water-logging, hilly terrain increase difficulty.

### 2.4.2 Demand-Side Factors
1. **Construction activity in the region** — boom periods drive prices up.
2. **Type and prestige of the project** — premium projects command premium prices.
3. **Client's specification and quality requirements.**

### 2.4.3 Market & Economic Factors
1. **Inflation / Price index escalation** (WPI, CPI).
2. **Currency exchange fluctuations** (for imported materials).
3. **Government policy changes** — demonetisation, GST reforms.

---

## 2.5 Price Indices and Escalation (Time Value of Price)

In long-duration projects, prices do not remain constant. The **escalated price** at time $t_2$ given the price at time $t_1$ is:

$$\boxed{\;P_{t_2} \;=\; P_{t_1} \times \left(1 + \frac{r \cdot n}{100}\right)\;}$$

where:
- $P_{t_1}$ = Original price at base year
- $P_{t_2}$ = Escalated price after $n$ years
- $r$ = Annual rate of price escalation (%)
- $n$ = Number of years between $t_1$ and $t_2$

A more rigorous version uses the **Price Index Ratio**:

$$P_{t_2} = P_{t_1} \times \frac{\text{Current Price Index}}{\text{Base Price Index}}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Price (Quick Reference Table)

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Master Price Equation** | $P = (1 + p/100)(DMC+DLC+DEC)(1 + q/100)$ | Rate analysis, tender build-up |
| **Material Cost** | $\text{DMC} = \sum Q_i \times M_i$ | Costing of raw materials |
| **Labour Cost** | $\text{DLC} = \sum H_j \times W_j$ | Wage calculation |
| **Overhead Cost** | $\text{OH} = (p/100)(DMC+DLC+DEC)$ | Indirect expenses |
| **Profit** | $\text{CP} = (q/100)(DMC+DLC+DEC+OH)$ | Contractor's margin |
| **Price Escalation (simple)** | $P_{t_2} = P_{t_1}(1 + rn/100)$ | Future price forecasting |
| **Price Escalation (index)** | $P_{t_2} = P_{t_1} \times (I_{t_2}/I_{t_1})$ | Inflation-adjusted valuation |
| **Price = Cost + Profit** | $P = C + \text{Profit}$ | Conceptual trifecta |
| **Unit Price** | $P_{\text{unit}} = (\text{Total Contract Value}) / (\text{Total Quantity})$ | Lump-sum to unit conversion |
| **Market Price** | Determined by $\text{Supply} \leftrightarrow \text{Demand}$ | Open-market transactions |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of the Price Concept

The price concept is not merely academic — it is the **operational backbone** of the following engineering activities:

1. **Tendering and Bidding:** Contractors prepare their bids by computing unit prices using the master equation above.
2. **Bill of Quantities (BOQ):** Every line item carries a unit rate (price) and quantity; their product gives the contract value.
3. **Valuation Reports:** Valuers apply current market prices to estimate the replacement cost of buildings.
4. **Arbitration and Dispute Resolution:** Contract price escalation clauses rely on the index-based price formula.
5. **Insurance Assessment:** Insured value = market price of the asset at the time of policy issuance.
6. **Government Project Sanctioning:** Authorities compare estimated price vs. tendered price before awarding work.
7. **PPP (Public-Private Partnership) Projects:** Viability Gap Funding (VGF) is computed based on price differentials.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Master Price Equation from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive the expression $P = (1 + p/100)(DMC+DLC+DEC)(1 + q/100)$ rigorously from the layered cost-buildup logic.

### Step 1: Define the Prime (Direct) Cost

Let us denote the **prime cost** (also called direct cost) as $C_d$, which aggregates only the costs that can be *directly* charged to a unit of work:

$$C_d \;=\; \text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC}$$

**Explanation:** The prime cost is the sum of all direct, traceable expenditures — what the contractor must *definitely* spend to execute one unit of the work item.

---

### Step 2: Add Overheads on Top of Prime Cost

Overheads are calculated as a percentage $p$ of the prime cost:

$$\text{OH} \;=\; \frac{p}{100} \times C_d$$

The total cost *after overheads* (let's call it $C_o$) becomes:

$$C_o \;=\; C_d + \text{OH} \;=\; C_d + \frac{p}{100} C_d$$

Factoring out $C_d$:

$$C_o \;=\; C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)$$

**Explanation:** This is the contractor's *break-even expenditure* — they have recovered all direct and indirect costs but earned no profit yet.

---

### Step 3: Add Profit on Top of the Cost-with-Overheads

Profit is calculated as a percentage $q$ of the cost-with-overheads $C_o$:

$$\text{CP} \;=\; \frac{q}{100} \times C_o \;=\; \frac{q}{100} \times C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)$$

The final **Price** $P$ is then:

$$P \;=\; C_o + \text{CP}$$

$$P \;=\; C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right) + \frac{q}{100} C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)$$

$$P \;=\; C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right) \left[1 + \frac{q}{100}\right]$$

Substituting back $C_d = \text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC}$:

$$\boxed{\;P \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right) \left(1 + \frac{q}{100}\right) (\text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC})\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical Price Equation.** Notice that the percentage multipliers $(1 + p/100)$ and $(1 + q/100)$ are **multiplicative, not additive** — this is a frequent KTU trap.

---

### Step 3.1: Why Multiplicative and Not Additive?

If overheads are calculated *on* prime cost, and profit is calculated *on* (prime + overhead), then mathematically:

- Additive (WRONG): $P = C_d + (p/100)C_d + (q/100)C_d = C_d(1 + (p+q)/100)$
- Multiplicative (CORRECT): $P = C_d(1 + p/100)(1 + q/100)$

Expanding the correct form:

$$P = C_d \left[1 + \frac{p}{100} + \frac{q}{100} + \frac{pq}{10000}\right]$$

The extra term $pq/10000$ represents **profit on overheads** — i.e., the contractor's profit margin is also applied to the overhead portion. This is industry standard and is **how CPWD/PWD schedules are built**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Price Escalation Formula

**Goal:** Derive the future-price formula $P_{t_2} = P_{t_1} \times (I_{t_2}/I_{t_1})$ from the index method.

### Step 1: Define Price Index

A **price index** $I$ is a normalised measure of price level relative to a chosen base year (where $I_{\text{base}} = 100$):

$$I_t = \frac{P_t}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Solve for Price at Time $t$

Rearranging:

$$P_t = \frac{I_t \times P_{\text{base}}}{100}$$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio for Two Time Periods

The price at time $t_1$ is $P_{t_1} = (I_{t_1} \times P_{\text{base}})/100$, and at time $t_2$ is $P_{t_2} = (I_{t_2} \times P_{\text{base}})/100$.

Dividing $P_{t_2}$ by $P_{t_1}$:

$$\frac{P_{t_2}}{P_{t_1}} = \frac{I_{t_2}}{I_{t_1}}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{t_2} \;=\; P_{t_1} \times \frac{I_{t_2}}{I_{t_1}}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Rate Analysis for M25 Concrete

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**

Compute the all-inclusive **rate (price) per m³** for M25 grade concrete (1:1:2 mix) using the following data:

| Item | Quantity per m³ | Unit Rate |
|---|---|---|
| Cement (50 kg bags) | 8.0 bags | ₹420/bag |
| Sand (Coarse aggregate size ≤ 4.75 mm) | 0.42 m³ | ₹1,800/m³ |
| Coarse aggregate (20 mm) | 0.84 m³ | ₹1,500/m³ |
| Skilled labour | 0.5 days | ₹800/day |
| Unskilled labour | 1.5 days | ₹500/day |
| Mixer + vibrator hire | Lump sum | ₹200/m³ |
| Overheads | — | 10% of prime cost |
| Contractor profit | — | 12.5% of (prime + overhead) |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Direct Material Cost (DMC):**

$$\text{Cement cost} = 8.0 \times 420 = ₹3,360$$

$$\text{Sand cost} = 0.42 \times 1,800 = ₹756$$

$$\text{Coarse aggregate cost} = 0.84 \times 1,500 = ₹1,260$$

$$\text{DMC} = 3,360 + 756 + 1,260 = ₹5,376/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Direct Labour Cost (DLC):**

$$\text{Skilled labour cost} = 0.5 \times 800 = ₹400$$

$$\text{Unskilled labour cost} = 1.5 \times 500 = ₹750$$

$$\text{DLC} = 400 + 750 = ₹1,150/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Direct Equipment Cost (DEC):**

$$\text{DEC} = ₹200/\text{m}^3 \quad (\text{given as lump sum})$$

**Step 4 — Prime Cost (Direct Cost):**

$$C_d = \text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC} = 5,376 + 1,150 + 200 = ₹6,726/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Add Overheads (10%):**

$$\text{OH} = \frac{10}{100} \times 6,726 = ₹672.60/\text{m}^3$$

$$C_o = 6,726 + 672.60 = ₹7,398.60/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Add Profit (12.5% of cost with overhead):**

$$\text{CP} = \frac{12.5}{100} \times 7,398.60 = ₹924.825/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Final All-Inclusive Price:**

$$P = 7,398.60 + 924.825 = ₹8,323.425/\text{m}^3$$

Rounding off to the nearest rupee (standard in KTU valuation):

$$\boxed{\;P \approx ₹8,323 \text{ per m}^3\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification using the master formula:**
> $$P = \left(1 + \frac{10}{100}\right) \left(1 + \frac{12.5}{100}\right) \times 6,726 = (1.10)(1.125)(6,726)$$
> $$= 1.2375 \times 6,726 = 8,323.425 \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Price Escalation Across Years

**Problem:** The unit price of a construction item was **₹5,000** in the year **2020**. The price index in 2020 was **120**, and in **2024** it is **168**. Compute the **escalated price in 2024**.

### Solution

Using the index-ratio method:

$$P_{2024} = P_{2020} \times \frac{I_{2024}}{I_{2020}}$$

$$P_{2024} = 5,000 \times \frac{168}{120}$$

$$P_{2024} = 5,000 \times 1.40$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{2024} = ₹7,000\;}$$

**Cross-verification using the simple formula:**

The total percentage escalation in 4 years:

$$\text{Total \% rise} = \left(\frac{168 - 120}{120}\right) \times 100 = 40\%$$

$$P_{2024} = 5,000 \times \left(1 + \frac{40}{100}\right) = 5,000 \times 1.40 = ₹7,000 \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Rate-Analysis Traps in KTU Exams

| Trap / Mistake | What Students Do (Wrong) | What Examiners Expect (Right) |
|---|---|---|
| Adding percentages instead of multiplying | $(1 + 0.10 + 0.125) = 1.225$ | $(1.10)(1.125) = 1.2375$ |
| Calculating profit on *direct cost* only | $\text{CP} = 0.125 \times C_d$ | $\text{CP} = 0.125 \times (C_d + \text{OH})$ |
| Forgetting wastage % on materials | Using exact theoretical quantity | Adding 5–10% wastage for cement, 10–15% for aggregate |
| Using wrong units (m² instead of m³) | Pricing plastering in ₹/m³ | Plastering is always ₹/m² |
| Confusing *contract price* with *estimated price* | Treating them as identical | Estimated = QS calculation; Contract = signed agreement figure |
| Ignoring labour for ancillary items | Forgetting helper/scaffolder wages | Include all skilled + unskilled categories |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — The Layered Structure of Price

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the **Price** of a construction work item is built up from its foundational cost layers, progressing from raw inputs up to the final all-inclusive price.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Direct Material Cost DMC"] --> E["Prime Cost Cd"]
    B["Direct Labour Cost DLC"] --> E
    C["Direct Equipment Cost DEC"] --> E
    E --> F["Apply Overheads p percent"]
    F --> G["Cost with Overheads Co"]
    G --> H["Apply Contractor Profit q percent"]
    H --> I["FINAL ALL-INCLUSIVE PRICE P"]
    I --> J["Recorded in BOQ as Unit Rate"]
    I --> K["Quoted as Tendered Price"]
    I --> L["Used in Valuation Reports"]
    J --> M["Contract Value equals P multiplied by Quantity"]
    K --> M
    L --> N["Market Value Replacement Cost Estimate"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow moves upward through five layers — three direct cost components combine into the *prime cost*, then overheads inflate it, then profit inflates it further, producing the final *price*. This price is then consumed by three downstream documents: the BOQ, the tender, and the valuation report.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — Price vs. Cost vs. Value Relationship

This diagram captures the conceptual *trifecta* that is a high-frequency KTU exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Seller_Perspective
        Cost["COST\nMoney spent by seller\nto produce the item"]
        Price["PRICE\nMoney exchanged in\nthe transaction"]
    end
    subgraph Owner_Perspective
        Value["VALUE\nWorth of item to\nits owner user"]
    end
    Cost -->|"add Profit margin"| Price
    Price -->|"may differ from"| Value
    Value -->|"determined by utility scarcity demand"| Value
```

**Interpretation:** Cost feeds into Price (via the addition of profit), but Value is a *parallel* concept determined by intangible factors — it is **not** mathematically derived from either Cost or Price.

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Price Formation in a Typical Construction Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Market Inputs\nMaterial rates\nLabour wages\nEquipment hire"] --> Analysis["Rate Analysis\nby Quantity Surveyor"]
    Analysis --> Estimate["Estimated Price\nusing SOR"]
    Estimate --> Tender["Tender Documents\nissued to bidders"]
    Tender --> Bid1["Bidder A Quote"]
    Tender --> Bid2["Bidder B Quote"]
    Tender --> Bid3["Bidder C Quote"]
    Bid1 --> Compare["Comparative Statement"]
    Bid2 --> Compare
    Bid3 --> Compare
    Compare --> Award["Award to Lowest Responsive Bidder"]
    Award --> Contract["Contract Price\nlegally binding"]
    Contract --> Execution["Project Execution"]
    Execution --> Final["Final Valuation\nat completion"]
```

**Reading:** The QS performs rate analysis to compute the *estimated price*. Bidders submit competing quotes; the lowest responsive bidder is awarded, locking in the *contract price*. At project completion, this contract price is reconciled against actuals to produce the *final valuation*.

---

## 4.4 Influence Diagram — Factors Affecting Price

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PriceNode["CONSTRUCTION PRICE"]
    M["Material Costs"] --> PriceNode
    L["Labour Wages"] --> PriceNode
    E["Equipment and Fuel"] --> PriceNode
    O["Overheads"] --> PriceNode
    P["Profit Margin"] --> PriceNode
    Inf["Inflation and Price Index"] --> PriceNode
    SC["Site Conditions"] --> PriceNode
    Tax["Government Taxes GST"] --> PriceNode
    Demand["Market Demand"] --> PriceNode
    Spec["Client Specifications"] --> PriceNode
```

**Interpretation:** Ten major factors converge on the final price. In KTU theory questions, students are typically expected to list at least **six to eight** such factors with one-line explanations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**"Distinguish between Price, Cost, and Value in the context of civil engineering contracts."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

- **Cost** (1 mark): It is the actual expenditure incurred by the contractor/seller to produce or acquire an item. It includes material, labour, equipment, and overheads but **excludes profit**.
- **Price** (1 mark): It is the monetary consideration actually exchanged between the buyer and seller in a transaction. It **includes cost plus the seller's profit margin**.
- **Value** (1 mark): It is the worth or utility of an asset to its owner, which depends on factors like location, demand, scarcity, and remaining useful life — it need **not** equal either cost or price.

**Key formula:** $\text{Price} = \text{Cost} + \text{Profit}$, while Value is independent and subjective.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"List any six factors affecting the price of a building."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):

1. Cost of raw materials (cement, steel, aggregate)
2. Labour wage rates and availability of skilled workers
3. Equipment hire charges and fuel costs
4. Transportation and lead distance
5. Government taxes and duties (GST)
6. Site conditions (soil type, terrain, water table)
7. Inflation and price index escalation
8. Contractor's profit margin expectations

*(Any six points with brief explanations earn full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation**, every Part B question offers an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. Below, both alternatives (Question A and Question B) are solved completely.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **master equation for all-inclusive price** of a construction item, showing how overheads and contractor's profit are layered on the prime cost. Clearly state the difference between *additive* and *multiplicative* percentage application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the following data, compute the all-inclusive **rate (price) per m³** for **RCC M20 concrete (1:1.5:3 mix)**:

| Item | Quantity per m³ | Unit Rate |
|---|---|---|
| Cement | 6.4 bags | ₹400/bag |
| Sand | 0.45 m³ | ₹1,600/m³ |
| Coarse aggregate (20 mm) | 0.90 m³ | ₹1,400/m³ |
| Skilled labour | 0.4 day | ₹750/day |
| Unskilled labour | 1.2 days | ₹450/day |
| Mixer hire (lump sum) | — | ₹180/m³ |
| Overheads | — | 12% of prime cost |
| Profit | — | 10% on (prime + overhead) |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Define Prime Cost** *(1 mark)*

$$C_d = \text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC}$$

**Step 2: Add Overheads** *(2 marks)*

Overheads are a percentage $p$ of prime cost:

$$\text{OH} = \frac{p}{100} \times C_d$$

$$C_o = C_d + \frac{p}{100} C_d = C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)$$

**Step 3: Add Profit on (Prime + Overhead)** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{CP} = \frac{q}{100} \times C_o = \frac{q}{100} \times C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)$$

**Step 4: Final Price** *(1 mark)*

$$P = C_o + \text{CP} = C_d \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right) \left(1 + \frac{q}{100}\right)$$

**Step 5: Additive vs. Multiplicative** *(1 mark)*

| Method | Formula | Numerical (p = 10%, q = 12.5%) |
|---|---|---|
| Additive (incorrect) | $C_d (1 + (p+q)/100)$ | $C_d \times 1.225$ |
| Multiplicative (correct) | $C_d (1 + p/100)(1 + q/100)$ | $C_d \times 1.2375$ |

The multiplicative form is correct because profit is computed on the cost *including* overheads, hence the extra factor $(1 + pq/10000)$ appears.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: DMC** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Cement} = 6.4 \times 400 = ₹2,560$$

$$\text{Sand} = 0.45 \times 1,600 = ₹720$$

$$\text{Aggregate} = 0.90 \times 1,400 = ₹1,260$$

$$\text{DMC} = 2,560 + 720 + 1,260 = ₹4,540/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: DLC** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Skilled} = 0.4 \times 750 = ₹300$$

$$\text{Unskilled} = 1.2 \times 450 = ₹540$$

$$\text{DLC} = 300 + 540 = ₹840/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3: DEC** *(0.5 mark)*

$$\text{DEC} = ₹180/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4: Prime Cost** *(1 mark)*

$$C_d = 4,540 + 840 + 180 = ₹5,560/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 5: Overheads (12%)** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{OH} = 0.12 \times 5,560 = ₹667.20/\text{m}^3$$

$$C_o = 5,560 + 667.20 = ₹6,227.20/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 6: Profit (10% on $C_o$)** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{CP} = 0.10 \times 6,227.20 = ₹622.72/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 7: Final Price** *(0.5 mark)*

$$P = 6,227.20 + 622.72 = ₹6,849.92/\text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\;P \approx ₹6,850 \text{ per m}^3\;}$$

**Cross-verification via master formula:**

$$P = (1 + 0.12)(1 + 0.10) \times 5,560 = 1.12 \times 1.10 \times 5,560 = 1.232 \times 5,560 = ₹6,849.92 \;\checkmark$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **concept of price escalation** in long-duration construction contracts. Derive the index-based price escalation formula and state its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential building was constructed at a contract price of **₹85,00,000** in **2019**. The construction price index in 2019 was **125** and in **2024** it has risen to **172**. If the owner wishes to construct an identical building in 2024, estimate the **current price** using the index method. Also compute the simple annual escalation rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Concept of Price Escalation** *(3 marks)*

In long-duration contracts (typically > 1 year), the prices of materials, labour, and equipment tend to rise due to inflation, currency fluctuations, and market forces. Since the contractor's quoted price was based on rates prevailing at the time of bidding, they would suffer losses if compensation for price rise is not built into the contract. **Price escalation clauses** are therefore inserted into contracts to periodically adjust the contract price using a published price index (such as the Wholesale Price Index — WPI, or Consumer Price Index — CPI for construction).

**Derivation of Index Formula** *(3 marks)*

Define $I_t$ as the price index at time $t$, with $I_{\text{base}} = 100$ at the base year.

$$I_t = \frac{P_t}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_t = \frac{I_t \times P_{\text{base}}}{100}$$

Forming the ratio between two periods $t_2$ and $t_1$:

$$\frac{P_{t_2}}{P_{t_1}} = \frac{I_{t_2}}{I_{t_1}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{P_{t_2} = P_{t_1} \times \frac{I_{t_2}}{I_{t_1}}}$$

**Limitations** *(1 mark)*

1. The chosen index may not perfectly reflect the contractor's actual material/labour mix.
2. Index data is published with a time lag, causing disputes over reference months.
3. Sudden price shocks (e.g., GST introduction) are not always captured smoothly.
4. The formula assumes uniform inflation across all components, which is unrealistic.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:** *(0.5 mark)*
- $P_{2019} = ₹85,00,000$
- $I_{2019} = 125$
- $I_{2024} = 172$
- Time gap $n = 5$ years

**Step 1: Apply Index Formula** *(2 marks)*

$$P_{2024} = P_{2019} \times \frac{I_{2024}}{I_{2019}}$$

$$P_{2024} = 85,00,000 \times \frac{172}{125}$$

$$P_{2024} = 85,00,000 \times 1.376$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{2024} = ₹1,16,96,000\;}$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Percentage Escalation** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Total \% rise} = \left(\frac{172 - 125}{125}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{47}{125}\right) \times 100 = 37.6\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Simple Annual Escalation Rate** *(2 marks)*

$$r_{\text{annual}} = \frac{\text{Total \% rise}}{n} = \frac{37.6\%}{5} = 7.52\% \text{ per annum}$$

**Verification using the simple formula:**

$$P_{2024} = 85,00,000 \times \left(1 + \frac{7.52 \times 5}{100}\right) = 85,00,000 \times 1.376 = ₹1,16,96,000 \;\checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Price-Related Questions:**
>
> 1. **Percentage Trap:** Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** if students add $(p+q)/100$ instead of multiplying $(1 + p/100)(1 + q/100)$.
> 2. **Profit Base Error:** Computing profit on *prime cost* alone (instead of prime + overhead) loses **1 to 1.5 marks**. Always re-read the question wording carefully — "profit on cost" vs. "profit on total cost" are different.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch:** Pricing plastering in ₹/m³ or earthwork in ₹/m² is an automatic **1-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Missing Wastage:** Forgetting to add wastage percentages (5% for cement, 10–15% for aggregate) costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 5. **No Final Statement:** A numerical answer without the boxed final figure (e.g., "₹6,850 per m³") may lose **0.5 mark** for lack of clarity.
> 6. **Currency/Index Mix-up:** Using ₹1,000 instead of ₹1,00,000 or misreading index values (125 vs. 152) leads to cascading errors — always recheck the given data before writing.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for PRICE:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Price = monetary consideration exchanged per unit of work (₹/unit).
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $P = (1 + p/100)(1 + q/100)(\text{DMC} + \text{DLC} + \text{DEC})$
- ✅ **Five Cost Layers:** Material → Labour → Equipment → Overheads → Profit.
- ✅ **Trifecta:** Price ≠ Cost ≠ Value. Price = Cost + Profit; Value is subjective.
- ✅ **Types of Price:** Market, Tender, Estimated, Contract, Schedule (SOR), Book, Salvage.
- ✅ **Escalation Formula:** $P_{t_2} = P_{t_1} \times (I_{t_2}/I_{t_1})$
- ✅ **Simple Formula:** $P_{t_2} = P_{t_1} (1 + rn/100)$
- ✅ **Typical Percentages:** Overheads = 10–25%, Profit = 10–15%.
- ✅ **Standard Units:** Concrete/Brickwork/Earthwork → ₹/m³; Plastering/Painting/Formwork → ₹/m²; Steel → ₹/kg or ₹/tonne.
- ✅ **Multiplier is Multiplicative, Not Additive** — critical for full marks.
- ✅ **Profit is Always Applied on (Prime + Overhead)**, Never on Prime Cost Alone.
- ✅ **Wastage %:** Cement 5%, Sand 10%, Aggregate 10–15% — add before computing DMC.
- ✅ **Index Method Limitations:** Time lag, basket mismatch, sudden shocks not captured.
- ✅ **Price vs. Estimated Price vs. Tender Price:** Estimated is QS-computed; Tendered is contractor-quoted; Contract is legally agreed.

> **Final Tip:** Whenever you see a rate-analysis question in KTU, immediately write the master formula on the answer sheet **before** plugging numbers — this guarantees the examiner sees your logical structure and earns you **partial credit** even if arithmetic slips occur.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinds of Values; Income – Gross Income
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION (Note: corrected to "QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION" per KTU 2024 syllabus registry)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kinds of Values; Income – Gross Income

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition — Kinds of Values

In the discipline of **Valuation Engineering** (as prescribed under the KTU 2024 Scheme for Civil Engineering), the term **"Value"** does **not** possess a single, universal meaning. The same physical property — say, a residential building in Thrissur or a commercial plot in Kochi — can have **simultaneously multiple values** depending on the *purpose* of the valuation, the *context* of the transaction, and the *time horizon* under consideration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Valuation):**
> *"Value is the present worth of all future benefits (rights) and obligations (liabilities) inherent in the ownership of a property, expressed in monetary terms, under a specified set of assumptions and at a particular point in time."*

The **Kinds of Values** therefore represent the *categorical labels* that valuers attach to a property depending on the **context of the transaction** and the **method of valuation** adopted. Mastering these distinctions is mandatory, as KTU board examiners frequently test whether a student can correctly identify *which kind of value* is being asked for in a given practical scenario (e.g., "find the salvage value" vs. "find the market value").

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Jewelry Shop" View of Value

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a **gold ring** that your grandmother gives you. To her, the ring is worth a lifetime of memories (**Sentimental Value**). If you walk into a jewelry shop today, the shopkeeper will weigh the gold and pay you the metal rate (**Scrap Value**). If you try to resell the *exact same design* through the same shop, the new replacement price is quoted (**Replacement Value**). In an emergency (e.g., hospital bill at midnight), you may have to sell it to a quick-buyer at a steep discount (**Distress Value**). Finally, if you were a corporation listing the ring as an asset on a balance sheet, the accountant would write down its depreciated worth (**Book Value**). **Same ring, six different values.** A property behaves identically — its value is *purpose-driven*.

### 1.3 Definition — Gross Income

In the **Income Capitalization Approach** (also called the **Rental Method** or **Investment Method** of valuation), the valuer works with two principal streams of cash:

1. **Gross Income (G.I.)** — the *total inflow* before any deductions.
2. **Net Income (N.I.)** — the *true take-home* after deducting all outgoings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Gross Income):**
> *"Gross Income of a property is the total annual monetary return receivable by the owner from all sources connected with the property, BEFORE making any deduction for expenses, outgoings, taxes, or sinking fund contributions. It is also referred to as the 'Total Rent Receivable' or 'Potential Gross Income' (PGI)."*

Mathematically, in symbolic shorthand:
$$\text{Net Income} = \text{Gross Income} - \text{Outgoings (Expenses)}$$

**Gross Income is the STARTING point** of every income-based valuation. The KTU syllabus explicitly demands that students distinguish **Gross Income** from **Net Income** because confusing the two is one of the highest-frequency errors in board exam valuation problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** Think of Gross Income as the **"Sticker Salary"** printed in an offer letter — the figure *before* tax, PF, ESI, professional tax, and insurance deductions. What hits your bank account is the **Net Income**. The valuation parallel is exact.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Values — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the various "kinds of values" into a logical hierarchy. The classification below is **exam-tested** and is presented in the order a student should memorize it.

#### A. Values Based on **Time / Lifecycle Stage** of the Asset

1. **Original Cost (Book Value at Acquisition)** — The actual amount paid (including registration, stamp duty, brokerage) at the time of original purchase. It is a *historical* figure.
2. **Book Value** — Original Cost minus accumulated depreciation, as recorded in the **ledger books** of the owner. It is an *accounting* figure, not a market figure.
3. **Market Value (Open Market Value / OMV)** — The estimated amount for which a property would exchange hands on the date of valuation, between a *willing buyer* and a *willing seller*, neither under compulsion, with reasonable marketing time. This is the **most commonly asked** value in KTU problems.
4. **Replacement Value (New Replacement)** — The current cost of constructing a *new building of identical utility* using *modern materials* and *prevailing labor rates*, on the *same plot*, without providing for depreciation of the existing structure.
5. **Salvage Value** — The expected sale value of the property (or a component) at the **end of its useful life**, assuming it is sold *as a complete unit* (not broken into pieces). For a building, this is typically **10% of the total cost of construction** (per KTU's adopted thumb rule from SP 7 / National Building Code norms).
6. **Scrap Value** — The value of a property when it is sold *after being dismantled* into individual materials (e.g., bricks, steel, timber). Always **less than or equal to** Salvage Value.

#### B. Values Based on **Transaction Context / Compulsion**

7. **Distress Value** — The value realized in a *forced sale* scenario (e.g., court auction, liquidation of a bankrupt estate). It is **less than** Market Value, typically **70% to 90%** of MV.
8. **Compulsory Acquisition Value** — The value payable by the Government when it acquires private property under statutory powers (e.g., Kerala Land Acquisition Act, RFCTLARR Act 2013). Statutory solatium (15% to 30%) is added on top.
9. **Speculative Value** — The value based on *anticipated future use* (e.g., a plot currently agricultural but likely to be re-zoned as commercial). **Highly subjective.**
10. **Sentimental Value** — A value arising purely from *personal attachment* (e.g., ancestral home, family heirloom property). **Not quantitatively determinable** — it is excluded from professional valuations.

#### C. Values Based on **Income / Capitalization Approach**

11. **Capitalized Value (CV)** — The lump-sum present worth of future net income streams. It is the *output* of the income method.
12. **Net Present Value (NPV)** — Used in development valuation, deducting all future costs from future revenues, then discounting.

#### D. Other Statutory / Specialized Values

13. **Assessed Value** — The value fixed by the **municipal or local authority** for the purpose of levying property tax. Often **lower** than market value.
14. **Fair Value (Accounting Standard – Ind AS 113 / IFRS 13)** — The price that would be received to sell an asset, or paid to transfer a liability, in an *orderly transaction* between market participants on the measurement date.
15. **Going Concern Value** — The value of an *operating business* (with all assets, workforce, and goodwill in place) versus the value of the *individual assets* if sold separately.
16. **Potential Value** — The latent value if the property is put to its *highest and best use* (e.g., a 2-storey building on a plot zoned for 4 storeys).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all formula-grade relationships a KTU student is expected to memorize verbatim for Module 4.**

| **#** | **Relationship / Formula** | **Symbolic Form** | **Standard Units / Notes** |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Capitalized Value from Net Income | $\text{CV} = \text{NI} \times \text{YP}$ | CV in ₹ ; NI in ₹/year ; YP dimensionless |
| 2 | Year's Purchase formula | $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r}$ where $r$ is the rate of interest/return | $r$ expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.08 for 8%) |
| 3 | Net Income from Gross Income | $\text{NI} = \text{GI} - \text{Outgoings}$ | All terms in ₹/year |
| 4 | Gross Income (Rental Method) | $\text{GI} = \text{Monthly Rent} \times 12$ | For single-tenant property |
| 5 | Gross Income (multi-tenant) | $\text{GI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \times 12$ | $R_i$ = monthly rent of $i^{\text{th}}$ unit ; $n$ = number of units |
| 6 | Gross Income with Vacancy | $\text{EGI} = \text{PGI} \times (1 - v)$ | PGI = Potential Gross Income ; EGI = Effective Gross Income ; $v$ = vacancy fraction |
| 7 | Book Value | $\text{BV} = \text{Original Cost} - \text{Accumulated Depreciation}$ | BV in ₹ |
| 8 | Salvage Value (KTU thumb rule) | $\text{SV} = 0.10 \times \text{Cost of Construction}$ | For buildings ; 10% is normative |
| 9 | Distress Value (typical range) | $\text{DV} = 0.70 \cdot \text{MV} \;\text{to}\; 0.90 \cdot \text{MV}$ | KTU commonly assumes 75% to 80% |
| 10 | Replacement Cost (Modern Equivalent) | $\text{RC} = \text{Plinth Area} \times \text{Current Rate} \times \text{Quality Factor}$ | All in ₹ ; quality factor adjusts for construction standard |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Market Value** drives every *sale-purchase* transaction, *bank loan* disbursement (typically 70%–80% of MV is lent), and *insurance* valuation.
- **Capitalized Value** is the cornerstone of *investment* decisions by REITs, NBFCs, and institutional buyers.
- **Salvage and Scrap Values** are critical inputs for *depreciation calculation* (Straight Line, Declining Balance, Sinking Fund).
- **Gross vs. Net Income** distinction mirrors the **P&L (Profit and Loss) accounting framework**, making it indispensable for *real estate equity research* and *rental yield* analytics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship between Capitalized Value, Net Income, and Year's Purchase

The Income Method of valuation rests on a single foundational premise: **a property is worth the present value of the perpetual (or finite) income stream it can generate.** We derive this formally.

**Assumption:** The property yields a *constant annual net income* of ₹NI, and the owner expects a *rate of return* of $r$ (per annum) on the capital invested.

**Step 1:** Consider the buyer paying a sum $C$ today. The annual return earned on $C$ at rate $r$ is:
$$\text{Annual Return} = C \times r$$

**Step 2:** For the investment to be "fair," this annual return must equal the net income the buyer expects from the property:
$$C \times r = \text{NI}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $C$:
$$C = \frac{\text{NI}}{r}$$

**Step 4:** Define *Year's Purchase (YP)* as the number of years' purchase of net income that a property is worth, i.e., $\text{YP} = \dfrac{1}{r}$:
$$\boxed{\text{Capitalized Value} = \text{NI} \times \text{YP}}$$

**Step 5 (Verification by worked example):** If NI = ₹1,00,000/year and $r = 8\% = 0.08$:
$$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \;\text{years}$$
$$\text{CV} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 12.5 = \text{₹}12{,}50{,}000$$

This is the **final value** the KTU board expects when the income method is applied.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Gross Income to Net Income (Itemized Deduction Sequence)

The KTU valuation textbook (Standard: Dutta's *Estimating and Costing*, plus M. Chakraborty's *Practical Valuation*) prescribes a **standardized order of deductions** from Gross Income. A student must apply deductions *in the order below* to avoid losing marks.

**Start with:** $\text{GI} = \text{Gross Income (Total rent receivable)}$

**Step 1:** Deduct **Property / Municipal Tax** ($T_{\text{tax}}$).
$$\text{Income after Tax} = \text{GI} - T_{\text{tax}}$$

**Step 2:** Deduct **Insurance Premium** ($I_{\text{ins}}$), typically 0.5% to 1% of construction cost annually.
$$\text{Income after Insurance} = \text{GI} - T_{\text{tax}} - I_{\text{ins}}$$

**Step 3:** Deduct **Repairs and Maintenance** ($R_{\text{m\&r}}$), typically 5% to 10% of Gross Income for residential; 10% to 15% for commercial.
$$\text{Income after Repairs} = \text{GI} - T_{\text{tax}} - I_{\text{ins}} - R_{\text{m\&r}}$$

**Step 4:** Deduct **Management and Collection Charges** ($M_{\text{mgmt}}$), typically 5% to 10% of Gross Income.
$$\text{Income after Management} = \text{GI} - T_{\text{tax}} - I_{\text{ins}} - R_{\text{m\&r}} - M_{\text{mgmt}}$$

**Step 5:** Deduct **Sinking Fund Contribution** ($S_{\text{sf}}$) — provided the building has a finite life; calculated as:
$$S_{\text{sf}} = \frac{\text{Cost of Building} \times i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$
where $i$ = rate of interest on sinking fund, and $n$ = remaining life of the building in years.

**Step 6:** The remainder is **Net Income (NI)**:
$$\boxed{\text{NI} = \text{GI} - T_{\text{tax}} - I_{\text{ins}} - R_{\text{m\&r}} - M_{\text{mgmt}} - S_{\text{sf}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Order of deduction matters for the board valuation key.** Always present deductions in the sequence: (i) Tax → (ii) Insurance → (iii) Repairs → (iv) Management → (v) Sinking Fund. Reversing this order does not change the final NI, but examiners often grade on *methodical presentation*, and a wrong order is a frequent mark-deducting error.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Kind of Value

The KTU examiner expects students to **identify the correct kind of value** for a given scenario. The matrix below is a high-yield, exam-oriented summary.

| **Scenario in Question** | **Kind of Value to Compute** | **Why** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| "Find the value for sale-purchase purposes" | **Market Value** | Willing-buyer-willing-seller assumption applies |
| "Owner is in financial trouble, needs urgent cash" | **Distress Value** | Forced-sale clause applies |
| "Building is 30 years old; account it as an asset" | **Book Value** | Depreciated ledger value is required |
| "Estimate the value at the end of useful life" | **Salvage Value** (if sold as unit) or **Scrap Value** (if dismantled) | End-of-life context |
| "Find the lump-sum value of a rental property" | **Capitalized Value** | Income method is mandated |
| "Government is acquiring the land for a highway" | **Compulsory Acquisition Value** | Statutory acquisition context |
| "What is the property tax based on?" | **Assessed Value** | Local authority's recorded value |
| "Valuation for a company's annual financial statements" | **Fair Value (Ind AS 113)** | Accounting standard requirement |
| "Highest value possible if property is developed to its best use" | **Potential Value** | Highest-and-best-use principle |
| "Family valuing ancestral home for inheritance" | **Market Value** (Sentimental Value is excluded professionally) | Inheritance tax requires MV, not personal sentiment |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Distinguishing Gross Income from Net Income

> **Problem:** A residential building in Ernakulam yields a monthly rent of **₹25,000**. Annual municipal tax is **₹15,000**. Repairs and maintenance cost is **10% of GI**. Insurance is **₹6,000/year**. Management is **5% of GI**. Compute (i) Gross Income, (ii) Net Income, and (iii) Capitalized Value at **8%** rate of return.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Gross Income.**
$$\text{GI} = 25{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000 \;\text{per year}$$

**Step 2: Apply deductions (in standard KTU order).**

Deduction A — Municipal Tax:
$$T_{\text{tax}} = \text{₹}15{,}000$$

Deduction B — Insurance:
$$I_{\text{ins}} = \text{₹}6{,}000$$

Deduction C — Repairs and Maintenance (10% of GI):
$$R_{\text{m\&r}} = 0.10 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}30{,}000$$

Deduction D — Management (5% of GI):
$$M_{\text{mgmt}} = 0.05 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}15{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compute Net Income.**
$$\text{NI} = 3{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}000 - 6{,}000 - 30{,}000 - 15{,}000$$
$$\text{NI} = \text{₹}2{,}34{,}000 \;\text{per year}$$

**Step 4: Compute Year's Purchase at 8%.**
$$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$

**Step 5: Compute Capitalized Value.**
$$\text{CV} = 2{,}34{,}000 \times 12.5 = \text{₹}29{,}25{,}000$$

**Final Answer:** GI = ₹3,00,000 ; NI = ₹2,34,000 ; CV = **₹29,25,000**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Board Pattern):** Many students apply the percentage deductions (Repairs 10%, Management 5%) on *Net Income* or on the *tax-already-deducted figure*. The KTU prescribed practice (and the standard textbook method) is to apply these percentages on **Gross Income itself** (the "base" value), not on the diminishing balance. Losing 1 mark for this in 14-mark problems is a near-universal board phenomenon.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Kinds of Values

The following Mermaid block renders the *hierarchical taxonomy* of valuation concepts. This diagram alone is a high-yield visual summary for the 3-mark direct-definition questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Kinds of Values] --> rootB[Time and Lifecycle Based]
    rootA --> rootC[Transaction Context Based]
    rootA --> rootD[Income Method Based]
    rootA --> rootE[Statutory and Specialized]

    rootB --> n01[Original Cost]
    rootB --> n02[Book Value]
    rootB --> n03[Market Value]
    rootB --> n04[Replacement Value]
    rootB --> n05[Salvage Value]
    rootB --> n06[Scrap Value]

    rootC --> n11[Distress Value]
    rootC --> n12[Compulsory Acquisition Value]
    rootC --> n13[Speculative Value]
    rootC --> n14[Sentimental Value]

    rootD --> n21[Capitalized Value]
    rootD --> n22[Net Present Value]

    rootE --> n31[Assessed Value]
    rootE --> n32[Fair Value]
    rootE --> n33[Going Concern Value]
    rootE --> n34[Potential Value]
```

### 4.2 Cash-Flow Deduction Schematic — From Gross to Net Income

The following diagram visualizes the **sequential deduction waterfall** that transforms Gross Income into Net Income. The order of arrows follows the KTU-prescribed sequence strictly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[GI: Gross Income] --> opA[Less: Municipal Tax]
    opA --> opB[Less: Insurance Premium]
    opB --> opC[Less: Repairs and Maintenance]
    opC --> opD[Less: Management Charges]
    opD --> opE[Less: Sinking Fund]
    opE --> outA[NI: Net Income]
    outA --> finA[Multiply by YP equals 1 divided by r]
    finA --> finB[CV: Capitalized Value]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct "Kind of Value"

Since "value selection" is fundamentally a *decision-making process* for a professional valuer, the following block-flow architecture maps how the choice is made in practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Start: Property Valuation Request] --> qB{What is the Purpose of Valuation}
    qB --> qC1[Purpose: Sale or Purchase]
    qB --> qC2[Purpose: Loan or Mortgage]
    qB --> qC3[Purpose: Insurance]
    qB --> qC4[Purpose: Rental Yield Investment]
    qB --> qC5[Purpose: Government Acquisition]
    qB --> qC6[Purpose: Accounting or Balance Sheet]

    qC1 --> rA1[Apply Market Value Method]
    qC2 --> rA2[Apply Market Value Method - 70 to 80 percent Loan Limit]
    qC3 --> rA3[Apply Replacement Cost minus Depreciation]
    qC4 --> rA4[Apply Capitalized Value from Net Income]
    qC5 --> rA5[Apply Statutory Acquisition Rules plus Solatium]
    qC6 --> rA6[Apply Fair Value or Book Value per Ind AS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every node label above is intentionally written in plain uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold/italics inside Mermaid labels) and uses purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (`n01`, `opA`, `qA`) to comply with Mermaid v10+ rendering safeguards and to prevent compilation errors during exam-time preview or PDF export.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2-Mark and 3-Mark Type)

> Each model answer is written to the **exact length expected by a KTU board examiner** for sub-parts of 2 to 3 marks.

#### Question 1. (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between Salvage Value and Scrap Value of a building.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Salvage Value** | **Scrap Value** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Value of a building when sold as a whole at the end of its useful life, without dismantling | Value of a dismantled building, recovered from sale of individual materials (bricks, steel, timber) |
| Magnitude | Higher (typically **10%** of the construction cost per KTU thumb rule) | Lower (less than Salvage Value) |
| Dismantling cost | Not deducted | Often deducted from gross material recovery |
| When applied | When the building as a whole retains some utility | When the building has no remaining structural / functional life |

*(3 marks — full credit is awarded for the contrast table; partial credit for 2 or more distinguishing points.)*

---

#### Question 2. (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define Gross Income. How is it different from Net Income in property valuation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Gross Income (G.I.):** The total annual monetary return receivable by the owner from a property, calculated *before* any deduction for expenses, taxes, or outgoings. In the rental method, it is simply the **monthly rent × 12**.

**Net Income (N.I.):** The actual *take-home* annual income after deducting from Gross Income: (i) municipal tax, (ii) insurance, (iii) repairs and maintenance, (iv) management and collection charges, and (v) sinking fund contribution.

**Key Difference:** Gross Income is the *gross inflow*; Net Income is the *surplus available for capitalization*. The relationship is:
$$\text{NI} = \text{GI} - \Sigma \text{Outgoings}$$

Net Income is then multiplied by the **Year's Purchase (YP)** to obtain the **Capitalized Value (CV)** of the property.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer / Numerical Questions (14-Mark Type)

> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, Module-level long-answer questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice**. Below are two complete, independent alternative questions — **Question A** and **Question B** — each fully solved.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **kinds of values** used in property valuation, with at least one practical example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A commercial building in Kozhikode has a constructed plinth area of **200 m²**. The construction rate is **₹2,500/m²**. The owner expects a **net return of 9%** per annum. The municipal tax is **12%** of the gross income, repairs and maintenance is **8% of gross income**, sinking fund contribution is **3% of gross income**, and management charges are **5% of gross income**. Compute the **Gross Rent** required from the building to yield the desired net return. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Solution — Kinds of Values (7 Marks):**

The valuer's vocabulary of "kinds of values" can be grouped into four functional families:

**1. Lifecycle-based values** *(2 marks)*:
- *Original Cost* — Actual historical acquisition price including stamp duty and registration.
- *Book Value* — Original cost minus accumulated depreciation as per owner's books.
- *Market Value* — The price a willing buyer would pay a willing seller, in an open market, on the valuation date. **Example:** A 3-BHK flat in Kakkanad currently quoted at ₹65 lakhs based on comparable recent sales in the same apartment complex.
- *Replacement Value* — Cost to build an equivalent modern building at current rates. **Example:** Rebuilding the same 3-BHK flat in 2024 at current construction rates of ₹2,500/ft² may cost ₹75 lakhs.
- *Salvage Value* — Sale value at the end of useful life as a whole unit. **Example:** A 50-year-old building sold in toto to a developer for ₹30 lakhs.
- *Scrap Value* — Sale value after dismantling. **Example:** Recovered steel, bricks, and timber from the same 50-year-old building yield ₹12 lakhs after dismantling cost.

**2. Context-based values** *(2 marks)*:
- *Distress Value* — Forced sale value. **Example:** Property auctioned by a bank for loan default at 75% of market value.
- *Compulsory Acquisition Value* — Paid by government. **Example:** Land acquired for NH-66 widening in Kannur, with statutory solatium of 30% added.
- *Speculative Value* — Future-anticipated value. **Example:** Agricultural land near a proposed metro station.
- *Sentimental Value* — Purely personal; excluded from professional valuation.

**3. Income-method values** *(1.5 marks)*:
- *Capitalized Value* — Lump-sum present worth of future net income. **Example:** A shop yielding ₹5,00,000/year NI at 8% YP = 12.5 → CV = ₹62,50,000.
- *Net Present Value* — Used in development projects with time-bounded cash flows.

**4. Statutory / Specialized values** *(1.5 marks)*:
- *Assessed Value* — For property tax. **Example:** Local Panchayat fixes ₹18 lakhs as taxable base.
- *Fair Value (Ind AS 113)* — For financial statements.
- *Going Concern Value* — For operating businesses.
- *Potential Value* — For highest-and-best-use scenarios.

---

**(b) Solution — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Plinth area $A = 200 \;\text{m}^{2}$
- Construction rate $C = \text{₹}2{,}500/\text{m}^{2}$
- Net return required $r = 9\% = 0.09$
- Outgoings: Tax = 12% of GI, Repairs = 8% of GI, Sinking Fund = 3% of GI, Management = 5% of GI

**Step 1: Total Cost of Construction.**
$$\text{Total Cost} = A \times C = 200 \times 2{,}500 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Net Income Required.**
By the income method, Capitalized Value equals Total Cost (assuming a perpetual income flow), and CV = NI × YP. Thus:
$$\text{NI Required} = \text{Total Cost} \times r = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09 = \text{₹}45{,}000 \;\text{per year}$$

**Step 3: Express Net Income in terms of Gross Income.**
Let GI = $G$ (the unknown we need to compute).
$$\text{Total outgoings} = (12\% + 8\% + 3\% + 5\%) \times G = 28\% \cdot G = 0.28 \cdot G$$
$$\text{NI} = G - 0.28 \cdot G = 0.72 \cdot G$$

**Step 4: Equate to Required NI and Solve for $G$.**
$$0.72 \cdot G = 45{,}000$$
$$G = \frac{45{,}000}{0.72}$$
$$\boxed{G = \text{₹}62{,}500 \;\text{per year (Gross Income)}}$$

**Step 5: Verification (Optional but highly recommended for KTU papers).**
- Outgoings = $0.28 \times 62{,}500 = \text{₹}17{,}500$
- Net Income = $62{,}500 - 17{,}500 = \text{₹}45{,}000$ ✓
- Year's Purchase = $1/0.09 = 11.11$
- Capitalized Value = $45{,}000 \times 11.11 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$ ✓ (matches Total Cost)

**Final Answer:** The owner must charge a **Gross Rent of ₹62,500 per annum** (i.e., approximately **₹5,208 per month**) from the building to achieve the desired **9% net return** on construction cost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Income Approach to Valuation** in detail. Explain the role of **Gross Income** in this method, with the standard sequence of deductions used to arrive at the **Net Income**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A property yields a **Gross Income of ₹4,80,000 per annum**. The annual outgoings are: municipal tax = ₹36,000, insurance = ₹8,000, repairs = 8% of GI, management = 6% of GI, and the owner sets aside a sinking fund to recover ₹5,00,000 at 6% interest over 25 years. Calculate the **Net Income** and the **Capitalized Value** at a **rate of return of 8%**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Solution — Income Approach to Valuation (7 Marks):**

**1. Concept (1.5 marks):** The Income Approach, also called the **Rental Method** or **Investment Method**, values a property by capitalizing the *future income stream* it is reasonably expected to generate. It is the *most appropriate* method for income-producing properties — rental flats, shops, offices, hotels, and commercial complexes.

**2. The central premise (1.5 marks):** A property's value equals the present worth of the *perpetual (or finite) net income* it can produce. Formally:
$$\text{CV} = \text{NI} \times \text{YP} \quad \text{where} \quad \text{YP} = \frac{1}{r}$$

**3. Role of Gross Income (2 marks):** Gross Income is the **starting point** of the income method. It is the *aggregate annual rent* the property can command, **before any deduction**. In residential properties: $\text{GI} = \text{monthly rent} \times 12$. In mixed-use / multi-tenant: $\text{GI} = \sum R_i \times 12$. A *Potential Gross Income (PGI)* figure may be adjusted for expected vacancy to give an *Effective Gross Income (EGI)*.

**4. Standard Sequence of Deductions (2 marks):** From Gross Income, the deductions *must* be applied in the order:

1. **Municipal / Property Tax** — statutory outgoing.
2. **Insurance Premium** — risk mitigation cost.
3. **Repairs and Maintenance** — usually 5% to 10% of GI for residential, up to 15% for commercial.
4. **Management and Collection Charges** — usually 5% to 10% of GI.
5. **Sinking Fund Contribution** — accumulates over the building's life to enable replacement:
$$S_{\text{sf}} = \frac{\text{Original Cost} \times i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

The remainder is **Net Income**, which when multiplied by YP gives the **Capitalized Value**.

---

**(b) Solution — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\text{GI} = \text{₹}4{,}80{,}000$ per year
- Municipal Tax = ₹36,000
- Insurance = ₹8,000
- Repairs = 8% of GI
- Management = 6% of GI
- Sinking Fund: target = ₹5,00,000, $i = 6\% = 0.06$, $n = 25$ years
- Rate of return $r = 8\% = 0.08$

**Step 1: Compute percentage-based deductions.**
- Repairs: $0.08 \times 4{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}38{,}400$
- Management: $0.06 \times 4{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}28{,}800$

**Step 2: Compute the Sinking Fund annual contribution.**
$$S_{\text{sf}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06}{(1+0.06)^{25} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.06)^{25}$:
$$(1.06)^{25} \approx 4.2919$$
$$(1.06)^{25} - 1 \approx 3.2919$$
$$S_{\text{sf}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06}{3.2919} = \frac{30{,}000}{3.2919} \approx \text{₹}9{,}112$$

**Step 3: Total Outgoings.**
$$\Sigma \text{Outgoings} = 36{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 38{,}400 + 28{,}800 + 9{,}112$$
$$\Sigma \text{Outgoings} = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}312$$

**Step 4: Net Income.**
$$\text{NI} = \text{GI} - \Sigma \text{Outgoings} = 4{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}312$$
$$\boxed{\text{NI} = \text{₹}3{,}59{,}688 \;\text{per year}}$$

**Step 5: Year's Purchase at 8%.**
$$\text{YP} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$

**Step 6: Capitalized Value.**
$$\text{CV} = \text{NI} \times \text{YP} = 3{,}59{,}688 \times 12.5$$
$$\boxed{\text{CV} = \text{₹}44{,}96{,}100}$$

**Final Answer:** Net Income = **₹3,59,688 per year** ; Capitalized Value = **₹44,96,100**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4 Specific):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Gross Income with Net Income:** The single most frequent mark-loss area. *Always* state the Gross Income explicitly first, *then* apply deductions. Presenting the Net Income alone (without showing the Gross Income and the deduction waterfall) costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Sinking Fund:** Students often compute the Sinking Fund contribution as a flat percentage (e.g., "assume 5%") rather than using the formula $\frac{C \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$. The KTU board valuation key explicitly tests the *sinking fund formula* in 14-mark problems.
> 3. **Applying Repairs/Management % on wrong base:** Use Gross Income, *not* Net Income, as the base for these percentage deductions.
> 4. **Mixing up Capitalized Value with Market Value:** Capitalized Value is the *output* of the income method; it may not always equal the Market Value. KTU problems that say "find the value of the property" without specifying the method often expect the student to state that CV has been computed using the rental method.
> 5. **Wrong Sinking Fund formula for finite life:** If the building has a *finite* remaining life, you must use the Sinking Fund formula. If *perpetual*, the Sinking Fund is *zero*. Always read the problem carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Value is purpose-driven.** The *same* property carries *multiple* values simultaneously.
- [x] **Market Value** is the *default* answer unless the question specifies otherwise (distress, salvage, scrap, etc.).
- [x] **Book Value ≠ Market Value.** Book Value is accounting-based; MV is market-based.
- [x] **Salvage Value** is the end-of-life value *as a whole unit*; **Scrap Value** is the end-of-life value *after dismantling*. Salvage ≥ Scrap.
- [x] **Distress Value** is always *less than* Market Value (typically 70% to 90% of MV).
- [x] **Sentimental Value** is *excluded* from professional valuation reports.
- [x] **Gross Income** = Total rent receivable per annum = Monthly Rent × 12, *before* any deduction.
- [x] **Net Income** = Gross Income − (Tax + Insurance + Repairs + Management + Sinking Fund).
- [x] **Order of deductions is fixed** — Tax → Insurance → Repairs → Management → Sinking Fund.
- [x] **Capitalized Value** = NI × YP, where YP = $\dfrac{1}{r}$ and $r$ is the *rate of return* (not the *rate of interest on sinking fund*, which is a *different* $i$).
- [x] **Sinking Fund formula** is $\dfrac{C \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$, *not* a flat percentage of cost.
- [x] **Percentage-based deductions** (Repairs, Management) are applied on **Gross Income**, not on Net Income.
- [x] **The Income Method** is the *preferred* method for income-producing properties (rentals, commercial).
- [x] **The choice of "kind of value"** is dictated by the *purpose* of the valuation (sale, loan, insurance, acquisition, accounting).
- [x] **For KTU board exam problems**, always show *every intermediate step*; the valuation key awards marks for method, not just the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
