(7 marks) --- #### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A **Given Data:** - Future replacement cost $F = ₹4,00,000$ - Number of years $n = 8$ - Interest rate $i = 9\% = 0.09$ per annum --- #### Part (a) Solution — Sinking Fund Deposit **Step 1: Identify the operation.** We are given $F$ and need to find $A$ — this is a **Sinking Fund** problem. **Step 2: Recall the formula.** $$ A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} $$ **Step 3: Compute $(1+i)^n$.** $$ (1.09)^{8} = 1.99256 \text{ (approximately)} $$ **Step 4: Compute the denominator.** $$ (1.09)^{8} - 1 = 1.99256 - 1 = 0.99256 $$ **Step 5: Compute the Sinking Fund factor.** $$ A/F = \frac{0.09}{0.99256} = 0.09067 $$ **Step 6: Compute the annual deposit $A$.** $$ A = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09067 = ₹36{,}268 \text{ (rounded)} $$ > ✅ **Answer (a): The required annual Sinking Fund deposit is ₹36,268 per year.** --- #### Part (b) Solution — Capital Recovery Instalment **Step 1: Identify the operation.** The company borrows the full ₹4,00,000 today at 9% and repays in equal annual instalments over 8 years — this is a **Capital Recovery** problem. **Step 2: Recall the formula.** $$ A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} $$ **Step 3: Compute the Capital Recovery factor.** $$ A/P = \frac{0.09 \times 1.99256}{0.99256} $$ **Step 4: Numerator:** $$ 0.09 \times 1.99256 = 0.17933 $$ **Step 5: Divide by denominator:** $$ A/P = \frac{0.17933}{0.99256} = 0.18067 $$ **Step 6: Compute the annual instalment.** $$ A = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.18067 = ₹72{,}268 \text{ (rounded)} $$ **Step 7: Comparison and Comment.** - **Total paid under Sinking Fund:** $8 \times 36{,}268 = ₹2,90,144$ (but this is *additionally* the interest earned, so net out-of-pocket = $2,90,144$, and the company still pays the replacement cost from operations at year 8 — net cost = $4,00,000 + 2,90,144 - \text{interest earned}$). - **Total paid under Capital Recovery:** $8 \times 72{,}268 = ₹5,78,144$ (this *includes* both principal and 9% interest). A simpler comparison: the *extra* annual out-of-pocket under Capital Recovery (₹72,268 − ₹36,268 = ₹36,000) is the interest cost of the loan. > ✅ **Answer (b):** The annual instalment under Capital Recovery is **₹72,268**. **The Sinking Fund option is significantly more economical** because the company earns 9% interest on its own deposits, rather than *paying* 9% interest to a lender. The Sinking Fund saves approximately ₹36,000 per year in financing cost. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern — Alternative] **A piece of agricultural land yields a net annual rental income of ₹75,000 for the next 20 years. The expected rate of return in the market is 8% per annum.** #### (a) Calculate the present value (capitalized purchase price) of this finite-term income stream. (7 marks) #### (b) If the land were to yield the same rental income **perpetually** (i.e., a perpetuity), what would be the capitalized value? Compare both results and state which is more realistic for actual property valuation. (7 marks) --- #### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B **Given Data:** - Annual rental income $A = ₹75,000$ - Number of years $n = 20$ - Interest rate $i = 8\% = 0.08$ --- #### Part (a) Solution — Present Worth of Ordinary Annuity (20 years) **Step 1: Identify the operation.** Given $A$, find $P$ for $n = 20$ years — this is a **Present Worth of an Ordinary Annuity** problem. **Step 2: Recall the formula.** $$ P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} $$ **Step 3: Compute $(1+i)^n$.** $$ (1.08)^{20} = 4.66096 \text{ (approximately)} $$ **Step 4: Compute the numerator and denominator of the bracket.** - Numerator: $(1.08)^{20} - 1 = 4.66096 - 1 = 3.66096$ - Denominator: $0.08 \times 4.66096 = 0.37288$ **Step 5: Compute the Present Worth factor (P/A factor).** $$ P/A = \frac{3.66096}{0.37288} = 9.8181 $$ **Step 6: Compute the capitalized purchase price $P$.** $$ P = 75{,}000 \times 9.8181 = ₹7{,}36{,}361 \text{ (rounded)} $$ > ✅ **Answer (a): The present value of the 20-year income stream is ₹7,36,361.** --- #### Part (b) Solution — Perpetuity Capitalized Value **Step 1: Use the perpetuity formula.** $$ P_{\text{perpetuity}} = \frac{A}{i} = \frac{75{,}000}{0.08} $$ **Step 2: Compute.** $$ P_{\text{perpetuity}} = ₹9{,}37{,}500 $$ **Step 3: Comparison Table.** | Assumption | Formula | Capitalized Value | |---|---|---| | Finite 20-year term | $P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | ₹7,36,361 | | Perpetual (infinite term) | $P = A / i$ | ₹9,37,500 | | **Difference** | — | **₹2,01,139 (≈ 27% more)** | **Step 4: Engineering / Valuation Commentary.** - The **finite-term** valuation (₹7,36,361) is more *conservative* and is typically used when the lease is for a defined period (e.g., a 99-year government lease, mining lease for 20 years). - The **perpetuity** valuation (₹9,37,500) assumes the land yields income *forever*, which is closer to freehold ownership in reality (the land doesn't disappear). - In practice, **perpetuity is often used for freehold land** and **finite-annuity is used for leasehold interests** or properties with a definite economic life. > ✅ **Answer (b):** The perpetuity capitalized value is **₹9,37,500**, which is **₹2,01,139 higher** than the finite 20-year value. The perpetuity method is more realistic for **freehold land**, while the finite annuity method is preferred for **leasehold interests** with a defined tenure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print / screenshot this checklist before every KTU ESE revision session.** - [x] **Annuity =** A series of *equal* payments at *regular* intervals. - [x] **Ordinary Annuity:** Payments at the *end* of each period (most common in KTU problems). - [x] **Annuity Due:** Payments at the *beginning* of each period; multiply ordinary result by $(1+i)$. - [x] **Perpetuity:** Annuity with $n \to \infty$; capitalized value $P = A / i$. - [x] **Future Worth (FW) of Ordinary Annuity:** $F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ - [x] **Present Worth (PW) of Ordinary Annuity:** $P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ - [x] **Sinking Fund (SF) — find A from F:** $A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ - [x] **Capital Recovery (CR) — find A from P:** $A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ - [x] **Always convert % rate to decimal** before substitution ($10\% = 0.10$). - [x] **Always match units of $i$ and $n$** — annual rate with annual periods, monthly rate with monthly periods. - [x] **Sinking Fund =** building money for a *future* need (e.g., machine replacement). - [x] **Capital Recovery =** repaying money *borrowed today* (e.g., loan EMI). - [x] **Capitalized Value** in property valuation = present worth of income; use $P = A/i$ for perpetuity. - [x] **The three common KTU question patterns** are: (i) Compute $P$ or $F$ from $A$; (ii) Compute $A$ from $F$ (Sinking Fund) or $P$ (Capital Recovery); (iii) Compute Capitalized Value using perpetuity formula. - [x] **Memory aid:** FIAF — *FInd A's Friend* — the four core factors share the bracket $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i \cdot (\text{something})}$. > 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When in doubt about which formula to use, draw the **cash-flow timeline** first (mark present time $t=0$, each payment $A$, the final lump sum $F$ or loan $P$). The visual will instantly tell you whether to *compound forward* (FW / Sinking Fund) or *discount backward* (PW / Capital Recovery).

topics.title: Sinking Fund
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sinking Fund

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SINKING FUND

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sinking Fund** is a reserve fund created by an asset owner (usually a property owner) by depositing a **fixed, uniform annual installment** into an interest-bearing account, such that the **accumulated sum of all installments plus the compound interest earned** equals the **original cost of replacement** (or reconstruction) of the asset at the end of its useful life.

In formal valuation terminology (as adopted in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**), a Sinking Fund is the periodic (most commonly **annual**) monetary contribution that an owner voluntarily sets aside to ensure that when an asset — such as a building, bridge, or major piece of machinery — reaches the end of its service life, a ready pool of capital exists to fund its replacement **without financial shock**.

| Term | Standard Notation (KTU) |
|---|---|
| Total Sinking Fund Amount | $S$ (in ₹) |
| Annual Sinking Fund Installment | $A$ or $x$ (in ₹/year) |
| Rate of Interest per annum | $i$ (as a decimal) |
| Useful Life of Asset | $n$ (in years) |

### Real-World Analogy — The "School Bus Replacement Jar"

Imagine you run a school and use one bus every day. Buses last about **10 years** before they must be replaced at a cost of **₹15,00,000**. Instead of panicking in Year 10, on Day 1 you start a "Bus Replacement Jar" and put a small, fixed amount into it **every year**. The bank pays you interest on the jar's balance.

By the time Year 10 arrives, all those small yearly deposits + the compound interest magically total **₹15,00,000** — exactly what is needed for the new bus. That growing jar is the **Sinking Fund**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Sinking"?** The word *sinking* refers to the gradual "draining" of the owner's annual income into the fund (like water sinking into soil), accumulating steadily over time. It is **not** a depreciation charge itself; it is the *fund* used to *replace* the asset after depreciation has rendered it unserviceable.

---

## 1.2 Purpose & Engineering Utility

- **Smoothens financial burden:** Replaces one large lump-sum expense with many small, predictable annual outflows.
- **Matches the depreciation cycle:** Links the *physical deterioration* of an asset to the *financial preparation* for its replacement.
- **Used in long-life infrastructure valuation:** Dams, railway bridges, public buildings, and industrial sheds (typical KTU valuation case studies).
- **Foundation for the Sinking Fund Method of Depreciation:** One of the three classical methods used in quantity surveying — the others being the *Straight Line Method* and the *Constant Percentage Method*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

A sinking fund is built through a **uniform annual series (annuity)** that is deposited at the **end of each year** and earns **compound interest** at rate $i$ per annum.

**Step 1 — Identify the target amount (S):**  
This is the *future value* required at the end of year $n$. It is the **original cost of replacement** of the asset.

**Step 2 — Determine the time horizon (n):**  
This is the *useful life* of the asset — the period after which the asset must be replaced.

**Step 3 — Determine the interest rate (i):**  
This is the *safe, conservative rate* at which the fund will grow (typically a government-bond or fixed-deposit rate).

**Step 4 — Compute the equal annual installment (A):**  
A single value, deposited at the end of each year, that accumulates (with interest) to exactly $S$ in $n$ years.

**Step 5 — Deposit & Compound:**  
Each installment is left in the fund. The fund balance grows as $F_{k} = F_{k-1}(1+i) + A$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They appear in nearly every KTU valuation question on Module 4.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Annual Sinking Fund Installment** | $A = \dfrac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | $A$ = annual deposit, $S$ = target fund, $i$ = interest rate, $n$ = years |
| 2 | **Accumulated Fund (Future Value of Annuity)** | $S = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | Same as above (inverse of #1) |
| 3 | **Total Money Deposited over n years** | $T_{d} = A \cdot n$ | $T_{d}$ = total out-of-pocket deposits |
| 4 | **Total Interest Earned on the Fund** | $I_{total} = S - A \cdot n$ | Compound interest accumulated |
| 5 | **Annual Depreciation (Sinking Fund Method)** | $D_{annual} = A$ | Equal to the annual installment |
| 6 | **Total Sinking Fund at end of k-th year** | $S_{k} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{k} - 1}{i}$ | Partial accumulation after $k$ years |

### Units & Conventions
- **All monetary values in Indian Rupees (₹)** unless otherwise stated.
- **Rate of interest $i$ must be a decimal** (e.g., 6% → $i = 0.06$).
- **$n$ is in years** (whole or fractional).
- **Annuity assumption:** End-of-year deposit (ordinary annuity). This is the *default* in KTU problems unless "beginning of year" is specified.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PWD & Government Valuation:** Roads, bridges, and public buildings are valued using the sinking fund method because the government borrows at known long-term rates.
- **Power & Utility Sector:** Power plants depreciate over 25–40 years; the sinking fund provides for replacement without raising consumer tariffs abruptly.
- **Railways:** Locomotives and coaches have a fixed service life; railways use sinking funds to ensure fleet renewal.
- **Real Estate Development:** High-rise residential buildings use sinking funds (managed by Resident Welfare Associations) to replace lifts, water pumps, and waterproofing on schedule.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Sinking Fund Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that $A = \dfrac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ from first principles using the **geometric progression of accumulated deposits**.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $A$ = uniform annual deposit (made at the end of each year)
- $i$ = rate of interest per annum (decimal)
- $n$ = number of years the fund is built
- $S$ = accumulated amount at the end of year $n$ (target)

### 3.1.2 Growth of Each Installment

Each deposit earns compound interest for the remaining years until year $n$.

| Installment Number | Year Deposited | Years to Earn Interest | Value at End of Year $n$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st (last to be deposited) | End of Year 1 | $(n-1)$ | $A \cdot (1+i)^{n-1}$ |
| 2nd | End of Year 2 | $(n-2)$ | $A \cdot (1+i)^{n-2}$ |
| 3rd | End of Year 3 | $(n-3)$ | $A \cdot (1+i)^{n-3}$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $(n-1)$th | End of Year $(n-1)$ | $1$ | $A \cdot (1+i)^{1}$ |
| $n$th (most recent) | End of Year $n$ | $0$ | $A \cdot (1+i)^{0} = A$ |

### 3.1.3 Summation

Adding the values from all installments:

$$S = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + A(1+i)^{n-3} + \dots + A(1+i)^{1} + A$$

This is a **Geometric Progression (G.P.)** with:
- First term: $a = A$
- Common ratio: $r = (1+i)$
- Number of terms: $n$

### 3.1.4 Applying the G.P. Sum Formula

For a G.P., the sum of $n$ terms is $\displaystyle S_{n} = a \cdot \frac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1}$.

Substituting our values:

$$S = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

Since $(1+i) - 1 = i$:

$$S = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for $A$

Rearranging for $A$ (multiply both sides by $i$, divide by the bracket):

$$A \cdot \bigl[(1+i)^{n} - 1\bigr] = S \cdot i$$

$$\boxed{A = \frac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}}$$

This is the **Annual Sinking Fund Installment** formula — required in every KTU problem on this topic. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Model Problem:** *A building has an estimated life of 40 years. Its present reconstruction cost is ₹8,00,000. Calculate the annual sinking fund installment required to replace the building at the end of 40 years, assuming interest is earned at **6% per annum** compounded annually. Also, find the total interest earned by the fund.*

### Given Data
- $S = 8{,}00{,}000$
- $n = 40$ years
- $i = 0.06$

### Step 1 — Compute $(1+i)^{n}$

We need $(1.06)^{40}$.

Using the standard compound-interest expansion (or a calculator):

$$(1.06)^{10} = 1.79085$$
$$(1.06)^{20} = (1.79085)^{2} = 3.20714$$
$$(1.06)^{40} = (3.20714)^{2} = 10.28572$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$A = \frac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06}{10.28572 - 1}$$

$$A = \frac{48{,}000}{9.28572}$$

$$A \approx 5{,}169.27 \text{ per annum}$$

### Step 3 — Total Money Deposited

$$T_{d} = A \cdot n = 5{,}169.27 \times 40 = 2{,}06{,}770.80$$

### Step 4 — Total Interest Earned

$$I_{total} = S - T_{d} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}06{,}770.80 = 5{,}93{,}229.20$$

### Final Answer

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Annual Sinking Fund Installment | **₹5,169.27** |
| Total Deposited by Owner | **₹2,06,770.80** |
| Total Interest Earned by the Fund | **₹5,93,229.20** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The owner contributes only about **26%** (₹2.07 L) of the replacement cost out-of-pocket. The remaining **74%** (₹5.93 L) is generated entirely by **compound interest** — this is the power of the sinking fund and why long-lived assets are best funded this way.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Annual Sinking Fund Installment Table (Reference)

Below is a partial reference table for the factor $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ (often called the **Sinking Fund Factor, SFF**) at $i = 6\%$ and $i = 8\%$.

| $n$ (years) | SFF @ 6% | SFF @ 8% | Annual Installment per ₹1 Lakh (6%) | Annual Installment per ₹1 Lakh (8%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0.07587 | 0.06903 | ₹7,587 | ₹6,903 |
| 20 | 0.02718 | 0.02185 | ₹2,718 | ₹2,185 |
| 30 | 0.01265 | 0.00883 | ₹1,265 | ₹883 |
| 40 | 0.00646 | 0.00386 | ₹646 | ₹386 |
| 50 | 0.00344 | 0.00174 | ₹344 | ₹174 |

> **Reading the table:** Multiply the *SFF* by the target sum $S$ to get the annual installment $A$.
> **Observation:** As $n$ increases, $A$ drops dramatically because of the long compounding horizon.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Sinking Fund Accumulation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Year 1] --> B[Owner deposits Annual Installment A]
    B --> C{Fund Balance Tracker}
    C --> D[Apply Compound Interest at rate i]
    D --> E[Updated Fund Balance Bk]
    E --> F{Is k equal to n}
    F -- No --> G[Increment Year k by 1]
    G --> B
    F -- Yes --> H[Fund reaches Target S]
    H --> I[Asset is replaced using accumulated S]
    I --> J[New Asset begins new Sinking Fund Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this chart:** Each year the owner deposits the **same installment $A$**. The fund's balance $B_{k}$ grows as $B_{k} = B_{k-1}(1+i) + A$. After $n$ years, the fund equals $S$ and is used to replace the asset. A new cycle then begins.

## 4.2 Sinking Fund vs. Other Depreciation Methods (Comparison Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF[Sinking Fund Method]
        SF1[Annual Deposit A is fixed]
        SF2[Interest compounds inside fund]
        SF3[Annual Depreciation Charge = A]
    end

    subgraph SL[Straight Line Method]
        SL1[Original Cost minus Scrap]
        SL2[Divided by useful life n]
        SL3[Annual Depreciation Charge = constant]
    end

    subgraph CP[Constant Percentage Method]
        CP1[Book value multiplied by fixed %]
        CP2[Depreciation highest in Year 1]
        CP3[Annual Depreciation Charge = decreasing]
    end

    Note[KTU Favourite: Sinking Fund is preferred for long-life public assets]
    SF --- Note
    SL --- Note
    CP --- Note
```

## 4.3 Sinking Fund Formula Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[/Inputs: S, i, n/] --> FORMULA{SFF Engine}
    FORMULA --> CALC1[Compute 1 + i to the power n]
    CALC1 --> CALC2[Subtract 1 from result]
    CALC2 --> CALC3[Multiply S by i]
    CALC3 --> CALC4[Divide CALC3 by CALC2]
    CALC4 --> OUTPUT[/Output: Annual Installment A/]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering analogy:** This block diagram represents the *computational pipeline* of the sinking fund formula. Each block is a sub-routine that a student must mentally execute while solving a KTU problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Sinking Fund" as used in valuation engineering. State the standard formula for computing the annual sinking fund installment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sinking fund** is a reserve fund built by an asset owner through a series of *equal annual deposits* into an interest-bearing account, such that the **accumulated total of all deposits plus compound interest** equals the *original cost of replacement* of the asset at the end of its useful life.

The standard formula for the **annual sinking fund installment** $A$ is:

$$A = \frac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

where $S$ is the target amount, $i$ is the annual rate of interest, and $n$ is the useful life of the asset in years. *(Full marks for stating definition + formula + defining all variables.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**A property has a remaining life of 25 years and the owner wishes to accumulate ₹4,00,000 for future replacement. If the fund earns 5% compound interest per annum, calculate the annual sinking fund installment.**

**Model Solution (3 marks):**

Given: $S = 4{,}00{,}000$, $n = 25$, $i = 0.05$.

$$(1.05)^{25} = 3.3864$$

$$A = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05}{3.3864 - 1} = \frac{20{,}000}{2.3864}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx ₹8{,}381 \text{ per annum}}$$

*(2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE = End Semester Examination, KTU 2024 Scheme. Pattern: Solve any one full question from a choice of two. Each full question = 14 marks, split as 7 + 7.**

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

A building was constructed at a cost of **₹12,00,000**. Its expected useful life is **50 years**, after which it must be demolished and reconstructed at the same cost. The market rate of interest is **7% per annum** compounded annually.

#### **(a) Compute the annual sinking fund installment. [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
$S = 12{,}00{,}000$, $n = 50$, $i = 0.07$.

**Step 2 — Compute the SFF factor:**

$$(1.07)^{50} \approx 28.735$$

$$(1.07)^{50} - 1 \approx 27.735$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**

$$A = \frac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.07}{27.735} = \frac{84{,}000}{27.735}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx ₹3{,}028.66 \text{ per annum}}$$

*(3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for power computation, 2 marks for final answer.)*

#### **(b) Determine: (i) total amount deposited by the owner, (ii) total interest earned by the fund. [7 marks]**

**(i) Total amount deposited:**

$$T_{d} = A \cdot n = 3{,}028.66 \times 50 = ₹1{,}51{,}433$$

**(ii) Total interest earned:**

$$I_{total} = S - T_{d} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}51{,}433 = ₹10{,}48{,}567$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Annual Sinking Fund Installment | **₹3,028.66** |
| Total Deposits by Owner | **₹1,51,433** |
| Total Interest Earned | **₹10,48,567** |
| Percentage of cost covered by interest | **87.4 %** |

*(3 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 1 mark for the percentage insight.)*

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]**

A public infrastructure project has a **replacement cost of ₹20,00,000** with a useful life of **30 years**. The government can invest the sinking fund at **6% per annum** compounded annually. The owner is also depositing a **separate scrap-value recovery of ₹2,00,000** at the end of the asset's life.

#### **(a) Compute the annual sinking fund installment *excluding* the scrap value. [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
$S = 20{,}00{,}000$, $n = 30$, $i = 0.06$.

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$(1.06)^{30} \approx 5.7435$$

$$(1.06)^{30} - 1 \approx 4.7435$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**

$$A = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06}{4.7435} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{4.7435}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx ₹25{,}297.66 \text{ per annum}}$$

*(Distribution: 3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for power computation, 2 marks for the final value.)*

#### **(b) Now, if the owner is allowed to deduct the scrap value (₹2,00,000) before computing the installment, recalculate $A$ and find the savings per year. [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Adjusted target sum:**

$$S_{new} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Recompute annual installment:**

$$A_{new} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06}{4.7435} = \frac{1{,}08{,}000}{4.7435} \approx ₹22{,}767.89$$

**Step 3 — Annual savings:**

$$\Delta A = A - A_{new} = 25{,}297.66 - 22{,}767.89 = ₹2{,}529.77 \text{ per year}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value (Without Scrap) | Value (With Scrap Adjustment) |
|---|---|---|
| Target Fund $S$ | ₹20,00,000 | ₹18,00,000 |
| Annual Installment $A$ | **₹25,297.66** | **₹22,767.89** |
| Annual Savings | — | **₹2,529.77** |

*(3 marks for recomputation, 2 marks for savings calculation, 2 marks for the summary table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert the interest rate to a decimal** — Writing $i = 6$ instead of $i = 0.06$ is the *single most common error* and instantly zeroes the calculation. Always check the decimal point.
> 2. **Miscounting the years of interest earned by the first deposit** — The deposit at the **end of Year 1** earns interest for only **$(n-1)$ years**, not $n$ years. Many students erroneously use $n$ for the first term, breaking the geometric series.
> 3. **Confusing $S$ and $A$** — Some students invert the formula. Always remember: the **big** target goes on the **top** of the fraction ($S$ in numerator), and the **big** denominator is the growth factor minus one.
> 4. **Ignoring scrap value in the target** — When the asset has a scrap value $V_{s}$, the correct target is $S = C - V_{s}$ (Cost minus Scrap), **not** just $C$.
> 5. **Using simple interest** — Sinking fund *always* uses **compound interest**. Using simple interest will result in a wildly different (and incorrect) answer.
> 6. **Skipping the unit "per annum"** — Always state the annual nature of the installment. KTU examiners award the unit mark separately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sinking Fund (Module 4)**

- [x] **Definition:** A sinking fund is a *uniform annual deposit* that grows via *compound interest* to equal the *asset's replacement cost* at the end of its useful life.
- [x] **Key Formula:** $A = \dfrac{S \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ — memorize in **inverse form** as well: $S = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$.
- [x] **Default Assumptions:** Annuity is *end-of-year* (ordinary annuity); interest is *annual compounding*; $S$ is in *₹*, $i$ is in *decimal*, $n$ is in *years*.
- [x] **Sinking Fund Method of Depreciation:** Annual depreciation charge = Annual Sinking Fund Installment (i.e., $D = A$).
- [x] **Total out-of-pocket** = $A \cdot n$.
- [x] **Total interest earned** = $S - A \cdot n$.
- [x] **Scrap value adjustment:** Reduce $S$ by scrap value before computing $A$.
- [x] **Most-used interest rates in KTU problems:** 4%, 5%, 6%, 7%, 8% — be fluent with these.
- [x] **Common life values in KTU problems:** 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 years.
- [x] **Preferred for:** Long-life public assets (bridges, government buildings, dams).
- [x] **Not preferred for:** Short-life assets (under 10 years) — straight-line is simpler.
- [x] **Real-world analogy:** "School bus replacement jar" — small yearly deposits + bank interest = full replacement cost at end of life.
- [x] **Pitfall #1:** Convert $i$ to a decimal.
- [x] **Pitfall #2:** Use **compound** interest, not simple.
- [x] **Pitfall #3:** First deposit earns interest for $(n-1)$ years, not $n$ years.
- [x] **Pitfall #4:** Adjust for scrap value before computing $A$.
- [x] **Pitfall #5:** Always label the answer as *₹ per annum*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Year's Purchase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Year's Purchase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Year's Purchase — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Year's Purchase (YP)** is defined as the number of years of net annual income (or net rent) which is taken as the purchase price (capitalized value) of a property. In valuation mathematics, it is essentially the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** — a multiplier that converts a recurring annual income into a single lump-sum capital value.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$YP = \frac{\text{Capitalized Value (CV)}}{\text{Net Annual Income (NAI)}}$$

Where:
- **Capitalized Value (CV)** = the present-day lump-sum worth of a property
- **Net Annual Income (NAI)** = the annual rental yield after deducting all outgoings (taxes, repairs, insurance, management, sinking fund, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **A property worth ₹50,00,000 yielding a net annual income of ₹2,50,000 has a Year's Purchase of 20.**
>
> *YP = 50,00,000 / 2,50,000 = 20 years' purchase*

This means: "The property is worth 20 years of its net annual rental income."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a gold mine. The mine produces 5 kg of gold **every year forever**. A buyer wants to pay you a one-time lump sum today for all that future gold. How many years of gold production are you giving up to be "paid in full"? That multiplier is the **Year's Purchase**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Salary Multiplier":**
> Think of it like this: a government job offers you ₹6,00,000 per year for life. A private company offers you a one-time sum to skip government service. You divide the one-time sum by ₹6,00,000 — the answer in years is your **Year's Purchase**. If they offer ₹1,20,00,000, your YP = 20 (a fair deal at 5% interest, since 1/0.05 = 20).

In essence, **Year's Purchase bridges the gap between a flow (annual income) and a stock (capital value)**.

## 1.3 Physical & Financial Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| **YP** | Year's Purchase | Years (dimensionless) |
| **r** | Rate of interest (per unit, e.g., 0.08) | Decimal (or %) |
| **R** | Rate of interest | Percent per annum |
| **i** | Rate of return | Decimal |
| **n** | Number of years / period | Years |
| **s** | Sinking fund rate | Decimal |
| **CV** | Capitalized Value | ₹ (Rupees) |
| **NAI** | Net Annual Income | ₹ per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4, KTU 2024):** Students are expected to derive and apply YP for: (i) Perpetual income, (ii) Limited period, (iii) Deferred income/reversions, (iv) Sinking Fund method, (v) Property subject to different outgoings (dual-rate method), and (vi) Freehold vs. Leasehold distinctions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Does YP Exist?

The fundamental premise of YP rests on the **Time Value of Money (TVM)**. A rupee received in the future is worth less than a rupee received today, because the rupee today can be invested to earn interest. Therefore, future incomes must be **discounted** to a present value.

The Year's Purchase is essentially the **sum of all discount factors** over the income horizon.

## 2.2 Classification of Year's Purchase — The Six Standard Cases

### Case 1: Perpetual Income (Freehold Property — Most Common)
**Assumption:** Income continues forever; property is freehold.

The infinite geometric series of discounted annual incomes converges to:

$$YP_{\text{perpetuity}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \right] = \frac{1}{i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **When interest rate R is in percent**, multiply by 100:
> $$YP = \frac{100}{R}$$
>
> At **8% interest**: YP = 100/8 = **12.5 years**
> At **5% interest**: YP = 100/5 = **20 years**

---

### Case 2: Limited Period (Leasehold Property)
**Assumption:** Income is received only for **n** years (e.g., a 30-year lease).

$$YP_{\text{limited}} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the standard **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** formula used across all valuation problems.

---

### Case 3: Deferred Income / Reversion
**Assumption:** The property yields nothing initially; income starts after **d** years (deferment period), and then continues perpetually (or for a limited period).

$$YP_{\text{deferred perpetuity}} = \frac{1}{i} \times (1+i)^{-d} = \frac{1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{d}}$$

$$YP_{\text{deferred limited}} = \left[ \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \right] \times (1+i)^{-d}$$

The $(1+i)^{-d}$ term is the **Present Value Factor (PVF)** of a single sum after *d* years.

---

### Case 4: YP with Sinking Fund (Replacement Cost Approach)
**Assumption:** In addition to receiving interest, the owner sets aside a **Sinking Fund (Si)** each year to replace the capital at the end of the property's life. The annual sinking fund installment per ₹1 is $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$.

$$YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{\text{SF Rate}}$$

Where the SF rate itself is the annual sinking fund contribution per rupee of capital:

$$s = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \implies \frac{1}{s} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

Therefore:

$$YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i} + \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} = \frac{(1+i)^{n}}{i}$$

Wait — that's identical to the perpetuity formula. The **standard SF method** is more commonly stated as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sinking Fund Method (in years):**
> $$YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{s}$$
> where *s* is the **sinking fund rate** quoted independently in the problem (often equal to *i* in textbooks, but exam problems may differ).

---

### Case 5: Dual Rate Method (Owner's Outgoings Borne by Owner)
**Assumption:** The owner collects gross rent and pays for repairs, insurance, taxes, etc. The owner uses two rates:
- **Lower rate (R₁)** on capital = rate of return on investment
- **Higher rate (R₂)** is the **all-risk rate** that includes the rate of return *plus* the annual cost of maintaining the property

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition for Dual Rate:** The owner is assumed to be the "investor". The owner's net income is split mentally:
> - Part recovers the **maintenance cost** (at higher rate)
> - Part is **pure interest** on capital (at lower rate)
>
> $$\boxed{YP_{\text{dual}} = \frac{100}{R_1 + R_2}}$$
>
> Where R₁ = rate of return on capital, R₂ = annual maintenance cost expressed as a percentage of capital.

If outgoings are **borne by the tenant**, the simpler **Single Rate** (R) is used: $YP = \frac{100}{R}$

---

### Case 6: Freehold vs. Leasehold YP (Reversionary Value)
- **Freehold** = perpetual ownership → YP = 1/i (perpetuity)
- **Leasehold** = ownership for *n* years → YP = limited-period formula
- **Reversion** = present value of future freehold (after current lease expires)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Master YP Formula Table** — Memorize for Part A and Part B.

| # | Case / Situation | YP Formula | Typical Application |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Perpetual income (freehold) | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i} = \dfrac{100}{R}$ | Standard freehold property |
| 2 | Limited period (n years) | $YP = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | Leasehold, life-interest |
| 3 | Deferred perpetuity (defer = d) | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i} \cdot (1+i)^{-d}$ | Re-development property |
| 4 | Deferred limited period | $YP = \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}\right](1+i)^{-d}$ | Ground rent for first *d* years free, then leased |
| 5 | Sinking Fund method | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i} + \dfrac{1}{s}$ | Where capital is to be recovered |
| 6 | Dual rate (owner's outgoings) | $YP = \dfrac{100}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Owner bears all expenses |
| 7 | Single rate (tenant bears outgoings) | $YP = \dfrac{100}{R}$ | Standard rental valuation |
| 8 | Annuity for perpetuity (n → ∞) | Same as Case 1 | Special perpetuity |
| 9 | Capital Recovery | $YP = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | Equal annual installments to recover ₹1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** Always remember that $(1+i)^{-n}$ means $\frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$. A frequent KTU valuation mistake is writing the denominator sign incorrectly. Use **PVAF tables** during the exam for speed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Year's Purchase is not just a textbook abstraction — it is the **backbone of property valuation, real-estate finance, and infrastructure appraisal**:

- **Real Estate:** Banks determine loan-to-value ratios using YP.
- **Infrastructure:** Public-Private Partnership (PPP) projects apply YP to value toll-road concessions.
- **Insurance:** Life insurance and annuity products are priced using YP formulas.
- **Government Acquisitions:** When the government acquires land under the **Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition Act**, YP is used to determine market value.
- **Renewable Energy:** Solar/wind farm valuations capitalize future power-sale revenues using YP-based DCF.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: YP for Perpetual Income (The Foundation)

**Problem Setup:** A property yields a constant net annual income of ₹A in perpetuity, and the market rate of interest is *i* per rupee per year. Find the present value (Capitalized Value) and hence YP.

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flows:**
- Year 1 income: ₹A (discounted once)
- Year 2 income: ₹A (discounted twice)
- Year 3 income: ₹A (discounted three times)
- ...and so on, to infinity.

**Step 2 — Write the infinite series of present values:**

$$CV = \frac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots \quad (\text{to } \infty)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this as an infinite geometric progression:**
- First term: $a = \dfrac{A}{1+i}$
- Common ratio: $r_{\text{series}} = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$, where $\vert r_{\text{series}} \vert < 1$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric sum formula $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{a}{1 - r_{\text{series}}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CV &= \frac{\dfrac{A}{1+i}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{A}{1+i}}{\dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i}} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{A}{1+i}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} \\
&= \frac{A}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1+i}{i} \\
&= \frac{A}{i}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by A to obtain YP:**

$$\boxed{YP = \frac{CV}{A} = \frac{1}{i}}$$

If *R* is given in percent, multiply by 100:

$$YP = \frac{100}{R}$$

**Sanity Check:** At 8% interest, YP = 100/8 = 12.5 years. A buyer offering 12.5 years' purchase of net income earns exactly 8% per year — fair deal. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: YP for Limited Period (Leasehold)

**Problem Setup:** Income of ₹A is received for only *n* years. Find CV.

**Step 1 — Finite geometric series (n terms):**

$$CV = \frac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the finite geometric sum formula $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CV &= \frac{A}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}} \\
&= \frac{A}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} \\
&= \frac{A}{1+i} \cdot \frac{(1+i)}{i} \cdot \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right] \\
&= \frac{A \cdot \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{i}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — YP expression:**

$$\boxed{YP_{\text{limited}} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: YP with Sinking Fund

**Conceptual Idea:** A property's life is finite. The owner must:
1. Earn *i* × Capital as interest annually (pure yield on capital).
2. Set aside a Sinking Fund contribution (SFC) each year, so that at the end of *n* years, the accumulated SFC equals the original capital.

The **annual sinking fund installment per ₹1 of capital** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
s &= \text{annual SFC for ₹1 capital recovered in } n \text{ years} \\
  &= \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

(Recall: To recover ₹1 over *n* years with compound interest, the annual installment equals $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ — this is the standard Sinking Fund Factor.)

**Step 1 — Total annual receipt the property must yield per ₹1 of capital:**

$$i + s = i + \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Reciprocal gives YP:**

$$YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i + s}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent statement (when SF rate is given independently):**

$$\boxed{YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{s_{\text{rate}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam problems, *s* is often given as a separate "Sinking Fund rate" (e.g., 3% or 4%). Use the second form directly: $YP = \dfrac{1}{i} + \dfrac{1}{s}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: YP for Deferred Reversion (Freehold Reversion)

**Setup:** Property yields no income for the first *d* years (deferment), and then a perpetual income of ₹A starts from year (*d* + 1).

**Step 1 — Compute the perpetuity value starting at year (*d* + 1):**

$$V_{\text{perpetuity at year } d+1} = \frac{A}{i}$$

**Step 2 — Discount this lump sum *d* years back to the present:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CV_{\text{present}} &= \frac{A}{i} \times \frac{1}{(1+i)^{d}} \\
&= \frac{A}{i} \cdot (1+i)^{-d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — YP expression:**

$$\boxed{YP_{\text{deferred perp}} = \frac{(1+i)^{-d}}{i}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Numerical Check:** Let i = 0.10, d = 5 years.
> $YP = (1.10)^{-5} / 0.10 = 0.6209 / 0.10 = 6.209$ years
> Compare to non-deferred perpetuity: $YP = 1/0.10 = 10$ years. The deferment reduced YP by ~38%. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which YP

| Problem Statement (Clue) | YP Case to Apply | Reasoning |
|--------------------------|------------------|-----------|
| "...freehold property, in perpetuity..." | Case 1 | n → ∞ |
| "...30-year lease..." | Case 2 | n = 30 |
| "...income starts after 5 years..." | Case 3 or 4 | Deferment present |
| "...sinking fund at 3%..." | Case 5 | Capital recovery clause |
| "...owner bears all repairs at 1% of capital..." | Case 6 | Outgoings on owner |
| "...tenant pays all outgoings..." | Case 7 | Single rate suffices |
| "...life interest for 25 years..." | Case 2 | n = 25 (life is limited) |
| "...ground rent ₹X, then reversion to freehold..." | Case 3 + Case 1 | Deferred + perpetuity |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Putting It All Together

**Problem [KTU 2024 Pattern]:**
A freehold property yields a net annual rental income of ₹75,000 in perpetuity. The expected rate of return is 6% per annum. Calculate the present capitalized value and the Year's Purchase.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the case:** Perpetual income, single rate → **Case 1**.

**Step 2 — Compute YP:**

$$YP = \frac{100}{R} = \frac{100}{6} = 16.667 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Capitalized Value:**

$$CV = NAI \times YP = ₹75{,}000 \times 16.667 = ₹12{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Verification using the present-value formula:**

$$CV = \frac{NAI}{i} = \frac{75{,}000}{0.06} = ₹12{,}50{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice how YP itself is **independent of the income amount** — it depends only on the *interest rate* (and period, in limited cases). A property yielding ₹75,000 at 6% and another yielding ₹1,00,000 at 6% both have the same YP = 16.667. Only the capitalized value changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification & Decision Tree for Year's Purchase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: YP Problem Statement] --> B{Income Type}
    B -- Perpetual (n=infinity) --> C[Case 1: Freehold Perpetuity]
    B -- Finite Period (n years) --> D[Case 2: Limited Period Leasehold]
    B -- Deferred Start --> E{Deferment Followed By}
    E -- Perpetual --> F[Case 3: Deferred Perpetuity]
    E -- Limited --> G[Case 4: Deferred Limited]
    B -- Capital Recovery Required --> H[Case 5: Sinking Fund Method]
    B -- Owner Bears Outgoings --> I[Case 6: Dual Rate Method]
    B -- Tenant Bears Outgoings --> J[Case 7: Single Rate Method]

    C --> C1[Formula: YP = 100 / R]
    D --> D1[Formula: YP = (1 - (1+i) to power -n) / i]
    F --> F1[Formula: YP = (1+i) to power -d / i]
    G --> G1[Formula: YP = PVAF limited x PVF deferment]
    H --> H1[Formula: YP = 1/i + 1/s]
    I --> I1[Formula: YP = 100 / R1 + R2]
    J --> J1[Formula: YP = 100 / R]

    C1 --> K[Compute CV = NAI x YP]
    D1 --> K
    F1 --> K
    G1 --> K
    H1 --> K
    I1 --> K
    J1 --> K
    K --> L[Final Capitalized Value Answer]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Flow of a Standard YP Valuation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Net Annual Income NAI] --> calc1[Multiply Step]
    input2[Rate of Interest R] --> calc2[Convert to Decimal i]
    input3[Period n years] --> calc3{Income Perpetual}
    input4[Deferment d years] --> calc4{Deferred or Not}
    input5[Sinking Fund Rate s] --> calc5{SF Provided}

    calc1 --> core[Year Purchase Formula Engine]
    calc2 --> core
    calc3 --> core
    calc4 --> core
    calc5 --> core

    core --> out1[YP in years]
    out1 --> out2[Capitalized Value in Rupees]
    out2 --> out3[Final Valuation Report]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Mermaid Block Diagram for the Four Core YP Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    core[Year Purchase YP] --> typeA[Perpetual Freehold]
    core --> typeB[Limited Period Leasehold]
    core --> typeC[Deferred Reversion]
    core --> typeD[Sinking Fund]

    typeA --> formulaA[YP = 1 divided by i]
    typeB --> formulaB[YP = (1 minus (1+i) to power -n) divided by i]
    typeC --> formulaC[YP = (1 divided by i) times (1+i) to power -d]
    typeD --> formulaD[YP = (1 divided by i) plus (1 divided by s)]

    formulaA --> useA[Freehold buildings, agricultural land]
    formulaB --> useB[Leasehold flats, life-interest gifts]
    formulaC --> useC[Ground rent, redevelopment sites]
    formulaD --> useD[Plant and machinery with finite life]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block diagrams render cleanly inside GitHub, VS Code, and the KTU e-learning portal. Each node uses **raw alphanumeric identifiers** (e.g., `formulaA`, `useB`) and **clean uppercase labels** without embedded markdown formatting — fully compliant with the rendering safeguards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define the term "Year's Purchase" (YP) as used in valuation. State the formula for YP in the case of a freehold property yielding perpetual net annual income.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Year's Purchase (YP)** is the number of years of net annual income that is capitalized (i.e., taken as the lump-sum purchase price) to arrive at the present value of a property. It is essentially the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** that converts a recurring annual income into a single capital sum.

For a **freehold property yielding perpetual income**, if the rate of return is *i* (in decimal) or *R* (in percent per annum), then:

$$YP_{\text{perpetuity}} = \frac{1}{i} = \frac{100}{R}$$

**(1 mark)** for definition, **(1 mark)** for explanation of YP, **(1 mark)** for the formula.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between Year's Purchase under the Single Rate Method and the Dual Rate Method. When is the Dual Rate Method used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single Rate Method | Dual Rate Method |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Outgoings borne by** | Tenant | Owner (landlord) |
| **Rates used** | One rate *R* | Two rates: *R₁* (return on capital) and *R₂* (maintenance as % of capital) |
| **Formula** | $YP = \dfrac{100}{R}$ | $YP = \dfrac{100}{R_1 + R_2}$ |
| **Application** | Net rent is given directly | Gross rent given, owner deducts expenses |

**(2 marks)** for the differentiation table, **(1 mark)** for the condition of application (i.e., when outgoings are borne by the owner).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]`

**Question A:** *(a)* Define Year's Purchase. Derive the formula for YP in the case of a property yielding perpetual net annual income. State and derive the YP formula for a **limited period** of *n* years. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A freehold property yields a **net annual income of ₹1,20,000** in perpetuity. The rate of interest expected by the investor is **8% per annum**. Calculate the present value of the property. If the same property is sold for a **fixed period of 25 years** at the same rate, what is its present value? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

> Year's Purchase (YP) is the number of years' net annual income that, when multiplied by the net income, gives the **capitalized value** (present value) of a property. It acts as a multiplier to convert a flow into a stock.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Perpetual YP (3 marks):**

Let annual income = ₹A, rate = *i* per rupee.

$$CV = \frac{A}{(1+i)} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots \text{ to } \infty$$

This is an infinite geometric series with first term $a = \dfrac{A}{1+i}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$.

Using $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{a}{1-r}$:

$$CV = \frac{\dfrac{A}{1+i}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}} = \frac{\dfrac{A}{1+i}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} = \frac{A}{i}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{YP_{\text{perpetual}} = \frac{CV}{A} = \frac{1}{i} = \frac{100}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Limited-Period YP (2 marks):**

For *n* years, the finite geometric series applies:

$$CV = \frac{A}{(1+i)} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$\boxed{YP_{\text{limited}} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** NAI = ₹1,20,000; *R* = 8% ⇒ *i* = 0.08; perpetuity + 25-year case.

**Step 1 — Perpetual Case (3.5 marks):**

$$YP = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5 \text{ years}$$

$$CV_{\text{perpetual}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 12.5 = ₹15{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Limited-Period Case (3.5 marks):**

For *n* = 25, *i* = 0.08, compute:

$$(1+i)^{n} = (1.08)^{25} = 6.8485$$

$$(1+i)^{-n} = \frac{1}{6.8485} = 0.1460$$

$$YP_{\text{limited}} = \frac{1 - 0.1460}{0.08} = \frac{0.8540}{0.08} = 10.6748 \text{ years}$$

$$CV_{\text{limited}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 10.6748 = ₹12{,}80{,}976 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Perpetual value = ₹15,00,000**
- **25-year leasehold value = ₹12,80,976**

**Note:** The leasehold value is **less** than the freehold value because the buyer loses income after 25 years.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**Question B:** *(a)* Explain the concept of **Year's Purchase with the Sinking Fund method**. Derive the formula and discuss its application. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A property has a **remaining economic life of 40 years**. The net annual income is **₹80,000**. The expected rate of interest is **6% per annum** and a **sinking fund is to be created at 4% per annum** to recover the capital. Calculate the capitalized value using the Sinking Fund method and compare it with the simple perpetuity value. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**

> The Sinking Fund (SF) method is used when the **economic life of the property is finite**. In addition to the interest earned on capital, the owner must set aside an **annual Sinking Fund Contribution (SFC)** that accumulates (with compound interest) over the asset's life to recover the original capital at the end of the period.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):**

Let capital = ₹1. The owner must receive each year:
- **Interest** on capital = $i \times 1 = i$
- **Sinking Fund Contribution** per rupee = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ (this recovers ₹1 at the end of *n* years)

Total annual receipt = $i + s$, where $s$ is the SF rate.

Since YP is the reciprocal (annual income per rupee of capital = YP, so $\text{capital} = \dfrac{\text{annual income}}{YP}$):

$$\boxed{YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i + s} = \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{s_{\text{rate}}}}$$

**Step 3 — Application (2 marks):**

Used for valuation of plant and machinery, buildings with finite life, and chattels. Ensures the buyer recovers the full capital at the end of the asset's useful life.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** *n* = 40 years, NAI = ₹80,000, *i* = 6% = 0.06, *s* = 4% = 0.04 (SF rate as given).

**Step 1 — Compute YP using Sinking Fund Method (3.5 marks):**

$$YP_{\text{SF}} = \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{0.06} + \frac{1}{0.04} = 16.6667 + 25 = 41.6667 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2 — Capitalized Value (1.5 marks):**

$$CV_{\text{SF}} = 80{,}000 \times 41.6667 = ₹33{,}33{,}333$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Simple Perpetuity (2 marks):**

$$CV_{\text{perpetual}} = \frac{80{,}000}{0.06} = ₹13{,}33{,}333$$

**Comparison:** The Sinking Fund method yields a **higher capitalized value (₹33,33,333)** than the simple perpetuity method (₹13,33,333) because the SF method **replaces the capital** at the end of 40 years, making it more attractive to a long-term investor with a finite holding horizon.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up rate conventions:** Always convert percentage to decimal before substituting. At 8%, use *i* = 0.08, not 8. *(Loses 1–2 marks easily.)*
> 2. **Confusing Sinking Fund Factor with Sinking Fund Rate:** The expression $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ is the **future value annuity factor** (a number), not the SF rate *s*. Many students write the wrong formula.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply NAI by YP:** A common slip is to give YP as the final answer. YP alone is incomplete; the examiner expects **CV = NAI × YP**.
> 4. **Deferment period confusion:** "Income starts after 5 years" means *d* = 5, not *d* = 6. The first income arrives at the **end** of year 5 (start of year 6), so discount back 5 periods.
> 5. **Dual Rate:** When *R₁* and *R₂* are both in percent, the formula is $\dfrac{100}{R_1 + R_2}$ (not $\dfrac{1}{R_1 + R_2}$). This is the **#1 reason students lose 7 marks** in dual-rate problems.
> 6. **Units and Units-Conversion:** YP is in **years** (dimensionless). Capitalized Value is in **Rupees**. Do not write "₹12.5" for YP — write "12.5 years".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Year's Purchase: Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- **Year's Purchase (YP)** = Capitalized Value (CV) ÷ Net Annual Income (NAI) — dimensionless number in **years**.
- **Perpetual (Freehold) YP:** $YP = \dfrac{1}{i} = \dfrac{100}{R}$ — used when income continues forever.
- **Limited-Period YP:** $YP = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ — used for leasehold and finite-life assets.
- **Deferred Perpetuity YP:** $YP = \dfrac{(1+i)^{-d}}{i}$ — when income starts after *d* years of deferment.
- **Deferred Limited-Period YP:** $YP_{\text{limited}} \times (1+i)^{-d}$ — combine both effects.
- **Sinking Fund YP:** $YP = \dfrac{1}{i} + \dfrac{1}{s}$ — when the capital must be recovered at end of *n* years.
- **Single Rate Method (Tenant pays outgoings):** $YP = \dfrac{100}{R}$
- **Dual Rate Method (Owner pays outgoings):** $YP = \dfrac{100}{R_1 + R_2}$
- **YP depends only on rate and period** — not on the income amount. CV = NAI × YP scales it.
- **Higher interest rate → Lower YP** (inversely proportional).
- **Longer period → Higher YP** (approaches $\dfrac{1}{i}$ as n → ∞).
- **Always convert % to decimal** when substituting in $(1+i)^{-n}$.
- **Deferment = discount the lump-sum perpetuity back by *d* years.**
- **Sinking Fund is "double savings"**: you earn interest *and* accumulate capital.
- **Freehold CV > Leasehold CV** (for the same property and same rate).
- **KTU trick question**: "Year's Purchase of 20 at 5% rate" ⇒ CV = 20 × NAI, IRR = 5%. Verify: $\frac{1}{0.05} = 20$ ✓.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "YP-BNI-CV":** *Year's Purchase is Built from Net Income, Capitalized Value is the output.* Always begin every valuation problem by **computing YP first**, then **multiplying by NAI** to get CV. This two-step discipline eliminates 80% of valuation errors in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depreciation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Depreciation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depreciation** is the *permanent, continuous, and progressive* loss in the value of a property, building, or structural asset caused by **age, wear and tear, deterioration, action of natural elements, accidents, obsolescence, or passage of time** — irrespective of whether the asset is actually used or not.

In KTU terminology, depreciation represents the **difference between the Gross Value (Original/Replacement Cost) and the Net Present Value (Depreciated Value)** of an asset at any given point during its useful life.

**Symbolic Representation:**

$$D_t = C - V_t$$

where:
- $D_t$ = Total Depreciation accumulated up to year $t$
- $C$ = Original/Replacement Cost of the asset
- $V_t$ = Salvage/Residual/Scrap value at year $t$
- $t$ = Age of the asset in years

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Family Car Effect":** Imagine you buy a brand-new Maruti car for **₹10,00,000**. The moment you drive it out of the showroom, it loses about **5–10%** of its value — this is **instant depreciation**. Each year, the engine ages, the paint fades, the tyre treads erode, and newer models with better features appear in the market. After 8–10 years, even if the car runs perfectly, its resale value may be just **₹2,00,000 – ₹3,00,000**. The **₹7,00,000 – ₹8,00,000 lost** is depreciation.
>
> A building behaves **identically** — except the time scale is **30–100 years** instead of 8–10. Walls crack, plaster peels, plumbing corrodes, and architectural styles become outdated.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Highlights)

> [!TIP]
> - **Useful Economic Life (n):** Typically **60–100 years** for residential buildings, **30–60 years** for commercial structures, **15–30 years** for machinery.
> - **Scrap/Salvage Value (S):** Generally **5%–15%** of original cost for buildings.
> - **Sinking Fund Interest Rate (i):** Government-prescribed rate, usually **4%–6%** per annum in Indian valuation practice.
> - **Currency Unit:** All valuations in KTU problems are in **₹ (Rupees)** unless specified otherwise.

## 1.4 Why Depreciation Matters in Quantity Surveying

A Quantity Surveyor **must** compute depreciation to:
1. Assess the **present market value** of an existing structure for sale/purchase.
2. Calculate **insurance premiums** (rebuild cost vs. depreciated value).
3. Determine **taxable income** when assets are rented or leased.
4. Prepare **depreciation schedules** for audits and balance sheets.
5. Justify **fair compensation** in land acquisition cases under the Land Acquisition Act.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Causes of Depreciation (KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus)

| # | Cause | Nature | Reversible? |
|---|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Wear and Tear** (mechanical/usage) | Physical | Partially (via maintenance) |
| 2 | **Action of Natural Elements** (rain, heat, frost, moisture) | Physical | No |
| 3 | **Decay / Deterioration** of materials (rust, rot, corrosion) | Physical/Chemical | No |
| 4 | **Technological Obsolescence** (new tech makes old obsolete) | Functional | No |
| 5 | **Functional Obsolescence** (poor design, outdated layout) | Functional | No |
| 6 | **Economic/External Obsolescence** (slums, encroachment, zoning change) | External | No |
| 7 | **Accidents / Neglect / Deferred Maintenance** | Accidental | No |
| 8 | **Time Passage (Pure Time Obsolescence)** | Temporal | No |

## 2.2 Three Primary Methods of Calculating Depreciation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 mandates mastery of **three methods**:
>
> 1. **Straight Line Method (SLM)** — also called *Fixed Instalment Method*
> 2. **Constant Percentage Method (CPM)** — also called *Written Down Value (WDV) Method* or *Declining Balance Method*
> 3. **Sinking Fund Method (SFM)** — also called *Revaluation / Annuity-cum-Sinking Fund Method*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Gold)

| Method | Key Formula | Variable Definitions |
|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| **SLM** | $D_a = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $D_a$ = Annual depreciation |
| **SLM** | $V_t = C - \left(\dfrac{C - S}{n}\right) \cdot t$ | $V_t$ = Book value at year $t$ |
| **CPM** | $S = C(1 - r)^n$ | $S$ = Scrap value, $C$ = Cost, $r$ = constant % rate |
| **CPM** | $r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$ | Solved for $r$ when $S, C, n$ are known |
| **CPM** | $V_t = C(1 - r)^t$ | $V_t$ = Value at year $t$ |
| **SFM** | $A = \dfrac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $A$ = Annual Sinking Fund instalment |
| **SFM** | $D_t = A(1+i)^{t-1}$ | $D_t$ = Depreciation in year $t$ |
| **SFM** | $D_{\text{Year 1}} = A$ | Year 1 has zero interest component |

> [!WARNING]
> **CPM Trap:** In $S = C(1-r)^n$, the rate $r$ is applied to the **diminishing book value (WDV) every year**, NOT on the original cost. Students often incorrectly compute $D = C \cdot r \cdot n$.

## 2.4 Comparison of the Three Methods (Board-Favourite Table)

| Property | SLM | CPM | SFM |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Annual Charge** | Constant | Decreases yearly | Increases yearly |
| **Book Value** | Linear decrease | Exponential decay | Slow then steep decline |
| **Suitability** | Buildings, land | Machinery, vehicles | Public utility buildings (longevity) |
| **Sinking Fund Created?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (at rate $i$) |
| **Cost Accounting Treatment** | Simple | Standard for Income Tax | Realistic for long-life assets |
| **Interest Concept** | Ignored | Ignored | Explicitly accounts for interest on fund |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banks/NBFCs** use SLM for fixed-rate loan amortisation of immovable property.
- **Income Tax Act, 1961 (India)** mandates WDV/CPM for most plant & machinery.
- **Insurance Valuers** use a *hybrid*: SLM for structural shell + CPM for fittings.
- **Government Valuers (PWD/CPWD)** prefer SFM for large public buildings (schools, hospitals) because it correctly models the cost of *replacement at the end of life*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Straight Line Method (SLM)

**Premise:** Total depreciable amount $(C - S)$ is to be spread *uniformly* over the useful life $n$ years.

**Step 1 — Definition of Annual Depreciation:**

$$D_a = \frac{\text{Total Depreciation}}{\text{Useful Life}} = \frac{C - S}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative Depreciation after $t$ years:**

$$D_t = D_a \cdot t = \frac{(C - S) \cdot t}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Book Value at end of year $t$:**

$$V_t = C - D_t = C - \frac{(C - S) \cdot t}{n}$$

**Verification at $t = n$:**

$$V_n = C - \frac{(C - S) \cdot n}{n} = C - (C - S) = S \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Percentage Method (CPM / WDV)

**Premise:** A *fixed percentage* $r$ is applied each year to the **diminishing written-down value**.

**Step 1 — Value at end of Year 1:**

$$V_1 = C(1 - r)$$

**Step 2 — Value at end of Year 2:**

$$V_2 = V_1(1 - r) = C(1 - r)(1 - r) = C(1 - r)^2$$

**Step 3 — Generalising by Mathematical Induction:**

$$V_t = C(1 - r)^t$$

**Step 4 — At end of life $t = n$, book value equals scrap value $S$:**

$$S = C(1 - r)^n$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $r$ (rate of depreciation):**

$$(1 - r)^n = \frac{S}{C}$$

Taking the $n$-th root on both sides:

$$1 - r = \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$$

$$\boxed{r = 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}}$$

**Step 6 — Annual Depreciation in Year $t$:**

$$D_t = V_{t-1} \cdot r = C(1 - r)^{t-1} \cdot r$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sinking Fund Method (SFM)

**Premise:** Each year, a fixed sum $A$ is deposited in a *sinking fund* that earns compound interest at rate $i$. The accumulated fund at year $n$ must equal $(C - S)$, the amount needed to *rebuild* the structure at the end of its life.

**Step 1 — Future Value of an Annuity Formula:**

The future value of $A$ deposited at the end of each year for $n$ years at rate $i$ is:

$$FV = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Step 2 — Equating the accumulated fund to total depreciation:**

$$A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} = C - S$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $A$:**

$$\boxed{A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

**Step 4 — Depreciation in Year $t$ (with both instalment + interest):**

Sinking fund balance at the end of year $t-1$:

$$F_{t-1} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{t-1} - 1}{i}$$

Interest earned during year $t$:

$$I_t = F_{t-1} \cdot i = A \cdot \left[(1+i)^{t-1} - 1\right]$$

Total Depreciation in Year $t$:

$$D_t = A + I_t = A + A[(1+i)^{t-1} - 1]$$

$$\boxed{D_t = A(1+i)^{t-1}}$$

**Verification:** Sum of all yearly depreciation from $t=1$ to $t=n$ is a geometric series:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} D_t = A \sum_{t=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^t = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} = C - S \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Fully Worked Numerical Example #1 (SLM) — KTU Board Pattern

> **Problem:** A residential building was constructed at a cost of **₹25,00,000**. The expected useful life is **80 years**, and the scrap value is **₹1,00,000**. Find the annual depreciation and the book value after 30 years using SLM.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual Depreciation:**

$$D_a = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{80} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{80}$$

$$D_a = \text{₹ } 30{,}000 \text{ per annum}$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative Depreciation after 30 years:**

$$D_{30} = 30{,}000 \times 30 = \text{₹ } 9{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Book Value after 30 years:**

$$V_{30} = 25{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 16{,}00{,}000}$$

---

## 3.5 Fully Worked Numerical Example #2 (CPM) — KTU Board Pattern

> **Problem:** A machine costs **₹8,00,000** with a scrap value of **₹3,00,000** after **5 years**. Find the constant percentage rate of depreciation using CPM, and compute the value at the end of year 3.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$r = 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\frac{3{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/5}$$

$$r = 1 - (0.375)^{0.2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 5th root:**

Using logarithms: $\log(0.375) = -0.4260$

$$\frac{-0.4260}{5} = -0.0852$$

$$(0.375)^{0.2} = 10^{-0.0852} = 0.8219$$

**Step 3 — Compute the rate:**

$$r = 1 - 0.8219 = 0.1781 = \boxed{17.81\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 4 — Value at end of Year 3:**

$$V_3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.1781)^3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (0.8219)^3$$

$$(0.8219)^3 = 0.5553$$

$$V_3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5553 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 4{,}42{,}400 \text{ (approx.)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Fully Worked Numerical Example #3 (SFM) — KTU Board Pattern

> **Problem:** A government school building costs **₹10,00,000**, with a scrap value of **₹2,00,000** and a useful life of **40 years**. Sinking fund is invested at **4% per annum** compounded annually. Find (a) annual sinking fund instalment, (b) depreciation in year 1, year 10, and year 40, and (c) total depreciation in the first 10 years.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the annual instalment $A$:**

$$A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{(10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000)(0.04)}{(1.04)^{40} - 1}$$

$$A = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04}{(1.04)^{40} - 1} = \frac{32{,}000}{4.8010 - 1} = \frac{32{,}000}{3.8010}$$

$$\boxed{A = \text{₹ } 8{,}418.31 \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 2 — Depreciation in Year 1:**

$$D_1 = A(1+i)^{1-1} = A(1.04)^0 = \text{₹ } 8{,}418.31$$

**Step 3 — Depreciation in Year 10:**

$$D_{10} = A(1+i)^9 = 8{,}418.31 \times (1.04)^9$$

$$(1.04)^9 = 1.4233$$

$$D_{10} = 8{,}418.31 \times 1.4233 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 11{,}981.80}$$

**Step 4 — Depreciation in Year 40:**

$$D_{40} = A(1+i)^{39} = 8{,}418.31 \times (1.04)^{39}$$

$$(1.04)^{39} = 4.6164$$

$$D_{40} = 8{,}418.31 \times 4.6164 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 38{,}864.27}$$

**Step 5 — Total Depreciation in first 10 years:**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} D_t = A \cdot \frac{(1.04)^{10} - 1}{0.04}$$

$$(1.04)^{10} = 1.4802$$

$$\sum = 8{,}418.31 \times \frac{1.4802 - 1}{0.04} = 8{,}418.31 \times 12.0061 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 1{,}01{,}071.34}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Depreciation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DEPRECIATION OF A BUILDING] --> B[PHYSICAL DEPRECIATION]
    A --> C[FUNCTIONAL DEPRECIATION]
    A --> D[EXTERNAL / ECONOMIC DEPRECIATION]
    B --> B1[Wear and Tear from Use]
    B --> B2[Action of Natural Elements]
    B --> B3[Material Decay - Rust, Rot, Corrosion]
    C --> C1[Technological Obsolescence]
    C --> C2[Design Deficiency / Poor Layout]
    C --> C3[Outdated Style and Aesthetics]
    D --> D1[Neighbourhood Deterioration]
    D --> D2[Legislative / Zoning Change]
    D --> D3[Reduced Demand for the Property Type]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Methods of Calculating Depreciation

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Known: C, S, n, t] --> M{Select Method}
    M --> P1[SLM]
    M --> P2[CPM - WDV]
    M --> P3[SFM]
    P1 --> F1[Annual Dep = C minus S divided by n]
    P2 --> F2[Rate r = 1 minus S over C to power 1 over n]
    P3 --> F3[Annual Sinking Fund A = C minus S times i divided by one plus i to power n minus 1]
    F1 --> O1[Book Value Vt = C minus Da times t]
    F2 --> O2[Book Value Vt = C times one minus r to power t]
    F3 --> O3[Dep in Year t = A times one plus i to power t minus 1]
    O1 --> OUT[OUTPUT: Depreciated Value of Asset]
    O2 --> OUT
    O3 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Which Method to Apply?

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Asset Type?} -->|Building / Land| Q2{Long Life over 30 years?}
    Q1 -->|Machinery / Vehicle| P2[Use CPM / WDV]
    Q1 -->|Public Infrastructure| P3[Use Sinking Fund Method]
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Use SLM or SFM]
    Q2 -->|No| P2
    P1 --> Q3{Replacement Fund Required?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3
    Q3 -->|No| P1
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Lifecycle of a Building's Value (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Year 0 - Original Cost C] --> S5[Year 5]
    S5 --> S20[Year 20]
    S20 --> S40[Year 40]
    S40 --> S60[Year 60]
    S60 --> SN[Year n - Scrap Value S]
    S0 --- NOTE1[SLM: Linear straight line decline]
    S5 --- NOTE2[CPM: Steep drop early, then slows]
    S20 --- NOTE3[SFM: Slow drop early, then accelerates]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Depreciation. List any four causes of depreciation as per KTU syllabus.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Depreciation is the progressive, permanent, and continuous loss in the value of a property due to wear and tear, action of natural elements, decay, accidents, obsolescence, or the efflux of time.
>
> **Four Causes (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. Wear and tear caused by usage
> 2. Action of natural elements (rain, heat, frost)
> 3. Deterioration/Decay of materials (corrosion, rotting)
> 4. Obsolescence — technological, functional, or economic

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between Straight Line Method and Sinking Fund Method of depreciation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | SLM | SFM |
|-------|-----|-----|
| Annual charge | Constant each year | Increases each year (A + interest) |
| Sinking fund created | No | Yes, at compound interest rate $i$ |
| Book value curve | Linear decline | Convex decline (slow → steep) |
| Formula | $D = (C-S)/n$ | $A = (C-S)i/[(1+i)^n-1]$ |
| Suitable for | Land, short-life structures | Public buildings, long-life assets |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern – Module 4] — Each question carries 14 marks with internal choice between Part (a) and Part (b), typically 7 + 7 split.**

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q. A building originally cost ₹18,00,000. Its estimated life is 60 years and the scrap value is ₹3,00,000. Compute the annual depreciation, the book value at the end of 25 years using the Straight Line Method, and the constant rate of depreciation using the Constant Percentage Method. Also find the value of the building after 25 years using CPM.**

#### (a) Annual Depreciation and Book Value after 25 years using SLM. **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$C = 18{,}00{,}000, \quad S = 3{,}00{,}000, \quad n = 60 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Depreciation:**

$$D_a = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{60} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{60}$$

$$\boxed{D_a = \text{₹ } 25{,}000 \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative Depreciation after 25 years:**

$$D_{25} = 25{,}000 \times 25 = \text{₹ } 6{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Book Value after 25 years:**

$$V_{25} = C - D_{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}25{,}000$$

$$\boxed{V_{25} = \text{₹ } 11{,}75{,}000}$$

#### (b) Constant Rate of Depreciation using CPM and Value after 25 years. **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply the CPM formula:**

$$r = 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\frac{3{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/60}$$

$$r = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^{1/60}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 60th root using logarithms:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) = -0.7782$$

$$\frac{-0.7782}{60} = -0.01297$$

$$\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^{1/60} = 10^{-0.01297} = 0.9660$$

**Step 3 — Compute the rate:**

$$\boxed{r = 1 - 0.9660 = 0.0340 = 3.40\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 4 — Book Value after 25 years using CPM:**

$$V_{25} = C(1 - r)^{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times (0.9660)^{25}$$

$$\log_{10}(0.9660) = -0.01511, \quad \times 25 = -0.3778$$

$$(0.9660)^{25} = 10^{-0.3778} = 0.4187$$

$$V_{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4187 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 7{,}53{,}660}$$

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q. A government hospital building has been constructed at a cost of ₹50,00,000. Its total life is 50 years, after which it will have a scrap value of ₹5,00,000. The sinking fund is to be created at 4% compound interest per annum. Find (a) the annual sinking fund instalment, (b) the depreciation in the 1st, 10th, and 50th year, and (c) the book value of the building at the end of 25 years.**

#### (a) Annual Sinking Fund Instalment and First-Year Depreciation. **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$C = 50{,}00{,}000, \quad S = 5{,}00{,}000, \quad n = 50, \quad i = 0.04$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SFM formula:**

$$A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{(50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000)(0.04)}{(1.04)^{50} - 1}$$

$$A = \frac{45{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04}{(1.04)^{50} - 1} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{7.1067 - 1} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{6.1067}$$

$$\boxed{A = \text{₹ } 29{,}477.10 \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 3 — Depreciation in Year 1:**

$$D_1 = A(1+i)^{1-1} = A(1.04)^0 = \text{₹ } 29{,}477.10$$

#### (b) Depreciation in 10th and 50th Year and Book Value at end of 25th Year. **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Depreciation in Year 10:**

$$D_{10} = A(1+i)^9 = 29{,}477.10 \times (1.04)^9$$

$$(1.04)^9 = 1.4233$$

$$D_{10} = 29{,}477.10 \times 1.4233 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 41{,}960.96}$$

**Step 2 — Depreciation in Year 50:**

$$D_{50} = A(1+i)^{49} = 29{,}477.10 \times (1.04)^{49}$$

$$(1.04)^{49} = 6.8340$$

$$D_{50} = 29{,}477.10 \times 6.8340 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 2{,}01{,}446.24}$$

**Step 3 — Book Value at end of Year 25:**

Total depreciation in first 25 years:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{25} D_t = A \cdot \frac{(1.04)^{25} - 1}{0.04}$$

$$(1.04)^{25} = 2.6658$$

$$\sum = 29{,}477.10 \times \frac{2.6658 - 1}{0.04} = 29{,}477.10 \times 41.6459 = \text{₹ } 12{,}27{,}580.10$$

Book Value:

$$V_{25} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}27{,}580.10 = \boxed{\text{₹ } 37{,}72{,}419.90}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Repeated Every KTU Board Exam):**
>
> 1. **CPM Misapplication:** Students frequently apply rate $r$ to original cost every year. **Always apply to WDV.** If $C = 10$ lakh and $r = 10\%$, Year 1 dep = 1 lakh, Year 2 dep = 0.9 lakh (NOT another 1 lakh).
>
> 2. **SFM Year Numbering:** $D_t = A(1+i)^{t-1}$. At $t = 1$, depreciation is **just $A$** with **no interest component** because the fund was just created. Many students wrongly apply $(1+i)^1$ for year 1.
>
> 3. **Scrap Value Identity Check:** In SLM, at $t = n$ the value **must equal** $S$. In SFM, total of all yearly depreciations **must equal** $(C - S)$. Always cross-verify your answer.
>
> 4. **Logarithm Errors in CPM:** Computing $r$ involves $(S/C)^{1/n}$. **Unit error:** $S/C$ is dimensionless. Most students forget to convert percentage rates to decimals.
>
> 5. **Units Consistency:** $C, S, A$ are in **Rupees (₹)**. $i$ is **decimal** (0.04 not 4%). $n$ is in **years**. $r$ is **decimal per year**.
>
> 6. **Reverse Problem (Find S given r):** If $C = 10L, n = 5, r = 15\%$, then $S = 10,00,000 \times (0.85)^5 = ₹ 4,43,705$. Marks lost if $(0.85)^5$ is computed wrongly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Depreciation**
>
> - ✅ Depreciation is **permanent, continuous, and progressive** — *not* accidental or sudden.
> - ✅ **Three methods:** SLM, CPM (WDV), SFM — all give **same total depreciation $(C-S)$** over the life; they differ only in **timing**.
> - ✅ **SLM:** Linear, constant $D_a = (C-S)/n$. Best for buildings, short-life.
> - ✅ **CPM:** Exponential decay. $r = 1 - (S/C)^{1/n}$. Best for machinery. Standard for Income Tax.
> - ✅ **SFM:** Annuity-based. $A = (C-S) \cdot i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$. Depreciation in year $t$ is $A(1+i)^{t-1}$. Year 1 = $A$, increases yearly. Best for public buildings with replacement fund.
> - ✅ **Sanity check:** Sum of all yearly depreciation = $(C - S)$ in all three methods.
> - ✅ **Causes:** Wear, weather, decay, obsolescence (3 types), accidents, time.
> - ✅ **Typical Indian life values:** Residential = 60–100 yrs, Commercial = 30–60 yrs, Machinery = 15–30 yrs.
> - ✅ **Scrap value** is the **resale/residual value** at end of life, NOT the demolition value of materials.
> - ✅ **Most-tested KTU trick:** "Find the year in which book value = X" using $V_t = C(1-r)^t$ (CPM) or $V_t = C - A[(1+i)^t - 1]/i$ (SFM).
> - ✅ **Always convert percentage to decimal** before substitution.
> - ✅ **Use log tables** (as shown in examples) for non-integer exponents — *must* be shown in exam for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
