topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Obsolescence; Freehold and Leasehold Properties; Methods of Calculating Depreciation – Straight Line Method, Constant Percentage Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: VALUATION FUNDAMENTALS

## 1.1 OBSOLESCENCE — The Silent Value Eroder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition**
> *Obsolescence* is the **loss in the value of a property due to reasons other than physical wear and tear**, arising from superannuation (outdated design, materials, or technology), changing fashion, public taste, or new innovations that render the existing structure functionally or economically inefficient.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Old Smartphone"

Imagine purchasing a flagship smartphone in 2015 for ₹60,000. Today, the phone still works perfectly (no *physical deterioration*). Yet, its market value has crashed to ₹5,000. Why?
- It cannot run modern 5G applications (**functional obsolescence**)
- The design looks outdated compared to bezel-less screens (**aesthetic obsolescence**)
- Repair parts are no longer manufactured (**economic obsolescence**)

Your building is no different. The bricks are intact, the plaster holds firm, but the building still loses value because the world has moved forward. **That is Obsolescence.**

### Categories of Obsolescence

| Type | Meaning | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Obsolescence** | Defects in design, layout, or materials | A 2BHK flat with no attached bathroom in 2025 |
| **Economic Obsolescence** | External factors outside the property | Building located beside a newly-built sewage plant |
| **Technological Obsolescence** | Outdated systems/equipment | An office still using analog telephone exchanges |
| **Aesthetic/Style Obsolescence** | Outdated fashion or taste | 1990s lime-green tiles in a luxury apartment |
| **Legal/Regulatory Obsolescence** | Non-compliance with new codes | A building violating the latest KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU valuation problems, **obsolescence is always estimated as a percentage deduction from the *Gross Depreciated Value* of the property**, typically ranging from **5% to 15%** depending on age, type, and use.

---

## 1.2 FREEHOLD vs. LEASEHOLD PROPERTIES

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freehold Property** — A property in which the owner holds *absolute and perpetual title* to the land and building, with no time-bound expiry of ownership. The owner enjoys *bundle of rights* (right to use, lease, sell, mortgage, bequeath).
>
> **Leasehold Property** — A property held for a *fixed, finite period* under a *lease agreement* with a landlord (lessor). The occupant (lessee) enjoys temporary rights that revert to the lessor upon lease expiry.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Owning a House vs. Renting a Mansion"

- **Freehold = Owning your own home.** You live in it forever, modify it, paint it pink, and pass it to your grandchildren. *No one can take it back.*
- **Leasehold = Renting a luxury mansion for 99 years.** You can live lavishly, but the mansion goes back to the original owner (or their heir) when the lease expires. You cannot claim permanent ownership.

### Comparative Matrix

| Parameter | Freehold | Leasehold |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration of Ownership** | Perpetual (infinite) | Finite (typically 30, 60, 90, or 99 years) |
| **Land Ownership** | Yes — absolute | No — only the *structure* or right to occupy |
| **Right of Transfer** | Unrestricted | Requires lessor's consent + premium |
| **Residual Value** | Always the *full market value* | *Diminishing* as lease expiry approaches |
| **Government Acquisition Risk** | Lower | Higher (land reverts to state/lessor) |
| **Capital Appreciation** | Higher (both land + building rise) | Limited (only building appreciates) |
| **KTU Treatment in Valuation** | Direct Capitalized Value | Capitalized Value = Years' Purchase × Net Annual Income (where YP depends on remaining lease life) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When a lease has, say, 25 years remaining, the *Years' Purchase (YP)* is calculated only over the **unexpired lease period**, not in perpetuity. This is a classic 14-mark question pattern.

---

## 1.3 DEPRECIATION — The Annual Value Tax

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depreciation** is the *gradual, permanent, and continuous reduction in the value of a property* due to **wear and tear, age, action of elements, and obsolescence**, until the property reaches its *scrap/salvage value*.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Car"

You buy a Maruti Swift for ₹8,00,000. Every year:
- The engine loses smoothness (wear)
- The paint fades (age)
- The model becomes "old" (obsolescence)

After 10 years, you might sell it for ₹3,00,000 (scrap value). The ₹5,00,000 lost is *depreciated value*. The yearly amount you "lose" is *annual depreciation*. **The same applies to buildings.**

### Why is Depreciation Important in Valuation?

- It enables a valuer to find the **present worth of an aged structure**.
- It helps recover the **capital outlay** over the asset's life.
- It is mandatory for **insurance claims, tax assessment, and accounting**.

---

## 1.4 Terminology Used in Depreciation

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Original Cost (Capital Outlay)** | Total amount spent to construct/buy the property | $C$ |
| **Salvage/Scrap Value** | Expected value at end of useful life | $S$ |
| **Useful Life** | Total serviceable years of the property | $n$ |
| **Book Value (Depreciated Value)** | Value at any given year $t$ | $D_t$ |
| **Annual Depreciation** | Amount subtracted per year | $D$ |
| **Rate of Depreciation** | Annual depreciation as % of original | $r$ |
| **Sinking Fund** | Money set aside annually to accumulate to $C-S$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Scrap Value** with **Salvage Value**. *Scrap* is the value recovered by demolishing; *Salvage* is the value if the asset is sold as-is. In KTU problems, both are treated as **terminal value $S$** unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 METHODS OF CALCULATING DEPRECIATION

KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasizes **four classical methods**, but the syllabus explicitly requires deep treatment of:

1. **Straight Line Method (SLM)**
2. **Constant Percentage Method (CPM) / Declining Balance Method**
3. Sinking Fund Method
4. Quantity Survey Method

> [!NOTE]
> This module focuses on the **first two** as per the topic brief, with mathematical derivations suitable for 14-mark problems.

---

## 2.2 STRAIGHT LINE METHOD (SLM)

### 2.2.1 Core Concept

In SLM, an **equal amount of depreciation is written off every year** throughout the useful life of the asset, irrespective of wear pattern. The annual depreciation is calculated as a *constant* linear function of time.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Determine the **Original Cost ($C$)** of the property.
2. Determine the **Scrap/Salvage Value ($S$)** at end of life.
3. Determine the **Useful Life ($n$)** in years.
4. Compute **Annual Depreciation = $\dfrac{C - S}{n}$**.
5. The **Book Value at year $t$** is: $D_t = C - t \cdot \dfrac{C - S}{n}$.
6. When $t = n$: $D_n = S$ (residual value).

### 2.2.3 Intuition

Think of a notebook of 100 pages. If you tear out **2 pages every day for 50 days**, the rate of tearing is constant. That's SLM. Buildings, however, often don't depreciate linearly — they lose value faster in later years. That's where CPM comes in.

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Income tax assessment** in India follows a near-SLM pattern (e.g., 10% for residential buildings under the IT Act).
- Used for **straightforward valuations** where age is short and wear is uniform.
- Preferred when the asset has a **long, stable useful life** (e.g., government buildings, RCC structures).

---

## 2.3 CONSTANT PERCENTAGE METHOD (CPM)

### 2.3.1 Core Concept

In CPM, a *fixed percentage* $r$ of the **book value at the start of each year** is written off as depreciation. This results in:
- **Higher depreciation in early years** (when the asset is new)
- **Lower depreciation in later years** (as book value shrinks)
- The book value **never reaches zero** mathematically — it approaches zero asymptotically.

### 2.3.2 Mathematical Form

If $V_0$ = Initial Value and $r$ = Constant percentage (expressed as decimal per year):

$$V_1 = V_0 (1 - r)$$

$$V_2 = V_1 (1 - r) = V_0 (1 - r)^2$$

Generalized form:

$$V_n = V_0 (1 - r)^n$$

This is the **declining balance** geometric decay equation.

### 2.3.3 Intuition

Think of a brand-new car losing 15% of *its current value* every year:
- Year 1: Loses 15% of ₹10,00,000 = ₹1,50,000
- Year 2: Loses 15% of ₹8,50,000 = ₹1,27,500
- Year 3: Loses 15% of ₹7,22,500 = ₹1,08,375

The **absolute amount depreciated decreases**, but the **rate stays the same**. Newer things lose more value in rupees.

### 2.3.4 Real-World Utility

- **Vehicle valuation, machinery, and equipment** commonly use CPM.
- KTU 2024 recommends CPM for **properties with rapid technological obsolescence** (e.g., commercial IT buildings, factory plants).

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU Module 4 numericals.**

| # | Method | Annual Depreciation ($D$) | Book Value at Year $t$ ($V_t$) | Rate $r$ Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Straight Line** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $V_t = C - t \cdot \dfrac{C-S}{n}$ | $r = \dfrac{C - S}{n \cdot C} \times 100$ |
| 2 | **Constant Percentage** | $D_t = r \cdot V_{t-1}$ | $V_t = C \cdot (1 - r)^t$ | $r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$ |
| 3 | **Sinking Fund** | $D = \dfrac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1 + i)^n - 1}$ | $V_t = C - D \cdot t + \text{SF accumulation}$ | — |
| 4 | **Quantity Survey** | $D = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Materials at Present}}{\text{Life in years}}$ | — | — |

### 2.4.1 Critical Boundary Conditions

- In **SLM**: If $S = 0$ (no scrap value), then $D = C/n$ and $V_t = C(1 - t/n)$.
- In **CPM**: To find $r$ given $C$, $S$, and $n$:

$$r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$$

> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** The "constant percentage $r$" in CPM is **NOT** the same as $\dfrac{C-S}{nC}$ from SLM. They are mathematically different.

### 2.4.2 Unit Conventions

- All costs in **INR (₹)** or lakhs/crores consistently.
- Useful life $n$ in **years**.
- Rate $r$ as a **decimal** in formulas; as a **percentage** in answers.
- Years' Purchase (YP) in **pure numbers**.

---

## 2.5 EFFECT OF OBSOLESCENCE ON VALUATION

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard KTU valuation formula incorporating obsolescence is:

$$\text{Valued Present Worth} = \text{Depreciated Value} - \text{Obsolescence Deduction}$$

Where:

$$\text{Obsolescence Deduction} = p\% \times \text{Depreciated Value}$$

Typical $p$ values:

| Property Condition | Obsolescence % ($p$) |
|---|---|
| Modern, well-maintained | 0–5% |
| Slightly outdated but functional | 5–10% |
| Significantly outdated | 10–15% |
| Severely obsolete (legal non-compliance) | 15–25% |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 4: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION: STRAIGHT LINE METHOD (SLM)

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Equation

Let:
- $C$ = Original cost of the property
- $S$ = Salvage value at the end of useful life
- $n$ = Useful life in years
- $V_t$ = Book value at end of year $t$

**Step 1:** Total depreciation over the asset's life = $C - S$.

**Step 2:** Since depreciation is uniform, the annual depreciation is:

$$D = \dfrac{\text{Total Depreciation}}{\text{Useful Life}} = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$$

**Step 3:** After $t$ years, accumulated depreciation = $t \cdot D$.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the book value at year $t$ is:

$$V_t = C - t \cdot D = C - t \cdot \dfrac{C - S}{n}$$

**Step 5:** Verification: At $t = n$,

$$V_n = C - n \cdot \dfrac{C - S}{n} = C - (C - S) = S \checkmark$$

This confirms the boundary condition.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example (SLM)

> **Problem:** A building was constructed at a cost of ₹25,00,000. Its estimated life is 50 years, with a salvage value of ₹5,00,000. Calculate the depreciation for the 30th year and the book value at the end of the 30th year.

**Solution:**

Annual Depreciation:

$$D = \dfrac{C - S}{n} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{50} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{50} = ₹40{,}000$$

Since SLM has **constant annual depreciation**, depreciation for the 30th year is also **₹40,000**.

Book value at end of 30th year:

$$V_{30} = C - 30 \cdot D = 25{,}00{,}000 - 30 \times 40{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000$$

$$V_{30} = ₹13{,}00{,}000$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION: CONSTANT PERCENTAGE METHOD (CPM)

### 3.2.1 Setting Up the Geometric Decay

Let:
- $C$ = Original cost
- $r$ = Constant annual rate of depreciation (as decimal)
- $n$ = Useful life
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of year $n$

**Step 1:** After Year 1, the book value is:

$$V_1 = C - rC = C(1 - r)$$

**Step 2:** After Year 2, depreciation is applied on the *reduced* book value $V_1$:

$$V_2 = V_1 - rV_1 = V_1(1 - r) = C(1 - r)^2$$

**Step 3:** Continuing this pattern, after $n$ years:

$$V_n = C(1 - r)^n$$

**Step 4:** By definition, $V_n = S$ (salvage value), so:

$$S = C(1 - r)^n$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $r$:

$$(1 - r)^n = \dfrac{S}{C}$$

$$1 - r = \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$$

$$\boxed{r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}}$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Example (CPM)

> **Problem:** A property is purchased for ₹10,00,000 with a useful life of 20 years and salvage value of ₹2,00,000. Calculate the constant percentage of depreciation and the book value after 5 years.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the constant rate $r$:

$$r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/20}$$

$$r = 1 - (0.2)^{0.05}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(0.2)^{0.05}$:

$$\ln(0.2) = -1.6094$$

$$0.05 \times (-1.6094) = -0.08047$$

$$e^{-0.08047} = 0.92266$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$r = 1 - 0.92266 = 0.07734 \approx 7.73\%$$

**Step 4:** Book value after 5 years:

$$V_5 = C(1 - r)^5 = 10{,}00{,}000 \times (0.92266)^5$$

**Step 5:** Compute $(0.92266)^5$:

$$(0.92266)^2 = 0.85130$$

$$(0.92266)^4 = 0.85130^2 = 0.72472$$

$$(0.92266)^5 = 0.72472 \times 0.92266 = 0.66866$$

**Step 6:** Final book value:

$$V_5 = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.66866 = ₹6{,}68{,}660$$

---

## 3.3 COMPARATIVE NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (SLM vs CPM)

> **Problem:** A building costs ₹20,00,000, with a 25-year life and ₹2,50,000 salvage value. Compare SLM and CPM by computing the book value at the end of 10 years.

### 3.3.1 SLM Approach

Annual Depreciation:

$$D = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000}{25} = \dfrac{17{,}50{,}000}{25} = ₹70{,}000$$

Book Value at Year 10:

$$V_{10} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 10 \times 70{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000 = ₹13{,}00{,}000$$

### 3.3.2 CPM Approach

Constant rate $r$:

$$r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/25} = 1 - (0.125)^{0.04}$$

$$\ln(0.125) = -2.0794$$

$$0.04 \times (-2.0794) = -0.08318$$

$$e^{-0.08318} = 0.92018$$

$$r = 1 - 0.92018 = 0.07982 \approx 7.98\%$$

Book Value at Year 10:

$$V_{10} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.07982)^{10} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times (0.92018)^{10}$$

**Computing $(0.92018)^{10}$:**

$$(0.92018)^2 = 0.84673$$

$$(0.92018)^4 = 0.84673^2 = 0.71695$$

$$(0.92018)^5 = 0.71695 \times 0.92018 = 0.65972$$

$$(0.92018)^{10} = (0.65972)^2 = 0.43523$$

Therefore:

$$V_{10} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.43523 = ₹8{,}70{,}460$$

### 3.3.3 Comparison Table

| Method | Annual Depreciation (Year 1) | Book Value at Year 10 | Depreciation Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SLM** | ₹70,000 (constant) | ₹13,00,000 | Linear, equal yearly |
| **CPM** | ₹1,59,640 (decreasing) | ₹8,70,460 | Geometric, front-loaded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** CPM depreciates the asset **more aggressively in early years** and is therefore a more *conservative* valuation approach. SLM is *more lenient*. KTU prefers CPM for **rapidly obsolescing assets**.

---

## 3.4 LEASEHOLD PROPERTY VALUATION — Analytical Derivation

### 3.4.1 Years' Purchase (YP) for Leasehold

For a freehold property held in perpetuity, the YP for annual income $I$ at rate $i$ is:

$$YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$$

For a **leasehold** with $m$ years remaining, using YP formula for a *limited period*:

$$YP = \dfrac{(1 + i)^m - 1}{i(1 + i)^m}$$

The **Capitalized Value (CV)** of the leasehold is:

$$CV = I \times YP_{limited}$$

### 3.4.2 Worked Example (Leasehold)

> **Problem:** A shop is on a 30-year lease with ₹60,000 net annual income. The market rate of interest is 8%. Calculate the capitalized value of the leasehold interest.

**Solution:**

$$YP_{30 \text{ years at } 8\%} = \dfrac{(1.08)^{30} - 1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^{30}}$$

Compute $(1.08)^{30}$:

$$(1.08)^{10} = 2.1589$$

$$(1.08)^{20} = 2.1589^2 = 4.6610$$

$$(1.08)^{30} = 4.6610 \times 2.1589 = 10.0627$$

Therefore:

$$YP = \dfrac{10.0627 - 1}{0.08 \times 10.0627} = \dfrac{9.0627}{0.8050} = 11.258$$

Capitalized Value:

$$CV = 60{,}000 \times 11.258 = ₹6{,}75{,}480$$

---

## 3.5 FULL INTEGRATED VALUATION PROBLEM

> **Problem:** A 2-storey RCC building on a freehold plot was constructed 25 years ago at ₹18,00,000. The estimated total life is 60 years with a salvage value of ₹3,00,000. The structure is technologically outdated (functional obsolescence = 10%). Calculate the present value of the property using: (a) SLM, (b) CPM. Assume $C = 18{,}00{,}000$, $S = 3{,}00{,}000$, $n = 60$, $t = 25$.

### 3.5.1 Part (a) — SLM Solution

**Step 1:** Annual Depreciation:

$$D = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{60} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{60} = ₹25{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Book Value at Year 25:

$$V_{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 25 \times 25{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}25{,}000 = ₹11{,}75{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Apply Obsolescence Deduction (10%):

$$\text{Present Value} = 11{,}75{,}000 - 0.10 \times 11{,}75{,}000 = 11{,}75{,}000 - 1{,}17{,}500$$

$$\boxed{\text{Present Value (SLM)} = ₹10{,}57{,}500}$$

### 3.5.2 Part (b) — CPM Solution

**Step 1:** Find $r$:

$$r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/60} = 1 - (0.1667)^{1/60}$$

$$\ln(0.1667) = -1.7918$$

$$\dfrac{-1.7918}{60} = -0.02986$$

$$e^{-0.02986} = 0.9706$$

$$r = 1 - 0.9706 = 0.0294 \approx 2.94\%$$

**Step 2:** Book Value at Year 25:

$$V_{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.0294)^{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times (0.9706)^{25}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(0.9706)^{25}$:

Using $\ln(0.9706) = -0.02985$:

$$25 \times (-0.02985) = -0.7462$$

$$e^{-0.7462} = 0.4742$$

**Step 4:** Book Value:

$$V_{25} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4742 = ₹8{,}53{,}560$$

**Step 5:** Apply Obsolescence Deduction (10%):

$$\text{Present Value} = 8{,}53{,}560 - 0.10 \times 8{,}53{,}560 = 8{,}53{,}560 - 85{,}356$$

$$\boxed{\text{Present Value (CPM)} = ₹7{,}68{,}204}$$

### 3.5.3 Result Comparison

| Method | Gross Depreciated Value | Obsolescence (10%) | **Net Present Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SLM** | ₹11,75,000 | ₹1,17,500 | **₹10,57,500** |
| **CPM** | ₹8,53,560 | ₹85,356 | **₹7,68,204** |

> [!NOTE]
> The CPM yields a **lower valuation** (₹7,68,204 < ₹10,57,500) because it depreciates the asset more steeply in early years — making it the **more conservative** method preferred for income tax and insurance assessments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Depreciation Valuation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Cost C] --> B[Determine Salvage Value S]
    B --> C[Determine Useful Life n]
    C --> D{Choose Method}
    D --> E1[Straight Line Method]
    D --> E2[Constant Percentage Method]
    D --> E3[Sinking Fund Method]
    E1 --> F1[D = C-S over n]
    E2 --> F2[r = 1 minus S/C power 1/n]
    E3 --> F3[D = C-S times i over power n minus 1]
    F1 --> G[Compute Book Value Vt]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Apply Obsolescence Deduction p percent]
    H --> I[Final Valued Present Worth]
```

## 4.2 SLM vs CPM — Decay Curve Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLM_GROUP["SLM Pattern"]
        A1[Year 0: Full Value C] --> A2[Year n/2: C minus half of C-S]
        A2 --> A3[Year n: Salvage Value S]
    end
    subgraph CPM_GROUP["CPM Pattern"]
        B1[Year 0: Full Value C] --> B2[Year n/2: C times 1-r power n/2]
        B2 --> B3[Year n: Salvage Value S]
    end
    SLM_GROUP -.->|Linear Decay| SHARED[Same Start and End]
    CPM_GROUP -.->|Geometric Decay| SHARED
```

## 4.3 Freehold vs Leasehold Ownership Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Property Type] --> FH[Freehold]
    P --> LH[Leasehold]
    FH --> FH1[Absolute Title in Perpetuity]
    FH --> FH2[Bundle of Rights Granted]
    FH --> FH3[YP equals 1 over i]
    FH --> FH4[Highest Capital Value]
    LH --> LH1[Fixed Term Lease Agreement]
    LH --> LH2[Limited Right to Occupy]
    LH --> LH3[YP depends on Unexpired Lease Life]
    LH --> LH4[Reverts to Lessor on Expiry]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Integrated Valuation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Input Parameters: C, S, n, t, r, p] --> METH{Method Selector}
    METH --> SLM[SLM Engine]
    METH --> CPM[CPM Engine]
    METH --> SFM[Sinking Fund Engine]
    SLM --> CALC1[Annual Dep D equals C-S over n]
    CPM --> CALC2[Rate r equals 1 minus S/C power 1/n]
    SFM --> CALC3[D equals C-S times i over power n minus 1]
    CALC1 --> BV[Book Value Vt Computation]
    CALC2 --> BV
    CALC3 --> BV
    BV --> OBS[Apply Obsolescence Factor p percent]
    OBS --> OUT[Final Present Worth of Property]
```

## 4.5 Obsolescence Influence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OBS_ROOT[Obsolescence Factors] --> F1[Functional Defects]
    OBS_ROOT --> F2[Economic Externalities]
    OBS_ROOT --> F3[Technological Outdating]
    OBS_ROOT --> F4[Aesthetic/Regulatory Non-Compliance]
    F1 --> DC[Deduct from Depreciated Value]
    F2 --> DC
    F3 --> DC
    F4 --> DC
    DC --> NPW[Net Present Worth of Property]
```

## 4.6 Leasehold YP Decay Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0: Full Lease Value] --> Y10[Year 10: 88 percent of Original]
    Y10 --> Y50[Year 50: 65 percent of Original]
    Y50 --> Y99[Year 99: 35 percent of Original]
    Y99 --> Y100[Year 100: Reverts to Lessor]
```

# MODULE 4: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Obsolescence" as applied to the valuation of a building. List any four causes of obsolescence.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Obsolescence** is the loss in the value of a property due to reasons other than physical wear and tear, arising from outdated design, materials, technology, or changes in public taste and legal requirements.

**Four Causes:**
1. **Functional Obsolescence** — Defects in design or layout (e.g., lack of ventilation in modern standards)
2. **Economic Obsolescence** — External factors like proximity to nuisance facilities
3. **Technological Obsolescence** — Outdated electrical/plumbing systems
4. **Aesthetic/Legal Obsolescence** — Old style or non-compliance with current building rules

**Marks Distribution:** Definition — 1 mark; 4 causes — 0.5 each = 2 marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Freehold and Leasehold properties in valuation context.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Freehold | Leasehold |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership Duration | Perpetual | Fixed term (30/60/99 years) |
| Title | Absolute | Limited/conditional |
| Capital Value | Higher (land + structure) | Lower (only structure) |
| YP Used | $1/i$ (in perpetuity) | Limited period YP |
| Reverts to Lessor | No | Yes, on expiry |

**Marks Distribution:** 3 key differences — 1 mark each.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS

### INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — SELECT ONE

---

### QUESTION A (14 MARKS) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A building was constructed at a cost of ₹30,00,000 with an estimated useful life of 50 years and a salvage value of ₹5,00,000. The building is 20 years old.**

**(a)** Calculate the annual depreciation and the present depreciated value using the **Straight Line Method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same building shows 8% obsolescence, determine the **net present value** of the property. Also briefly explain why obsolescence is deducted **after** depreciation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

### Part (a) — SLM Calculation

**Given:**
- $C = ₹30,00,000$
- $S = ₹5,00,000$
- $n = 50$ years
- $t = 20$ years

**Step 1:** Annual Depreciation:

$$D = \dfrac{C - S}{n} = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{50} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{50} = ₹50{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Depreciated Value at Year 20:

$$V_{20} = C - t \cdot D = 30{,}00{,}000 - 20 \times 50{,}000$$

$$V_{20} = 30{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹20{,}00{,}000$$

**Answer (a):** Annual Depreciation = **₹50,000/year**; Present Depreciated Value = **₹20,00,000**.

---

### Part (b) — Obsolescence Deduction

**Step 3:** Calculate 8% obsolescence on depreciated value:

$$\text{Obsolescence} = 0.08 \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Net Present Value:

$$NPV = 20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 = ₹18{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 5: Why is obsolescence deducted *after* depreciation?**

Obsolescence is a **secondary, conditional loss** that is applied only **after** computing the physical/age-based value. The logical order is:
1. First, account for **physical wear and tear** (depreciation).
2. Then, account for **value lost due to obsolescence** (since the building is otherwise physically sound).

If obsolescence is deducted first, the depreciation base becomes artificially small, underestimating the actual physical loss. Hence, KTU mandates the sequence: **Depreciation → Obsolescence → Net Value**.

**Answer (b):** Obsolescence = **₹1,60,000**; Net Present Value = **₹18,40,000**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 MARKS) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A property was purchased for ₹15,00,000. The expected useful life is 25 years with a salvage value of ₹3,00,000. The property is currently 10 years old.**

**(a)** Determine the **constant percentage rate of depreciation** per annum and the **present book value** using the **Constant Percentage Method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the present book value obtained in (a) with what would be obtained using the **Straight Line Method** for the same age, and comment on which method is more appropriate for **rapidly obsolescing commercial buildings**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

### Part (a) — CPM Calculation

**Given:**
- $C = ₹15,00,000$
- $S = ₹3,00,000$
- $n = 25$ years
- $t = 10$ years

**Step 1:** Find the constant rate $r$:

$$r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/25}$$

$$r = 1 - (0.2)^{0.04}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(0.2)^{0.04}$:

$$\ln(0.2) = -1.6094$$

$$0.04 \times (-1.6094) = -0.06438$$

$$e^{-0.06438} = 0.93765$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$r = 1 - 0.93765 = 0.06235 \approx 6.24\%$$

**Step 4:** Present Book Value at Year 10:

$$V_{10} = C(1 - r)^{10} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times (0.93765)^{10}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $(0.93765)^{10}$:

$$\ln(0.93765) = -0.06438$$

$$10 \times (-0.06438) = -0.6438$$

$$e^{-0.6438} = 0.5254$$

**Step 6:** Book Value:

$$V_{10} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5254 = ₹7{,}88{,}100$$

**Answer (a):** Constant rate $r \approx$ **6.24% per annum**; Present Book Value = **₹7,88,100**.

---

### Part (b) — SLM Comparison & Method Justification

**Step 1:** SLM Annual Depreciation:

$$D_{SLM} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{25} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{25} = ₹48{,}000$$

**Step 2:** SLM Book Value at Year 10:

$$V_{10, SLM} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 10 \times 48{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = ₹10{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Comparison Table:

| Method | Book Value at Year 10 |
|---|---|
| **CPM** | ₹7,88,100 |
| **SLM** | ₹10,20,000 |
| **Difference** | ₹2,31,900 |

**Step 4: Which is more appropriate for rapidly obsolescing commercial buildings?**

The **Constant Percentage Method (CPM)** is more appropriate because:

1. **Front-loaded depreciation** — Newer commercial assets lose value rapidly due to technological changes (HVAC, IT cabling, automation). CPM captures this.
2. **Realistic reflection of market behavior** — Used commercial buildings in tech parks (e.g., Kerala's Infopark) lose 30–40% value in the first 5 years and stabilize later — exactly the CPM pattern.
3. **SLM is too lenient** — It assumes equal wear throughout life, which doesn't match modern commercial real estate trends.

**Answer (b):** SLM gives ₹10,20,000; CPM gives ₹7,88,100. **CPM is more appropriate** for rapidly obsolescing commercial buildings.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4 (KTU 2024)**
>
> 1. **Confusing $r$ in CPM with SLM rate** — Students often compute $r = (C-S)/nC$ and apply it to $(1-r)^t$. This is **mathematically wrong**. Use $r = 1 - (S/C)^{1/n}$.
> 2. **Wrong order of deductions** — Obsolescence is **deducted AFTER** depreciation, not before. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 3. **Leasehold YP confusion** — For limited-period leases, YP ≠ $1/i$. Use the **inwoodized annuity formula** with unexpired lease life.
> 4. **Not mentioning salvage value** — If $S = 0$ is given, say so explicitly. Don't write SLM formula without verifying $S$.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — Write costs in **₹ or lakhs**, $n$ in **years**, $r$ in **% per annum**. Examiner penalizes inconsistent units.
> 6. **No final sentence in Part A** — Always conclude with a 1-line summary or definition recall.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Depreciation & Obsolescence**

### Obsolescence
- ☐ Loss of value **NOT due to physical wear**
- ☐ Five types: Functional, Economic, Technological, Aesthetic, Legal
- ☐ Deducted **after** depreciation, as a **% of depreciated value** (typically 5–15%)

### Freehold vs Leasehold
- ☐ **Freehold** → Perpetual ownership, YP = $1/i$, full capital value
- ☐ **Leasehold** → Fixed term, YP = limited period annuity factor, value reverts at expiry
- ☐ **Lease expiry** is a critical valuation event in KTU problems

### Straight Line Method (SLM)
- ☐ Annual Depreciation: $D = (C - S)/n$
- ☐ Book Value at year $t$: $V_t = C - t(C-S)/n$
- ☐ **Constant** yearly depreciation
- ☐ Used for: long-life structures, government buildings, tax assessment

### Constant Percentage Method (CPM)
- ☐ Rate: $r = 1 - (S/C)^{1/n}$
- ☐ Book Value: $V_t = C(1-r)^t$
- ☐ **Geometric decay** — front-loaded depreciation
- ☐ Used for: machinery, vehicles, rapidly obsolescing commercial assets

### Key Numerical Habits
- ☐ Always write **Given Data** at the start of the solution
- ☐ Show **substitution step** before final answer
- ☐ Round $r$ to **2 decimal places** in %
- ☐ Always verify boundary: $V_n = S$ (sanity check)
- ☐ For leasehold: state **unexpired lease life** explicitly

### Common Examiner-Expected Phrases
- ☐ *"Obsolescence is a secondary loss applied after depreciation."*
- ☐ *"SLM assumes uniform wear, which is unrealistic for modern commercial assets."*
- ☐ *"CPM is preferred for income tax and insurance assessments due to its conservative nature."*
- ☐ *"Freehold property commands higher YP as it is held in perpetuity."*

### KTU 2024 Mandatory Topics (This Module)
- ☐ **Obsolescence** — definition, types, deduction logic
- ☐ **Freehold & Leasehold** — comparison, YP distinction
- ☐ **SLM** — formula, derivation, numerical
- ☐ **CPM** — formula, derivation, numerical
- ☐ **Comparison** — when to use which method
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sinking Fund Method and Quantity Survey Method; Methods of Valuation – Rental Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sinking Fund Method

### Formal Definition
The **Sinking Fund Method** is a depreciation calculation technique in valuation engineering wherein the owner of a depreciable asset (typically a building) sets aside a fixed sum of money every year, which when accumulated at compound interest, equals the original cost of the asset at the end of its useful life. The total annual depreciation is the sum of the annual interest on the capital invested and the annual sinking fund installment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This method is explicitly listed under the "Methods of Depreciation" sub-module and is favoured by public sector bodies, insurance companies, and government departments because it ensures the full replacement cost is available at the end of the asset's life.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you own a car worth ₹10,00,000 expected to last **10 years**. Rather than paying a large lump sum for a new car later, you open a "new car piggy bank." Every year you deposit a fixed amount, say around ₹70,000 (depending on the interest rate). Because the bank pays you interest on your savings, the deposit grows larger each year. After **10 years**, the piggy bank — original deposits + accumulated interest — holds exactly ₹10,00,000, enough to buy a brand-new car.

In engineering terms, the **piggy bank deposit** is the *sinking fund installment*, the **interest earned** on the original cost is the *interest on capital*, and the **two combined** represent the *true annual depreciation* of the car.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics: Original cost is denoted as **P** (₹), Useful life as **n** (years), Rate of interest as **r** (decimal), and annual sinking fund installment as **A** (₹/year).

---

## 1.2 Quantity Survey Method

### Formal Definition
The **Quantity Survey Method** (also called the *Detailed Estimate Method* or *Replacement Cost Method*) is the most accurate and scientific procedure for determining the present value of a property. It involves preparing a detailed item-wise estimate of quantities of all materials (brickwork, plaster, woodwork, steel, etc.) and labour, then valuing these quantities at **prevailing market rates**. Appropriate depreciation is then deducted from the gross replacement cost to obtain the depreciated value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is "accurate"**: Unlike rough thumb-rule methods, this technique actually counts cubic metres of concrete, square metres of plastering, and tonnes of steel — making it indispensable for insurance valuation, court disputes, and valuation of heritage or altered buildings.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of reconstructing a vintage bungalow today. A valuer does not guess; he prepares a "shopping list" — *10,000 bricks, 50 bags of cement, 80 kg of steel, 200 m² of vitrified tiles, 45 carpenter-days of labour* — and then asks the local hardware store for current prices. Total cost = today's cost of an identical new building. Subtract age-based wear-and-tear, and you have today's value of the old bungalow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predefined Constants:** The life of various building components (e.g., RCC roof = **100 years**, plastered surface = **5–10 years**, teak woodwork = **60–80 years**) are standard IS-code-derived values often provided in the KTU exam problem statements.

---

## 1.3 Rental Method of Valuation

### Formal Definition
The **Rental Method of Valuation** is a capitalization-based approach used to determine the capital (present market) value of a property that is — or could reasonably be — let out on rent. The **Net Annual Rent** (gross rent minus all outgoings such as property tax, insurance, repairs, management charges, and vacancy allowance) is multiplied by the **Years' Purchase (YP)** factor to obtain the capitalized value of the property.

$$\text{Capitalized Value} = \text{Net Annual Rent} \times \text{YP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** This topic is consistently asked in Part-A (3 marks) and Part-B (14 marks) sections. Mastery of the *Years' Purchase* (YP) formula table is non-negotiable.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Suppose a shop in your locality earns the owner a steady **net** profit of ₹5,00,000 per year after all expenses. If a safe investment (e.g., a bank fixed deposit) earns 10% interest annually, what lump sum should you invest today to get ₹5,00,000 every year? The answer is ₹50,00,000 (because 10% of 50 lakhs = 5 lakhs). This 10 is the "Years' Purchase" (1/r). The principle: *a stream of future income is worth a present lump sum equal to that income divided by the safe interest rate.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sinking Fund Method — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify Original Cost (P):** The capital initially invested in the property (excluding land cost, since land is not depreciable).

**Step 2 — Identify Useful Life (n):** The economic life-span of the building in years, beyond which it must be replaced.

**Step 3 — Identify Rate of Interest (r):** The safe compound interest rate at which the sinking fund deposits earn interest (e.g., 4%, 5%, 6% per annum).

**Step 4 — Calculate the Sinking Fund Coefficient (SFC):**
This is the annual deposit that, together with compound interest, accumulates to Re. 1 at the end of *n* years.

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Annual Sinking Fund Installment (A):**

$$A = P \times \text{SFC} = \frac{P \cdot r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Annual Interest on Capital:**

$$I = P \times r$$

**Step 7 — Total Annual Depreciation:**

$$D = I + A = P \cdot r + \frac{P \cdot r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 8 — Total Depreciation over 'n' years:** By definition, the *sum* of all sinking fund installments over the life equals the original cost *P*, so the *total* accumulated depreciation is exactly the original cost of the building (excluding land).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why":** Why two components? Because the owner suffers TWO losses every year — (i) the loss of interest on the locked capital (opportunity cost), and (ii) the wear-and-tear (which is recovered via the sinking fund so that a new building can be bought at the end of *n* years).

---

## 2.2 Quantity Survey Method — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Detailed Measurement:** Take off quantities of every trade — earthwork, concrete, brickwork, plastering, flooring, woodwork, painting, plumbing, electrification, etc., using standard methods of measurement (IS 1200).

**Step 2 — Rate Analysis:** Use current Public Works Department (PWD) Schedule of Rates (SOR) or local market rates for each item.

**Step 3 — Gross Replacement Cost:** Multiply quantities × rates and sum to obtain the present-day cost of rebuilding.

**Step 4 — Age of Property:** Determine the actual age (or effective age) of the building.

**Step 5 — Apply Depreciation:** Apply appropriate depreciation — either a flat percentage, an observed-condition method, or a combination.

**Step 6 — Depreciated Value:**

$$\text{Depreciated Value} = \text{Gross Replacement Cost} - \text{Depreciation}$$

---

## 2.3 Rental Method — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Gross Annual Rent:** Collect the prevailing gross rent per month × 12.

**Step 2 — Compute Outgoings:** Sum all annual recurring expenses:
- Property / Municipal Tax
- Repairs & Maintenance
- Insurance Premium
- Management Charges (5–10% of gross rent)
- Sinking Fund for renewals
- Vacancy Allowance (usually 2–5%)

**Step 3 — Net Annual Rent:**

$$\text{NAR} = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{Total Outgoings}$$

**Step 4 — Select Appropriate YP:**

| Income Type | Years' Purchase (YP) Formula |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| Perpetual (freehold) income | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ |
| Limited period of *n* years | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$ |
| Deferred annuity (after *t* years, for *n* years) | $YP = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{t}} \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$ |
| Reversion after *n* years of Rs. 1 | $YP = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}$ |

**Step 5 — Capitalized Value:**

$$\text{Capitalized Value} = \text{NAR} \times \text{YP}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Key Formula | Application / Units |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Sinking Fund Coefficient | $SFC = \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n}-1}$ | Re. 1 to accumulate to Re. 1 in *n* years at rate *r* |
| Annual Sinking Fund | $A = P \times SFC$ | ₹ per year |
| Interest on Capital | $I = P \cdot r$ | ₹ per year |
| Annual Depreciation | $D = P \cdot r + P \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n}-1}$ | ₹ per year |
| Quantity Survey Gross Cost | $C_{g} = \sum (Q_{i} \times R_{i})$ | ₹ |
| Depreciated Value (QS) | $V_{d} = C_{g} - D_{total}$ | ₹ |
| Net Annual Rent | $NAR = \text{Gross Rent} - \text{Outgoings}$ | ₹ per year |
| YP — Perpetual | $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ | Dimensionless |
| YP — Limited Period | $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | Dimensionless |
| YP — Deferred | $YP_{def} = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{t}} \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Capitalized Value | $V_{c} = NAR \times YP$ | ₹ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The sinking fund method is used by municipal corporations and government PWDs to fund the replacement of bridges, public buildings, and water-supply assets. The rental method is used by chartered valuers, banks (for home-loan collateral), and income-tax officers (for property tax assessment).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sinking Fund Coefficient

**Given:** A sum of Re. 1 deposited at the end of each year for *n* years earns compound interest at rate *r* per annum. We want the accumulated sum to equal Re. 1 at the end of *n* years (i.e., the cost *P* of the asset).

Let the annual deposit be *A*. The first deposit made at the end of Year 1 will earn interest for (*n* − 1) years; the second deposit will earn interest for (*n* − 2) years; …; the last deposit made at the end of Year *n* will earn zero interest.

$$\text{Total Accumulated Sum} = A(1+r)^{n-1} + A(1+r)^{n-2} + \dots + A(1+r)^{1} + A(1+r)^{0}$$

$$\text{Total} = A \cdot \left[ (1+r)^{n-1} + (1+r)^{n-2} + \dots + (1+r) + 1 \right]$$

This is a geometric progression with first term 1, common ratio (1+r), and *n* terms. The sum of a G.P. is:

$$\text{Sum} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{(1+r) - 1} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

**Setting the accumulated sum equal to the original cost P:**

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

$$\Rightarrow A = P \cdot \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1} = P \cdot \text{SFC}$$

> This is the **Sinking Fund Installment** formula. The factor $\dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ is, by definition, the Sinking Fund Coefficient (SFC).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Sinking Fund Method

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A building was constructed at a cost of ₹15,00,000 (excluding land). The expected life is **50 years** and the rate of interest is **5% per annum**. Calculate the annual sinking fund installment, the annual interest on capital, and the total annual depreciation using the Sinking Fund Method. Also find the book value at the end of 20 years.

### Given Data
- $P = 15{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $n = 50$ years
- $r = 5\% = 0.05$

### Step 1 — Calculate Sinking Fund Coefficient

$$(1+r)^{n} = (1.05)^{50}$$

Computing $(1.05)^{50}$:
$$(1.05)^{10} \approx 1.62889$$
$$(1.05)^{20} \approx (1.62889)^{2} \approx 2.65330$$
$$(1.05)^{50} = (1.05)^{20} \times (1.05)^{20} \times (1.05)^{10}$$
$$(1.05)^{50} \approx 2.65330 \times 2.65330 \times 1.62889$$
$$(1.05)^{50} \approx 11.4674$$

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{0.05}{11.4674 - 1} = \frac{0.05}{10.4674} \approx 0.004777$$

### Step 2 — Annual Sinking Fund Installment (A)

$$A = P \times \text{SFC} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.004777$$
$$\boxed{A \approx \text{₹ } 7{,}166 \text{ per year}}$$

### Step 3 — Interest on Capital (I)

$$I = P \cdot r = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = \text{₹ } 75{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 4 — Total Annual Depreciation (D)

$$D = I + A = 75{,}000 + 7{,}166$$
$$\boxed{D \approx \text{₹ } 82{,}166 \text{ per year}}$$

### Step 5 — Book Value at the End of 20 Years

$$\text{Depreciation accumulated} = D \times 20 = 82{,}166 \times 20 = \text{₹ } 16{,}43{,}320$$

But this exceeds the original cost *P* = ₹15,00,000. Hence, the book value is **clamped to zero** at the moment depreciation equals or exceeds *P*. In KTU exam practice, the book value is computed as:

$$\text{Book Value} = P - (\text{Accrued Sinking Fund})$$

Accrued Sinking Fund = Total accumulated value of deposits for 20 years:

$$\text{Sum of Sinking Fund for 20 yrs} = A \cdot \frac{(1.05)^{20} - 1}{0.05}$$

$$(1.05)^{20} \approx 2.6533$$

$$\text{Sum} = 7{,}166 \times \frac{2.6533 - 1}{0.05} = 7{,}166 \times \frac{1.6533}{0.05} = 7{,}166 \times 33.066$$

$$\text{Sum} \approx \text{₹ } 2{,}36{,}951$$

$$\text{Book Value} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}36{,}951 = \text{₹ } 12{,}63{,}049$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Quantity Survey Method

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A residential building has the following details. Estimate its present value using the Quantity Survey Method. The building is 15 years old.

| Item | Quantity | Rate (₹) |
|------|----------|----------|
| Earthwork excavation | 120 m³ | 350 |
| PCC for foundation | 45 m³ | 6,500 |
| Brickwork in foundation | 60 m³ | 7,200 |
| Brickwork in superstructure | 280 m³ | 7,800 |
| RCC roof slab | 38 m³ | 18,500 |
| Cement plastering | 950 m² | 280 |
| Flooring (vitrified tiles) | 220 m² | 1,400 |
| Teak woodwork (doors & windows) | 6.5 m³ | 1,80,000 |
| Painting (2 coats) | 1,150 m² | 95 |
| Plumbing & Sanitary | Lump sum | 1,20,000 |
| Electrification | Lump sum | 95,000 |

Assume the average useful life of the building is 60 years and the salvage value is 10% of the gross cost.

### Step 1 — Calculate Gross Replacement Cost

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Earthwork} & = 120 \times 350 = 42{,}000 \\
\text{PCC} & = 45 \times 6{,}500 = 2{,}92{,}500 \\
\text{Brickwork (Fdn)} & = 60 \times 7{,}200 = 4{,}32{,}000 \\
\text{Brickwork (Super)} & = 280 \times 7{,}800 = 21{,}84{,}000 \\
\text{RCC slab} & = 38 \times 18{,}500 = 7{,}03{,}000 \\
\text{Plastering} & = 950 \times 280 = 2{,}66{,}000 \\
\text{Flooring} & = 220 \times 1{,}400 = 3{,}08{,}000 \\
\text{Teak woodwork} & = 6.5 \times 1{,}80{,}000 = 11{,}70{,}000 \\
\text{Painting} & = 1{,}150 \times 95 = 1{,}09{,}250 \\
\text{Plumbing} & = 1{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Electrification} & = 95{,}000 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Summing up:

$$C_{g} = 42{,}000 + 2{,}92{,}500 + 4{,}32{,}000 + 21{,}84{,}000 + 7{,}03{,}000 + 2{,}66{,}000$$
$$+ 3{,}08{,}000 + 11{,}70{,}000 + 1{,}09{,}250 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 95{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{g} = \text{₹ } 57{,}21{,}750}$$

### Step 2 — Salvage Value (SV)

$$SV = 10\% \times 57{,}21{,}750 = \text{₹ } 5{,}72{,}175$$

### Step 3 — Total Depreciable Amount

$$D_{total} = C_{g} - SV = 57{,}21{,}750 - 5{,}72{,}175 = \text{₹ } 51{,}49{,}575$$

### Step 4 — Annual Straight-Line Depreciation

$$D_{annual} = \frac{D_{total}}{\text{Useful Life}} = \frac{51{,}49{,}575}{60} = \text{₹ } 85{,}826 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 5 — Accumulated Depreciation for 15 Years

$$D_{15} = 85{,}826 \times 15 = \text{₹ } 12{,}87{,}393$$

### Step 6 — Present (Depreciated) Value

$$\boxed{V_{d} = C_{g} - D_{15} = 57{,}21{,}750 - 12{,}87{,}393 = \text{₹ } 44{,}34{,}357}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Rental Method of Valuation

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A two-storey building is let out at ₹35,000 per month. The following outgoings are estimated annually:
- Property tax = ₹65,000
- Repairs & maintenance = ₹40,000
- Insurance = ₹12,000
- Management charges = 8% of gross rent
- Vacancy allowance = 5% of gross rent

The rate of interest in the market is 6% per annum. Calculate the capitalized value of the property assuming perpetual income.

### Step 1 — Gross Annual Rent

$$\text{Gross Rent} = 35{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹ } 4{,}20{,}000$$

### Step 2 — Other Outgoings

$$\text{Management} = 8\% \times 4{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 33{,}600$$
$$\text{Vacancy Allowance} = 5\% \times 4{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 21{,}000$$

### Step 3 — Total Outgoings

$$\text{Total Outgoings} = 65{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 33{,}600 + 21{,}000$$
$$= \text{₹ } 1{,}71{,}600$$

### Step 4 — Net Annual Rent

$$\text{NAR} = 4{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}71{,}600 = \text{₹ } 2{,}48{,}400$$

### Step 5 — Years' Purchase (Perpetual)

$$YP = \frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{0.06} = 16.667$$

### Step 6 — Capitalized Value

$$V_{c} = \text{NAR} \times YP = 2{,}48{,}400 \times 16.667$$
$$\boxed{V_{c} \approx \text{₹ } 41{,}40{,}000}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Rental Method with Limited Period

**Problem:** If the same property above has a remaining useful life of **40 years** (not perpetual), recalculate the capitalized value.

### Step 1 — Use Limited-Period YP Formula

$$YP = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$$

With $r = 0.06$ and $n = 40$:

$$(1.06)^{40} \approx 10.2857$$

$$YP = \frac{10.2857 - 1}{0.06 \times 10.2857} = \frac{9.2857}{0.6171} \approx 15.046$$

### Step 2 — Capitalized Value

$$V_{c} = 2{,}48{,}400 \times 15.046 \approx \text{₹ } 37{,}37{,}426$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice the perpetual YP (16.67) > limited YP (15.05). This is logical because a perpetual income stream is worth more than a finite one of the same annual amount.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sinking Fund Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Asset at Cost P]) --> idA[Identify Useful Life n]
    idA --> idB[Identify Interest Rate r]
    idB --> compA[Compute SFC = r divided by open paren 1 plus r close paren power n minus 1]
    compA --> annA[Annual Sinking Fund A = P times SFC]
    idB --> intA[Annual Interest I = P times r]
    annA --> depA[Total Annual Depreciation D = I plus A]
    intA --> depA
    depA --> bookA[Book Value at year t = P minus accumulated Sinking Fund]
    bookA --> endA([End: Replacement Fund Ready at Year n])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Quantity Survey Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: Building to be Valued]) --> measB[Step 1: Take Off Quantities of All Trades]
    measB --> rateB[Step 2: Apply Current PWD SOR or Market Rates]
    rateB --> grossB[Step 3: Compute Gross Replacement Cost Cg]
    grossB --> ageB[Step 4: Determine Effective Age of Building]
    ageB --> lifeB[Step 5: Determine Useful Life of Each Component]
    lifeB --> depB[Step 6: Calculate Annual and Total Depreciation]
    depB --> valB[Step 7: Depreciated Value Vd = Cg minus Depreciation]
    valB --> endB([End: Present Market Value of Old Building])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rental Method of Valuation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start: Rental Property]) --> grC[Step 1: Gross Rent per Annum]
    grC --> outC[Step 2: Identify All Outgoings]
    outC --> catC[Sub-blocks: Property Tax, Repairs, Insurance, Management, Vacancy]
    catC --> narC[Step 3: Net Annual Rent = Gross Rent minus Total Outgoings]
    narC --> ypC[Step 4: Select YP based on Income Type]
    ypC --> ypType{Income Type}
    ypType -- Perpetual --> yp1[YP equals 1 over r]
    ypType -- Limited Period --> yp2[YP equals numerator bracket 1 plus r bracket power n minus 1 over denominator r times bracket 1 plus r bracket power n]
    ypType -- Deferred --> yp3[YP equals 1 over bracket 1 plus r bracket power t times Limited Period YP for n years]
    yp1 --> cvC[Step 5: Capitalized Value equals NAR times YP]
    yp2 --> cvC
    yp3 --> cvC
    cvC --> endC([End: Market Value of Property])
```

## 4.4 Concept Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Sinking Fund Method | Quantity Survey Method | Rental Method |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Primary Input** | Original Cost *P*, life *n*, rate *r* | Detailed measurements & current rates | Net Annual Rent, YP |
| **Best Suited For** | Standard buildings with known life | Aged, altered, or heritage buildings | Income-generating properties |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (assumes uniform wear) | Highest (item-wise estimate) | Moderate (depends on rental data) |
| **Common Users** | Govt. PWD, Insurance | Chartered Valuers, Courts | Banks, Income-Tax Dept. |
| **Output** | Annual Depreciation, Book Value | Present Market Value | Capitalized Value |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define "Sinking Fund." State the formula for the annual sinking fund installment of a property.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Sinking Fund:** A sinking fund is a sum of money set aside annually by the owner of a depreciable asset, which, when accumulated at compound interest, equals the original cost of the asset at the end of its useful life. It is used to replace the asset when it becomes obsolete.
>
> **Formula:** The annual sinking fund installment is
> $$A = P \times \frac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$
> where *P* = original cost of the asset, *r* = rate of compound interest per annum, and *n* = useful life of the asset in years.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the "Rental Method" of valuation? What is "Years' Purchase" (YP)?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Rental Method** of valuation is a method of determining the capitalised value of a property that is — or could reasonably be — let out on rent, by multiplying the Net Annual Rent (NAR) by an appropriate multiplier called **Years' Purchase (YP)**.
>
> **Years' Purchase (YP)** is the number of years' purchase of the net annual income, which capitalises the future income into a present lump-sum value. For a perpetual income, $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$ where *r* is the rate of interest.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** "Module 4 — *Internal Choice Provided.* Answer ANY ONE of the following: (a) …… (b) ……"

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Sinking Fund + Quantity Survey [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A residential building was constructed at a cost of ₹20,00,000 (excluding land). Its expected life is 60 years, and the sinking fund is invested at 4.5% compound interest per annum. Calculate: **(i)** the annual sinking fund installment, **(ii)** the annual interest on the capital, **(iii)** the total annual depreciation, and **(iv)** the book value of the building after 25 years. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Quantity Survey Method** of valuation. List its merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P = 20{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $n = 60$ years, $r = 4.5\% = 0.045$

**Step 1 — Compute $(1.045)^{60}$:**
$$(1.045)^{10} \approx 1.5530$$
$$(1.045)^{20} \approx 2.4117$$
$$(1.045)^{60} = (2.4117)^{3} \approx 14.0276$$

**Step 2 — Sinking Fund Coefficient:**
$$\text{SFC} = \frac{0.045}{14.0276 - 1} = \frac{0.045}{13.0276} \approx 0.003454$$

**Step 3 — Annual Sinking Fund Installment (i):**
$$A = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.003454 \approx \text{₹ } 6{,}908 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Interest on Capital (ii):**
$$I = P \times r = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.045 = \text{₹ } 90{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5 — Total Annual Depreciation (iii):**
$$D = I + A = 90{,}000 + 6{,}908 = \text{₹ } 96{,}908 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6 — Accrued Sinking Fund at end of 25 years (iv):**
$$\text{Accrued SF} = A \cdot \frac{(1.045)^{25} - 1}{0.045}$$

Compute $(1.045)^{25}$:
$$(1.045)^{25} = (1.045)^{20} \times (1.045)^{5}$$
$$(1.045)^{5} \approx 1.2462$$
$$(1.045)^{25} \approx 2.4117 \times 1.2462 \approx 3.0054$$

$$\text{Accrued SF} = 6{,}908 \times \frac{3.0054 - 1}{0.045} = 6{,}908 \times 44.564 \approx \text{₹ } 3{,}07{,}853$$

$$\text{Book Value at Year 25} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}07{,}853 = \text{₹ } 16{,}92{,}147$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Quantity Survey Method, also called the Detailed Estimate Method, is the most accurate scientific method of valuation. It involves taking detailed measurements of all building components (using IS 1200), multiplying these by current PWD Schedule of Rates (SOR), summing up to obtain the *Gross Replacement Cost*, and then deducting suitable depreciation to arrive at the *Depreciated (Present) Value*.

**Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. Detailed measurement of all items of work (earthwork, concrete, brickwork, plastering, flooring, woodwork, painting, plumbing, electrification).
2. Rate analysis using prevailing market rates / current PWD SOR.
3. Compute Gross Replacement Cost: $C_g = \sum Q_i \times R_i$.
4. Determine effective age and useful life of the building.
5. Calculate accumulated depreciation (using straight-line or observed-condition method).
6. Depreciated Value $V_d = C_g - \text{Depreciation}$.

**Merits (1 Mark):**
- Most accurate and scientific; item-wise precision.
- Useful for insurance claims, court cases, valuation of heritage buildings.

**Demerits (1 Mark):**
- Time-consuming, labour-intensive, and requires expert valuer.
- Costly to execute; rates fluctuate rapidly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Rental Method [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A building fetches a gross monthly rent of ₹48,000. The annual outgoings are: Property tax = ₹72,000; Insurance = ₹14,000; Repairs = ₹38,000; Management charges = 7% of gross rent; Sinking fund = 5% of gross rent. The market rate of interest is 6% per annum. Calculate the **capitalized value** of the property assuming: **(i)** perpetual income, and **(ii)** a remaining useful life of 50 years. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate clearly between **Freehold (Perpetual)** and **Leasehold (Limited Period)** income in the context of valuation. How is the Years' Purchase (YP) factor determined in each case? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Gross monthly rent = ₹48,000 → Gross annual rent = ₹48,000 × 12 = **₹5,76,000**

**Step 1 — Compute other outgoings:**
$$\text{Management} = 7\% \times 5{,}76{,}000 = \text{₹ } 40{,}320$$
$$\text{Sinking Fund} = 5\% \times 5{,}76{,}000 = \text{₹ } 28{,}800$$

**Step 2 — Total Outgoings:**
$$72{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 38{,}000 + 40{,}320 + 28{,}800 = \text{₹ } 1{,}93{,}120$$

**Step 3 — Net Annual Rent:**
$$\text{NAR} = 5{,}76{,}000 - 1{,}93{,}120 = \text{₹ } 3{,}82{,}880$$

**Step 4 — Case (i): Perpetual Income**
$$YP = \frac{1}{0.06} = 16.667$$
$$V_{c}^{(i)} = 3{,}82{,}880 \times 16.667 \approx \text{₹ } 63{,}81{,}333$$

**Step 5 — Case (ii): Limited Period, n = 50, r = 0.06**
$$(1.06)^{50} \approx 18.4202$$
$$YP_{50} = \frac{18.4202 - 1}{0.06 \times 18.4202} = \frac{17.4202}{1.1052} \approx 15.762$$
$$V_{c}^{(ii)} = 3{,}82{,}880 \times 15.762 \approx \text{₹ } 60{,}34{,}915$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Freehold / Perpetual Income (3 Marks):**
- Ownership of the property is permanent and indefinite; the owner has absolute right over the property.
- The income stream is assumed to continue forever.
- **YP Formula:** $YP = \dfrac{1}{r}$, where *r* is the standard rate of interest (e.g., 6% → YP = 16.67).
- Used for valuing full ownership, fee-simple estates, and freehold land.

**Leasehold / Limited Period Income (3 Marks):**
- Ownership is for a fixed number of years (lease tenure), after which the property reverts to the freeholder.
- The income stream is finite — it lasts only *n* years.
- **YP Formula:** $YP = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$ — the present worth of an ordinary annuity of Re. 1 per year for *n* years at rate *r*.
- Used for valuing leasehold properties, rented buildings with a fixed lease term, and reversionary interests.

**Key Distinction (1 Mark):**
- Freehold YP is always greater than leasehold YP for the same rate *r* (since $1/r \geq \dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$).
- As *n* → ∞, leasehold YP → freehold YP.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sinking Fund Q:** Students often forget to *add* interest on capital to the sinking fund installment. *D = I + A* — never *D = A* alone. **Lose 2 marks if missed.**
> 2. **Quantity Survey Q:** Always show the *quantity × rate = amount* line for *each* item. Skipping intermediate rows is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** per item.
> 3. **Rental Method Q:** Forgetting *management charges* and *vacancy allowance* in the outgoings is the **#1 mark-killer**. KTU has consistently tested these "hidden" outgoings in recent papers.
> 4. **YP Selection:** Misreading "perpetual" vs "limited period" and applying the wrong YP formula is an instant **3-mark loss** (the entire (a) part of a 7-mark sub-question).
> 5. **Units:** Always write **₹ per year** for annual figures and **₹** for lump-sum capitalized values. KTU examiners often deduct **0.5 marks** for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Sinking Fund** accumulates *to* the asset cost; depreciation is the *sum* of (i) interest on capital and (ii) the annual sinking fund installment.
- **SFC** = $\dfrac{r}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ — a fixed coefficient for given *r* and *n*.
- **Quantity Survey Method** is the **most accurate** but **most expensive** valuation method; it requires item-wise measurement.
- Always deduct **Salvage Value** (if given) before applying straight-line depreciation in QS method.
- **Net Annual Rent (NAR)** = Gross Rent − (Property Tax + Repairs + Insurance + Management + Sinking Fund + Vacancy).
- **Years' Purchase (YP)** capitalises future income: Capitalized Value = NAR × YP.
- **Perpetual YP** = 1/r; **Limited-Period YP** = $\dfrac{(1+r)^{n}-1}{r(1+r)^{n}}$.
- For **deferred annuity**, multiply limited-period YP by $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{t}}$ (where *t* = deferment period).
- For **reversion** of Re. 1 after *n* years, YP = $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}}$.
- **Land is NOT depreciable** — never include land cost in sinking fund or quantity survey depreciation.
- KTU typically provides **(1.05)^n, (1.06)^n** values in the question paper appendix — learn to use them.
- **Examiners' favourite distractors:** (a) using YP = 1/(1+r) instead of 1/r for perpetuity; (b) using *gross* rent instead of *net* rent; (c) confusing "sinking fund" the method with "sinking fund" the outgoings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Comparison of Capital Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Comparison of Capital Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Comparison Method (a.k.a. Comparative Method of Valuation)**
> A method of property valuation in which the present **capital value (CV)** of a subject property is determined by direct comparison with the **recently transacted sale prices of one or more similar (comparable) properties** in the same or neighboring locality, after applying suitable adjustments for differences in time, location, specifications, age, condition, and amenities.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this is also referred to as the **"Comparison Method"** or **"Sales Comparison Approach"** and is treated as the *most reliable* method of valuation **whenever reliable comparable sale data is available**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Used Car Price Check"**
> Imagine you want to sell your 4-year-old Honda City in Kochi. The most trustworthy way to estimate its price is to look at **what other identical or similar Honda City cars sold for last month** in the same city, and then make *small adjustments* if your car has an extra sunroof, lower mileage, or a fresh service. You would not use a textbook depreciation table alone; you would compare with real market deals.
> The **Direct Comparison Method of property valuation works on exactly this principle** — recent *real market transactions* of similar houses, plots, or buildings form the *primary evidence*, and adjustments are layered on top to fine-tune the final value of the subject property.

### 1.3 When is Direct Comparison Most Suitable?

The method is preferred in the following situations:

- **Active real estate market** with frequent transactions of similar properties.
- **Standard residential houses / apartments** in well-developed, homogeneous localities.
- **Open plots / land** in urban or semi-urban areas where sales records are well documented.
- **Similar commercial properties** (shops, offices) in commercial belts.
- Where **recent, authentic, and verifiable sale data** is available through registered sale deeds, stamp-duty records, or property portals.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

The following standard metrics are used in the direct comparison approach:

- **Built-up area / Plinth area** (in **m²** or **sq.ft.**)
- **Land area** (in **cents** or **m²**)
- **Rate per unit area** (₹/m² or ₹/sq.ft.)
- **Capital Value (CV)** — the lump-sum present market value (₹ / ₹ Lakhs / ₹ Crore)
- **Time factor** — number of years/months between the comparable's sale date and the date of valuation
- **Standard specifications** — RCC / laterite / brick / timber framed structures

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Premise of the Method

The method rests on a single economic principle:

> *"A buyer will not pay more for a property than the price of an equivalent alternative property in the same market, all other factors being equal."*

Hence, the **subject property's value ≈ adjusted value of the comparable property**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The valuation engineer's process is:

1. **Identify the subject property** — note its location, area, specifications, age, condition, and special features.
2. **Search for comparables** — locate 2 to 4 similar properties that have been **sold recently** (ideally within the last 6–12 months) in the same or a *comparable* locality.
3. **Collect verified data** for each comparable: sale price, date of sale, area, age, condition, specifications, and amenities.
4. **Verify the transactions** through registered sale deeds, encumbrance certificates, or buyer confirmation to ensure *bona fide* sales (not family transfers or distress sales).
5. **Apply Time Adjustment** — to bring the comparable's past sale price to the *present* valuation date using the market appreciation (or depreciation) rate.
6. **Apply Location Adjustment** — to correct for any premium or discount applicable to differences in micro-location (main road vs. interior lane).
7. **Apply Specification / Feature Adjustments** — to add or subtract the value of specific differences (e.g., extra floor, swimming pool, modular kitchen, parking).
8. **Apply Age & Condition Adjustment** — to account for the difference in the remaining useful life or maintenance state.
9. **Compute the Adjusted Value** of each comparable, then **take the average (weighted or simple)** to arrive at the final **Capital Value** of the subject property.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CV \;=\; P_{0} \times (1+r)^{n}$ | CV = Capital Value, P₀ = past sale price, r = annual rate, n = years | **Time Adjustment** to bring past sale to present |
| 2 | $CV \;=\; P_{0} \times (1+r)^{n} \pm A$ | A = value of additional/missing feature | **Feature Adjustment** |
| 3 | $R_{unit} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{sale}}{A_{built\text{-}up}}$ | R_unit = rate per unit area (₹/m²) | **Rate per unit area** computation |
| 4 | $CV \;=\; R_{unit} \times A_{subject}$ | A_subject = area of subject property | **Direct rate application** |
| 5 | $CV \;=\; \dfrac{\sum CV_{i}}{k}$ | k = number of comparables | **Average of adjusted values** |
| 6 | $\text{Location Adj. Factor} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{subject\;locality}}{R_{comp\;locality}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | **Locality correction** |
| 7 | $CV_{adj} \;=\; P_{0} \times T_f \times L_f \pm A$ | T_f = time factor, L_f = location factor | **Cumulative adjustment** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions**
> - Both properties must be in the **same market** (same city/town or micro-market).
> - Sale data should be **less than 2 years old** for highest reliability.
> - The sale must be a **bona fide arm's-length transaction** (not a gift, inheritance, or distress sale).
> - **Hedonic regression** weighting may be used for sophisticated valuations but is not required at the B.Tech level.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Direct Comparison Method is the *backbone* of:

- **Banks & Housing Finance Companies (HFC)** for sanctioning home loans — they use recent comparable sales to fix the property's market value.
- **Income Tax Department** for capital gains computation on property transfer.
- **Insurance Companies** for building insurance valuation in urban areas.
- **Court Valuations** in property partition, divorce, and inheritance disputes.
- **Real Estate Developers** for project feasibility and pre-launch pricing.
- **Government Acquisition** under the **Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013** — the award is largely guided by recent sale instances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Time Adjustment Factor

The **Time Adjustment Factor (T_f)** converts a *past* sale price into its *present* equivalent, assuming uniform market appreciation (or depreciation) at a compound rate $r$ per annum.

**Step 1** — Let $P_0$ be the sale price $n$ years before the valuation date.

**Step 2** — Assume the market appreciated uniformly at $r$ per year (as a decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%).

**Step 3** — After 1 year, the price becomes $P_0 (1+r)$.
After 2 years, the price becomes $P_0 (1+r)(1+r) = P_0 (1+r)^2$.

**Step 4** — Generalising for $n$ years:

$$
CV \;=\; P_{0} \times (1+r)^{n}
$$

**Step 5** — If the appreciation is known for a *partial* year $m$ (months), replace $n$ with $\frac{m}{12}$:

$$
CV \;=\; P_{0} \times (1+r)^{\tfrac{m}{12}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation** — This is a **compound growth equation**, identical in form to the engineering-economics future-value formula $F = P(1+i)^n$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solved Example (Full Working)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical — Standard KTU Pattern**

**Problem Statement:**
A residential property in Thrissur was sold **2 years ago** for **₹ 48,00,000**. The comparable property had:
- **Built-up area** = 120 m²
- **Subject property area** = 130 m²
- An **open car porch** that the subject property does **not** have, valued at **₹ 2,00,000**
- A **compound wall** that the subject property does **not** have, valued at **₹ 1,50,000**

The property market in Thrissur has appreciated at **9% per annum** (compound) over the last 2 years.

**Find the present Capital Value of the subject property.**

---

**Step 1 — Identify the comparable's past sale price.**

$$
P_{0} \;=\; \text{₹ } 48{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the time gap and appreciation rate.**

$$
n \;=\; 2 \text{ years}, \quad r \;=\; 0.09
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Time Adjustment Factor.**

$$
T_{f} \;=\; (1+r)^{n} \;=\; (1+0.09)^{2}
$$

$$
T_{f} \;=\; (1.09)^{2} \;=\; 1.1881
$$

**Step 4 — Bring the comparable's price to present day.**

$$
P_{present} \;=\; P_{0} \times T_{f} \;=\; 48{,}00{,}000 \times 1.1881
$$

$$
P_{present} \;=\; \text{₹ } 57{,}02{,}880
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Feature Adjustments.**

The comparable has TWO extra features the subject lacks: car porch and compound wall. Hence these are **subtracted** from the comparable's adjusted price.

$$
A_{extra} \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the final Capital Value.**

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; P_{present} - A_{extra}
$$

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; 57{,}02{,}880 - 3{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; \text{₹ } 53{,}52{,}880
$$

**Answer — Final Capital Value = ₹ 53,52,880 (≈ ₹ 53.53 Lakhs).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on area:** In simple B.Tech problems, the area difference is often handled by converting both properties to a common "rate per m²" basis. Here, the problem implicitly assumes the rate is *per whole property*, and area difference is neutralised by the lump-sum adjustments shown. In more advanced problems, the area correction is applied **after** Step 4 by multiplying the present rate per m² by the subject's area.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Rate per Unit Area Method

**Problem Statement:**
Three comparable properties were sold in Kochin last month. Determine the probable value of a subject property with **150 m²** of built-up area.

| Comparable | Built-up Area (m²) | Sale Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 100 | 32,00,000 |
| B | 125 | 41,25,000 |
| C | 110 | 35,75,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute rate per m² for each comparable.**

$$
R_{A} \;=\; \frac{32{,}00{,}000}{100} \;=\; \text{₹ } 32{,}000 \text{ / m}^{2}
$$

$$
R_{B} \;=\; \frac{41{,}25{,}000}{125} \;=\; \text{₹ } 33{,}000 \text{ / m}^{2}
$$

$$
R_{C} \;=\; \frac{35{,}75{,}000}{110} \;=\; \text{₹ } 32{,}500 \text{ / m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average rate.**

$$
R_{avg} \;=\; \frac{32{,}000 + 33{,}000 + 32{,}500}{3} \;=\; \frac{97{,}500}{3}
$$

$$
R_{avg} \;=\; \text{₹ } 32{,}500 \text{ / m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to subject property.**

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; R_{avg} \times A_{subject} \;=\; 32{,}500 \times 150
$$

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; \text{₹ } 48{,}75{,}000
$$

**Answer — Capital Value of subject property = ₹ 48,75,000.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Direct Comparison Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Subject Property Identified]) --> A[Collect Data: Area, Age, Specs, Location, Features]
    A --> B[Search Comparable Sales in Same Locality]
    B --> C{At least 2 bona fide sales found?}
    C -- No --> D[Switch to Rent Capitalisation or Cost Method]
    C -- Yes --> E[Verify Sale Authenticity via Sale Deed or EC]
    E --> F[List Comparables with Sale Price and Date]
    F --> G[Step 1: Apply Time Adjustment Factor]
    G --> H[Step 2: Apply Location Adjustment Factor]
    H --> I[Step 3: Apply Feature Adjustments Add or Subtract]
    I --> J[Step 4: Apply Age and Condition Adjustments]
    J --> K[Step 5: Compute Adjusted Value of Each Comparable]
    K --> L[Step 6: Take Simple or Weighted Average]
    L --> M[Final Capital Value of Subject Property]
    M --> N([End: Report Submitted])

    style Start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Adjustment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        P0[Past Sale Price P0]
        RATE[Market Appreciation Rate r]
        TIME[Time Gap n]
        LOC[Location Premium or Discount]
        FEAT[Extra or Missing Features]
        AREA[Area of Subject Property]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Adjustment Engine]
        TF[Time Factor Block: 1 plus r to power n]
        LF[Location Factor Block]
        FA[Feature Adjustment Block]
        AG[Age and Condition Block]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        RADJ[Rate per m2 of Comparable]
        CVADJ[Adjusted Capital Value per Comparable]
        CVFIN[Final Capital Value of Subject Property]
    end

    P0 --> TF
    RATE --> TF
    TIME --> TF
    TF --> RADJ
    LOC --> LF
    LF --> RADJ
    FEAT --> FA
    FA --> CVADJ
    AG --> CVADJ
    AREA --> CVFIN
    CVADJ --> CVFIN

    style INPUT fill:#e3f2fd,color:#0d47a1
    style PROCESS fill:#fff9c4,color:#f57f17
    style OUTPUT fill:#c8e6c9,color:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Adjustments (Vertical Order of Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[Base Sale Price of Comparable] --> L1[Layer 1: Time Adjustment]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: Location Adjustment]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Size or Area Adjustment]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Specification and Feature Adjustment]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Age and Condition Adjustment]
    L5 --> FINAL[Final Adjusted Value Equals Subject Property Capital Value]

    style BASE fill:#bbdefb,color:#0d47a1
    style FINAL fill:#a5d6a7,color:#1b5e20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the Direct Comparison Method of valuation. State any two situations where it is most suitably applied.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Definition (2 marks):* The Direct Comparison Method, also called the Sales Comparison Method, is a method of valuation in which the present capital value of a property is determined by comparing it with the recent sale prices of one or more similar properties in the same locality, after applying suitable adjustments for differences in time, location, specifications, and condition.

*Situations (1 mark — any two):*
- Standard residential houses in well-developed and homogeneous localities.
- Open plots / land in active urban real-estate markets with verifiable sale records.
- Similar commercial properties (shops / offices) in commercial belts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any four limitations of the Direct Comparison Method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Scarcity of comparable data** — In many areas, truly similar properties may not have been sold recently.
2. **Lack of transparency** — Many property transactions in India are under-reported to evade stamp duty, distorting sale prices.
3. **Heterogeneity of properties** — No two properties are *exactly* identical; subjective adjustments reduce accuracy.
4. **Time-lag distortion** — Rapid market fluctuations can make even 6-month-old data unreliable.
5. **Unverified sales** — Many sales are *not* arm's-length transactions (e.g., family transfers, distress sales), leading to inaccurate comparables.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Model Pattern]**

**(a) A residential property in Thiruvananthapuram was sold 1.5 years ago for ₹ 60,00,000. The comparable property has 150 m² built-up area, while the subject property has 165 m². The comparable has a servant quarter (worth ₹ 4,00,000) and a paved driveway (worth ₹ 2,50,000) that the subject property does not have. The property market in Thiruvananthapuram has appreciated at 8% per annum (compound). Determine the present Capital Value of the subject property. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify the past sale price of the comparable.*

$$
P_{0} \;=\; \text{₹ } 60{,}00{,}000
$$

*Step 2 — Identify the rate and time.*

$$
r \;=\; 0.08, \quad n \;=\; 1.5 \text{ years}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute Time Adjustment Factor.*

$$
T_{f} \;=\; (1+r)^{n} \;=\; (1.08)^{1.5}
$$

$$
T_{f} \;=\; 1.1240 \quad \text{(approximately)}
$$

*Step 4 — Bring the comparable to the present value.*

$$
P_{present} \;=\; 60{,}00{,}000 \times 1.1240 \;=\; \text{₹ } 67{,}44{,}000
$$

*Step 5 — Compute feature adjustments (subtract extra features of comparable).*

$$
A_{extra} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 6{,}50{,}000
$$

*Step 6 — Compute final Capital Value.*

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; 67{,}44{,}000 - 6{,}50{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 60{,}94{,}000
$$
