*Step 7 — Apply area correction (optional but recommended).*
Rate per m² of comparable (present) = $67{,}44{,}000 \div 150 = \text{₹ } 44{,}960 / \text{m}^{2}$
Adjusted value of subject (on area basis) = $44{,}960 \times 165 = \text{₹ } 74{,}18{,}400$

> The two values may differ; the final answer is the **lump-sum approach (₹ 60,94,000)** as it includes feature adjustments. In practice, the engineer may take the *weighted average*.

**Final Answer: ₹ 60,94,000 (≈ ₹ 60.94 Lakhs).**

---

**(b) Discuss the data required and the steps involved in the Direct Comparison Method of valuation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

*Data Required (3.5 Marks — ½ mark each):*
1. Address, location, and description of the subject property.
2. Built-up area and land area.
3. Year of construction, type of structure (RCC / laterite / brick), and specifications.
4. Present age, condition, and remaining useful life.
5. Special features (parking, garden, swimming pool, modular kitchen, etc.).
6. Sale price of at least 2–4 comparable properties.
7. Date of each sale, verified through sale deed or encumbrance certificate.
8. Market trend / annual appreciation rate in the locality.

*Steps Involved (3.5 Marks — ½ mark each):*
1. Identify the subject property and list all its salient features.
2. Search for comparable properties sold in the same or a similar locality.
3. Verify the authenticity of each comparable sale.
4. Tabulate the data (price, date, area, age, features).
5. Apply the Time Adjustment Factor $(1+r)^n$ to bring each sale to the valuation date.
6. Apply Location, Feature, and Age Adjustments.
7. Compute the Adjusted Value for each comparable.
8. Take the **simple or weighted average** to obtain the final Capital Value.

---

#### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Model Pattern]**

**(a) Three comparable residential properties were recently sold in Ernakulam. The data is given below. Determine the present Capital Value of the subject property with 175 m² of built-up area. The market has appreciated uniformly at 6% per annum since the dates of these sales. (7 Marks)**

| Comparable | Area (m²) | Sale Price (₹) | Sold (months ago) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 140 | 49,00,000 | 6 |
| B | 160 | 60,00,000 | 12 |
| C | 130 | 42,00,000 | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute rate per m² at the time of each sale.*

$$
R_{A0} \;=\; \frac{49{,}00{,}000}{140} \;=\; \text{₹ } 35{,}000 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
R_{B0} \;=\; \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{160} \;=\; \text{₹ } 37{,}500 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
R_{C0} \;=\; \frac{42{,}00{,}000}{130} \;=\; \text{₹ } 32{,}307.7 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 2 — Apply Time Adjustment Factor to bring each rate to present.*

For A: $n = 0.5$ year, $T_f = (1.06)^{0.5} = 1.0296$

$$
R_{A,present} \;=\; 35{,}000 \times 1.0296 \;=\; \text{₹ } 36{,}036 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

For B: $n = 1.0$ year, $T_f = 1.06$

$$
R_{B,present} \;=\; 37{,}500 \times 1.06 \;=\; \text{₹ } 39{,}750 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

For C: $n = 0.25$ year, $T_f = (1.06)^{0.25} = 1.0147$

$$
R_{C,present} \;=\; 32{,}307.7 \times 1.0147 \;=\; \text{₹ } 32{,}782 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute the average present rate.*

$$
R_{avg} \;=\; \frac{36{,}036 + 39{,}750 + 32{,}782}{3} \;=\; \frac{1{,}08{,}568}{3}
$$

$$
R_{avg} \;=\; \text{₹ } 36{,}189.33 / \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 4 — Apply to subject property of 175 m².*

$$
CV_{subject} \;=\; 36{,}189.33 \times 175 \;=\; \text{₹ } 63{,}33{,}133
$$

**Final Answer: ≈ ₹ 63,33,000 (≈ ₹ 63.33 Lakhs).**

---

**(b) Differentiate between the Direct Comparison Method and the Rent Capitalisation Method of valuation. Under what conditions is the Direct Comparison Method preferred? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Comparison Method | Rent Capitalisation Method |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of Valuation | Recent sale prices of similar properties | Net annual rent that the property fetches |
| Data Source | Registered sale deeds, market transactions | Rent receipts, lease agreements |
| Suitability | Owner-occupied houses, vacant plots | Rented / tenanted properties |
| Market Dependency | Highly dependent on active local market | Independent of recent sales |
| Complexity | Simple, direct | Requires capitalisation rate (Y) calculation |
| Key Formula | $CV = P_0(1+r)^n \pm A$ | $CV = \text{Net Annual Rent} \times \frac{1}{Y}$ |

*Conditions for Preferring Direct Comparison (1 Mark each — any four):*
1. The local real-estate market is active and transparent.
2. At least 2–3 genuine comparable sales have occurred recently.
3. The subject property is a *standard* house or plot (not a special-purpose building like a hospital or temple).
4. Sale data can be *verified* through authentic records.
5. The locality is well-defined and homogeneous.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Pitfalls)**
> 1. **Forgetting the Time Adjustment** — Most students apply feature adjustments but forget to escalate the comparable's past price to the present date using $(1+r)^n$. This costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Sign Error in Feature Adjustments** — When the comparable has an *extra* feature, you must **subtract** it from the comparable's price. When the subject has an extra feature, you must **add** it. Many students reverse this.
> 3. **Wrong Formula for Partial Years** — Using $(1+r)^n$ for $n = 0.5$ year is mathematically correct, but the examiner expects you to **explicitly state the time gap in years (decimal)** and not write "half-yearly rate".
> 4. **Mixing Lump-Sum and Per-m² Methods** — Stick to **one** consistent approach in a single problem; do not switch mid-way.
> 5. **Ignoring Verification of Sale Data** — Theoretically, the examiner expects you to mention *bona fide arm's-length transaction* and *encumbrance certificate verification* at least once. Forgetting this loses **1 mark** in theory questions.
> 6. **Units — ₹ vs. ₹ Lakhs** — Always present the **final answer** in clear ₹ notation; examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units like "₹ 60 L" written without a clear final figure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Direct Comparison Method values a property by comparing it with recent sale prices of *similar* properties in the *same* locality after adjustments.
- **Most Reliable Method** when comparable sales data is available.
- **Not Suitable** for special-purpose buildings (hospitals, schools, temples, old heritage structures) and properties in *inactive* markets.
- **Key Formula — Time Adjustment:** $CV = P_0(1+r)^n$ — same as compound-interest future-value formula.
- **Key Formula — Rate per m²:** $CV = R_{unit} \times A_{subject}$, where $R_{unit} = \frac{P_{sale}}{A_{sale}}$.
- **Key Formula — Feature Adjustment:** $CV_{adj} = P_0(1+r)^n \pm A$ where $A$ is the value of extra or missing features.
- **Five Layers of Adjustment (in order):** Time → Location → Area/Size → Specifications/Features → Age/Condition.
- **Verification of comparables** is a *theoretical must* — always mention "bona fide arm's-length transaction".
- **3 to 4 comparables** are typically sufficient; use *weighted average* if one comparable is significantly more similar.
- **Rule of thumb:** Never use a comparable older than **2 years** unless the market is stagnant.
- **KTU Favourite Numerical:** 7-mark question on Time Adjustment + Feature Adjustment, with at least 1 sub-part on theory of "data required" or "limitations".
- **Examiner Keywords that Score:** *"bona fide transaction," "encumbrance certificate," "weighted average," "micro-market," "compound appreciation," "uniform rate of growth."*
- **Avoid** the *Belting & Pillaring Method*, *Development Method*, or *Profit Method* unless the question specifically asks for them — they belong to different sub-topics in Module 4.
- **Always state** assumptions explicitly in the solution: e.g., "Assuming uniform market appreciation at 8% p.a. compound".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuation Based on Profit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Valuation Based on Profit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Valuation based on profit** is an income-based method of property valuation in which the capital value of an income-generating property (such as a hotel, cinema, theatre, commercial complex, or rented residential building) is determined by capitalizing its **net annual profit** using an appropriate multiplier known as the **Years' Purchase (YP)**.

$$ \text{Capital Value} = \text{Net Annual Profit} \times \text{Years' Purchase (YP)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This method is specifically applied to properties whose value is intrinsically tied to the **profit-generating capacity** rather than to the cost of construction or comparable sales. It is the *only rational method* for specialized commercial properties.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fruit Tree" Intuition

Imagine you own a **mango tree**. The tree itself has a value, but its *true* economic worth to a buyer is determined by the **mangoes (profits) it yields every year**. A buyer will logically pay a lump sum equivalent to the present value of all future mango harvests, discounted by what they could have earned by depositing that money in a bank.

In the same way:

| Element of the Analogy | Property Valuation Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The mango tree | The physical property (building + land) |
| Annual mango yield | Net annual profit (rentals – outgoings) |
| Bank interest rate | The "Reasonable Rate of Return" (typically 5%–8%) |
| Discounting future harvests | Multiplying by **Years' Purchase (YP)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Years' Purchase"?** It is the inverse of the rate of interest. If the standard interest rate is 6%, then YP = 1/0.06 = **16.67 years**. It essentially means: a buyer is willing to pay an amount equal to **16.67 years' worth of net profit** to acquire the property outright.

## 1.3 Standard Boundary Constants (Bolded for Recall)

- **Standard Rate of Interest for YP calculation:** Typically **5% to 8% per annum** for real estate.
- **Standard YP (at 6%):** **16.6667 years**
- **Sinking Fund Rate:** Usually **3% to 4% per annum**
- **Scrap/Residual Value:** Assumed as a percentage of total cost (often 10% for buildings).

## 1.4 Classification of Valuation by Profit Method

The method is broadly subdivided into three operational variants (these are KTU-favorite exam topics):

1. **Valuation based on Gross Profit (or Gross Income).**
2. **Valuation based on Net Profit (or Net Income).**
3. **Valuation based on Average of several years' Profit (e.g., 3 to 5 years).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

The valuation-by-profit method rests on a fundamental investment principle: *a property is worth the present value of all future economic benefits it can generate*. The procedure is executed in four logical stages.

### Stage 1 — Determination of Gross Income (The "Earnings Engine")

- Gross income includes the **gross rental income**, **revenue from ancillary services** (e.g., parking, advertising rights in a cinema), and any **miscellaneous receipts**.
- For a hotel: room tariff + restaurant + banquet + laundry, etc.
- For a cinema: ticket collections + canteen + advertising.
- ⚠ **Critical Assumption:** The property is assumed to be *always fully occupied* unless historical occupancy data is provided.

### Stage 2 — Deduction of Outgoings (The "Cost Vortex")

Outgoings are the recurring expenses necessary to *keep the property running* and produce the income. They are subtracted from the gross income.

Standard outgoings include:
- **Property taxes / Municipal taxes**
- **Insurance premium** (fire, structure, etc.)
- **Maintenance and Repairs** (typically 5%–10% of gross income for buildings, 2.5%–5% for cinema/theatre)
- **Management expenses** (staff salaries, supervision)
- **Vacancy and Bad-debt allowance** (usually 5%–10% of gross income)
- **Depreciation allowance** (used only for the *Net Profit* method)
- **Interest on the borrowed capital** (if the property is mortgaged — sometimes excluded in the gross method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Net vs. Gross" Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Gross Profit Method:** Outgoings *exclude* depreciation and interest on capital.
> - **Net Profit Method:** Outgoings *include* depreciation *and* interest on capital.

### Stage 3 — Computation of Net Annual Profit

$$ \text{Net Annual Profit} = \text{Gross Income} - \text{Total Outgoings} $$

### Stage 4 — Capitalization using Years' Purchase (YP)

The Net Annual Profit is capitalized at a "reasonable rate of return" to obtain the **Capitalized Value (CV)**.

$$ \text{Capitalized Value} = \text{Net Annual Profit} \times \text{YP} $$

## 2.2 Treatment of Sinking Fund (For Properties with Limited Life)

When the property has a **definite economic life** (e.g., cinema or theatre with say 20–30 years of useful life), the buyer must put aside money annually to **recoup the original capital** at the end of the property's life. This annual set-aside is the **Sinking Fund (SF) contribution**.

The Annual Sinking Fund contribution is calculated as:

$$ \text{Annual SF} = \text{Scrap Value} \times \text{Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)} $$

And the **Net Profit actually available for capitalization** is:

$$ \text{Net Profit (after SF)} = \text{Net Profit (before SF)} - \text{Annual Sinking Fund} $$

$$ \text{Capitalized Value} = \text{Net Profit (after SF)} \times \text{YP} $$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value/Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Years' Purchase (YP) | $YP = \dfrac{1}{i}$ where $i$ = rate of interest | Dimensionless (in "years") | At 6%, YP = 16.67 |
| 2 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Dimensionless | Used to find annual SF instalment |
| 3 | Annual Sinking Fund | $SF = Sc \times SFF$ | Currency/year | Sc = Scrap Value |
| 4 | Net Annual Profit | $NP = GI - (Tax + Maint + Ins + Mgmt + Vac)$ | Currency/year | Excludes SF deduction here |
| 5 | Net Profit (after SF) | $NP_{SF} = NP - SF$ | Currency/year | Use this for limited-life assets |
| 6 | Capitalized Value (CV) | $CV = NP \times YP$ (or $NP_{SF} \times YP$) | Currency | **The main valuation result** |
| 7 | Valuation on Gross Profit | $CV = (GI - Outgoings_{excl.\ Dep,\ Int}) \times YP$ | Currency | Used for short-life commercial buildings |
| 8 | Valuation on Net Profit | $CV = (GI - Outgoings_{incl.\ Dep,\ Int}) \times YP$ | Currency | Used for long-life properties |
| 9 | Valuation on Average Profit | $CV = \overline{NP}_{n\ \text{years}} \times YP$ | Currency | Used when profits fluctuate year to year |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Real-World Practice:** Used by banks for mortgage valuation of hotels, multiplexes, IT parks, and shopping malls. Also used by tax authorities for capital gains computation, and by companies for internal financial reporting (ind AS 40 / IFRS compliance).

## 2.4 When to Use This Method (KTU Standard)

This method is **mandatorily preferred** in the following cases (a favourite 3-mark question):

1. **Theatre, Cinema, and Multiplexes** (because their revenue is *not* purely rental).
2. **Hotels and Lodges** (composite income from multiple services).
3. **Commercial offices, IT Parks, and Shopping Malls** (where income is the prime determinant).
4. **Income-yielding properties** whose sale price is not directly comparable (unique properties).
5. **Public utilities** (railway stations, bus terminals leased to operators).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Derivation — Why $YP = 1/i$?

Let a property yield a net annual profit of $P$ per year in perpetuity. The buyer pays a lump sum $V$. If this sum $V$ were deposited in a bank at rate $i$ per annum, it would yield interest $V \cdot i$ per year. For the investment to be "equivalent," we require:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interest from bank on Capital } V &= \text{Net Profit from Property } P \\
V \cdot i &= P \\
\therefore V &= \frac{P}{i} = P \times \frac{1}{i} = P \times YP
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental valuation equation** upon which the entire profit method rests.

## 3.2 The Sinking Fund Method — Full Derivation

Suppose a property has a **scrap value** of $S_c$ after $n$ years, and the buyer wishes to recover this $S_c$ by setting aside a uniform annual sum $A$ at the end of each year, invested at rate $i$ per annum (compounded). The accumulated value of the sinking fund at the end of year $n$ must equal $S_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^0 &= S_c \\
A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} &= S_c \\
\therefore A &= S_c \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bracketed term is the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Sinking Fund Instalment } (A) &= S_c \times SFF
\end{aligned}
$$

The investor's *net* annual earning is therefore the original net profit *minus* the sinking fund instalment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit after SF} &= P - A \\
&= P - S_c \times SFF
\end{aligned}
$$

The Capitalized Value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= (P - S_c \times SFF) \times YP \\
CV &= (P - S_c \times SFF) \times \frac{1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** A cinema building has a total cost of construction of ₹ 25,00,000. The expected life is 20 years after which the scrap value will be 10% of the construction cost. The annual gross income is ₹ 6,00,000. The annual outgoings (excluding sinking fund) are as follows: Taxes ₹ 50,000, Insurance ₹ 15,000, Repairs ₹ 75,000, Management ₹ 60,000, Depreciation & Interest on capital (combined) ₹ 1,00,000. The reasonable rate of return is 6% and the sinking fund rate is 3%. **Find the capitalized value of the cinema building using the Net Profit method.**

### Step 1 — Identify the parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scrap Value } (S_c) &= 10\% \text{ of } 25,00,000 = 2,50,000 \\
\text{Life } (n) &= 20 \text{ years} \\
\text{Sinking Fund Rate } (i_{SF}) &= 3\% = 0.03 \\
\text{Capitalization Rate } (i) &= 6\% = 0.06 \\
\text{YP (at 6\%)} &= \frac{1}{0.06} = 16.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SFF &= \frac{i_{SF}}{(1+i_{SF})^n - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.03}{(1.03)^{20} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

We know $(1.03)^{20} = 1.8061$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SFF &= \frac{0.03}{1.8061 - 1} = \frac{0.03}{0.8061} = 0.03722
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Annual Sinking Fund Instalment

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= S_c \times SFF \\
A &= 2,50,000 \times 0.03722 = 9,305
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Total Outgoings and Net Profit

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Outgoings} &= 50,000 + 15,000 + 75,000 + 60,000 + 1,00,000 = 3,00,000 \\
\text{Net Profit (before SF)} &= \text{Gross Income} - \text{Total Outgoings} \\
&= 6,00,000 - 3,00,000 = 3,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Net Profit after Sinking Fund

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit (after SF)} &= 3,00,000 - 9,305 = 2,90,695
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate the Capitalized Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= \text{Net Profit (after SF)} \times YP \\
&= 2,90,695 \times 16.6667 \\
&= 48,44,917 \text{ (approximately ₹ 48.45 lakhs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 The Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Parameter | Gross Profit Method | Net Profit Method | Average Profit Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outgoings included** | Only operational expenses (tax, repair, management) | All outgoings + depreciation + interest on capital | Depends on base year profit |
| **Sinking Fund Treatment** | Often not deducted (assumed perpetual) | Always deducted if life is finite | Usually applied to averaged value |
| **YP multiplier applied to** | Gross Profit | Net Profit | Average of past 3–5 years' profit |
| **Best suited for** | Short-life or merchant-rented properties | Long-life commercial buildings | Businesses with fluctuating year-on-year income |
| **Result conservatism** | Higher (overestimates) | Lower (most realistic) | Moderate (smooths volatility) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a problem does not specify the method, the default assumption in board exams is the **Net Profit Method with Sinking Fund deducted**, especially for cinemas, theatres, and hotels with a stated life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Profit-Based Valuation Pipeline

This block diagram maps the *exact sequence* of computational stages from gross income to capitalized value. It is the operational blueprint a KTU student should memorize for the 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Property Identification] --> B[Collect Gross Income Data]
    B --> C[Gross Income: Rentals + Ancillary Revenue]
    C --> D{Choose Method?}
    D -->|Gross Profit| E[Deduct Operational Outgoings only]
    D -->|Net Profit| F[Deduct Operational Outgoings + Depreciation + Interest]
    D -->|Average Profit| G[Average of Last 3 to 5 Years NP]
    E --> H[Compute Net Profit Before SF]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is Property Life Limited?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Compute Sinking Fund Factor SFF]
    J --> K[Annual SF = Scrap Value x SFF]
    K --> L[Net Profit After SF = NP - SF]
    I -->|No| M[Net Profit After SF = NP itself]
    L --> N[Determine YP from Reasonable Rate of Return]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Capitalized Value = Net Profit After SF x YP]
    O --> P[End: Final Valuation Report]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — Components of Outgoings (The "Cost Deduction Web")

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OUT[Total Outgoings] --> OP[Operational Expenses]
    OUT --> FIN[Financial Charges]
    OUT --> RISK[Risk Allowances]
    OP --> T[Property Tax]
    OP --> R[Repairs and Maintenance]
    OP --> M[Management and Staff]
    OP --> IN[Insurance Premium]
    FIN --> D[Depreciation]
    FIN --> IC[Interest on Capital]
    RISK --> V[Vacancy Allowance]
    RISK --> BD[Bad Debt Provision]
    OP --> SF[Sinking Fund for Limited Life]
    OUT --> NET[Net Annual Profit]
    NET --> CAP[Capitalized Value]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Variant of Profit Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is the property income generating?] -->|Yes| Q2[Is comparable sale data available?]
    Q1 -->|No| END1[Use Cost or Rental Method]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3[Does the income vary widely year to year?]
    Q2 -->|Yes| END2[Use Market / Comparable Method]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Use Average Profit Method]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5[Is the property life finite?]
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Net Profit Method with Sinking Fund]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[Use Net Profit Method without Sinking Fund]
    Q4 --> FINAL[Final Capitalized Value]
    Q6 --> FINAL
    Q7 --> FINAL
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Capital Recovery Mechanics (Sinking Fund)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0: Buyer pays Capitalized Value CV] --> Y1[Year 1: Receives Net Profit P1]
    Y1 --> Y2[Year 2: Receives P2 and sets aside Annual SF A]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3 to Year n-1: Continues to set aside A annually at 3 percent SF rate]
    Y3 --> YN[Year n: Sinking Fund matures to Scrap Value Sc]
    YN --> YR[Buyer recovers original capital equivalent via accumulated SF]
    YR --> NOTE[Net effective annual earning for buyer = P minus A]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"What do you mean by valuation based on profit? List any two properties for which this method is most suitable."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Valuation based on profit is the method in which the capital value of a property is determined by capitalizing the net annual profit earned by the property using an appropriate Years' Purchase (YP). The formula is $CV = \text{Net Annual Profit} \times YP$, where $YP = 1/i$ and $i$ is the reasonable rate of return.
- **Two suitable properties (1 mark):** (i) Cinema halls / Theatres / Multiplexes and (ii) Hotels / Commercial complexes.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between Gross Profit method and Net Profit method of valuation with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Gross Profit method (1.5 marks):** Only operational outgoings (taxes, repairs, insurance, management) are deducted. Depreciation and interest on capital are *not* deducted. Example: Valuation of a small shop rented out for a short term.
- **Net Profit method (1.5 marks):** Depreciation and interest on capital are *also* deducted. This is more realistic and is used for long-life commercial properties like hotels and cinemas.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Choice 1 — Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the procedure for valuation of a cinema building by the Net Profit method. Why is the sinking fund deducted in such cases?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Data Collection:** Estimate the gross annual income from ticket sales, canteen, advertisements, and parking.

**Step 2 — Outgoings (excluding SF):** Sum up property tax, insurance, repairs (≈ 5%–7.5% of gross income for cinema), management staff salary, lighting, depreciation, and interest on capital.

**Step 3 — Net Profit (before SF):** $NP = \text{Gross Income} - \text{Total Outgoings}$.

**Step 4 — Compute Sinking Fund:** A cinema has a finite life (≈ 20–30 years), so the investor must recoup the capital. Annual SF = Scrap Value × Sinking Fund Factor, where $SFF = \dfrac{i_{SF}}{(1+i_{SF})^n - 1}$.

**Step 5 — Net Profit after SF:** $NP_{SF} = NP - \text{Annual SF}$.

**Step 6 — Capitalize:** $CV = NP_{SF} \times YP$, where $YP = 1/i$ at the reasonable rate of return (usually 6%).

**Why Sinking Fund is deducted (2 marks within part-a):**
A cinema building has a limited useful life. The buyer of the property must create a fund to *recover the original capital* at the end of the building's life. If sinking fund is not deducted, the capitalized value would be overestimated, as the buyer would be earning the *full* net profit without setting aside anything for capital replacement. In essence, deducting the SF converts the case from a perpetuity to a **limited-life annuity**, which is the actual economic situation.

**(b) [7 Marks] A hotel has a gross annual income of ₹ 8,00,000. The annual expenses are: Taxes ₹ 60,000, Insurance ₹ 20,000, Maintenance ₹ 90,000, Management ₹ 70,000. The building will last for 25 years with a scrap value of ₹ 2,00,000. Assume a reasonable return of 6% and sinking fund rate of 3%. Find the capitalized value of the hotel.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Outgoings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Outgoings} &= 60,000 + 20,000 + 90,000 + 70,000 = 2,40,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Profit (before SF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NP &= 8,00,000 - 2,40,000 = 5,60,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sinking Fund Factor at 3% for 25 years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.03)^{25} &= 2.0938 \\
SFF &= \frac{0.03}{2.0938 - 1} = \frac{0.03}{1.0938} = 0.02743
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Sinking Fund:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 2,00,000 \times 0.02743 = 5,486
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Profit after SF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NP_{SF} &= 5,60,000 - 5,486 = 5,54,514
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — YP at 6%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
YP &= \frac{1}{0.06} = 16.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Capitalized Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 5,54,514 \times 16.6667 = 92,41,900 \\
&\approx \text{₹ 92.42 Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** The capitalized value of the hotel is **₹ 92,41,900 (≈ ₹ 92.42 lakhs)**.

---

### Choice 2 — Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks] What is Years' Purchase (YP)? Derive the relation $YP = 1/i$ and explain its significance in the profit method of valuation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Years' Purchase (YP) is the number of years' purchase of the net annual income/profit, used as a multiplier to convert a periodic income into a capital sum. Mathematically, $YP = 1/i$, where $i$ is the rate of interest per unit currency per annum.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $P$ = net annual profit, $V$ = capital value, and $i$ = reasonable rate of return. For the buyer, investing $V$ at rate $i$ should yield an income equal to the net profit from the property, otherwise the investment is not attractive. Equating the two:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \times i &= P \\
V &= \frac{P}{i} = P \times \frac{1}{i} \\
V &= P \times YP
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):** YP acts as a *discount factor* — it converts future recurring income into a single present-day capital sum. A higher rate of return gives a lower YP and hence a lower valuation; a lower rate of return gives a higher YP and a higher valuation.

**(b) [7 Marks] A property yields a gross annual rent of ₹ 5,00,000. Annual outgoings are: Tax 8% of gross rent, Insurance ₹ 10,000, Repairs 5% of gross rent, Management ₹ 15,000. The owner also provides a depreciation allowance of ₹ 25,000 per year. Calculate the capitalized value of the property using (i) Gross Profit method, and (ii) Net Profit method. Take the rate of return as 6% per annum.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Outgoings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tax} &= 8\% \times 5,00,000 = 40,000 \\
\text{Repairs} &= 5\% \times 5,00,000 = 25,000 \\
\text{Insurance} &= 10,000 \\
\text{Management} &= 15,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Gross Profit Method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outgoings (excl. Depreciation)} &= 40,000 + 25,000 + 10,000 + 15,000 = 90,000 \\
\text{Gross Profit} &= 5,00,000 - 90,000 = 4,10,000 \\
CV_{Gross} &= 4,10,000 \times 16.6667 = 68,33,347 \\
&\approx \text{₹ 68.33 Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Net Profit Method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Outgoings (incl. Depreciation)} &= 90,000 + 25,000 = 1,15,000 \\
\text{Net Profit} &= 5,00,000 - 1,15,000 = 3,85,000 \\
CV_{Net} &= 3,85,000 \times 16.6667 = 64,16,679 \\
&\approx \text{₹ 64.17 Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $CV_{Gross} = \text{₹ 68.33 Lakhs}$ and $CV_{Net} = \text{₹ 64.17 Lakhs}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (3 marks lost per exam paper!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Sinking Fund:** For limited-life properties (cinema, hotel), not deducting the Annual SF leads to **overvaluation by 3%–8%** — instant loss of 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Gross vs. Net Profit:** The Net Profit method *must* include depreciation and interest on capital as outgoings. Forgetting them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **YP Unit Mismatch:** Always write YP as **16.67 years** (a pure number, not in currency). The CV is the only figure that should carry the ₹ symbol.
> 4. **Skipping the SFF derivation step:** Board examiners expect the formula $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ to be written explicitly before substitution — losing the derivation costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Core Formula:** $CV = \text{Net Annual Profit} \times YP$ (master this equation first).
- **YP Identity:** $YP = 1/i$. At 6%, YP = **16.67**; at 5%, YP = **20**; at 8%, YP = **12.5**.
- **Three Variants:** Gross Profit (excludes depreciation and interest), Net Profit (includes both), and Average Profit (used when yearly income fluctuates).
- **Outgoings Order:** Tax → Insurance → Repairs → Management → Vacancy → Depreciation → Interest on Capital.
- **Sinking Fund is mandatory** for properties with stated finite life; scrap value and SF rate must be given in the problem.
- **SFF Formula:** $\dfrac{i_{SF}}{(1+i_{SF})^n - 1}$ — do not confuse with YP.
- **Best-suited properties:** Cinemas, Hotels, Theatres, Multiplexes, Shopping Malls, IT Parks.
- **Not suitable for:** Purely residential owner-occupied houses, agricultural land, government buildings.
- **Examiner's Favourite Question Pattern:** "Calculate the capitalized value of a cinema / hotel given income, outgoings, life, scrap value, and rates." (Directly test the four-stage pipeline.)
- **Unit Convention:** YP is in *years*, NP in *₹/year*, CV in *₹*.
- **Conservative Practice:** Net Profit Method with SF is the *most academically preferred* approach in KTU valuation problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depreciation Method; Valuation of Land (Brief Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET601 - QUANTUM SURVEYING AND VALUATION *(Corrected: QUANTITY SURVEYING AND VALUATION)* > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Depreciation Method; Valuation of Land (Brief Description Only) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Depreciation — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Depreciation** is the permanent, continuous, and gradual decrease in the **market value** of a property (building, machinery, or structure) due to one or more of the following causes: wear and tear, age, decay, obsolescence, structural deterioration, or passage of time. It represents the **loss in worth** of an asset from its **original cost** to its **present (residual/scrap) value** at any given point in its useful life. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, depreciation is fundamentally an **economic concept** used to determine the **present depreciated value (D.V.)** of a building or property — which forms the **primary input** in the **Rental Method of Valuation** (i.e., D.V. = Net Income / Years' Purchase). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Depreciation is the *core computational engine* behind the income-based (rental) valuation method. A clear understanding of the depreciation formula is mandatory for solving any 14-mark rental valuation problem in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ageing Car" Imagine you buy a brand-new car for ₹10,00,000. After 5 years, the same car in the used-car market fetches only ₹6,00,000 — not because it stopped working, but because it has: - **Worn-out tyres, brakes, and clutch** (Wear and Tear) - **An outdated music system / no touchscreen** (Obsolescence) - **Cosmetic dents and faded paint** (Decay) The loss of ₹4,00,000 spread over 5 years (≈ ₹80,000/year) is **depreciation**. A building behaves the *exact same way* — except it depreciates much slower because it has a 60–100 year life. ## 1.3 Valuation of Land — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Valuation of Land** is the scientific process of estimating the **fair market price** of a vacant plot of land at a given point in time. Unlike buildings, land is **non-depreciable** (it does not wear out) and is therefore valued using **unique, area-centric methods** such as the Belting Method, Development Method, Direct Comparison Method, and Government Guideline Value. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Gold Versus the Jewellery" Think of **land as gold biscuits** and **buildings as gold jewellery**. The biscuits (land) only appreciate with time and never lose intrinsic value. But the jewellery (building) tarnishes, breaks, and goes out of fashion. So land and buildings are valued using *entirely different* mathematical tools. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Land ≠ Building in valuation.** This is the most common conceptual error KTU students make. Land has **infinite life**, no scrap value, and **no depreciation** — its value is purely driven by **location, area, and market comparables**. ## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Life of a Building** = **60 to 100 years** (RCC structures) and **40 to 60 years** (Load-bearing / wooden structures) - **Scrap Value (Salvage Value)** = usually **10% of the original cost** of the building (assumed standard unless stated otherwise) - **Years' Purchase (Y.P.)** = the present value of ₹1 per annum received for *n* years at a given rate of interest - **Guideline Value** = officially notified by the **Government of Kerala** (Basic Value Register) per **Are (100 m²)** or per **Cent (40.47 m²)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why is Depreciation Needed in Valuation? A valuer's *real* task is to find the **Present Worth (Present Value)** of a building. Since the building's *future* earning capacity keeps reducing every year (due to ageing), the valuer must **deduct the accumulated depreciation** from the original construction cost to arrive at the **Depreciated Value (D.V.)**. This Depreciated Value is then plugged into the **Standard Rent Formula**: $$ \text{Rent} = \frac{\text{D.V.} \times \text{Y.P. at } S\% \text{ for } n \text{ years}}{\text{Number of years' purchase}} $$ *(Where S = standard rate of interest, n = remaining life)* ## 2.2 Classification of Depreciation Methods There are **four principal methods** prescribed in the KTU syllabus for computing depreciation of a building: | Sl. No. | Method | Core Logic | Best Suited For | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Straight Line Method (SLM)** | Equal annual depreciation over the full life | Simple, short-life assets | | 2 | **Constant Percentage / Declining Balance Method (DBM / WDV)** | Fixed % of *remaining* (book) value is written off each year | Buildings, IT equipment | | 3 | **Sinking Fund Method (SFM)** | Depreciation = Annual sinking fund deposit + Interest earned on accumulated fund | Long-life public buildings, Govt. properties | | 4 | **Annuity Method** | Depreciation = Annual instalment of the *capital recovery* (compound interest + principal repayment) | Long-life assets with known interest rate | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — at least 2 of these formulas appear in every KTU ESE paper.** | Method | Annual Depreciation (D) | Depreciated Value after *n* years (D.V.) | Key Symbols | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Straight Line Method** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $D.V. = C - n \cdot D = C - \dfrac{n(C-S)}{n}$ | C = Cost, S = Scrap, n = Life | | **Constant Percentage Method** | $D = C \cdot \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}\right)$ | $D.V. = C \cdot \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{n/n} = S$ | Rate *r* = constant % | | **Sinking Fund Method** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n} + S \cdot i \cdot \left(\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{n}$ | D.V. = Original Cost − Sinking Fund Accumulated | i = interest rate | | **Annuity Method** | $D = (C - S) \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} + S \cdot i$ | D.V. = Original Cost − ΣD (cumulative) | i = interest rate | > [!NOTE] > **Simplified Sinking Fund Formula (most used in KTU):** > $$D = \frac{C - S}{n} + \text{Interest on Accumulated Fund}$$ > $$D.V. = C - \left[ \frac{C - S}{n} \cdot n + \text{Sinking Fund Accumulated} \right]$$ > **Sinking Fund Accumulated after *n* years:** > $$Q = \frac{q \cdot \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right]}{i}$$ > where $q$ is the annual deposit. ## 2.4 Methods of Valuation of Land (Brief Description) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 syllabus, this is a **Brief Description Only** topic — focus on the *concept*, *applicable condition*, and *key formula* for each method. ### (A) Belting Method - **Concept:** The plot is divided into belts (strips) based on **depth from the road**. Front belts near the road have higher value; rear belts have progressively lower value. - **Formula (Trapezoidal Rule):** $$\text{Land Value} = b \cdot d \cdot (F_1 + F_n) + 2 \cdot b \cdot d \cdot (F_2 + F_3 + \dots)$$ where $b$ = width of belt, $d$ = depth of each belt, $F_n$ = rate of that belt. - **Use:** Narrow plots abutting a road. ### (B) Development Method (or Hypothetical Method) - **Concept:** Value of developed land is found by deducting the **development cost** (filling, levelling, fencing, road, drainage) and **developer's profit** from the **anticipated selling price** of the developed plot. - **Formula:** $$\text{Land Value} = \text{Total Sale Proceeds} - \text{Development Cost} - \text{Developer's Profit (15\% to 20\%)}$$ - **Use:** Large undeveloped estates, sites in interior areas. ### (C) Direct Comparison Method (Sales Comparison Method) - **Concept:** Compare recent **sale prices of similar plots** in the same locality, then adjust for differences in size, location, frontage, and amenities. - **Formula:** $$\text{Value} = \text{Price of comparable plot} \times \frac{\text{Area of subject plot}}{\text{Area of comparable plot}} \times \text{Adjustment Factor}$$ - **Use:** Active real-estate markets, urban plots. ### (D) Guideline Value Method - **Concept:** Adopt the **Government-notified Guideline Value** (Basic Value Register by Kerala Revenue Department) for the locality and apply it to the area of the plot. - **Formula:** $$\text{Land Value} = \text{Guideline Value (per Are / Cent)} \times \text{Area of plot}$$ - **Use:** Stamp duty assessment, mortgage valuation, all legal transactions in Kerala. ### (E) Area Basis / Abstractive Method - **Concept:** Total property value is split into **land value + building value**. Building cost is computed from the *current construction rate* (PWD Scheduled Rate); remaining value is attributed to land. - **Formula:** $$\text{Land Value} = \text{Total Market Price} - \text{Depreciated Building Cost}$$ - **Use:** Old buildings where market value is known but land value is needed separately. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bank Loans:** Banks approve loan amounts based on the **depreciated value** of mortgaged buildings. - **Insurance:** Insurance premium and claim settlement use the **replacement cost minus accumulated depreciation**. - **Income Tax:** Depreciation is an allowable expense under **Section 32 of the Income Tax Act, 1961**. - **Rent Fixing:** Standard rent is calculated using the **Rental Method**, which is *entirely* based on the **depreciated value**. - **Land Acquisition:** Government acquires land for public projects at **Guideline Value × Area + Solatium** under the **Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Straight Line Method (SLM) ### Given - Original Cost of Building $= C$ - Scrap Value at the end of useful life $= S$ - Useful life of building $= n$ years - **Assumption:** Equal amount is written off every year. ### Step 1: Total Depreciation Over the Life The building must lose value from $C$ to $S$ over $n$ years. $$ \text{Total Depreciation} = C - S $$ ### Step 2: Annual Depreciation (D) Since the loss is uniform per year: $$ D = \frac{C - S}{n} $$ ### Step 3: Depreciated Value after *t* years If the building has aged by $t$ years, total depreciation incurred = $t \cdot D$. $$ \begin{aligned} D.V._{(t)} &= C - t \cdot D \\ &= C - t \cdot \left(\frac{C - S}{n}\right) \\ &= C - \frac{t}{n}(C - S) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Verification (boundary condition) At $t = 0$ (new building): $D.V. = C - 0 = C$ ✓ (no depreciation) At $t = n$ (end of life): $D.V. = C - (C - S) = S$ ✓ (only scrap value left) ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Percentage (Declining Balance) Method ### Given - Original Cost $= C$ - Scrap Value $= S$ - Life $= n$ years - Let $r$ = fixed annual percentage of the *diminishing* book value. ### Step 1: Recursive Formula for Book Value After Year 1: $V_1 = C(1 - r)$ After Year 2: $V_2 = V_1(1 - r) = C(1 - r)^2$ After Year *n*: $V_n = C(1 - r)^n$ ### Step 2: Apply Boundary Condition At the end of life, the book value must equal the scrap value $S$. $$ S = C(1 - r)^n $$ ### Step 3: Solve for *r* $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\frac{S}{C}\right) &= (1 - r)^n \\ \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} &= 1 - r \\ r &= 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Annual Depreciation in Year *t* $$ D_t = C \cdot (1 - r)^{t-1} \cdot r $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is this method also called Written Down Value (WDV)?** Because every year the depreciation is computed on the *written down* (i.e., reduced) book value, not the original cost. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sinking Fund Method (SFM) ### Given - Cost $= C$, Scrap $= S$, Life $= n$ years, Interest rate $= i$ per annum. ### Step 1: Concept The owner sets aside a **fixed annual deposit $q$** into a sinking fund such that the *principal* of $C - S$ is recovered at the end of $n$ years, *plus* interest on the accumulated fund. ### Step 2: Principal Recovery Component A simple way to recover $C - S$ over $n$ years is by straight line: $$ q_1 = \frac{C - S}{n} $$ ### Step 3: Total Amount Recovered in Sinking Fund after *n* years With compound interest at rate $i$, the accumulated value of the sinking fund is: $$ Q = q \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} $$ where $q$ is the annual sinking fund instalment. ### Step 4: Equate Principal to Accumulated Fund The amount deposited should equal the principal to be recovered: $$ q \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} = C - S $$ ### Step 5: Solve for Annual Sinking Fund Instalment *q* $$ q = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} $$ ### Step 6: Total Annual Depreciation (D) This is the *instalment + interest lost on the book value remaining*: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= q + (C - S) \cdot i \quad \text{(approximate form)} \\ D &= \frac{C - S}{n} + i \cdot \left[\text{Accumulated Sinking Fund at end of } t \text{ years}\right] \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7: Depreciated Value after *t* years $$ D.V._{(t)} = C - \left[ q \cdot t + i \cdot \frac{q \cdot \left[(1+i)^{t} - 1\right]}{i} \right] $$ Simplifying: $$ D.V._{(t)} = C - q \cdot t - q \cdot \left[(1+i)^{t} - 1\right] $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Annuity Method ### Given - Cost $= C$, Scrap $= S$, Life $= n$ years, Interest $= i$ per annum. ### Step 1: Concept The building is treated as a **financial annuity**. The owner receives a fixed annual income (rent) and pays an *annuity* that simultaneously returns the principal $(C - S)$ and the interest on the *outstanding capital*. ### Step 2: Annual Instalment of Capital Recovery The present worth of ₹1 per annum for $n$ years at $i\%$ is the annuity factor: $$ A = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} $$ ### Step 3: Annual Recovery of Principal The annual instalment that recovers $(C - S)$ over $n$ years is: $$ q = \frac{C - S}{A} = (C - S) \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} $$ ### Step 4: Interest Component Interest is paid on the *original cost* (in Year 1) and gradually on the *outstanding balance*. Approximated as: $$ \text{Interest per year} = C \cdot i $$ ### Step 5: Total Annual Depreciation $$ \begin{aligned} D &= q + (\text{Interest on Outstanding Capital}) \\ D &\approx (C - S) \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} + S \cdot i \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sinking Fund vs Annuity Method — the key difference:** > - In **Sinking Fund Method**, the interest is earned on the *accumulated fund*. > - In **Annuity Method**, the interest is paid on the *outstanding capital**. > - The Annuity Method **always yields a higher annual depreciation** than the Sinking Fund Method. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Question:** A building was constructed at a cost of ₹10,00,000. After 80 years, its scrap value is ₹2,00,000. Find the **Annual Depreciation** and **Depreciated Value after 20 years** using: > (a) Straight Line Method > (b) Constant Percentage Method > (c) Sinking Fund Method at 5% interest ### Given - $C$ = ₹10,00,000 - $S$ = ₹2,00,000 - $n$ = 80 years - $t$ = 20 years (for D.V. calculation) - $i$ = 5% = 0.05 (for SFM) ### (a) Straight Line Method $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{10,00,000 - 2,00,000}{80} = \frac{8,00,000}{80} = ₹10,000 \text{ per year} \\[6pt] D.V._{(20)} &= C - t \cdot D = 10,00,000 - 20 \times 10,000 = 10,00,000 - 2,00,000 = ₹8,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ ### (b) Constant Percentage Method $$ \begin{aligned} r &= 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\frac{2,00,000}{10,00,000}\right)^{1/80} = 1 - (0.2)^{1/80} \end{aligned} $$ Computing $(0.2)^{1/80}$: $$ \ln(0.2) = -1.6094 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{-1.6094}{80} = -0.02012 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-0.02012} = 0.9801 $$ $$ \therefore r = 1 - 0.9801 = 0.0199 = 1.99\% \text{ per year} $$ $$ D.V._{(20)} = C \cdot (1 - r)^{20} = 10,00,000 \cdot (0.9801)^{20} $$ Computing $(0.9801)^{20}$: $$ 20 \cdot \ln(0.9801) = 20 \cdot (-0.0201) = -0.402 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-0.402} = 0.669 $$ $$ \therefore D.V._{(20)} = 10,00,000 \times 0.669 = ₹6,69,000 \text{ (approximately)} $$ ### (c) Sinking Fund Method at 5% Annual Sinking Fund Instalment: $$ q = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \frac{8,00,000 \times 0.05}{(1.05)^{80} - 1} $$ Compute $(1.05)^{80}$: $$ 80 \cdot \ln(1.05) = 80 \times 0.04879 = 3.9032 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{3.9032} = 49.56 $$ $$ \therefore (1.05)^{80} - 1 = 48.56 $$ $$ q = \frac{8,00,000 \times 0.05}{48.56} = \frac{40,000}{48.56} = ₹823.7 \text{ per year (deposit only)} $$ Total Annual Depreciation (Year 1) ≈ $q + i(C - S) = 823.7 + 0.05 \times 8,00,000 = 823.7 + 40,000 = ₹40,823.7$ per year. $$ \therefore D \approx ₹40,824 \text{ per year (Year 1)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison Insight:** Notice that for the same building, SLM gives ₹10,000/year, while SFM gives ₹40,824/year in Year 1. The SFM depreciation *increases* with time (because the accumulated fund earns more interest), while SLM stays constant. This is why SFM is **unfair to long-life buildings** in early years but is the most **scientific method for long-term assets** like government buildings and bridges. ## 3.6 Worked Example — Belting Method for Land Valuation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Question:** A rectangular plot has a road frontage of 20 m and depth of 30 m. The plot is divided into three belts, each 10 m deep. The rates per square metre are: Belt 1 (front) = ₹5,000/m², Belt 2 (middle) = ₹4,000/m², Belt 3 (rear) = ₹3,000/m². Find the total value of the plot. ### Solution **Step 1:** Identify dimensions - Width of each belt $b = 20$ m - Depth of each belt $d = 10$ m - Total depth = 30 m **Step 2:** Area of each belt $$ A_1 = A_2 = A_3 = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3:** Value of each belt $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= 200 \times 5,000 = ₹10,00,000 \\ V_2 &= 200 \times 4,000 = ₹8,00,000 \\ V_3 &= 200 \times 3,000 = ₹6,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Total Value of Land $$ \text{Total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 10,00,000 + 8,00,000 + 6,00,000 = ₹24,00,000 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Average rate per m²** = ₹24,00,000 / 600 m² = **₹4,000/m²**, which lies between the front and rear rates — a sanity check that the calculation is correct. ## 3.7 Comparative Analysis Table | Method | Depreciation Pattern | Fair to New Building? | Fair to Old Building? | Best Application | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | Straight Line | Constant every year | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Short-life assets | | Constant Percentage (DBM) | Decreasing every year | ❌ High burden | ✅ Low burden | Buildings, machinery | | Sinking Fund | Increasing every year | ✅ Low burden | ❌ High burden | Long-life Govt. assets | | Annuity | Increasing (similar to SFM) | ✅ Low burden | ❌ High burden | Long-life assets with known *i* | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Classification of Depreciation Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Depreciation of a Building]) --> Methods Methods{Choose Method Based on
Asset Type and Life} Methods -->|Simple / Short Life| SLM[Straight Line Method
Equal Annual Deduction] Methods -->|Long Life Building| DBM[Declining Balance Method
Constant Percentage of WDV] Methods -->|Public / Govt Asset| SFM[Sinking Fund Method
Equal Annual Deposit + Interest] Methods -->|Known Interest Rate| ANM[Annuity Method
Capital Recovery + Interest] SLM --> Formula1[D = C - S divided by n] DBM --> Formula2[r = 1 - S to power 1/n divided by C] SFM --> Formula3[q equals C minus S times i
divided by 1+i to power n minus 1] ANM --> Formula4[D equals capital recovery
plus interest on outstanding] Formula1 --> DV1[D.V. = C minus t into D] Formula2 --> DV2[D.V. = C into 1 minus r to power t] Formula3 --> DV3[D.V. = C minus accumulated fund] Formula4 --> DV4[D.V. = C minus cumulative annuity] DV1 --> End([Present Depreciated Value
Used in Rental Method]) DV2 --> End DV3 --> End DV4 --> End style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style End fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Methods fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style SLM fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style DBM fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style SFM fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style ANM fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style Formula1 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style Formula2 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style Formula3 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style Formula4 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style DV1 fill:#E6E6FA,color:#000 style DV2 fill:#E6E6FA,color:#000 style DV3 fill:#E6E6FA,color:#000 style DV4 fill:#E6E6FA,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Land Valuation Methods (Brief Description) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Plot of Land] --> B{Valuation Method
Selection} B -->|Narrow plot
with road frontage| C[Belting Method
Trapezoidal Rule] B -->|Large undeveloped
estate| D[Development Method
Hypothetical Sale] B -->|Active urban
real estate| E[Direct Comparison
Sales Method] B -->|Legal / Govt
transaction| F[Guideline Value
Kerala Basic Value] B -->|Old building
plot| G[Area Basis
Abstractive Method] C --> H1[Front Belt: Higher Rate] C --> H2[Middle Belt: Moderate Rate] C --> H3[Rear Belt: Lower Rate] H1 --> I[Total Land Value] H2 --> I H3 --> I D --> J[Anticipated Sale Price] J --> K[Less Development Cost] K --> L[Less Developer Profit 15 to 20 percent] L --> I E --> M[Comparable Sale Records] M --> N[Adjustments for Size
Location and Frontage] N --> I F --> O[Govt Notified Rate
per Are or per Cent] O --> P[Multiply by Plot Area] P --> I G --> Q[Total Market Price] Q --> R[Less Depreciated
Building Cost] R --> I I[Final Land Value]:::result style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style I fill:#90EE90,color:#000,stroke:#000 classDef result fill:#90EE90,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Depreciation Feeds into Rental Valuation ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT1[Original Cost of Building C] --> ENGINE INPUT2[Scrap Value S] --> ENGINE INPUT3[Life of Building n years] --> ENGINE INPUT4[Interest Rate i percent] --> ENGINE INPUT5[Age of Building t years] --> ENGINE ENGINE{Depreciation Engine
Which method to apply?} ENGINE -->|SLM| A1[Annual D equals C minus S divided by n] ENGINE -->|DBM| A2[Rate r equals 1 minus S by C to power 1 by n] ENGINE -->|SFM| A3[Instalment q from compound interest formula] ENGINE -->|Annuity| A4[Annuity factor A formula] A1 --> DV[D.V. = Present Depreciated Value] A2 --> DV A3 --> DV A4 --> DV DV --> RENT[Annual Rent = D.V. into Y.P. divided by 12] NETRENT[Net Rent = Gross Rent minus all outgoings] --> RENT RENT --> CAPITAL[Capitalised Value = Net Rent into Y.P.] YP[Years Purchase from Y.P. table] --> CAPITAL CAPITAL --> FINAL([Final Valuation of Property]):::result style INPUT1 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style INPUT2 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style INPUT3 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style INPUT4 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style INPUT5 fill:#FFFACD,color:#000 style ENGINE fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style DV fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style FINAL fill:#90EE90,color:#000,stroke:#000 classDef result fill:#90EE90,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram:** The block diagram shows the *seamless data flow* from the **5 raw inputs** (cost, scrap, life, interest, age) through the **Depreciation Engine** to the **Depreciated Value**, which is the *single most important* intermediate output in valuation. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Question Pattern Reference:** Tagged questions are modelled on actual KTU University Examination papers (2019–2024 scheme) and follow the **2024 Scheme ESE pattern** (Part A: 3 marks × 2 questions; Part B: 14 marks × internal choice with sub-parts). --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "depreciation" as applied to valuation of buildings. List the various methods of computing depreciation.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Depreciation is the gradual, permanent decrease in the value of a property (building) due to wear and tear, age, decay, obsolescence, or passage of time, from its **original cost** to its **present (residual/scrap) value** over its useful life. > > **Methods of Depreciation (1.5 Marks):** > 1. **Straight Line Method (SLM)** > 2. **Declining Balance Method (DBM / Constant Percentage Method / WDV)** > 3. **Sinking Fund Method (SFM)** > 4. **Annuity Method** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Briefly explain any two methods of valuation of land.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) > **1. Belting Method (1.5 Marks):** Used for narrow plots abutting a road. The plot is divided into parallel belts/strips of equal depth perpendicular to the road. The front belt (nearest the road) has the highest value and each successive rear belt has a lower value. The total land value is the sum of the areas of all belts multiplied by their respective rates per square metre. > > **2. Guideline Value Method (1.5 Marks):** This method uses the **Government-notified Guideline Value (Basic Value Register)** issued by the Kerala Revenue Department for the locality. The value of the land is simply the product of the guideline rate (per Are or per Cent) and the total area of the plot. This method is widely used for legal transactions, stamp duty assessment, and mortgage valuations. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE provides an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] A residential building was constructed at a cost of **₹20,00,000**. The expected life of the building is **80 years**, and the scrap value at the end of its life is **₹2,00,000**. The building is **25 years old**. #### (a) [7 Marks] Calculate the annual depreciation and the present depreciated value of the building using: 1. Straight Line Method 2. Constant Percentage Method #### (b) [7 Marks] If the standard rate of interest is **5%** per annum, calculate the present depreciated value using the **Sinking Fund Method** and find the **annual rent** (assume Years' Purchase = 1/i = 20 years' purchase). --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** - $C = ₹20,00,000$ - $S = ₹2,00,000$ - $n = 80$ years - $t = 25$ years **(i) Straight Line Method (3.5 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Annual Depreciation } D &= \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{20,00,000 - 2,00,000}{80} = \frac{18,00,000}{80} = ₹22,500 \text{ per year} \\[6pt] D.V._{(25)} &= C - t \cdot D = 20,00,000 - 25 \times 22,500 \\ &= 20,00,000 - 5,62,500 = ₹14,37,500 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Constant Percentage Method (3.5 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} r &= 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\frac{2,00,000}{20,00,000}\right)^{1/80} = 1 - (0.1)^{1/80} \end{aligned} $$ Computing $(0.1)^{1/80}$: $$ \ln(0.1) = -2.3026 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{-2.3026}{80} = -0.02878 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-0.02878} = 0.9716 $$ $$ \therefore r = 1 - 0.9716 = 0.0284 = 2.84\% \text{ per year} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D.V._{(25)} &= C \cdot (1 - r)^{25} = 20,00,000 \cdot (0.9716)^{25} \end{aligned} $$ Computing $(0.9716)^{25}$: $$ 25 \cdot \ln(0.9716) = 25 \times (-0.0288) = -0.720 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-0.720} = 0.4868 $$ $$ \therefore D.V._{(25)} = 20,00,000 \times 0.4868 = ₹9,73,600 $$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Sinking Fund Method at $i = 5\% = 0.05$ (4 Marks):** Annual Sinking Fund Instalment: $$ q = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \frac{18,00,000 \times 0.05}{(1.05)^{80} - 1} $$ From our earlier calculation, $(1.05)^{80} = 49.56$, so $(1.05)^{80} - 1 = 48.56$: $$ q = \frac{18,00,000 \times 0.05}{48.56} = \frac{90,000}{48.56} = ₹1,853.4 \text{ per year (deposit only)} $$ For Year 1, total annual depreciation: $$ D_{\text{Year 1}} = q + i(C - S) = 1,853.4 + 0.05 \times 18,00,000 = 1,853.4 + 90,000 = ₹91,853 \text{ (approximately)} $$ **Annual Rent Calculation (3 Marks):** Years' Purchase (Y.P.) at 5% interest for the remaining life (assume 55 years remaining, or use given Y.P. = 20): $$ \text{Annual Rent} = D.V. \times \frac{Y.P.}{12} $$ Assuming SFM gives depreciated value = $C - (C - S) = S = ₹2,00,000$ (since SFM recovers the *entire* principal via the sinking fund over 80 years), the *present* book value after 25 years (approximated) = $C - 25 \times q = 20,00,000 - 25 \times 1,853.4 = 20,00,000 - 46,335 = ₹19,53,665$ (only the sinking fund *deposits*, not including accumulated interest, are deducted here — full SFM calculation would also subtract the accumulated fund value). $$ \text{Annual Rent} = \frac{19,53,665 \times 20}{12} = \frac{3,90,73,300}{12} = ₹32,56,108 \text{ per annum} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pitfall 1):** Students often confuse **Years' Purchase (Y.P.)** with the **annuity period (n)**. Y.P. at $i\%$ for $n$ years is $\frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ (a complex value less than $n$). Using $n$ in place of Y.P. is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pitfall 2):** In the Constant Percentage Method, students often compute $r$ as $\frac{1}{n}$ (i.e., 1/80 = 1.25%) — **this is wrong**. The correct formula is $r = 1 - (S/C)^{1/n}$, which gives a *higher* rate (2.84% in our example) precisely because the percentage is applied to the *diminishing* balance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pitfall 3):** In SFM, students frequently omit the **interest component** of annual depreciation. Always remember: $D = q + i \times \text{Outstanding Capital}$. Forgetting the $i(C - S)$ term is a guaranteed **3-mark deduction**. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] A vacant plot of land in a suburban locality measures **30 m × 20 m** and is to be valued. The plot has a road frontage of **30 m**. #### (a) [7 Marks] Value the plot using the **Belting Method**, given that the plot is divided into **3 belts of equal depth** with the following rates per square metre: - Belt 1 (front, near road): ₹6,000/m² - Belt 2 (middle): ₹4,500/m² - Belt 3 (rear): ₹3,000/m² #### (b) [7 Marks] Verify the result using the **Average Rate Method** (i.e., simple area × uniform rate), and also compute the plot's value using the **Kerala Government Guideline Value** of **₹4,00,000 per Are**, comparing both with the Belting Method result. --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Belting Method — 7 Marks **Given:** - Total area = 30 × 20 = 600 m² - Road frontage (width) $b = 30$ m - Plot depth = 20 m - Number of belts = 3, so each belt depth $d = 20/3 = 6.67$ m **Step 1: Area of each belt** $$ A_1 = A_2 = A_3 = b \cdot d = 30 \times 6.67 = 200 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Value of each belt** $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= 200 \times 6,000 = ₹12,00,000 \\ V_2 &= 200 \times 4,500 = ₹9,00,000 \\ V_3 &= 200 \times 3,000 = ₹6,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Total value** $$ \text{Total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 12,00,000 + 9,00,000 + 6,00,000 = ₹27,00,000 $$ > **Average rate** = 27,00,000 / 600 = **₹4,500/m²** #### Part (b) — Comparison — 7 Marks **(i) Average Rate Method (2 Marks):** $$ \text{Value} = \text{Total Area} \times \text{Uniform Rate} = 600 \times 4,500 = ₹27,00,000 $$ **Note:** This matches the Belting result *only* because the rate was specifically chosen as the average — in practice, the average rate is derived from the Belting result, not the reverse. **(ii) Guideline Value Method (3 Marks):** Convert area to **Are** (1 Are = 100 m²): $$ \text{Area in Are} = \frac{600}{100} = 6 \text{ Ares} $$ $$ \text{Land Value} = 6 \times 4,00,000 = ₹24,00,000 $$ **(iii) Comparison (2 Marks):** | Method | Land Value | Remarks | |:---|:---|:---| | Belting Method | ₹27,00,000 | Most realistic — accounts for frontage premium | | Average Rate Method | ₹27,00,000 | Same as Belting (by design) | | Guideline Value Method | ₹24,00,000 | Conservative — used for stamp duty only | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The Belting Method gives a higher value because it captures the **frontage premium** — the extra worth of the road-side belt, which the flat Guideline Value ignores. The Guideline Value is the legal floor for stamp duty and registration but is usually **10–20% below the market value**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pitfall 4):** In Belt Method problems, students often forget to convert **depth correctly** when belts are *unequal* (e.g., 6 m + 6 m + 8 m). Each belt's area must be **width × its own depth**, not width × (total depth / number of belts). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pitfall 5):** In Guideline Value Method, students confuse **Are** (100 m²) with **Cent** (40.47 m²). Kerala Revenue Department uses **Are** officially, while Tamil Nadu and some other states use **Cent**. Always check the unit specified in the question. Mixing units = **full 7-mark loss** for that sub-part. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — 60-Second Rapid Recap** ### 🔑 Depreciation — The Five Non-Negotiables 1. **Definition:** Permanent, gradual loss in value from $C$ to $S$ over $n$ years. 2. **Straight Line Method (SLM) formula:** $D = (C - S)/n$ — *constant annual deduction*. 3. **Constant Percentage Method (DBM) formula:** $r = 1 - (S/C)^{1/n}$ — *decreasing annual deduction*. 4. **Sinking Fund Method (SFM) formula:** $q = (C - S) \cdot i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ — *deposit + interest*. 5. **Annuity Method formula:** $D = (C - S) \cdot A + S \cdot i$ — *capital recovery + interest*. ### 🔑 Land Valuation — The Five Methods (Brief) 1. **Belting Method** — Used for *narrow plots with road frontage*; trapezoidal rule. 2. **Development Method** — Used for *undeveloped land*; sale price − development cost − profit. 3. **Direct Comparison Method** — Used in *active markets*; compare with recent similar sales. 4. **Guideline Value Method** — Kerala Govt. *Basic Value Register* × area; legal floor value. 5. **Abstractive / Area Basis Method** — *Total value − depreciated building cost* = land value. ### 🔑 Common Conversion Factors (Bookmark This) - **1 Are** = **100 m²** - **1 Cent** = **40.47 m²** (or **1/100 of an Acre**) - **1 Acre** = **4046.86 m²** = **40.47 Ares** = **100 Cents** - **1 Hectare** = **10,000 m²** = **100 Ares** = **247.1 Cents** ### 🔑 The Golden Rule of Depreciation > *"The total depreciation over the useful life of a building is ALWAYS equal to (Original Cost − Scrap Value), regardless of the method used."* Verify every method by checking that the boundary conditions $D.V._{(0)} = C$ and $D.V._{(n)} = S$ are satisfied. ### 🔑 KTU Exam Strategy Pointers - In **SLM problems**, you can solve in 2 lines — don't waste time on long derivations. - In **SFM problems**, show all 4 steps clearly: $q$ calculation → $Q$ accumulation → total $D$ → final D.V. - In **Belting Method**, draw a neat *sketch* of the plot with belts shaded — examiners award **bonus 1 mark** for a clean diagram. - Always state the **assumed life** of the building (60/80/100 years) at the start of your answer. - For **land valuation**, always mention whether you used Are or Cent — *units are marks-deciding*. - **Avoid late-binding errors**: If the problem gives "Y.P. = 20", use it as **20** (not 1/0.05 = 20 — that's coincidentally the same here, but the concept is different!). ### 🔑 Memorization Mnemonics - **"SDSA"** → **S**traight, **D**eclining, **S**inking, **A**nnuity (order of methods). - **"BDDGA"** → **B**elting, **D**evelopment, **D**irect, **G**uideline, **A**bstractive (land methods). - **"CSn"** → **C**ost − **S**crap, divided by **n** (SLM formula in 3 letters).
